problem: Multicellular biological systems require models integrating cell signalling, population behaviour, and the extracellular environment, but existing tools do not bridge intracellular Boolean signalling with agent-based multicellular modelling.
method: Combined Boolean modelling (MaBoSS) for intracellular signalling with agent-based modelling (PhysiCell) using OpenMP parallelization and BioFVM diffusion solver, simulating off-lattice cells as soft spheres in 2D/3D.
finding: PhysiBoSS bridges single-cell genotype to emergent multicellular behaviour; demonstrated heterogeneous cell fate decisions under TNF treatment and showed spatial resource competition (oxygen) critically influences population dynamics.
dataset: Simulated tumour cell populations with TNF-induced cell fate Boolean model (Calzone et al. 2010)
metric: Cell fate ratios (survival/apoptosis/NonACD), population dynamics over time
result: Scales to 10^5–10^6 cells; hours of compute for thousands of cells over several simulated days

problem: The nature of cellular constraints driving metabolic rewiring in compartmentalised eukaryal cells, particularly the Crabtree effect and overflow metabolism in yeast, remains undetermined.
method: Comprehensive proteome-constrained genome-scale metabolic model (pcYeast) of S. cerevisiae coupling metabolic fluxes to enzyme synthesis/degradation with compartment-specific protein pool constraints and growth rate maximisation; validated with chemostat and batch experiments.
finding: Under glucose limitation a mitochondrial protein constraint triggers ethanol formation (Crabtree effect); under sugar excess a cytosolic volume constraint dictates overflow metabolism. Active constraints are condition-dependent.
dataset: S. cerevisiae CEN.PK 113-7D, chemostats at D=0.2–0.34 h⁻¹, ~3000 proteins quantified by label-free proteomics
metric: Growth rate (h⁻¹), metabolic fluxes (mmol/gDW/h), proteome mass fractions
result: Correctly predicted mCherry overexpression growth rate reduction; identified distinct mitochondrial vs cytosolic constraints at critical dilution rate

problem: Whether a head-restrained mouse virtual reality system permitting horizontal rotations can reproduce the 2D spatial firing patterns of place, grid, and head-direction cells characteristic of real open arenas.
method: Novel VR system with air-supported Styrofoam ball, 4 LCD screens + 2 projectors, and tetrode electrophysiology from hippocampal CA1 and entorhinal cortex; compared spatial firing properties between VR and matched real environment.
finding: Place and grid cells fired with ~1.4× broader spatial tuning in VR; place cells were more directional in VR; theta-speed relationship was reduced; but firing rate–speed modulation and theta phase precession were similar between VR and real.
dataset: 231 CA1 cells from 7 mice (179 place cells in real, 185 in VR, 154 in both); 11 mice total
metric: Spatial information (bits/spike), spatial tuning width, directional selectivity, theta frequency, phase precession slope
result: Spatial scale ~1.4× larger in VR vs real; fading-beacon task learned within 2–3 weeks (p<0.001, F(3,30)=39.03)

problem: Systematic gene knockout experiments are prohibitively expensive; a computational virtual KO tool is needed to infer gene function from single-cell RNA-seq data without requiring KO samples.
method: scTenifoldKnk constructs a gene regulatory network from wild-type scRNA-seq via principal component regression and tensor decomposition, virtually deletes a target gene, then uses manifold alignment to identify differentially regulated genes.
finding: Virtual KO analysis recapitulates main findings of three real in vivo KO experiments (Nkx2-1, Klf5, Foxl1) and recovers expected gene functions in relevant cell types using only wild-type data.
dataset: Mouse lung epithelial cells (2,312 WT cells, Nkx2-1 KO), mouse intestinal cells (Klf5, Foxl1 KO studies)
metric: Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA), differentially regulated (DR) gene overlap
baseline: Real in vivo KO experiments with scRNA-seq + bulk RNA-seq + ChIP-seq + immunostaining
result: Overall consistency across 3 KO validations; DR genes enriched for expected biological functions (AT1/AT2 markers, gastrointestinal markers)

problem: Need integrative tools to identify individual cells making transitions between states, infer transition-driving genes, and uncover the role of intermediate cell states (ICS) in epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition.
method: QuanTC: unsupervised learning combining consensus clustering, NMF soft clustering, and Cell Plasticity Index (CPI) with multiscale agent-based stochastic modelling of a core EMT gene regulatory network.
finding: ICS-mediated transition trajectories attenuate noise and enhance signal adaptation in EMT; cancer EMT strengthens adaptation while embryogenesis EMT favours effective population transition toward mesenchymal states.
dataset: 12 published single-cell EMT datasets in cancer and embryogenesis (including SCC, A549 lung cancer)
metric: Cell Plasticity Index (CPI), Spearman's rank correlation (threshold 0.64), transition efficiency
baseline: Monocle 3 pseudotime inference, direct clustering methods
result: QuanTC distinguishes transition cells with higher resolution than Monocle 3; reveals trade-off between adaptation, noise attenuation, and transition efficiency

problem: Computational tools for genome design are scarce; algorithms are needed to enable design-simulate-test cycles for genome minimisation using whole-cell models.
method: Two algorithms—Minesweeper (divide-and-conquer) and GAMA (biased genetic algorithm)—iteratively delete genes from the M. genitalium whole-cell model, simulating cell division as the functional assay.
finding: Discovered in silico minimal genomes smaller than JCVI-Syn3.0; identified 10 low essential genes and evidence for at least two distinct M. genitalium in silico minimal genomes.
dataset: M. genitalium whole-cell model (401 genes, 359 protein-coding)
metric: Genome size (gene count), cell division (binary pass/fail), replicate consistency (10 replicates)
baseline: JCVI-Syn3.0 (473 genes), previous reduced Mycoplasma predictions (413 genes)
result: Minesweeper_256 (36% reduction), GAMA_237 (41% reduction); 58,071 total genomes simulated

problem: Whole-cell modelling lacks community standards and software tools, hindering construction and sharing of comprehensive computational cell models.
method: Organised the 2015 Whole-Cell Modeling Summer School to evaluate needs by recoding the M. genitalium whole-cell model in SBML; conducted community discussions to identify gaps.
finding: Identified critical needs: high-performance parallel simulator supporting SBML Multistate/Spatial packages, model design software, data curation tools, and extended SBGN for hybrid diagrams.
dataset: M. genitalium whole-cell model (Karr et al. 2012)
baseline: Existing SBML standards and Karr et al. whole-cell model implementation

problem: The mechanisms by which type IV pili govern formation and dynamics of Neisseria gonorrhoeae microcolonies—from single-cell motility to colony self-assembly—are poorly understood.
method: Agent-based model with explicit pili dynamics, force generation, and pili-pili interactions; Langevin-type equations for cell motion driven by pili retraction forces on surfaces and between cells.
finding: Pili-generated forces are central to colony formation; model reproduces bimodal velocity distributions, persistent motion, and colony merging dynamics consistent with experimental observations.
dataset: N. gonorrhoeae microcolonies (simulated single cells to multi-colony aggregates)
metric: Cell velocity distribution, velocity autocorrelation function (double-exponential fit), colony merging dynamics
baseline: Available experimental observations of N. gonorrhoeae surface motility and colony formation
result: Single-pilus peak velocity ~2 μm/s; short correlation time τ₁~0.1s, long persistence τ₂~seconds; agreement with experiments

problem: Refined biophysical models of C. elegans neuron dynamics are needed to describe electrophysiological processes at cellular and network scales, given the fully mapped nervous system.
method: Hodgkin-Huxley-based models of 15 ion channel types fitted to C. elegans and homologous organism data, combined into whole-cell models of AWCon (chemosensory) and RMD (motor) neurons with intracellular calcium dynamics.
finding: Model reproduces regenerative responses of both neurons; RMD exhibits heterogeneous dynamics including bistable regimes and sustained oscillations, critically regulated by CCA-1 T-type calcium and leakage currents.
dataset: C. elegans neurons AWCon and RMD (electrophysiology data from C. elegans and homologous organisms)
metric: Peak/steady-state current amplitudes, activation/inactivation time constants (least-squares nonlinear fitting)
baseline: Experimental voltage-clamp recordings from C. elegans and heterologous expression systems
result: Reproduced regenerative responses; identified bistable and oscillatory regimes in RMD via CCA-1 and leak current modulation

problem: Rules-based models are needed to explain non-intuitive emergent behaviour of heterogeneous cell populations in dynamic microenvironments, encouraging computational-experimental collaboration.
method: ARCADE: multi-scale agent-based model in Java/MASON with interface-based extensible framework incorporating intracellular metabolism (Michaelis-Menten) and signalling (PLCγ) modules for cell agents in hexagonal grid environments.
finding: Emergent behaviour differs between colony and tissue contexts; parameter variation produces linear, non-linear, and multimodal effects on co-culture competition; cell and population heterogeneity variably impact outcomes.
dataset: Simulated tissue cell populations (healthy, cancerous, cancer stem cell subtypes)
metric: Growth dynamics, population cell counts, spatial distribution over 14-day simulations
baseline: Literature-derived parameters with no additional fitting
result: 14-day simulation runs in 5–10 min CPU; biologically realistic growth dynamics without parameter fitting

problem: How to mechanistically simulate growth rate control and dynamic environmental responses in E. coli by incorporating ppGpp regulation, amino acid biosynthesis, and translation into a whole-cell model.
method: Expanded ODE-based whole-cell model incorporating ppGpp synthesis/degradation kinetics (RelA/SpoT), amino acid biosynthesis with allosteric feedback inhibition, and transcriptional regulation; simulated nutrient upshift/downshift conditions across 16 generations and 16 initial seeds.
finding: Small molecule feedback inhibition pathways, beyond expression regulation, play a role in ppGpp-mediated growth control. Dysregulated amino acid synthesis yields average concentrations matching experiments but reveals unexpected single-cell oscillatory fluctuations. Both nutrient upshift and downshift produce a transient increase in mRNA:rRNA ratio.
dataset: E. coli whole-cell model (16 generations, 16 seeds; 8 generations, 4 seeds for mutant analysis)
metric: amino acid concentration fold change, mRNA:rRNA ratio, growth rate, oscillation frequency (concavity changes)
baseline: previous E. coli whole-cell model with fixed RNA/protein polymerization rates
result: amino acid concentrations in allosteric inhibition mutants comparable to experimental data (Sander et al.)

problem: Existing mathematical models of influenza A virus replication fail to capture infection dynamics across different MOI conditions, limiting prediction of virus yields for vaccine production and DIP therapeutic use.
method: Extended multiscale ODE model combining intracellular virus replication with cell population-level segregated infection age dynamics; introduced infection age-dependent fraction of infectious virus release (FIVR) and RdRp-mediated mRNA synthesis inhibition; calibrated to high MOI MDCK cell data.
finding: The extended model fitted to high MOI data successfully predicts low MOI viral release dynamics with the same parameter set. DIP interference at high MOI depends exclusively on seed virus DIP content, while at low MOI it is controlled by de novo DIP generation.
dataset: MDCK cells infected with influenza A/PR/8/34 (H1N1), MOI range 0.025–73 TCID50/cell
metric: virus titer (log TCID50/mL), HA titer (log HAU/100μL), cell population fractions (%), intracellular vRNA/cRNA/mRNA levels
baseline: original multiscale model by Heldt et al. (2013)
result: successful prediction of low MOI dynamics using high MOI-calibrated parameters; model captures both intra- and extracellular dynamics simultaneously

problem: Understanding cortical function requires integrating molecular, cellular, circuit, and behavioral scales, but no biophysically detailed model of mouse primary motor cortex (M1) existed to predict in vivo cell-type-specific dynamics across behavioral states.
method: Biophysically detailed computational model of mouse M1 with >10,000 neurons and 35 million synapses constrained by experimental data from 31 studies; developed using NetPyNE/NEURON with iterative grid search optimization; >20,000 simulations and 8 million HPC core hours.
finding: Model accurately predicts layer- and cell-type-specific firing rates and LFP across quiet wakefulness and movement states, including bidirectional L5B responses. LFP power shifts from delta to gamma during movement. Model reproduces effects of thalamus inactivation and noradrenaline receptor blockade.
dataset: mouse primary motor cortex (M1), 300μm diameter cylinder, >10,000 neurons, 35M synapses, 7 long-range input regions
metric: firing rates (Hz), LFP power spectral density, population-specific rate changes across behavioral states
baseline: in vivo experimental recordings (Sauerbrei et al.)
result: cell-type-specific firing rates consistent with in vivo data across quiet/movement states; physiological delta/beta/gamma LFP oscillations emerge spontaneously

problem: What conditions favor the evolution of evolvability versus phenotypic plasticity as adaptive strategies, and does evolving one strategy facilitate or hinder the other?
method: Computational evolutionary modeling with Virtual Cell populations (N=1024) using ODE-based internal dynamics (metabolites, enzymes, pumps, transcription factors); varied environmental change nature and frequency across simulations with pre-evolved homeostatic wild-type strains.
finding: Pre-evolved homeostasis regulation predisposes lineages to high evolvability. Low-frequency environmental changes drive genome restructuring enabling faster adaptation with very few mutations. High-frequency changes select for phenotypic plasticity, which is slower to evolve but stable. Under intermediate frequencies, both strategies coexist.
dataset: Virtual Cell populations (N=1024), circular genomes encoding metabolic and regulatory proteins
metric: fitness (homeostasis maintenance), adaptation speed (generations to readapt), number of mutations for readaptation
baseline: wild-type Virtual Cell strains with pre-evolved homeostasis regulation
result: rapid evolutionary adaptation with few mutations after novel change; evolvability comparable fitness to plasticity except in most rapidly changing environments

problem: How epigenetic regulation heterogeneity drives the entry-exit mechanisms of phenotype reprogramming and cell fate decision-making between pluripotent and differentiated states.
method: Stochastic model coupling epigenetic regulation (ER) with a gene regulatory network (GRN) of two mutually inhibiting genes; multiscale asymptotic WKB method for stochastic model reduction; hybrid simulation scheme for efficient numerical analysis of the ER-GRN system.
finding: The coupled system exhibits tristability where pluripotent stem-like, differentiated, and an indecisive state coexist. ER heterogeneity of differentiation genes confers abnormal robustness to stem-like states. Epigenetic heterogeneity-based strategies can unlock differentiation-refractory states through kinetic changes in epigenetic factors alone.
dataset: two-gene regulatory circuit model (pluripotency vs differentiation gene) with epigenetic regulation
metric: steady-state stability, transition barrier heights, switching kinetics between cell fates
baseline: GRN model without epigenetic regulation coupling
result: tristability regime identified; tunable epigenetic switches modify nature of cell fate reprogramming

problem: Robust methods for measuring bystander effects of hypoxia-activated prodrugs (HAPs) are lacking, limiting understanding of whether cytotoxin diffusion from targeted cells can overcome intra-tumor heterogeneity.
method: Agent-based models coupled with 3D co-culture experiments (monolayer, multilayer, spheroid) using engineered HCT116 activator (POR-overexpressing) and target (POR-knockout) cell lines; tested SN30000 and PR104A HAPs under anoxia; ABM parameterized from monolayer/multilayer data to predict spheroid outcomes.
finding: SN30000 shows no bystander effect due to rapid metabolic consumption limiting tissue penetration. PR104A demonstrates a significant bystander effect via more stable, lipophilic dichloro mustard metabolites not previously recognized. ABM accurately predicts SN30000 spheroid results but required inclusion of novel PR104A metabolites.
dataset: HCT116 isogenic co-cultures (POR overexpression/knockout), multicellular spheroids
metric: clonogenic cell survival (log cell kill), POR activity (FSL61 fluorescence, 24-fold differential), metabolite diffusion coefficients
baseline: monolayer monoculture cytotoxicity; POR-null target cells alone
result: PR104A bystander effect demonstrated; SN30000 penetration inhibited by metabolic consumption; novel dichloro mustard metabolites identified

problem: Understanding the heterogeneity and intercellular communication of fibro-adipogenic progenitors (FAPs) in skeletal muscle, which are crucial for homeostasis and regeneration but poorly characterized at the single-cell level.
method: Re-analysis of published scRNAseq data from mouse and human skeletal muscle; virtual secretome analysis using OutCyte prediction tool to characterize FAP secretory profiles in resting and regenerating conditions.
finding: Virtual secretome analysis of scRNAseq data reveals FAP communication potential and highlights the value of integrating multiple omics databases for comprehensive cell-cell interaction mapping in skeletal muscle.
dataset: mouse and human skeletal muscle scRNAseq (previously published datasets)

problem: How to link in vitro toxicity assays to in vivo predictions for estragole-induced hepatotoxicity and DNA adduct formation, enabling chemical risk assessment without animal testing.
method: Integration of a physiologically based kinetic (PBK) model with a virtual cell based assay (VCBA) into a PBK/D model; HepaRG cell viability and mitochondrial membrane potential measured via high-content analysis; KNIME workflow for automated IVIVE and forward/backward dosimetry.
finding: The PBK/D-VCBA model successfully performs in vitro to in vivo extrapolation for estragole. At average dietary intake (0.07 mg/kg bw), predicted viability is 1.0 (no harm) with DNA adducts of 0.00084/10^8 nucleotides. Cell viability drops starting at 12.5 mM estragole in vitro.
dataset: HepaRG cells exposed to estragole (0–75 mM, 24h), 5 biological replicates
metric: cell viability (%), mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨ), DNA adducts per 10^8 nucleotides, NEC (g/gww), kr (s⁻¹)
baseline: PBK model by Punt et al. (2009) without virtual cell integration
result: NEC=0.00533 g/gww, kr=0.0469/s; safe at dietary intake 0.07 mg/kg bw; viability drop at 12.5 mM

problem: Adoption of MRI for stem cell tracking has been limited by the difficulty of contrast agent synthesis and reliance on gadolinium-based agents, restricting use to specialized sites and small animal models.
method: Synthesized gadolinium-free manganese porphyrin (MnPNH2) via scalable route; labeled human embryonic stem cells at varying concentrations and durations; MRI at 3T clinical scanner; ICP-AES for Mn quantification; fluorescence microscopy; in vivo rat transplant.
finding: MnPNH2 produces nearly two-fold T1 reduction in labeled hESCs, maintained for 24 hours. Cell viability, proliferation, and differentiation are unaffected. Agent localizes at cell-cell boundaries and nuclei. Labeled cells show superior sensitivity for in vivo tracking in rats.
dataset: human embryonic stem cells (hESCs), in vivo rat transplant model, 3T clinical MRI scanner
metric: T1 relaxation time (ms), Mn concentration per cell (mol, ICP-AES), cell viability (Trypan blue), embryoid body differentiation
result: ~2-fold T1 decrease (P<0.05); contrast retained 24h post-labeling (P<0.05); 5.59e-11 mol Mn/cell at 0.5mM/24h

problem: Studying pancreatic epithelial cell biology in vivo lacks the temporal and spatial resolution needed to resolve cellular and subcellular processes during epithelialization and endocrinogenesis.
method: Matrigel-based 3D cyst culture from mouse E14.5 pancreatic progenitors and human iPSC-derived PPs; live imaging at subcellular resolution; reanalysis of published scRNAseq data for differential expression of TFs, adhesion molecules, and polarity components.
finding: Successfully generated polarized pancreatic epithelial cysts expressing progenitor markers (Sox9, Pdx1, Foxa2) with proper apicobasal polarity. System enables time-resolved monitoring of polarity establishment, lumen formation, and endocrine differentiation. Both mouse and human cysts differentiate toward endocrine fate.
dataset: mouse E14.5 pancreatic progenitors, human iPSC-derived pancreatic progenitors (HMGUi001-A)
metric: expression of polarity markers (F-Actin, aPKC, β-catenin), TF expression (Sox9, Pdx1, Nkx6-1), endocrine differentiation rate
baseline: 2D culture protocols and in vivo observations
result: endocrine differentiation achieved but at lower rate than 2D or 3D suspension protocols; subcellular resolution live imaging enabled

problem: Increasing current amplitude impacts accuracy of biophysical analyses of macroscopic ion currents
method: Kinetic modeling, patch-clamp electrophysiology
dataset: rat

problem: Single-cell RNAseq requires tools for exploring heterogeneity within cell populations
method: Virtual reality visualization of single-cell data
finding: VR is excellent medium for scrutinizing complex single-cell data
dataset: rat

problem: CAR T-cell therapy for treating cancers requires optimization of design features
method: Agent-based modeling of CAR T-cell therapy
finding: Tuning individual features revealed key insights for CAR T-cell design
dataset: cho

problem: Testing efficacy of learning module for Virtual Cell Animation Collection
method: Educational assessment of animation-based learning module
finding: Animation-based module effectively conveyed meiosis conceptual understanding
dataset: students
metric: correlation

problem: Adaptive immune response initiation through DC-T cell contact in lymph nodes
method: Stochastic simulation, agent-based modeling
finding: Modeled dendritic cell-T cell interactions in lymph nodes
metric: sensitivity

problem: HCC characterized by whole-chromosome instability and near-homozygous genome
method: Cellular characterization of cancer cell lines
finding: FTC-236 showed diminished complex activities compared to other cell lines
dataset: cell lines

problem: Understanding cellular processes through fundamental biochemical interactions
method: Metabolic network modeling
finding: Almost all known cellular processes found among reaction nodes
dataset: rat
metric: correlation

problem: Predicting in vivo toxic doses from in vitro concentrations for animal testing replacement
method: Differential equations-based PBPK modeling

problem: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation relapse in acute myeloid leukemia
method: Clinical analysis of stem cell transplantation outcomes
dataset: patient cohort

problem: Incorporating tRNA aminoacylation and polypeptide elongation into whole-cell model
method: Stochastic simulation of translation machinery
finding: Required kcat values were higher than expected
dataset: E. coli
metric: sensitivity

problem: How iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) induce thromboinflammatory responses in human whole blood interacting with endothelial cells, and whether complement inhibition can attenuate these adverse effects.
method: Lepirudin-anticoagulated human whole blood co-incubated with HLMECs and 10-30nm IONPs; complement activation measured by ELISA (C3bc, sC5b-9); 27-cytokine profiling; flow cytometry for cell activation markers; complement inhibitors Cp40 (C3) and PMX53 (C5aR1).
finding: IONPs induced dose-dependent complement activation primarily at C3-level, significantly increased 21/27 cytokines, activated endothelial cells, monocytes, granulocytes, and platelets; C3 inhibitor Cp40 significantly attenuated ICAM-1 and selectin expression, demonstrating complement-dependent thromboinflammation.
dataset: Human whole blood from healthy volunteers + human lung microvascular endothelial cells (HLMECs), n=3-6 donors
metric: p-value, C3bc/sC5b-9 ELISA, cytokine levels, cell activation markers (ICAM-1, P/E-selectin, CD11b, CD62P, CD63, LDH, heme)
baseline: PBS negative control, zymosan positive control
result: 21/27 cytokines p<0.05; ICAM-1 p<0.0001; Cp40 attenuated ICAM-1 (p<0.01) and selectins (p<0.05)

problem: How to achieve high-resolution spatiotemporal imaging of endothelial cell-pericyte association in vivo, combining 4D intravital dynamics with molecular subtype identification.
method: Adapted lightweight PEEK dorsal skinfold chamber (1.1g total) for 4D intravital multiphoton imaging combined with subsequent whole-mount immunohistology; transgenic fluorescent reporter mice (eNOStag-GFP for endothelial cells, Cspg4-DsRed for pericytes).
finding: The lightweight PEEK chamber (3.4x lighter than titanium) improved animal welfare while enabling longitudinal 4D tracking of endothelial cell-pericyte dynamics; whole-mount staining preserved spatial positions for molecular subtype identification at single-cell resolution.
dataset: C57BL/6 transgenic mice with B16BL6 melanoma or Lewis lung carcinoma tumors
metric: Chamber weight, animal body weight, imaging resolution, animal welfare indicators
baseline: Classical titanium dorsal skinfold chamber (Jain et al. design)
result: 3.4-fold lighter than titanium; full motion capacity retained; weight gain comparable to no-window controls

problem: How PKCδ (pro-nociceptive) and SOM (anti-nociceptive) neuron populations in the central amygdala interact to produce system-level pain responses, and what role their relative proportion plays in pain prediction.
method: Agent-based computational model of bilateral CeA with PKCδ and SOM neurons; inhibitory connectivity network; parameters estimated from ex vivo slice electrophysiology and immunohistochemistry; stochastic firing rate updates with damage accumulation during noxious stimulation.
finding: The relative proportion of PKCδ:SOM neurons is a key parameter predicting pain output; model reproduced expected pain changes during and after injury; comparison with behavioral data revealed areas of similarity and distinctions suggesting future experiments.
dataset: Mouse CeA electrophysiology (PKCδ-Cre/Sst-Cre x Ai9 reporter mice, sciatic cuff injury model)
metric: Firing rate (Hz), pain index (cumulative pro- vs anti-nociceptive output), standardized mean difference
baseline: Equal proportion (50:50) PKCδ:SOM model; sham/naïve control electrophysiology
result: PKCδ spontaneous 2.838 Hz, SOM 4.887 Hz; model validated against behavioral data across three proportion scenarios

problem: Whether necroptosis (RIPK-dependent programmed necrosis) is an alternative cell death mechanism to apoptosis in bovine ovarian follicle atresia.
method: RT-qPCR for RIPK1/RIPK3 mRNA in granulosa and theca cells from healthy vs atretic follicles classified by E2:P4 ratio; protein-protein interaction network analysis using IMEx database in Cytoscape; correlation networks via CluePedia with MIC and Pearson correlation.
finding: RIPK1 and RIPK3 mRNA were significantly upregulated in granulosa cells from atretic follicles; network analysis confirmed interactions between necroptosis and apoptosis pathways; both death mechanisms occur in granulosa cells of dominant follicles undergoing luteinization and theca cells of newly selected follicles.
dataset: Bovine ovarian follicles (granulosa and theca cells, healthy E2:P4>1 vs atretic E2:P4<0.01)
metric: mRNA expression (normalized to GAPDH), p-value, E2:P4 ratio, network topology (hubs, bottlenecks)
result: RIPK1 p<0.05 and RIPK3 p<0.05 higher in atretic granulosa; no significant difference in theca (p>0.05)

problem: How to predict human in vivo toxic doses from in vitro data by simulating intracellular and intra-mitochondrial chemical concentrations to replace animal testing.
method: Virtual Cell Based Assay (VCBA) mathematical model extended with mitochondrial compartment using Trapp-Horobin uptake approach; simulates chemical fate (partitioning to plastic, headspace, serum proteins, cells, mitochondria) coupled with cell growth and toxicodynamic models.
finding: VCBA successfully simulated mitochondrial membrane potential disruption in both HepaRG hepatocytes and ICell cardiomyocytes for three chemicals; cardiomyocytes showed higher sensitivity to FCCP than hepatocytes; proof of concept for organelle-level toxicity simulation.
dataset: HepaRG hepatocytes and ICell cardiomyocytes (iPSC-derived) exposed to FCCP, caffeine, amiodarone
metric: Cell viability (%), mitochondrial membrane potential (RFU), no effect concentration (NEC), killing rate (kr)
result: FCCP: mmp ~40% at 10μM in HepaRG, effect at 5μM in cardiomyocytes; caffeine: viability decrease from 1.5mM

problem: How to simulate trabecular bone remodeling at the single-cell level integrating mechanomics, receptor-ligand kinetics, and cytokine signaling to predict the effects of OPG/RANKL/Scl changes on bone microarchitecture.
method: 3D micro-multiphysics agent-based (micro-MPA) model with single-cell agents, receptor-ligand kinetics, diffusion/decay of cytokines, and micro-FE mechanical analysis; validated against longitudinal in vivo micro-CT data from PolgA premature aging mice over 4 weeks.
finding: OPG and RANKL levels were positively and negatively correlated with BV/TV respectively; sclerostin was the main driver of remodeling equilibrium; model successfully simulated homeostatic, anabolic, anti-anabolic, catabolic, and anti-catabolic bone responses.
dataset: PolgA (D257A/D257A) premature aging mice, 6th caudal vertebra, n=5, micro-CT at 10.5μm resolution
metric: BV/TV (bone volume fraction), Tb.Sp, Tb.N, Tb.Th (trabecular morphometry)
baseline: In vivo sham loading (0N) data; basal cytokine parameter configuration
result: In vivo BV/TV ~2% decrease over 4 weeks; in silico BV/TV not significantly different from in vivo on average

problem: How stochastic single-cell gene expression heterogeneity in E. coli affects population-level antibiotic resistance, particularly for sub-generationally expressed resistance genes.
method: Whole-cell E. coli model embedded in shared spatial environment via Vivarium framework for colony-scale simulation; parallelized cloud computing (224 CPUs, Google Compute Engine); new submodels for tetracycline (protein synthesis inhibition) and ampicillin (cell wall disruption) response pathways.
finding: Over 50% of E. coli genes are sub-generationally expressed; stochastic variation in AmpC beta-lactamase was the most significant factor in determining cell survival under ampicillin; tetracycline response showed less cell-to-cell variation despite heterogeneous OmpF/TolC expression.
dataset: Simulated E. coli colonies on minimal M9 medium + 1mM glucose, 3 replicate seeds per condition
metric: MIC, growth rate, periplasmic/cytoplasmic antibiotic concentration, mRNA/protein counts, spatial autocorrelation
result: >50% genes sub-generationally expressed; ampC variation dominated ampicillin survival determination

problem: Design and evaluate novel naphthol diazenyl scaffold-based Schiff base derivatives as potential antimicrobial and anticancer agents against colorectal carcinoma.
method: Synthesis of 18 diazenyl Schiff bases via diazotization-coupling-condensation; antimicrobial evaluation by MIC/MBC; cytotoxicity by MTT assay on HT-29 cells; apoptosis by Annexin-V/PI flow cytometry; cell cycle analysis by flow cytometry.
finding: NS-2, NS-8, NS-21, and NS-23 showed potent cytotoxicity against HT-29 (IC50 4-19 μg/ml); NS-2 induced >90% apoptosis surpassing doxorubicin (68%); active derivatives arrested cell growth in S and G2/M phases.
dataset: HT-29 human colorectal carcinoma cell line; E. coli, S. enterica, B. subtilis, A. fumigatus
metric: IC50 (μg/ml), MIC, % apoptotic cells, cell cycle phase distribution
result: NS-2/NS-8/NS-21/NS-23 IC50 4-19 μg/ml; NS-2 >90% apoptosis vs 68% doxorubicin

problem: How to integrate the heterogeneous molecular and cellular interactions of dendritic cells across biological scales to create a predictive platform for studying immune responses and discovering therapeutics.
method: Logic-based (Boolean) mechanistic model with 281 components and 702 interactions spanning molecular signaling to cell-cell communication; constructed from 92 publications; validated against literature-reported DC responses; implemented in Cell Collective platform.
finding: Model successfully characterized DC response to SARS-CoV-2/influenza co-infection (107 molecules analyzed), predicted DC-T cell crosstalk in cancer microenvironment, and identified 45 diseases and 24 molecular pathways addressable by the DC model via KEGG enrichment.
dataset: Human dendritic cell subtypes (pDC, cDC1, cDC2, MoDC); 92 publications (83 original, 9 reviews)
metric: Activity level (Boolean 0/1), KEGG enrichment analysis, number of addressable diseases/pathways
baseline: Published literature-reported DC responses to specific extracellular stimuli
result: 281 components, 702 interactions; 107 molecules in co-infection analysis; 45 diseases, 24 pathways identified

problem: How to sparsely label specific neuron types throughout the whole brain to screen morphological changes in disease models such as autism spectrum disorder.
method: AAV-hSyn-DIO-EGFP-P2A-EGFPf with FLEX Cre-switch system delivered by retro-ocular injection in CaMKIIα-Cre mice; Sholl analysis for dendritic complexity; dendritic spine density quantification in Shank3 KO autism model.
finding: Shank3 KO mice showed brain region-specific morphological deficits: reduced dendritic complexity in striatal medium spiny neurons, increased complexity in cortical layer 5 neurons, and altered spine density in hippocampal CA1, demonstrating differential vulnerability across projection neuron populations.
dataset: Shank3 KO x CaMKIIα-Cre mice and littermate controls; striatum, cortex, hippocampus
metric: Dendritic complexity (Sholl analysis, intersection count), dendritic spine density (spines/μm)
result: Differential dendritic deficits across striatum (reduced), cortex (increased), hippocampus (altered) in Shank3 KO

problem: The cellular expression pattern of the endolysosomal cation channel TRPML3 on a whole-tissue level remains largely unknown, unlike the ubiquitously expressed TRPML1.
method: Generated a τGFP reporter mouse model (Trpml3 IRES-Cre/eR26-τGFP) and compared expression data via immunofluorescence, immunohistochemistry with Trpml3-/- knockout controls, in situ hybridization, and RT-qPCR across multiple organs.
finding: TRPML3 is expressed in alveolar macrophages, melanocytes, kidney collecting duct principal cells, olfactory sensory neurons, and multiple endocrine/secretory glands (parathyroid, thyroid, salivary, adrenal, pituitary, testes, ovaries), suggesting roles in hormone secretion or uptake.
dataset: Trpml3 IRES-Cre/eR26-τGFP reporter mice, CD1 mice, Trpml3-/- knockout littermates (~75% C57BL/6, ~25% Sv129/Ola)
metric: RT-qPCR relative expression normalized to 18S rRNA
baseline: Trpml3-/- knockout mice (negative control for immunoreactivity specificity)
result: Trpml3 mRNA detected in RPE, thymus, back skin, kidney, lung; not detected in cerebellum, heart, muscle, stomach, small intestine, colon

problem: Understanding the autoimmune progression in Type 1 Diabetes through simulation of CD8+ T cell and beta cell interactions at tissue scale, which is difficult to observe in clinical studies.
method: Agent-based model on high-performance computing simulating a significant portion of mouse pancreas islets, incorporating stochastic T cell motility, cell cycle, differentiation, and target killing with 100 repeated simulations per scenario.
finding: Basement membrane strength delays autoimmune onset; paradoxically, beta cell regeneration increases inflammation severity through an epitope-spreading-like mechanism where proliferating beta cells increase T cell contact probability.
dataset: Simulated mouse pancreas islets with literature-derived parameters (islet diameter 100-160 μm, islet density 0.7-7.7%)
metric: Beta cell count, T cell population dynamics over 10-week simulation period
baseline: Scenarios without beta cell regeneration (0% rate)
result: Beta cell regeneration >5%/day unrealistic; basement membrane strength range 1440-20160 arbitrary units tested across 100 stochastic replicates

problem: Limited data exists on multi-serovar cross-protection of whole-cell vaccines against Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae, a major bacterial respiratory pathogen in pigs.
method: Standardized challenge dose trials (13 studies) using intranasal or aerosol chamber inoculation across 8 serovars with uniform lung lesion scoring per European Pharmacopoeia protocols; mixed-effects meta-analysis with ICC for reproducibility.
finding: The whole-cell vaccine (Coglapix) provides highly significant serovar-independent protection against both homologous (serovars 1, 2) and heterologous (serovars 4, 5, 6, 7, 9/11, 13) strains, with high reproducibility between studies.
dataset: 13 challenge trials across 8 A. pleuropneumoniae serovars, multiple pig breeds (Danbred, Hungaro-Seghers, Topigs-Norsvin), N=182 vaccinated + 161 positive controls
metric: Lung Lesion Score (LLS), p-value, intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC)
result: Homologous: p=0.00007 (serovar 1), p=0.00124 (serovar 2); heterologous: p=2.9×10⁻¹⁰ to p=0.00953; ICC=2.2-4.8% (high reproducibility except serovar 6 at 35%)

problem: Whole-cell models are the ultimate goal of computational systems biology, but the field faces challenges in data collection, model integration, and simulation speed.
method: Review of whole-cell modeling progress covering hybrid modeling methods, multi-scale simulation approaches, and high-performance computing platforms for WCM execution.
finding: WCMs have identified novel biological phenomena but their most important contribution is highlighting the discrepancy between available data and what is needed for parameterization/validation; current simulators are too slow for multicellular models.

problem: Single-host monocultures have limited protein expression capacity for consolidated bioprocessing of complex substrates; it is unclear when division of labour in consortia would be beneficial.
method: Resource-aware whole-cell ODE model adapted from the Weiße framework, explicitly modeling ribosome allocation and translational burden in a two-strain consortium vs monoculture for starch degradation.
finding: Once a burden threshold is reached, the consortium consistently outperforms the monoculture; DOL reduces ribosome redirection toward heterologous genes, enabling higher per-cell enzyme production and independent optimization of each strain.
dataset: Simulated two-strain starch degradation consortium (endohydrolase + exohydrolase), Weiße model parameters
metric: Growth rate, per-cell enzyme production
baseline: Equivalent single-cell monoculture co-expressing both enzymes
result: Consortium outperforms monoculture above burden threshold; each consortium cell produces more of its enzyme per cell than the monoculture

problem: Using carriage prevalence as a vaccine trial endpoint is complicated by nonlinear prevalence-incidence relationships, multiple pathogen strains, and age-dependent natural immunity, making sample size estimation difficult.
method: Individual-based computational model of pneumococcal carriage incorporating serotype-specific and non-specific immunity, fitted to epidemiological settings, with simulated vaccine trials at different ages and transmission intensities.
finding: Sampling time and age at vaccination strongly influence required sample size; infant trials in lower-transmission settings with optimal sampling windows (15-24 months post-vaccination) dramatically reduce participant requirements.
dataset: Simulated pneumococcal carriage in two transmission settings (under-five prevalence: 66% higher, 55% lower), 50 simulation runs per scenario
metric: Required sample size, carriage prevalence reduction
result: Vaccine efficacy of 3/5/10 colonization equivalents (26%/39%/63% duration reduction) reduces prevalence up to 7%/10%/17%; infant trial in lower-transmission setting needs <800/330/110 participants

problem: The COVID-19 transition to remote learning required adapting an in-person cell culture lab practical to a virtual assessment format.
method: Virtual lab practical administered via course LMS using video tutorials, data analysis questions, multiple-choice questions, and a hemocytometer simulator.
finding: Average student scores and grade distributions were comparable between in-person and virtual formats, with fewer grading concerns and reduced student stress in the virtual version.
dataset: Introductory BME lab course students (in-person vs virtual cohorts)
metric: Average student scores, grade distributions
result: Comparable scores and grade distributions across formats; fewer grading concerns for virtual practical

problem: Extracting, integrating, and presenting 3D representations of cells that are both scientifically accurate and visually appealing poses substantial computational and aesthetic challenges.
method: Collaboration of computer artists and cell biologists using Maya, Zbrush, Houdini, and Unity3D game engine; protein structures from PDB, receptor densities from flow cytometry, lipid bilayer from CHARMM-GUI simulation.
finding: Produced Nanoscape, a multi-scale explorable 3D cell environment; receptor density simulations based on MDA-MB-231 flow cytometry data; immersive virtual field trips may improve understanding of cellular complexity.
dataset: MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell surface receptor data (CD44, EGFR, EpCAM, Her2, ICAM1, αVβ3 integrin), PDB protein structures, CHARMM-GUI lipid simulations

problem: Computational tools for in silico analysis of how cell growth and division affect photosynthesis are scarce, limiting understanding of leaf anatomy–physiology relationships.
method: Virtual leaf tissue generator based on Voronoi tessellation and cell growth model coupled to a 2D microscale CO2 gas transport model; 270 mesophyll geometries generated with varying growth anisotropy, growth extent, and airspace formation.
finding: Virtual tissue was statistically similar to real tomato leaf tissue; light-saturated photosynthesis is influenced by interactive effects of growth anisotropy, growth extent, and airspace formation through exposed mesophyll surface area.
dataset: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum L.) leaf tissue, 270 virtual mesophyll geometries, 5 real leaf cross-section replicates
metric: KS test p-value, mean cell area, aspect ratio, Lm (mesophyll surface area per leaf area), net photosynthesis rate (A)
baseline: Real tomato leaf tissue microscopy cross-sections
result: KS test p-values: 41% (spongy cell area), 70% (spongy aspect ratio), 37% (palisade cell area), 18% (palisade aspect ratio) — distributions not significantly different

problem: The Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis) is energetically counter-intuitive; the role of metabolic symbiosis between oxygenated and hypoxic tumour cells in promoting tumour growth is not well understood.
method: Multi-scale agent-based model in NetLogo integrating diffusion-reaction equations, stoichiometric equations, and Boolean gene regulatory networks (MAPK pathway); sensitivity analysis via Latin hypercube sampling and partial rank correlation.
finding: Metabolic symbiosis reduces glucose depletion and promotes glycolysis by depleting oxygen at the tumour boundary; blocking glucose transporters (GLUT1) is predicted to be more effective at reducing tumour growth than blocking lactate intake transporters (MCT1).
dataset: Breast cancer cell lines RNAseq data (normoxic/hypoxic), simulated tumour organoid
metric: Symbiosis index, OXPHOS/glycolytic population ratio, partial rank correlation coefficient
result: OXPHOS population ~4.5% of glycolytic under MCT1 knockout vs co-existing populations under symbiosis; minimum glucose level for glycolysis identified as key sensitivity parameter

problem: Whole-cell modelling applications in lab experiment design and predictive drug testing
method: Machine learning pipeline for whole-cell model analysis
finding: Pipeline for weak labelling, neural network classification, and network analysis
dataset: 270 simulations

problem: High-throughput omics technologies impact on fundamental and translational research
method: Genome-scale metabolic model reconstruction for red blood cells
finding: Generated refined RBC-GEM reconstruction from iAB-RBC-283
dataset: RBC

problem: Developing whole-cell computational platform for HBV infection cycle in hepatocytes
method: Combined spatial and kinetic models of HBV infection cycle
finding: Constructed spatially-resolved hepatocyte model for HBV infection
dataset: hepatocyte

problem: Realistic electrophysiological descriptions of C. elegans neurons
method: Electrophysiological modeling of interneurons and motor neurons
finding: Models of three interneurons (AVA, RIM, AIY) and three motor neurons (VA5, VB6, VD5)
dataset: C. elegans

problem: Understanding cell-cell interactions from single-cell data
method: Agent-based modeling framework integrating gene expression with ligand-receptor interactions
finding: CellAgentChat framework for comprehensive cell-cell interaction analysis
dataset: single-cell datasets

problem: Subunit vaccines have lower immunogenicity compared to whole-cell vaccines
method: Immunogenicity evaluation of vaccine formulations
finding: Evaluated reactogenic responses to vaccine formulations
dataset: animal model

problem: Whole-microbe inactivation technology for rapid pathogen inactivation retaining epitopes
method: Whole-microbe inactivation technology development
finding: Technology enables rapid pathogen inactivation while retaining epitope integrity
dataset: pathogen

problem: Novel approach using dedicated hardware for whole-cell simulation of mRNA translation
method: Hardware-based whole-cell simulation of mRNA translation
finding: Demonstrated approach for whole-cell translation simulation

problem: Immunological synapse formation and TCR-pMHC affinity impact on T cell activation
method: Agent-based model replicating T cell activation on supported lipid bilayers
finding: Model replicated T cell activation experiments
dataset: T cells

problem: High quality data prerequisite for precise whole-cell models
method: Data curation for whole-cell model parameterization
finding: Reviewed data requirements for whole-cell model construction
dataset: yeast

problem: How LQT14-associated calmodulin mutations that reduce calcium-dependent inactivation (CDI) of L-type calcium channels can produce a CPVT4-like arrhythmic phenotype in cardiac myocytes.
method: Stochastic whole-cell simulation of rat ventricular myocyte with 20,000 independent calcium release units (CRUs), each containing 49 RyR2s and 7 LCCs, under varying pacing rates and beta-adrenergic stimulation.
finding: Small reductions in CaM-dependent CDI (factors of 0.75 for D96V and 0.90 for N98S) promote Ca2+ overload and arrhythmia under beta-adrenergic stimulation with rapid pacing, providing a mechanistic link between LQT14 mutations and CPVT4 phenotype.
dataset: Rat cardiac ventricular myocyte computational model (20,000 CRUs, 49 RyR2 + 7 LCC per CRU)
metric: APD50, APD90, Ca2+ transient amplitude, Ca2+ alternans onset
result: CDI 0.75 (D96V) and 0.90 (N98S) induced Ca2+ alternans at 6 Hz and arrhythmia above 2 Hz under beta-adrenergic stimulation

problem: Understanding the complex pathophysiology of inflammatory bowel diseases and evaluating different treatment modalities using a mechanistic systems biology approach.
method: ODE-based systems biology model (47 species, 133 reactions, 79 parameters) of mucosal immune response across three spatial compartments, with virtual patient population defined by phenotype rather than a priori parameter assumptions.
finding: IBD patients characterized by persistent inflammation after trigger event reproduced diverse predispositions; model identifies characteristics of individual drug therapy and promising combination therapies for anti-TNF-α non-responders.
dataset: Virtual IBD patient population, mucosal immune system (lumen, lamina propria, lymph nodes)
metric: Qualitative immune response dynamics, treatment response classification
result: Model identifies alternative treatments with best prospects for anti-TNF-α non-responders and promising combination therapies

problem: Limited high-resolution methods exist to analyze cell-biomaterial interactions in thick, opaque 3D decellularized ECM scaffolds without compromising spatial information.
method: Optical clearing, whole-mount light sheet fluorescence microscopy, and automated image analytics pipeline for decellularized ECM (dECM)-based in vitro models of colorectal cancer liver metastasis.
finding: Pipeline enables volumetric quantification of cell invasion depth and distribution in CRLM models; decellularization achieved 96% DNA reduction while preserving collagen and GAG composition.
dataset: Porcine liver dECM scaffolds, HCT116 human colorectal cancer cells (CRLM model)
metric: DNA content (ng/μL/mg), hydroxyproline (% total protein), GAG content, cell invasion depth
result: 96% DNA reduction (104.8±15.6 to 3.9±1.0 ng/μL/mg, p<0.0001), hydroxyproline 26.13±4.63% of total protein in dECM

problem: Lack of effective treatments for ischemic stroke and slow progress in neuron-centered drug targets; need to understand the complex multifaceted roles of brain microvascular endothelial cells (BMECs) in stroke pathophysiology.
method: Transcriptomics-informed (GEO GSE163752) mechanistic systems biology model of BMEC intracellular signaling with high-throughput sensitivity analyses and virtual target perturbations, validated in vitro and in vivo.
finding: Targeting succinate effectively reversed detrimental BMEC phenotype under oxygen-glucose deprivation/reoxygenation, validated for regulating inflammatory factor production, free radical generation, and tight junction protection.
dataset: GEO GSE163752 (mouse BMECs after tMCAO), in vitro OGD/R BMEC model, in vivo stroke model
metric: GSEA enrichment, model sensitivity indices, inflammatory factor levels, ROS generation, tight junction integrity
result: Succinate identified as novel target; reversed detrimental BMEC phenotype under OGD/R in vitro and in vivo

problem: Standard deterministic methods for calibrating ion channel gating models cannot distinguish stochastic channel gating from measurement error noise, yielding biased parameter estimates, while conventional stochastic approaches are computationally demanding.
method: State-space model based on stochastic reaction networks with maximum likelihood inference using differential moment equations (mean and covariance) of the Itô-type SDE process, applied to five-state and eight-state hERG channel models.
finding: Method infers parameters with low uncertainty, outperforms deterministic approaches in distinguishing stochastic gating from measurement error, and correctly identified 50-Hz measurement artifact in experimental CHO cell recordings.
dataset: Whole-cell voltage-clamp recordings from 9 CHO cells overexpressing hERG1a (Kv11.1)
metric: Parameter uncertainty, negative log-likelihood, computational time vs Kalman filter
baseline: Deterministic four-state ion channel model (Beattie et al.), Kalman filter approach
result: Low parameter uncertainty; correctly distinguished 50-Hz measurement error from stochastic gating; computationally efficient vs Kalman filter

problem: Need for imaging tools to noninvasively monitor androgen receptor (AR) signaling dynamics in castrate-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) driven by AR signaling.
method: Synthesis of fluorescent AR inhibitors (ARi-FL) based on aryloxy cyclohexane scaffold with neolinker conjugation to BODIPY fluorophores; in silico molecular modeling across wild-type and mutant AR; in vivo/ex vivo validation in prostate cancer models.
finding: ARi-FL probes showed potent AR inhibition and localized to AR-positive tumors in vivo; in silico modeling revealed consistent nanomolar binding affinities across WT and clinically relevant AR mutants.
dataset: Human prostate cancer models (in vivo/ex vivo), AR WT and mutants (F877L, T878A, H875Y, W742C, F877L/T878A)
metric: IC50, Kd, R², extinction coefficient, absorbance/fluorescence linearity
result: IC50 ~13 nM, Kd ~1-2 nM across WT and mutant AR, R²=0.98-0.99 linearity

problem: Single cell dendritic spine modeling has not been used to explain the synaptic tagging and capture (STC) hypothesis, and it is difficult to relate physical properties of STC pathways to structural changes and synaptic strength.
method: Mathematical model based on reported synaptic tagging networks built in Virtual Cell (VCell) software using ODE solvers and SBML-compatible open-source code, with compartmentalized single cell dendritic spine geometry.
finding: The model provides a framework for analyzing STC hypothesis candidates, assessing their behavior against requirements outlined in the synaptic tagging hypothesis using VCell's reaction network and mass action/Michaelis-Menten kinetics.
dataset: Single cell dendritic spine (SCDS) model, synaptic tagging candidate molecules

problem: Current single-molecule simulation models are limited to idealized systems (unit volumes or planar surfaces), lacking realistic 3D cellular structures for interpreting experimental fluorescence microscopy data.
method: Object-oriented 3D voxelated virtual cell model combining Monte Carlo random walks with subcellular structures (nucleus, ER, cytoskeleton, vesicles, filopodia), producing simulated fluorescence video sequences with realistic noise.
finding: Membrane curvature and simulation time-step significantly affect measured diffusion coefficients; model produces realistic video sequences directly comparable to experimental single-molecule tracking data.
dataset: Simulated yeast cell (R=2 μm), 100 membrane proteins, D=0.1 μm²/s
metric: Diffusion coefficient (D), MSD vs dT slope, spatial resolution (nm)
baseline: Known input D=0.1 μm²/s, rectangular cell (flat membrane) control
result: Accurate D recovery at Δt=0.1 ms; curved membrane restricts long-range diffusion vs flat surfaces; benchmark 57s for 1000×1000 molecules

problem: Need for computational models integrating multimodal omics data to enable high-precision predictions of drug responses, gene perturbations, and disease progression while reducing animal testing.
method: Review of AI-driven virtual cell models integrating deep generative models and graph neural networks with single-cell transcriptomics and proteomics, emphasizing closed-loop computational-to-experimental validation workflows.
finding: Virtual cell models show potential for personalized drug screening and disease modeling but face challenges in regulatory acceptance, data privacy, and model interpretability; global standardization efforts are driving clinical translation.

problem: Need for novel mycobacterial efflux-pump inhibitors (EPIs) as adjuvants in combination therapy to address antimicrobial resistance in mycobacterial infections.
method: Whole-cell EtBr efflux-pump accumulation assays against M. smegmatis and M. abscessus, combined with machine learning-based homology modeling and full atomistic molecular dynamics simulations of MmpL4/5 embedded in lipid bilayer.
finding: Compound Il identified as most potent EPI selective towards MmpL4, blocking critical Asp-Tyr dyad via hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking; showed additive profile with bedaquiline against M. abscessus.
dataset: M. smegmatis mc²155 (ATCC 700084), M. abscessus (NCTC 13031)
metric: MIC, relative fluorescence unit (RFU), fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC) index, binding free energy
baseline: Verapamil (positive control EPI, 125 μg/mL for M. smegmatis, 256 μg/mL for M. abscessus)
result: Compound Il most potent EPI in series; additive FIC index with bedaquiline against M. abscessus

problem: Correction to a biophysical model of C. elegans neurons (AWCon and RMD), addressing fitted parameters for ion channel currents.
method: Hodgkin-Huxley-type biophysical modeling with least-squares nonlinear fitting and genetic algorithm optimization for ion channel kinetics.
finding: Corrected model parameters for multiple ion channels (SHL1, KVS1, KQT3, SHK1, EGL2, EGL36, IRK) with calibrated half-activation/inactivation potentials to match voltage clamp and current clamp data.
dataset: C. elegans AWCon and RMD neurons
metric: least-squares fit to voltage clamp/current clamp data
baseline: homologous channels from other species or muscle cells

problem: Understanding vacuole morphology and formation mechanisms in early-stage root cortex cells of Arabidopsis thaliana.
method: Whole-cell electron tomography using high-pressure freezing, freeze substitution, and dual-axis tilt series reconstruction with IMOD software at 4.3 and 1.5 nm pixel resolution.
finding: Provided 3D reconstructions of vacuole structures in 15 cells including cortical, QC, epidermal, and columella cells, revealing vacuole morphology in early root development.
dataset: 5-day-old Arabidopsis root tips, 15 cells (4 half-cortical, 7 partial cortical, 1 QC, 1 epidermal, 2 columella)
metric: pixel size (4.3 nm and 1.5 nm), z-scale factor 1.6

problem: Existing molecular visualization software cannot handle real-time interactive visualization of massive molecular systems (hundreds of millions of atoms).
method: Impostor-based meshless rendering with adaptive level-of-detail tessellation shaders and Screen-Space Ambient Occlusion (SSAO), implemented in the open-source VTX software.
finding: VTX maintains consistent interactive frame rates with a 114-million-bead Martini whole-cell model on standard hardware, outperforming VMD, PyMOL, and ChimeraX in rendering performance and memory efficiency.
dataset: 114-million-bead Martini minimal whole-cell model (Stevens et al., 2023)
metric: frame rate, memory usage
result: interactive frame rates on standard hardware with 114M atoms; existing tools unable to match

problem: Automated differential cell counts on bone marrow aspirate smears require extensive annotations and have poor generalization across medical centers and diseases.
method: VFM-SSL-BMADCC-Framework combining SAM (vision foundation model) for cell segmentation, MAE (self-supervised) for cell classification, TextureUnet for ROI segmentation, and TCNet for tile classification on whole-slide images.
finding: The framework achieved high accuracy across all stages and generalized well across different medical centers (Chongqing Cancer Hospital and Southwest Hospital) and diseases, with differential cell counts highly consistent with manual analysis.
dataset: 40 WSIs from Chongqing Cancer Hospital (training), 13 WSIs from Chongqing + 5 WSIs from Southwest Hospital (validation); diseases include AML, Burkitt, CLL, DLBCL, MM
metric: IoU, Dice, PA, Recall75, accuracy
baseline: PSPNet, FCN, DeepLabV3, U-Net (for ROI segmentation)
result: ROI IoU=46.19%, Dice=63.19%, PA=95.38%; TOI accuracy=90.45%; cell segmentation Recall75=99.01%; cell classification accuracy=77.92%

problem: Incorporating macromolecular complex assembly into a whole-cell kinetic model of the minimal bacterium JCVI-syn3A to understand time-dependent cellular behavior.
method: Hybrid stochastic-deterministic CME-ODE algorithm in Lattice Microbe, simulating 21 unique macromolecular complex assemblies (ribosome, RNA polymerase, degradosome, ATP synthase, ABC transporters) with 100 cell replicates, plus machine learning analysis of metabolomics.
finding: The augmented model predicted cell cycle timing consistent with experiments (median 102 min vs 105 min measured), revealed DnaA binding rate effects on replication initiation, dTTP shortage-induced replication slowdown, and 73% ribosome translational occupancy at steady state.
dataset: JCVI-syn3A (minimal cell), 100 independent cell replicates
metric: cell cycle time, replication initiation time, ribosome occupancy, protein abundance scaling
baseline: previous spatially homogeneous WCM of Syn3A, 4DWCM (3D+time)
result: median cell cycle 102 min (exp: 105 min), median protein scaling 2.07x, 73% ribosome translational occupancy

problem: Defining the molecular and cellular mechanisms of gadolinium-based contrast agent (GBCA) toxicity, specifically endolysosomal dysfunction in renal proximal tubule cells.
method: In vitro exposure of mouse renal proximal tubule epithelial cells (MRPTEpiCs) to linear and macrocyclic GBCAs with ICP-MS for Gd quantification, XTT viability, immunofluorescence, western blot, and lysosomal/mitochondrial functional assays.
finding: GBCAs display lysosomotropic behavior causing early lysosomal enlargement and membrane destabilization, leading to cathepsin B/D translocation to cytosol; lysosomal membrane permeabilization occurs upstream of mitochondrial dysfunction, partially rescuable by protease inhibitors. Linear GBCAs showed greater toxicity than macrocyclic.
dataset: mouse renal proximal tubular epithelial cells (MRPTEpiCs), linear GBCA (Omniscan) and macrocyclic GBCA (Dotarem) at 2 mM
metric: Gd concentration (ICP-MS), XTT cell viability, KIM-1 expression, CTSB/CTSD localization
result: significant Gd accumulation from 1h; 24h linear GBCA reduced viability and increased KIM-1; partial rescue with protease inhibitors

problem: Evaluating novel pyrimidine-based compounds (P12 and P14) as anticancer agents that induce tumor cell senescence in breast cancer and melanoma.
method: 2D and 3D cell culture models (colony formation, spheroid assays), cell cycle analysis, RT-qPCR for senescence/SASP markers, SA-beta-gal staining, and apoptosis assays in MCF7 (ER+/HER2−) and A375 (BRAF-mutant) cells.
finding: P12 and P14 reduced cancer cell proliferation dose-dependently, induced cell-cycle delay and senescence-like phenotype with altered SASP patterns, and showed selective activity against cancer cells with minimal effects on non-tumor chondrocytes.
dataset: MCF7 (ER+/HER2− breast cancer), A375 (BRAF-mutant melanoma), TC28a2 (non-tumoral chondrocytes)
metric: IC50, colony formation, spheroid area
result: MCF7 IC50: P12=30.22 μM, P14=2.018 μM; A375 IC50: P12=2.915 μM, P14=1.887 μM; P12 non-toxic to TC28a2 up to 35 μM

problem: Predicting CAR-NK cell cytotoxicity across different target cell types is difficult because NK cell responses integrate signals from multiple innate receptors beyond the engineered CAR, showing nonmonotonic relationships with antigen expression.
method: Multiscale mechanistic in silico model integrating heterogeneous CAR, NKR, adhesion receptor, and ligand expression (quantitative flow cytometry), signal transduction ODEs, and NK-target population kinetics, with Pareto optimization for exploring CAR proportion and signaling effects.
finding: The model accurately predicted short-term (4h), long-term (48-72h), and in vivo CAR-NK cytotoxicity across multiple tumor cell lines and donors, captured the nonmonotonic relationship between antigen density and cytotoxicity, and identified differential cytotoxicity windows between cancer and healthy cells via Pareto optimization.
dataset: Kasumi-1, HL-60, K562, SKOV3, DIPG36 cell lines; primary monocytes; multiple healthy NK cell donors; CD33CAR and Her2CAR constructs
metric: cytotoxicity (% lysis), receptor/ligand quantification (molecules/cell via PE quantitation kit)
baseline: CAR-T cell monotonic activation models, WT NK cells
result: accurate prediction of cytotoxicity across hematologic and solid tumor targets; nonmonotonic CAR-NK response captured

problem: Understanding the intracellular mechanisms underlying hippocampal place cell rate and temporal coding during spatial navigation.
method: In vivo whole-cell patch clamp recordings from hippocampal CA1 neurons in head-restrained mice navigating a virtual linear track (180 cm) on a spherical treadmill with 270° visual projection display.
finding: Identified three subthreshold signatures of place fields: asymmetric ramp-like depolarization of baseline membrane potential, increased intracellular theta oscillation amplitude, and phase precession of intracellular theta relative to LFP theta. Results are most consistent with a soma-dendritic interference model.
dataset: C57BL/6J mice (8-12 weeks), dorsal hippocampus CA1, virtual linear track
metric: firing rate maps (80 spatial bins), membrane potential, theta phase (6-10 Hz bandpass)
baseline: dual oscillator models, network models, experience-dependent models
result: robust place-cell activity with three subthreshold signatures; data consistent with soma-dendritic interference model

problem: Developing methods to image hippocampal place cell activity at cellular resolution during spatial navigation behavior in mice.
method: Custom two-photon microscope combined with virtual reality system (spherical treadmill + toroidal screen), chronic hippocampal window (cortex aspirated), and AAV2/1-synapsin-1-GCaMP3 calcium indicator in CA1 neurons.
finding: Successfully imaged populations of ~80-100 CA1 neurons with subcellular resolution during virtual navigation, optically identified place cells with properties similar to electrophysiologically defined place cells, and determined spatial correlation between place field location and anatomical position.
dataset: CA1 hippocampal neurons in head-restrained mice, virtual linear track (180 cm), populations of ~80-100 neurons per imaging session
metric: calcium transient amplitude (ΔF/F), place field spatial information, anatomical position correlation
baseline: tetrode recordings of place cells, real environment place cell studies
result: subcellular resolution imaging of place cells; stray light <5% of baseline fluorescence; place fields consistent with electrophysiology

problem: Integrating cellular knowledge across scales to comprehend cellular behaviors
method: Computational modeling approaches for virtual cell construction
finding: Next generation virtual cells achievable goal for computational biology
dataset: cell

problem: Students have difficulty conceptualizing dynamic nature of cells at multiple scales
method: Virtual Cell Animations for education
finding: Animations effectively improved student conceptual understanding
dataset: students

problem: Virtual memory CD8 T cells found in unchallenged mice, mechanism unclear
method: Analysis of virtual memory T cell development and homeostatic proliferation
finding: VM cell development involves homeostatic proliferation with stratified responsiveness
dataset: mice

problem: Drug repurposing strategy for SARS-CoV-2 antiviral agents
method: Molecular docking and drug screening against viral protease
finding: Identified antiviral compounds targeting SARS-CoV-2
dataset: SARS-CoV-2
metric: binding affinity

problem: Automated cell nucleus segmentation for quantitative pathology analysis
method: Contour tracing for grayscale image segmentation
finding: Improved automated cell nucleus segmentation method
dataset: cell images
metric: precision, recall

problem: Virtual staining for morphological analysis of biological cells
method: Digital holography-based virtual staining without chemical stains
finding: Virtual staining circumvents toxicity, saves time, enables concurrent staining
dataset: human cells

problem: Mechanism of morphogenetic signals engaging regulatory networks in embryonic patterning
method: Gene regulatory network model embedded in cellular agents (GARMEN)
finding: Integrated three hierarchical levels of cell-fate control
dataset: human iPSCs

problem: SARS-CoV-2 entry mechanism via spike protein binding to ACE2 receptor
method: Molecular dynamics simulation with GROMOS96 force field
finding: Understanding virus-receptor recognition mechanism
dataset: SARS-CoV-2
metric: binding

problem: Assessing educational impact of VR on cell biology learning and engagement
method: VR-based cell biology course using HTC Vive platform
finding: VR enhanced student learning experience in cell biology
dataset: 62 students
metric: survey responses

problem: Path integration error correction during spatial navigation
method: Direct recording of grid-like neuronal activity during spatial tasks
finding: Novel cue cells in MEC with spatial firing patterns
dataset: mouse
metric: correlation

problem: Selective, cell-active small molecule inhibitors for cancer-promoting PRL phosphatases are lacking despite PRL-3's clear role in cancer metastasis.
method: Ligand-based (USR, ROCS, MACCS, UFSRAT) and structure-based (Vina docking) virtual screening of ZINC database (~3.5M molecules), followed by biochemical DiFMUP assay and SAR optimization.
finding: Ligand-based approaches outperformed docking for PRL-3. A novel 2-cyano-2-ene-ester chemotype was identified and optimized to a low micromolar, cell-active PRL inhibitor that selectively reverts PRL-3-induced cell migration.
dataset: ZINC database v8 (3,472,461 lead-like molecules, 641M conformers), recombinant PRL-1/2/3, VHR, PTP1B, TCPTP
metric: IC50, hit rate, selectivity over other PTPs, cell migration reversion
result: Low micromolar PRL inhibitor; USR screened 641M conformers in 57s vs ROCS 128h; ligand-based hit rate superior to docking

problem: Automated detection of specific cells (c-Fos+) in 3D whole-brain light-sheet image stacks is challenging due to variable signal-to-noise ratios across large volumes.
method: VR-trained deep learning pipeline (DELiVR, 3D BasicUNet) with SHANEL tissue clearing, light-sheet microscopy, and registration to Allen Brain Atlas.
finding: VR annotation was significantly faster than 2D slice-based annotation and improved annotation quality. DELiVR outperformed all tested cell segmentation approaches including ClearMap.
dataset: Cleared mouse brains (c-Fos+ neurons, CX3CR1-GFP microglia), cancer-related brain activity models
metric: Instance F1 score, annotation speed, Mann-Whitney U test
baseline: ClearMap (threshold-based), ITK-SNAP (2D slice annotation), random forest and other deep learning approaches
result: F1 improved from 0.7383 (2D) to 0.8032 (VR); VR annotation significantly faster (P=0.0005)

problem: How does ageing impair CD8+ T cell immune responses through asymmetric cell division (ACD), and can this decline be rescued?
method: Confocal microscopy of CD8 polarization on mitotic cells from young/aged P14 mice, in vitro stimulation with anti-CD3/CD28/ICAM-1, transient mTOR inhibition with rapamycin, adoptive transfer, and LCMV infection.
finding: Ageing impairs ACD in CD8+ T cells, but transient mTOR inhibition with rapamycin restores ACD rates to young levels. Virtual memory T cells retain ACD ability and memory potential during ageing, while naive CD8+ T cells lose it.
dataset: Murine CD8+ T cells from P14 mice (young 8-16wk, middle-aged >40wk, old >70wk), LCMV-WE infection model
metric: ACD rate (CD8 polarization), T-bet/pS6 asymmetry, expansion and memory potential
result: Rapamycin restored aged ACD rates to young levels; P=0.0005 for VR vs 2D speed (different paper — for this paper: significant ACD impairment with ageing, rescue by rapamycin, unpaired two-tailed Student's t test)

problem: How do PKD1/PKD2 mutations drive cell behaviors underlying ADPKD cystogenesis, and what are the respective roles of cell adhesion and proliferation?
method: GGH/Cellular Potts model virtual-tissue simulation in CompuCell3D of a 15-cell-long renal tubule segment, validated with in vitro HK-2 3D collagen cyst formation assays using cadherin-8 ectopic expression.
finding: Reduced cell-cell adhesion alone suffices to initiate cystogenesis matching cad8-induced cysts in vitro. Full range of in vivo cyst morphologies requires changes in both adhesion and proliferation; saccular cysts arise from decreased contact inhibition.
dataset: HK-2 renal epithelial cells in 3D collagen, cadherin-8 adenoviral expression, ADPKD cyst morphologies
metric: Cyst morphology classification, adhesion energy parameters, contact inhibition thresholds
baseline: Normal renal tubule with wild-type adhesion and proliferation parameters
result: Loss-of-adhesion simulations reproduced in vitro cad8-induced cysts; both adhesion and proliferation changes required for full in vivo morphology range

problem: Cell biology education lacks accessible, realistic tools for teaching cell ultrastructure, especially in developing countries without electron microscopy access.
method: Three 3D EM strategies (single-section vectorization via Rhinoceros, serial-section TEM reconstruction, serial electron tomography via IMOD) combined with 3D printing to produce physical cell models.
finding: 3D prototypes of blood cells were successfully produced at up to 34,000x magnification, and digital models were made freely available for download and printing at any institution.
dataset: Rat blood leukocytes (neutrophils, lymphocytes) from Wistar rats
metric: Scale factor, structural fidelity to EM images
baseline: Traditional textbook illustrations and 2D EM images
result: Neutrophil prototype 34,000x larger than original cell; models deposited for free download

problem: How can AI virtual cell (AIVC) technology transform cancer research by enabling in silico experimentation across molecular to tissue scales?
method: Perspective paper proposing AIVC framework with universal representations, manipulator/decoder virtual instruments, and a computational-experimental iterative paradigm for oncology.
finding: AIVC technology is particularly suited for oncology due to cancer's bottom-up pathogenic pattern at the cellular level, enabling multi-scale tumor simulation, virtual drug screening, and dynamic tumor evolution reconstruction.

problem: How do micro-environmental selection pressures drive the somatic evolution of tumor cell aggression, particularly changes in adhesion properties?
method: Multi-cell virtual-tissue model using GGH/Cellular Potts model in CompuCell3D with heritable parameter variation (cadherins/integrins), nutrient-limited growth, stem cell dynamics, and senescence.
finding: Emergent stratification into regions with different survival rates drives evolution toward less cohesive cells (lower cadherins, higher integrins). Less cohesive clusters outgrow cohesive ones due to greater nutrient access, eventually producing invasive dispersed cells.
dataset: In silico generic solid tumor with stem-like and somatic cancer cells, glucose-limited microenvironment
metric: Cell population dynamics, adhesion parameter evolution (cadherin/integrin levels), tumor diameter
baseline: Initial cohesive tumor state (high cadherin, low integrin)
result: Tumor reaches ~500μm diameter in ~1 month; necrotic core ~250μm; invasive dispersed cells dominant after ~3 simulated years

problem: Traditional industrial robotic work cell design and programming are inefficient, requiring complex commands and trained personnel while posing safety risks.
method: VR-based system (VR-Rocell) integrating VR-RoWL for layout planning and VR-RoT for robot teach-by-demonstrate programming, built with C++, OpenGL, and VRML on open platform architecture.
finding: VR-generated layout models are compatible with commercial software without information loss, and generated KUKA robot commands are workable. Actual operation errors stem from robot dynamics, not program accuracy.
dataset: Electronics casing assembly task with two KUKA KR16 industrial robots
metric: Model compatibility with commercial software, command accuracy, assembly task completion
baseline: Traditional robot programming and commercial simulation software (e.g., KUKA SimPro)
result: Output models compatible with commercial software; generated KUKA commands workable in simulator and actual robot

problem: Can compressive shearing forces induce mechanochemical oligomerization of glycine, relevant to prebiotic peptide bond formation under extreme conditions?
method: DFTB quantum molecular dynamics simulations in a virtual rotational diamond anvil cell (V-RDAC) with ensembles of 10 independent 25 ps trajectories per condition, spanning 2.9-15.6 GPa and shear rates 0.5-1.0 Å/ps.
finding: Significant oligomerization occurs at pressures ≥10.2 GPa, forming glycine mass equivalent oligomers up to 5-GME. Lower shear rates produce higher oligomer yields with less molecular degradation. Peptide-like bonds form through condensation reactions.
dataset: Amorphous glycine (48 molecules per simulation cell), diamond anvil surfaces
metric: Mass fraction of oligomers, GME classification, oligomer lifetime, number of distinct molecular species
baseline: Unreacted glycine at low pressures (2.9-6.7 GPa, few/no reactions observed)
result: Up to 256 distinct species at 15.6 GPa; significant oligomerization at ≥10.2 GPa; 4.5 ns total trajectory time across all conditions

problem: What are the unresolved questions in applying AI virtual cells for cancer research?
method: Commentary/perspective article discussing open challenges (no experimental methods).

problem: Whether helminth infection, typically associated with immunosuppression, can confer cross-protection against unrelated bacterial infections, and what immune mechanism mediates this effect.
method: In vivo mouse infection model: C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice infected with Heligmosomoides polygyrus, drug-cured, then challenged with Listeria monocytogenes (i.p.) or Yersinia pseudotuberculosis (oral). Flow cytometry for CD8 TVM cell phenotyping (CD44hi CD49dlo CD62Lhi). Adoptive transfer, mixed bone marrow chimeras, IL-4 reporter mice, and KO strains.
finding: Helminth infection drives IL-4-dependent, antigen-independent expansion of virtual memory CD8 T (TVM) cells that disseminate systemically and confer innate non-cognate 'collateral protection' against bacterial infection, transferable to naïve animals.
dataset: C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice, H. polygyrus / L. monocytogenes / Y. pseudotuberculosis infection models
metric: survival rate, bacterial burden (CFU in liver/spleen), CD8 TVM cell frequency and number, median survival time
result: ~50% survival in Hp-cured vs 0% naïve for Yp challenge, 11-day median survival prolongation, significantly reduced Lm liver burden at day 3

problem: Whether virtual non-contrast (VNC) images and virtual monoenergetic images (VMIs) from dual-layer spectral CT can replace true non-contrast and corticomedullary phase scans for renal cell carcinoma diagnosis, thereby reducing radiation dose.
method: Retrospective analysis of 62 RCC patients who underwent four-phase contrast-enhanced dual-layer spectral CT.
