Quantitative comparison of attenuation (HU), noise (SD), SNR between TNC and VNC images; comparison of corticomedullary phase with 40-100 keV VMIs from nephrographic phase. Subjective image quality scoring by two radiologists.
finding: VNC images are equivalent to TNC for RCC lesion characterization (no significant difference in attenuation, noise, SNR). VMIs from nephrographic phase can approximate corticomedullary phase information. Eliminating TNC and corticomedullary phases reduces radiation dose by 50.5%.
dataset: 62 pathologically confirmed RCC patients (51 ccRCC, 5 papillary, 3 chromophobe, 3 other)
metric: CT attenuation (HU), image noise (SD), SNR, subjective image quality score, radiation dose (DLP)
baseline: True non-contrast (TNC) images and corticomedullary phase images
result: TNC vs VNC tumor attenuation p>0.05, attenuation difference <3 HU for all tissues except fat, 96.78% within 10 HU, 50.5% dose reduction

problem: Existing VR tools for single-cell data visualization require expensive hardware ($2,300-$4,550) and advanced computational skills, limiting accessibility for experimental biologists.
method: Web-based VR platform (singlecellVR) compatible with low-cost hardware (Google Cardboard, ~$8). Companion package scvr for one-command data conversion from Scanpy, Seurat, PAGA, STREAM, scVelo. Supports clustering, trajectory inference, and RNA velocity visualization in 3D VR.
finding: singlecellVR provides accessible VR visualization of single-cell transcriptomic, epigenomic, and proteomic data at minimal cost, with a growing database of pre-analyzed datasets and streamlined data preparation.
dataset: Multiple single-cell RNA-seq, scATAC-seq, and CITE-seq datasets (preloaded database)
result: Hardware cost ~$8 (Google Cardboard) vs $2,300-$4,550 for competing tools

problem: Identifying selective small-molecule modulators of E-cadherin-mediated cell-cell adhesion as potential anti-cancer agents, leveraging the first crystal structure of a cadherin-inhibitor complex.
method: Structure-based virtual screening using Glide (Schrödinger) against E-cadherin X-dimer (PDB: 4ZTE) with MD-reconstructed adhesion arms. Screened Asinex and LifeChemicals databases. Validated by spheroid cell-cell adhesion assays and cell invasion assays in BxPC-3 cells with selective E-cadherin/P-cadherin knockdown.
finding: Two compounds (AS9 and AS11) selectively inhibit E-cadherin-mediated cell-cell adhesion at micromolar concentrations without affecting P-cadherin or cell viability. AS11 also shows anti-invasive properties.
dataset: BxPC-3 human pancreatic tumor cells (E-cadh/P-cadh, E-cadh only, P-cadh only knockdown lines)
metric: spheroid compactness (area), cell invasion assay, cell viability (trypan blue exclusion)
baseline: DMSO control, FR159 peptidomimetic inhibitor (active at 1 mM)
result: AS9 and AS11 effective at 0.05 mM (50 µM), selective for E-cadherin over P-cadherin (p values significant)

problem: Lack of user-friendly web-based tools for comprehensive gene expression profiling, network analysis, and meta-analysis that integrate multiple network types and modern visualization including VR.
method: Web platform supporting 17 species, with Galaxy-based RNAseq pipeline (HISAT2, kallisto), differential expression (limma, edgeR, DESeq2), multiple network types (PPI, gene regulatory, co-expression, tissue-specific), and visualization in 2D, 3D, and VR. Interactive heatmaps, enrichment networks, Venn/chord diagrams for meta-analysis.
finding: NetworkAnalyst 3.0 fills gaps in web-based network analysis, meta-analysis, and visual analytics for gene expression data, with new features including raw RNAseq processing, cell-type-specific networks, project management, and VR visualization.
dataset: 17 species transcriptome databases, multiple PPI/regulatory network databases

problem: Whether environmental sensory information and self-motion are encoded in complementary or unified representations by place and grid cells, and whether these cell types differentially weight visual vs. physical motion inputs.
method: Virtual reality system for head-fixed mice navigating 2D virtual open arenas with dissociated visual and physical motion gains (G=2/3, G=1, G=2). Tetrode recordings of CA1 place cells and mEC grid cells. Motor influence (MI) scores computed from firing rate maps and population vectors.
finding: Place cell firing predominantly reflects visual environmental inputs (MI near 0), while grid cell firing shows greater influence of physical motion (MI closer to 1). Even simultaneously recorded place and grid cells need not be mutually coherent.
dataset: CA1 place cells and mEC grid cells in head-fixed mice navigating virtual 2D arenas
metric: motor influence score (MI), spatial information (bits/spike), firing rate, gridness score, stretch factor F
baseline: Baseline gain G=1 trial (visual and physical motion matched)
result: Place cells MI near 0 (visual), grid cells MI significantly higher (p<0.001); place field size ratio unchanged in visual coords (p=0.77) but significant in motor coords (p<0.001)

problem: Identifying novel non-SAM-competitive PRMT5 inhibitors for non-small cell lung cancer therapy, targeting the newly discovered EPZ015666 binding site rather than the SAM-binding pocket.
method: Three-level structure-based virtual screening (HTVS, SP, XP) using Glide against PRMT5-SAM complex (PDB: 4X61). Screened 1,671,908 compounds from Chemdiv, Specs, TargetMol. Validated by MTT assay, Western blot (H4R3me2s, FGFR3, eIF4E, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, ERK), MD simulation and normal mode analysis.
finding: Compound T1551 identified as a non-SAM-competitive PRMT5 inhibitor that reduces H4R3me2s levels, downregulates FGFR3 and eIF4E, and disrupts PI3K/AKT/mTOR and ERK signaling in NSCLC cells.
dataset: A549 and H460 non-small cell lung cancer cell lines
metric: IC50 (cell viability), enrichment factor (EF), AUC-ROC, H4R3me2s level
baseline: EPZ015666 (known PRMT5 inhibitor), PRMT5-EPZ015666 docking model without SAM (EF1%=38.3, AUC=0.92)

problem: Generating fluorescence microscopy images from bright-field images (virtual staining) for adipocyte cells, where cellular structures like nuclei and lipid defects are poorly visible in bright-field, to enable downstream feature extraction without toxic staining.
method: Deep learning models (DenseNet-based U-Net) tailored per channel: privileged information (segmentation masks via LUPI) for nuclear channel, image gradient loss + adversarial training (GAN) for lipids channel. Ablation study across three training-validation splits. Feature-level evaluation using CellProfiler (morphology, intensity, count).
finding: LUPI paradigm with segmentation masks significantly improved nuclear morphology and count features. Gradient + adversarial loss faithfully captured lipid defects (intensity depressions). Feature-level model selection outperforms pixel-level MAE for downstream biological analysis.
dataset: Adipocyte cell images (bright-field and fluorescence, nuclei/lipids/cytoplasm channels)
metric: MAE (pixel-level), CellProfiler feature MAE (morphology, intensity, count), Mann-Whitney rank test
baseline: L1-only base model (MAE loss), models without LUPI or adversarial components
result: Significant improvements (p<0.05 Mann-Whitney) in morphology and count features with LUPI; adversarial training improved lipid defect reconstruction

problem: Whether grid cells or other spatially-tuned neural populations are truly responsible for path integration, and how grid cell remapping relates to place cell remapping across multiple environments.
method: Continuous attractor recurrent neural network (CARNN) trained on path integration across multiple environments with global place cell remapping. Dimensionality reduction (UMAP), persistent homology (Ripser), DBSCAN clustering. Pruning studies to assess contribution of cell subpopulations to path integration performance.
finding: Toroidal cells (band-like, not high grid score) are crucial for path integration while grid cells are not. A persistent torus manifold across environments underlies path integration. The model reveals generalization limits when scaling to many environments.
dataset: Simulated 2D random walk trajectories in square environments with remapped place cell ensembles
metric: decoding error (MSE), grid score, toroidal structure persistence (persistent homology), path integration accuracy
baseline: Random cell pruning, grid cell pruning, full unpruned network
result: Pruning toroidal cells rapidly degrades path integration; pruning grid cells has relatively little impact on decoding accuracy

problem: Discovering novel ATAD2 bromodomain inhibitors with selective scaffolds for breast cancer therapy, as ATAD2 is a validated oncology target with few known inhibitors.
method: Structure-based virtual screening (pharmacophore + CDOCKER docking) against ATAD2 (PDB: 5LJ0) from 1.7M compounds (Specs, Chemdiv). Counter-screening against BRD4 for selectivity. Validated by TR-FRET binding assay, MTT viability, Western blot, flow cytometry (apoptosis/autophagy markers).
finding: AM879 is a potent selective ATAD2 bromodomain inhibitor with no BRD2-4 cross-reactivity. It suppresses c-Myc expression, induces apoptosis and autophagy via PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling in MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells.
dataset: MDA-MB-231 triple-negative breast cancer cells
metric: IC50 (enzyme inhibition, cell viability), inhibition rate at 20 µM, apoptosis rate
baseline: BAY850 (positive control ATAD2 inhibitor), BRD2-4 selectivity panel
result: AM879 ATAD2 IC50=3565 nM, MDA-MB-231 IC50=2.43 µM, 90% inhibition at 20 µM, no BRD2-4 activity

problem: Need for user-friendly tools for multimodal single-cell data visualization
method: Single-Cell Virtual Cytometer software for flow cytometry-like visualization
finding: Open-source software for cell population definition and UMAP/t-SNE mapping
dataset: PBMC CITE-seq

problem: Understanding aging effects on CD8 T cell responses to influenza
method: Comparative analysis of CD8 T cell response in aged versus young mice
finding: Aged mice CD8 T cell response dominated by memory T cell pool-derived cells
dataset: aged and young mice

problem: Quantifying bacterial abundance in cell culture requires high-throughput methods
method: Virtual Colony Counting for automated bacterial quantification
finding: VCC enables automated threshold-based bacterial quantification
dataset: bacterial strains
metric: absorbance threshold

problem: 3D organoid cultures require scaffold materials complicating cell interactions
method: Acoustic virtual scaffold for scaffold-free tumor organoid culture
finding: Contact-free 3D cell manipulation for organoid-immune cell interactions
dataset: tumor organoids

problem: Grid cells represent spatial location during navigation
method: Direct recording of grid-like neuronal activity in human patients
finding: Grid cell activity recorded from human brain during navigation
dataset: human patients

problem: Virtual screening for compounds targeting SARS-CoV-2 spike protein
method: Protein-protein docking of host proteases with spike protein
finding: Phytochemicals bind at active sites of furin and TMPRSS2
dataset: SARS-CoV-2
metric: binding affinity

problem: T cell activation requires integration of multiple signals from dendritic cells
method: Virtual lymph node model for T cell priming dynamics
finding: T cell priming spans 3-4 days before clonal expansion and egress
dataset: mice
metric: expansion kinetics
result: 3-4 day priming

problem: Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma lacks effective therapeutic options
method: Differential gene expression analysis from TCGA and GEO databases
finding: Identified potential therapeutic targets in ESCC
dataset: ESCC patients
metric: p-value

problem: Whole-cell models require parameter estimation for genotype-to-phenotype prediction
method: DREAM8 Parameter Estimation Challenge for M. genitalium submodels
finding: 45 researchers from 16 institutions tested parameter estimation methods
dataset: M. genitalium
metric: parameter estimation accuracy

problem: Whole-cell simulations need visualization tools for data exploration
method: WholeCellViz web-based interactive visualization software
finding: 14 animated visualizations including metabolic and signaling pathways
dataset: whole-cell simulations
result: 14 modules

problem: Researchers lack tools to organize, search, and retrieve large-scale whole-cell model simulation data for analysis.
method: Developed WholeCellSimDB, a hybrid relational/HDF database architecture combining fast metadata search with efficient retrieval of large simulation result datasets, with a Web-based graphical interface.
finding: The hybrid architecture enables researchers to quickly search metadata and retrieve large amounts of results data; the database stores simulations of M. genitalium whole-cell model and three E. coli models.
dataset: M. genitalium whole-cell model, E. coli ODE central carbon metabolism model

problem: Whole-cell modeling requires spatial constraints and structural data from imaging that cannot be obtained by computation alone.
method: Soft X-ray Tomography (SXT) generates 3D reconstructions of cellular ultrastructure at ~25 nm resolution; combined with correlative fluorescence microscopy and cryoET for molecular-level detail.
finding: SXT provides a foundational structural framework for whole-cell modeling by quantifying organelle landscapes, detecting biochemical composition changes, and enabling overlay of molecular information from correlative imaging.
dataset: Whole cells imaged at mesoscopic scale (~25 nm)
metric: Spatial resolution (~25 nm), linear absorption coefficient (LAC)

problem: Comprehensive characterization of somatic structural variations (SVs) and their mutational mechanisms in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) is largely unclear.
method: Re-analyzed whole-genome sequencing data from 31 ESCCs using Meerkat algorithm for somatic SV prediction and Patchwork for copy-number changes; validated with PCR (98% accuracy).
finding: Deletions and translocations with NHEJ/alt-EJ signatures dominate; chromothripsis, kataegis, and BFB events occur in 55% of ESCCs leading to oncogene amplification (FGFR1, CCND1, EGFR, MYC); ~30% harbor BFB-derived CCND1 amplification; high frequency of whole-genome duplication and recurrent CDCA7 amplification identified.
dataset: 31 paired ESCC tumors and matched normal tissues (WGS, EGA)
metric: SV count per genome, PCR validation rate
result: Average 168 SVs/tumor (range 10-364), 98% PCR validation (48/49), 55% with chromothripsis/kataegis/BFB, ~30% with BFB-derived CCND1 amplification

problem: Single-channel recording limitations discourage physiologists from determining ion channel kinetic mechanisms; existing methods analyze stimulation traces individually rather than simultaneously.
method: Genetic search algorithm combined with gradient descent, using NEURON simulator on a 10-node parallel cluster, to fit whole-cell voltage-clamp data to Hodgkin-Huxley and Markov chain models via full-trace analysis of all stimulation protocols simultaneously.
finding: The GA with adaptive Gaussian mutation range successfully fits ion channel kinetics from whole-cell recordings without requiring initial parameter guesses, reproducing voltage-gated sodium and potassium channel behavior from rat cortical pyramidal neurons.
dataset: Layer 5 pyramidal neurons of rat cortex (nucleated outside-out patch configuration)
metric: Cost function (sum of squared differences), parameter convergence
baseline: Gradient descent algorithms, disjoint conventional single-trace analysis
result: Successful convergence from random starting points within ~500 generations; adaptive Gaussian mutation proved more efficient than fixed ranges

problem: Optimal gene expression levels for whole cell biosensor regulatory components are nonintuitive, and classical iterative approaches cannot efficiently explore multidimensional experimental space.
method: Design of experiments (DoE) methodology with definitive screening design to systematically vary genetic regulatory elements (promoters, RBS) of PCA and ferulic acid biosensors in E. coli, building statistical models of component interactions.
finding: DoE-guided optimization achieved up to 30-fold increase in maximum signal output, >500-fold dynamic range improvement, ~4-order sensing range expansion, and >1500-fold sensitivity increase; enabled both digital and analogue dose-response behavior.
dataset: E. coli DH5α, BL21, BW25113 with PCA and ferulic acid biosensors
metric: Dynamic range (ON/OFF ratio), sensitivity (EC50), signal output (fluorescence), sensing range
result: 30-fold max signal increase, >500-fold dynamic range, ~4-order magnitude sensing range, >1500-fold sensitivity increase

problem: Multidrug resistant A. baumannii infections are increasing, and whole-cell vaccines contain high endotoxin levels from LPS that complicate human use.
method: Developed LPS-deficient A. baumannii strain IB010 (462-nt lpxD deletion), formalin-inactivated, with Alhydrogel adjuvant; tested in C57BL/6 and BALB/c mouse disseminated infection models with cyclophosphamide-induced immunosuppression.
finding: IB010 vaccination produced robust IgG1/IgG2c antibody responses, significantly reduced post-infection tissue bacterial loads and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6), and protected mice against both ATCC 19606 and a clinical isolate.
dataset: C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice, A. baumannii ATCC 19606 and clinical isolate Ab-154
metric: Endotoxin units (EU), bacterial CFU/organ, serum cytokine levels, antibody titers (ELISA)
baseline: PBS + adjuvant control, wild-type ATCC 19606 whole cells (LPS-containing)
result: Endotoxin <1.0 EU/10^6 cells (vs wild-type), significantly lower tissue bacterial loads (P<0.05), significantly lower IL-1β/TNF-α/IL-6 (P<0.05)

problem: Non-invasive assessment of mesenchymal stem cell integration and bio-distribution is needed to evaluate cell therapy for rheumatoid arthritis and enable clinical translation.
method: SPION (SiMAG)-labelled murine MSCs tracked via MRI in a collagen-induced arthritis mouse model; in vitro and in vivo detection thresholds established with varying particle concentrations (0-10 μg/ml) and cell doses (10³-5×10⁵).
finding: SiMAG labelling did not affect cell viability, proliferation, or differentiation; labelled MSCs were trackable in the synovial joint up to 7 days by MRI; SiMAG-labelled and unlabelled MSCs both significantly reduced joint swelling.
dataset: BALB/c and C57Bl/6 mice, collagen-induced arthritis model, 300,000 mMSCs
metric: T2eff (MRI), joint swelling measurement, Prussian blue staining
result: In vitro threshold: 1 μg/ml (300K cells) or 100K cells (5-10 μg/ml); significant joint swelling decrease (P<0.01); in vivo tracking up to 7 days

problem: PD-L1 immunohistochemistry is needed to guide PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor therapy in NSCLC, but the association between PD-L1 status and tumor histopathological patterns is poorly understood.
method: Multi-field-of-view deep learning model with residual neural network backbone trained on >145,000 tiles from H&E whole-slide images (48 training, 82 test NSCLC patients) to predict PD-L1 status.
finding: The model accurately predicted tumor PD-L1 status from H&E images, remaining robust across PD-L1 cutoff thresholds and simulated interpathologist disagreement, suggesting PD-L1 expression correlates with morphological features of the tumor microenvironment.
dataset: 130 NSCLC patients (48 train, 82 test), paired H&E and PD-L1 immunostained WSIs
metric: AUC-ROC, P-value
result: AUC=0.80 (P<<0.01), AUC=0.67-0.81 across cutoff thresholds, AUC=0.63-0.77 with label shuffling

problem: Cancer vaccine application is limited because few cancer-associated antigens are known to induce tumor rejection, especially in cancers lacking specific antigens.
method: Ex vivo infection of murine CRC cells (CT26, CMT93) with Ad881 oncolytic adenovirus (E1 driven by midkine promoter) to induce immunogenic cell death; formalin-free whole cell vaccination in BALB/c mouse colorectal cancer model.
finding: Repeated vaccination with Ad881-infected cells induced prophylactic immunity with 38% tumor rejection; Ad881 induced significantly higher immunogenic cell death markers (HMGB1 and ATP) compared to replication-defective Ad884 or oxaliplatin.
dataset: BALB/c mice, CT26 and CMT93 murine CRC cell lines, DLD-1 human CRC cells
metric: Tumor rejection rate, HMGB1 (ng/ml), ATP (nmol/l), cell viability (Alamar blue)
baseline: Replication-defective Ad884, oxaliplatin treatment, no vaccination
result: Tumor rejection: 0% (no vax), 4% (single), 38% (repeated); HMGB1: 24.3 vs 8.6 (Ad884) vs 3.7 (oxaliplatin) ng/ml; ATP: 48.2 vs 15.4 vs 1.8 nmol/l

problem: Understanding the utility and limitations of genetically engineered whole-cell bioreporters for environmental monitoring of bioremediation and pollution.
method: Review of P. fluorescens HK44, a bioluminescent (luxCDABE) bioreporter with naphthalene-responsive catabolic pathway, applied across solids, liquids, and volatile environments; genome recently sequenced.
finding: HK44 serves as a model bioreporter for PAH detection in environmental matrices, complementing standard analytical methods through speed, cost, and bioavailability assessment; regulatory restrictions and single-chemical focus limit real-world applications.
dataset: P. fluorescens HK44 in soils, sands, water, wastewater, air matrices
metric: Bioluminescence emission (luxCDABE), detection limits, response time

problem: Whole cell model with codon resolution for mRNA translation is lacking
method: Single-cell translation elongation model operating at cell, mRNA and codon levels
finding: High correlations between simulated and empirical values
dataset: E. coli
metric: accuracy

problem: Cardiac pump function modeling requires multiscale electromechanics framework
method: Four-chamber electromechanics simulation framework
finding: Simulated pressure-volume loops for cardiac chambers
dataset: patient
metric: sensitivity

problem: In vivo crowding effects on biological macromolecule structure and dynamics
method: All-atom molecular dynamics of atomistic cytoplasmic models
finding: Studied protein and nucleic acid behavior in crowded cytoplasm
dataset: M. genitalium

problem: NSCLC management cost estimation across disease stages
method: Whole-disease model of NSCLC diagnostic and therapeutic actions
finding: Mean expected cost per NSCLC patient estimated
dataset: patients
metric: cost

problem: PD-L1 role in neuroblastoma resistance to immunity
method: Mouse model with whole cell vaccine and checkpoint inhibition
finding: Immunogenic neuroblastoma acquires adaptive immune resistance via PD-L1
dataset: mouse
metric: correlation

problem: Neural induction and differentiation from human embryonic stem cells
method: hESC neural differentiation with defined protocols
finding: Generated functional neural derivatives from hESCs
dataset: human cells

problem: Breast cancer treatment requires patient-specific drug sensitivity profiling
method: Ex vivo whole tumor cell culture for drug sensitivity testing
finding: Established breast cancer whole tumor cell culture system
dataset: patient tumors
metric: accuracy

problem: Q-VAX whole cell vaccine causes reactogenic responses requiring improvement
method: Evaluation of vaccine reactogenicity in sensitized animal models
finding: Sensitized mice produce local reactogenic responses to whole cell vaccine
dataset: mouse

problem: Quantifying mHA variation impact on GVHD in stem cell transplant
method: Whole exome sequencing of donor-recipient pairs
finding: Analyzed exome sequencing data for 78 donor-recipient pairs
dataset: 78 patients
metric: p-value

problem: Multiscale modeling of cardiac excitation-contraction coupling
method: Fully coupled spatially-detailed simulation tool for cardiac ECC
finding: Developed fully coupled simulation tool for cardiac modeling
dataset: cardiac cells

problem: Predicting chemotherapeutic response in patients using baseline tumor gene expression data, bridging in vitro cell line drug sensitivity to in vivo clinical outcomes.
method: Whole-genome ridge regression models fitted on gene expression vs drug IC50 in CGP cell lines, with ComBat batch correction, applied to baseline tumor expression from clinical trials.
finding: Cell line-derived ridge regression models predict in vivo drug response in 3 of 4 clinical trials, with performance comparable to or better than gene signatures derived directly from clinical data.
dataset: CGP cell line panel (~10,000 genes), docetaxel/bortezomib/cisplatin/erlotinib clinical trials
metric: Classification accuracy, cross-validation performance
baseline: Gene signatures derived from clinical trial data, random forests, PAM, Lasso, ElasticNet
result: Predictions equally good or better than clinical-derived gene signatures in 3/4 trials

problem: Providing a freely accessible, comprehensive collection of small molecule crystal structures to support world-wide crystallographic collaboration.
method: Open-access database with web-based search (unit cell parameters, chemical composition, SMARTS/SMILES substructure), CIF format storage, and user-driven deposition platform.
finding: COD aggregated ~150,000 crystal structures with open access, enabling material identification via powder diffractograms, teaching, and grid computing applications.
dataset: ~150,000 crystal structures (organic, inorganic, metal-organic, minerals)
result: ~150,000 structures collected, estimated ~800,000 total known structures

problem: Understanding how extracellular vesicles interact with peripheral blood mononuclear cells in whole blood to inform EV-based targeted drug delivery.
method: Ex vivo whole blood model using PalmGRET/MemGlow-labeled EVs spiked into macaque blood, quantified by flow cytometry and nanoluciferase assays across dose and time courses.
finding: EVs preferentially associate with CD20+ B cells in ex vivo whole blood at levels similar to in vivo, validating ex vivo blood as a reliable model for studying EV-blood cell interactions.
dataset: Pig-tailed macaque (Macaca nemestrina) whole blood, Expi293F- and U-87 MG-derived EVs
metric: % EV-positive cells by flow cytometry, nanoluciferase RLU normalized by total protein
baseline: In vivo intravenous EV administration in macaques
result: >60% CD20+ B cells associated with EVs, ex vivo levels similar to in vivo

problem: Developing a vaccine against Vibrio harveyi vibriosis to reduce mortality in marine aquaculture fish as an alternative to antibiotics.
method: Formalin-killed whole cell V. harveyi (FKVh) vaccine administered intraperitoneally with booster dose at week 2, evaluated by ELISA (IgM), lysozyme activity assays, and live challenge at 10^8 CFU/fish.
finding: FKVh vaccination significantly enhanced survival and immune response (IgM antibodies in serum, mucus, gut lavage and lysozyme activity) in marine red hybrid tilapia against vibriosis.
dataset: 180 marine red hybrid tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus × O. mossambicus), V. harveyi strain Vh1
metric: Relative percentage survival, IgM antibody titer (ELISA OD), lysozyme activity (U/mg)
result: 87% survival (vaccinated) vs 20% (control), p<0.05

problem: Creating a comprehensive, executable mechanistic model of the cell division cycle molecular network to enable genome-scale signaling models and eventually eukaryotic whole-cell models.
method: rxncon reaction-contingency language for genome-scale mechanistic modeling with parameter-free Boolean network simulation (BoolNet), validated by genotype-to-phenotype predictions at residue resolution.
finding: The cell division cycle control network comprises three distinct control circuits (G1/S, G2/M, M/G1) monitoring three independent replication cycles, rather than a single closed cycle as previously modeled.
dataset: Saccharomyces cerevisiae cell division cycle (357 components, 229 proteins, 790 reactions, 1238 states, 598 contingencies)
metric: Phenotype prediction accuracy (viable/inviable mutants at residue resolution)
baseline: Previous closed-cycle CDC models without explicit replication cycle dependence
result: 357 components, 790 reactions, 3 distinct control circuits identified

problem: Developing a computational framework for iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes that incorporates experimental variability to predict patient-specific cardiac arrhythmia vulnerability.
method: Whole-cell Hodgkin-Huxley model with single exponential voltage-dependent gating, parameterized to multiple experimental iPSC-CM datasets, with population-of-models variability analysis across 5 key currents.
finding: Experimentally-informed population of cellular models reproduces the full range of inter-subject variability observed in iPSC-CMs and links molecular mechanisms to immature vs mature phenotypes.
dataset: iPSC-CM experimental data from multiple laboratories (Ma et al., Jalife Lab — conventional and PDMS preparations)
metric: Action potential duration, calcium transient amplitude, spontaneous beating rate, maximum diastolic potential
baseline: ten Tusscher 2004 and Shannon 2004 cardiomyocyte model formulations
result: Population models predict robust inter-subject variability matching experimental iPSC-CM phenotypes

problem: Determining the frequency and functional importance of extrachromosomal DNA (ecDNA) amplification across human cancers.
method: Whole-genome sequencing, FISH, metaphase cytogenetic analysis of 2,572 cells across 17 cancer types, with ECdetect software for automated ecDNA detection and mathematical modeling of amplification dynamics.
finding: ecDNA is found in nearly half of human cancers but almost never in normal cells; driver oncogenes are most commonly amplified on ecDNA, increasing transcript levels and intratumoral heterogeneity.
dataset: 17 cancer types, 2,572 metaphase cells, NCI-60 cell line panel, patient-derived xenografts
metric: ecDNA frequency, oncogene copy number, transcript levels, intratumoral heterogeneity
result: ecDNA in ~50% of cancers, almost never in normal cells

problem: Understanding genomic diversity and mutational evolution within individual breast tumors at single-cell resolution.
method: Nuc-seq: single nucleus whole-genome and exome sequencing using G2/M nuclei, combined with single nuclei copy number profiling and targeted single-molecule sequencing for validation.
finding: Aneuploid rearrangements occurred early in punctuated bursts and remained stable during clonal expansion, while point mutations evolved gradually generating extensive clonal diversity; TNBC showed 13.3× increased mutation rate.
dataset: ER+ breast cancer and triple-negative ductal carcinoma (two patients), single tumor and normal nuclei
metric: Coverage breadth, mutation frequency, mutation rate
baseline: Normal cells from same patients, bulk tumor sequencing
result: 91% mean coverage breadth, 13.3× increased mutation rate in TNBC, diverse mutations at <10% frequency

problem: Understanding how chromosome conformation and organization change across the cell cycle at single-cell resolution in mammalian cells.
method: High-throughput single-cell Hi-C with Mbo I digestion, Tn5 tagmentation, and FACS sorting into 96-well plates, analyzing thousands of mouse ESCs with whole-genome 3D structural modeling.
finding: Chromosomal compartments, TADs, insulation and loops are governed by distinct cell-cycle dynamics; DNA replication builds compartments while reducing TAD insulation; loops remain stable from G1 through S/G2.
dataset: 2,924 F1 hybrid mouse ESCs (129 × Castaneus), 1,992 passed QC
metric: Contact frequency, trans-contact fraction, TAD insulation score, compartment strength
baseline: Bulk Hi-C maps, sorted M-phase cell Hi-C
result: 1,992 cells analyzed, median 127,233 distinct contacts/cell, 5.87% median trans-contacts

problem: Systematically identifying and characterizing E. coli K-12 genes that lack experimental evidence of function to support whole-cell modeling efforts.
method: Semi-automated workflow integrating annotations from EcoCyc, EcoGene, UniProt and RegulonDB with keyword-based categorization, consensus rules, and manual curation.
finding: 1,600 of 4,623 E. coli genes (34.6%) lack experimental evidence of function; y-ome genes have lower expression, are enriched in the chromosome termination region, and most often encode membrane proteins and transporters.
dataset: E. coli K-12 MG1655 genome (4,623 unique genes across 4 knowledge bases)
metric: Gene count, percentage of genome, expression levels
baseline: Previous estimate by Hu et al.
result: 1,600/4,623 genes (34.6%) in y-ome, 111 with absolutely no evidence of function

problem: The efficiency, specificity, and off-target effects of CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing in human pre-implantation embryos are poorly understood, representing a critical knowledge gap for clinical applications.
method: CRISPR/Cas9 with three gRNAs targeting HBB was applied to human tripronuclear (3PN) zygotes; editing outcomes were assessed by T7E1 assay, Sanger sequencing, and whole-exome sequencing.
finding: CRISPR/Cas9 effectively cleaved HBB but HDR efficiency was low (14.3% ssDNA-mediated), embryos were mosaic, off-target cleavage was detected, and the endogenous HBD gene competed with exogenous donor oligos as repair template via non-crossover HDR.
dataset: Human tripronuclear (3PN) zygotes (N=54 injected, 28 survived) and 293T cells
metric: HDR efficiency, NHEJ frequency, T7E1 cleavage assay, whole-exome sequencing off-target detection
baseline: GFP mock vector (293T cells), oligo-only control
result: 48.3% HDR in 293T clones (14/29), 14.3% ssDNA-mediated editing in embryos (4/28), 25% endogenous HDR, off-target at C1QC despite 8bp mismatch

problem: The clonal relationships among metastases and the principles governing cancer cell dissemination to distant organs in lethal metastatic prostate cancer are poorly understood.
method: Whole-genome sequencing of multiple metastases from 10 prostate cancer patients with integrated subclonal architecture analysis to reconstruct metastatic spread patterns.
finding: Metastasis-to-metastasis spread was common, with polyclonal seeding observed in 5 patients; tumor suppressor mutations occurred as single events while androgen receptor pathway mutations showed convergent evolution across metastases.
dataset: Multiple metastases from 10 androgen-deprived metastatic prostate cancer patients
metric: Subclonal architecture, clonal relationship inference, convergent mutation events
result: Polyclonal seeding in 5/10 patients, convergent AR pathway mutations across independent metastases

problem: Tumor vaccines have shown little clinical success due to poor antigen presentation and tumor-exploited immunosuppression; effective vaccination strategies for neuroblastoma are needed.
method: Id2 knockdown Neuro2a whole tumor cell vaccine combined with anti-CTLA-4 checkpoint blockade was tested in syngeneic A/J mouse neuroblastoma models, with immune cell depletion and flow cytometry analysis.
finding: Id2-kd N2a cells were rejected by 60% of immune-competent mice and induced lasting immunity; combination with anti-CTLA-4 eradicated large established tumors via CD8+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity supported by CD4+ T helper cells.
dataset: Murine neuroblastoma (Neuro2a) in A/J, SCID, and nude mice
metric: Tumor rejection rate, survival, tumor volume, CD8+ T cell infiltration, IFN-gamma production
baseline: Wild-type Neuro2a cells, scrambled shRNA controls, IgG2b isotype control
result: 60% tumor rejection (Id2-kd alone), 8/9 protected on re-challenge, complete eradication of established tumors with combination therapy

problem: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a multidrug-resistant opportunistic pathogen causing severe infections, and effective immunotherapy-based treatments are needed to combat MDR-PA infections.
method: Polyclonal avian IgY antibodies raised against inactivated whole cells of PAO1 were evaluated in vitro (motility, biofilm, opsonophagocytic killing) and in vivo (acute pneumonia and burn wound murine models) against homologous and heterologous PA strains.
finding: Anti-PAO1-IgY reduced motility, biofilm formation, and cell internalization, enhanced opsonophagocytic killing, and completely protected mice against all PA strains in both acute pneumonia and burn wound models.
dataset: P. aeruginosa PAO1, PAK, MDR-R5 strains; BALB/c mice burn wound and pneumonia models
metric: Survival rate, bacterial load (CFU), motility reduction, biofilm inhibition, opsonophagocytic killing percentage
baseline: Control IgY (C-IgY from non-immunized hens), PBS control
result: 100% survival in immunotherapy groups, 32-89% growth reduction (PAO1), 17-91% (PAK), 21-63% (R5)

problem: Computational methods for inferring copy number alterations and loss of heterozygosity while accounting for tumor subclonal heterogeneity in whole-genome sequencing data remain underdeveloped.
method: TITAN, a probabilistic model using a two-chain factorial HMM, jointly analyzes allelic ratios and read depth at heterozygous SNP loci to infer CNA/LOH events and estimate cellular prevalences of tumor subpopulations.
finding: TITAN accurately detected subclonal CNA/LOH events and estimated cellular prevalences, outperforming existing methods; predictions were experimentally validated by FISH and single-cell sequencing in ovarian cancer samples.
dataset: 5 HGS ovarian cancer specimens (DG1136) at ~30x WGS, 23 breast tumor whole genomes
metric: True positive rate (TPR), false positive rate (FPR), cellular prevalence estimation accuracy
result: TPR>=0.9 for events>=100 SNPs (54/54 detected), FPR=0.04, prevalence estimates within range (0.52 vs expected 0.52, 0.36 vs expected 0.39)

problem: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma has a dismal prognosis and insights into genetic diversity, disease etiology, and therapeutic targets are needed.
method: Whole-exome sequencing of 109 micro-dissected PDA cases with MutSigCV analysis, copy number profiling, and pathway analysis; validated with Sanger sequencing and an independent cohort.
finding: PDA harbors greater mutational burden than previously reported (~67 non-synonymous mutations/case); MYC amplification associates with poor outcome, RBM10 mutations with longer survival, KRAS Q61 alleles with improved survival, and oncogenic BRAF mutations define vemurafenib sensitivity.
dataset: 109 micro-dissected pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma cases with clinical annotation
metric: Mutation burden, MutSigCV q-value, overall survival, Sanger validation rate
baseline: Prior PDA sequencing studies (~26 mutations/case), GFP mock
result: 67 non-synonymous mutations/case (vs 26 prior), 92% Sanger validation, >90% KRAS mutated, BRAF mutations mutually exclusive with KRAS

problem: Parameter estimation in large-scale systems biology ODE models is computationally prohibitive due to non-convexity and high dimensionality, requiring days of computation time.
method: A self-adaptive cooperative enhanced scatter search (saCeSS) parallelized with MPI, featuring asynchronous cooperation between heterogeneous metaheuristic threads with self-tuning mechanisms.
finding: saCeSS consistently solved medium and large-scale parameter estimation benchmarks with computation time reductions from days to minutes compared to previous methods, even with small numbers of processors.
dataset: BioPreDyn-bench suite: S. cerevisiae (1759 params), E. coli (116-178 params), CHO cells (117 params), D. melanogaster (37 params), signal transduction (86 params)
metric: Computation time, convergence to best known objective value, speedup ratio
baseline: Non-cooperative parallel eSS (np-eSS), synchronous cooperative CeSS
result: Days to minutes computation time reduction, robust convergence across all 6 benchmarks with small processor counts

problem: Metachromatic leukodystrophy (MLD) is a rare neurodegenerative disease with no effective treatments, and developing brain organoid models for drug repurposing is expensive and time-consuming.
method: DeepNEU (v6.2) hybrid deep-machine learning platform simulating artificially induced whole-brain organoids (aiWBO) using a literature-validated RNN with 4,660 genes/proteins and 43,498 relationships, validated against peer-reviewed data.
finding: aiWBO simulations accurately predicted neural cell types, brain regions, cortical layers, and MLD disease pathology; identified combined metabolic alkalosis and respiratory acidosis in MLD, and enabled evaluation of potential therapeutic options.
dataset: Literature-derived gene/protein network (4,660 nodes, 43,498 relationships) for MLD simulation
metric: Binomial test p-values for prediction accuracy of cell types, brain regions, cortical layers
baseline: aiWBO wild-type simulation, peer-reviewed wet lab MLD disease profile
result: 9/9 neural cell types predicted (p=0.023), 9/9 ventral-dorsal regions (p=0.023), 6/6 cortical layers (p=0.081)

problem: The precise temporal and spatial dynamics of the microglial population in the adult brain, including turnover rate and maintenance mechanisms, are unknown.
method: BrdU pulse-chase studies, chronic two-photon in vivo imaging in CX3CR1-GFP mice, in utero intra-liver lentiviral tracing, and mouse models of dysregulated apoptosis (Bcl-2, Bim knockout) in mice and human postmortem tissue.
finding: Microglia turn over remarkably fast (~96 days for complete renewal in mouse, ~4.2 years in humans), with population homeostasis maintained by tight spatial and temporal coupling of proliferation and apoptosis rather than long-lived self-renewal.
dataset: CX3CR1-GFP/+ mice, human postmortem brain tissue (N=15/group, age 20-79)
metric: BrdU incorporation rate, Ki67+ proliferation index, activated caspase-3+ apoptosis rate, microglial density
baseline: Perinatal monocyte infiltration contribution (shown negligible in adults)
result: ~0.69% daily proliferation, ~96-day full population renewal in mice, ~4.2 years in humans, coupled proliferation-apoptosis rates

problem: Ion channel models used in biophysics (Markov models) and whole-cell neural modeling (Hodgkin-Huxley) are disconnected, preventing integration of detailed channel data into realistic neuron simulations.
method: Review and analysis of Markov model fitting approaches (inverse problem), parameter constraint analysis using different voltage-clamp protocols, and exploration of computational infrastructure needed to bridge biophysical and neural modeling.
finding: Complex (naturalistic or random) voltage-clamp waveforms provide parameter constraints typically 10x tighter than standard step protocols; new computational infrastructure is needed to integrate diverse channel data sources into whole-cell models.
dataset: Simulated delayed rectifier sodium channel (4-state Markov model)
metric: Parameter constraint strength (second derivative of likelihood function), model identifiability
result: >=10x tighter parameter constraints with complex waveforms vs standard step sequences

problem: How does partial inhibition of Cdk1 from early G2 phase affect mitotic progression, and what is the balance between Cdk1 kinase activity and PP2A phosphatase activity during mitosis?
method: Dose-titration of Cdk1 inhibitor RO3306 on thymidine-synchronized HeLa cells with semi-automated IncuCyte live-cell imaging, western blot for Cdk1 substrate phosphorylation, flow cytometry, and live-cell microscopy (H2B-mCherry/EB3-GFP).
finding: Mild Cdk1 inhibition from G2 decouples mitotic events—cells initiate nuclear envelope breakdown but fail to complete pro-metaphase, undergoing simultaneous cytokinesis and metaphase despite active SAC, producing viable polyploid cells. PP2A inhibition by Okadaic acid rescued the defects.
dataset: HeLa cells stably expressing H2B-mCherry and EB3-GFP
metric: mitotic index (%), polyploidy proportion, Cdk1 substrate phosphorylation (pSerCdk) levels
result: low-dose RO: 15-20% vs 10% control mitotic cells; 7.5-10µM arrested in G2; OA rescued mitotic defects

problem: What are the timing and evolutionary trajectories of landmark genomic events driving clear cell renal cell carcinoma, particularly chromosome 3p loss?
method: Whole-genome sequencing (average 67x depth) of 95 multi-regional biopsies from 33 ccRCC patients, with phylogenetic reconstruction, molecular clock analysis, and mathematical modeling of age-incidence curves for sporadic vs inherited cancers.
finding: Chromosome 3p loss—often via chromothripsis generating simultaneous 3p loss and 5q gain—is typically the initiating event, occurring in childhood or adolescence decades before diagnosis. The number of initiating cells capable of sporadic tumors is no more than a few hundred.
dataset: 95 biopsies across 33 ccRCC patients (TRACERx Renal prospective cohort)
metric: mutation burden, clonality, molecular timing of driver events, age-incidence modeling
baseline: matched germline DNA from blood samples
result: 3p loss in childhood/adolescence; 36% patients show t(3;5) chromothripsis; TERT 5'UTR hotspot mutations; avg 7,680 somatic substitutions per patient

problem: What is the pathophysiology underlying cardiomyopathy in Barth syndrome (BTHS), a mitochondrial disorder caused by TAZ mutation, and can it be modeled using iPSC and tissue engineering?
method: Patient-derived and CRISPR Cas9-engineered iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes cultured on muscular thin film 'heart-on-chip' constructs; metabolic profiling, ROS measurement, gene replacement via modRNA, and genome editing to establish causality.
finding: TAZ mutation causes sparse sarcomere assembly and weak tissue contraction via excess ROS, not global ATP depletion. Gene replacement and genome editing confirmed TAZ mutation is necessary and sufficient. ROS scavenging with MitoTEMPO rescued contractile function.
dataset: BTHS patient iPSC-CMs (2 patients), CRISPR-corrected isogenic controls, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes
metric: twitch stress, peak systolic stress, sarcomere organization, ROS levels, cardiolipin profiles
baseline: wild-type iPSC-CMs (WT1/WT2/WT3) and TAZ-corrected isogenic lines
result: BTHS heart-on-chip tissues contracted weakly; MitoTEMPO rescued contraction; normal whole-cell ATP despite impaired contractility

problem: How do megakaryocytes regulate hematopoietic stem cell pool size and quiescence in the bone marrow niche, and what is the molecular mediator?
method: 3D whole-mount imaging of bone marrow, selective in vivo megakaryocyte depletion (Cxcl4-cre;iDTR + diphtheria toxin), CXCL4 injection studies, Cxcl4 knockout mice, computational spatial modeling, and competitive transplantation assays.
finding: HSCs are nonrandomly located adjacent to megakaryocytes, which regulate HSC quiescence through CXCL4 secretion. MK depletion caused loss of HSC quiescence and marked expansion of functional HSCs; CXCL4 injection increased quiescence while Cxcl4−/− mice showed increased HSC proliferation.
dataset: C57BL/6 mice, Cxcl4-cre;iDTR mice, Cxcl4−/− mice, Nes-GFP mice
metric: HSC cell cycle status (Ki67/DAPI), HSC frequency, spatial Euclidean distance, competitive repopulation
baseline: wild-type/non-depleted controls, random spatial distribution models (Random 1 and Random 2)
result: MK depletion: specific loss of HSC quiescence + marked HSC expansion; CXCL4 injection: reduced HSC number via increased quiescence; Cxcl4−/−: increased HSC number and proliferation

problem: What is the mechanism underlying off-target cell death effects induced by morpholino oligonucleotides (MOs) and other knockdown technologies?
method: MO and gripNA injections in zebrafish embryos compared directly with well-characterized genetic mutants (wnt5/ppt, smo/smu); TUNEL and acridine orange assays for cell death; qRT-PCR, microarrays, and whole-mount in situ hybridization for transcriptional profiling.
finding: MOs and gripNAs induce off-target neural cell death via p53 activation pathway, accompanied by diagnostic transcription of a Δ113 truncated p53 isoform. Co-knockdown of p53 specifically suppresses off-target cell death without affecting on-target gene silencing efficiency.
dataset: zebrafish (Danio rerio) embryos, wnt5/ppt and smo/smu mutant lines
metric: neural cell death (TUNEL, acridine orange), p21 transcriptional activation, Δ113p53 isoform expression
baseline: genetic mutants (wnt5/ppt, smo/smu), uninjected embryos, alternative-sequence MOs
result: p53 co-knockdown ameliorated off-target neural death; Δ113p53 is diagnostic marker; effect is sequence-specific but target-independent

problem: Do gene expression changes observed in neurodegenerative disease whole-tissue profiling reflect true transcriptional regulation or merely altered cellular composition from neuronal loss and gliosis?
method: Acute FACS purification of neurons, astrocytes, and microglia from adult mouse brains followed by RNA-seq; applied to LPS endotoxemia model and PS2APP Alzheimer's model; compared cell-type-specific with whole-tissue transcriptomes.
finding: Whole-tissue expression changes primarily reflect altered cell type composition, not transcriptional regulation. Cell-type-specific sorting revealed hundreds of changes undetected in bulk tissue and showed the astrocytic inflammatory response requires Tnfr1.
dataset: PS2APP Alzheimer's model mice (7 and 13 months), C57BL/6 mice (LPS-treated), GRN-mutant FTD patient cortex
metric: differential expression (fold change ≥2, adjusted P ≤0.05), cell type composition mapping, Z-score normalized expression
baseline: PBS-injected controls, non-transgenic age-matched mice, non-diseased human controls
result: hundreds of cell-type-specific changes undetected in whole tissue; astrocytic LPS response abolished in Tnfr1−/−

problem: How well do single-nucleus RNA-seq (SNS) transcriptomes compare to whole-cell transcriptomes for accurately predicting cell-type-specific gene expression?
method: Single-nucleus RNA-seq from frozen mouse somatosensory cortex using Fluidigm C1 with modified SmartSeq protocol; compared with published whole-cell scRNA-seq datasets; developed normalization strategy to reduce method-specific length biases.
finding: High concordance between nuclear and whole-cell transcriptomes for cell type markers and metabolic modeling genes, but reduced concordance for mitochondrial respiration genes. SNS accurately resolves transcriptional subpopulations and cortical layer identity.
dataset: mouse somatosensory cortex (S1) nuclei (P21 CD1 mice), published S1 cortical and CA1 hippocampal whole-cell data
metric: Pearson correlation, gene detection count, cell-type marker concordance, ERCC spike-in consistency
baseline: published whole-cell single-cell RNA-seq (Zeisel et al., S1 cortex and CA1 hippocampus)
result: ERCC Pearson r=0.86 (nuclei); mean 5,619 genes (nuclei) vs 4,797 (S1 cells); high concordance for cell type/metabolic markers

problem: Can a unified genome-scale model integrating metabolism and gene expression accurately predict multi-scale bacterial growth phenotypes from molecular to whole-cell level?
method: Constructed an ME-Model for E. coli integrating metabolic reconstruction with gene expression network (1,541 ORFs, 109 RNA genes); growth optimization via sequential linear programming with growth-rate-dependent coupling constraints.
finding: The ME-Model computes ~80% of the functional proteome by mass and accurately predicts growth rate, nutrient uptake, by-product secretion, metabolic fluxes, and gene expression levels, unifying many existing principles of bacterial growth.
dataset: E. coli K-12
metric: gene essentiality accuracy, precision, recall, growth rate prediction, proteome mass fraction
baseline: M-Model (metabolic model alone: accuracy 91.2%, precision 81%, recall 68%)
result: gene essentiality accuracy 88.8% (precision 60.4%, recall 75%); 1,295 functional protein complexes; ~80% proteome coverage by mass

problem: How does systemic nutritional status (undernutrition and overnutrition) affect immune cell metabolism and function, particularly T cell responses?
method: Literature review synthesizing human clinical studies and mouse models examining effects of nutritional status on circulating cytokines, immune cell populations, and T cell metabolic pathways.
finding: Undernutrition causes immunosuppression via reduced leptin and mTOR signaling, while overnutrition promotes chronic low-grade inflammation through elevated leptin and pro-inflammatory cytokines. Leptin is a key mediator linking nutritional status to T cell metabolism and function.

problem: Can a killed whole-cell Chlamydia vaccine be developed that preserves surface epitope integrity during inactivation to elicit protective immunity?
method: Gamma-irradiation of Chlamydia muridarum elementary bodies with Mn2+-Decapeptide Phosphate (MDP) antioxidant protection; murine intravaginal challenge model; ELISA for antibody quantification; TEM for structural integrity; IFU assays for bacterial burden.
finding: MDP preserves EB structural integrity during gamma inactivation while allowing DNA destruction. Ir-Cm(+MDP) vaccinated mice showed elevated Cm-specific IgG and IgA, reduced bacterial burden, accelerated clearance, and distinctive cytokine responses compared to controls.
dataset: C57BL/6 mice, Chlamydia muridarum Nigg strain, C. trachomatis serovar L2
metric: IFU (bacterial burden), IgG/IgA antibody titers, clearance kinetics, cytokine levels
baseline: unvaccinated controls and EBs irradiated without MDP
result: ~1,000 Gy inactivates ~10^8 IFU; Ir-Cm(+MDP) showed significant protection with elevated IgG/IgA, reduced burden, and accelerated clearance vs controls

problem: Plants used for medical purposes, chemotherapeutic drug identification from natural products
method: Cell viability assays, combination index analysis
finding: Evaluated anti-cancer activity of plant-derived compounds
dataset: cell lines
metric: CI, IC50

problem: Genomic characterization of breast cancer cell lines and PDX models
method: Deep whole genome sequencing using Illumina X10
finding: Identified novel genomic alterations in commonly used models
dataset: cell lines
metric: coverage

problem: Computational modeling of biological systems across scales
method: Constraint-based metabolic network reconstruction
finding: Comprehensive ME-model integrating metabolism and macromolecular synthesis for E. coli
dataset: E. coli

problem: Single-cell epigenomics methods establishment for understanding heterogeneity
method: Low-input and single-cell whole genome bisulfite sequencing
finding: Enabled single-cell DNA methylation profiling
dataset: mouse

problem: Microfluidic devices for drug carrier development and screening
method: Microfluidic device fabrication review
finding: Developments in microfluidic devices for drug delivery and screening

problem: Accurate detection of subclonal mutations in heterogeneous tumors
method: MuSE somatic mutation caller using NGS data
finding: Two-step approach for subclonal mutation detection
dataset: tumor samples
metric: sensitivity

problem: Mechanistic models computational demands require ML surrogates
method: ML surrogate models for biological process emulation
finding: Growing evidence ML surrogates emulate mechanistic models effectively

problem: Identifying peptide toxin blocking stretch-activated ion channels
method: Electrophysiology and toxin isolation from spider venom
finding: GsMTx-4 toxin blocks cationic stretch-activated channels
dataset: rat astrocytes
metric: IC50

problem: Pharmacokinetics of voxelotor in sickle cell disease patients
method: Population pharmacokinetic modeling
finding: Characterized PK of voxelotor in SCD patients
dataset: N=246 patients
metric: CI

problem: ABA-induced stomatal closure mechanism in plants
method: Computational model with experimental validation
finding: Model predictions validated experimentally for guard cell signaling
dataset: Arabidopsis

problem: Predicting metabolic states from gene expression data is limited; existing methods do not utilize metabolic network models to directly predict changes in flux capacity.
method: E-Flux extends Flux Balance Analysis by modeling maximum flux constraints as a function of measured gene expression, applied to mycolic acid biosynthesis and genome-scale M. tuberculosis models.
finding: E-Flux correctly predicts 7 of 8 known fatty acid inhibitors and their specificity for fatty acid biosynthesis, and identifies additional potential modulators of mycolic acid production.
dataset: M. tuberculosis, Boshoff TB expression compendium (400+ microarrays, 75 drugs/conditions)
metric: Prediction accuracy of known inhibitor identification, specificity of inhibition prediction
result: 7/8 known fatty acid inhibitors correctly predicted (87.5%)

problem: Whether physiologically relevant caffeine concentrations affect intervertebral disc cell viability and extracellular matrix composition, contributing to IVD degeneration.
method: Whole organ culture model with 190 IVD explants from rat tails in injured/uninjured groups, treated with three caffeine concentrations (5, 10, 15 mg/L) in rotating wall vessel bioreactor for 21 days.
finding: Caffeine causes significant dose-dependent loss of IVD cell viability and ECM composition, effects magnified and expedited in injured discs compared to controls.
dataset: 190 IVD whole organ explants from 16 skeletally mature Sprague-Dawley rats
metric: Viable cell density (VCD), glycosaminoglycan concentration, PG-to-HP ratio, p-value
baseline: Day 0 control IVDs (no caffeine, no injury, n=22)
result: Low vs high caffeine VCD P=.037 (day 7), P≤.004 (day 21)

problem: Determining expression patterns of low-abundance transcripts in planarians remains challenging with established WISH and FISH protocols.
method: Formamide bleaching step, optimized blocking with horse serum, copper sulfate autofluorescence quenching, iterative tyramide signal amplification, and heat-induced antigen retrieval for regenerating tissues.
finding: Formamide bleaching dramatically enhances signal intensity and sensitivity for WISH and FISH; combined modifications enable detection of previously undetectable low-abundance transcripts in planarians.
dataset: Schmidtea mediterranea (planarian), CIW4 asexual clonal line
metric: Signal intensity, signal-to-noise ratio, development time
baseline: Established planarian WISH protocol with overnight methanol/peroxide bleach
result: Dramatically reduced development time, improved signal-to-noise ratio for all probes tested

problem: Non-invasive monitoring of T-cell location and function during PDAC tumor growth and checkpoint blockade treatment is needed to predict in vivo response.
method: TbiLuc dual-color bioluminescence reporter mouse with AkaLumine substrate for red-shifted T-cell activation imaging, applied to hot/cold KPC PDAC clones with anti-PD-1/anti-CTLA-4 treatment.
finding: TbiLuc mice distinguish T-cell infiltration between immunologically hot and cold tumors; checkpoint blockade increases bioluminescence in tumor-draining lymph nodes at day 7 post-treatment.
dataset: TbiLuc mice with murine KPC PDAC clones (2838c3 'hot', 6694c2 'cold')
metric: Bioluminescence signal intensity (photons), fold induction of active vs naive T-cells
result: AkaLumine 20-fold induction in active T-cells vs 5-fold for CycLuc1

problem: How do whole cell vs LPS components of Porphyromonas gingivalis differentially modulate TLR2 and TLR4 innate inflammatory responses in periodontal pathogenesis?
method: 28 Wistar rats randomized into 7 groups receiving intrasulcular injections of P. gingivalis LPS (3 doses) or whole cell (3 doses) or placebo; TLR2/TLR4 measured by ELISA at days 0, 4, and 11.
finding: Whole cell P. gingivalis exposure increases TLR2 but decreases TLR4, while LPS exposure increases TLR4 but decreases TLR2, indicating different molecular mechanisms of pathogen recognition.
dataset: 28 male Wistar rats (8-10 weeks, 120-150g), 7 treatment groups
metric: TLR2/TLR4 serum levels (ELISA), ANOVA, paired t-test, Shapiro-Wilk normality
baseline: Placebo intrasulcular group (Group A) and day 0 pre-injection baseline
result: Significant TLR2/TLR4 divergence at day 11 (P<0.05)

problem: Synthetic biology circuits are typically transferred to E. coli which may be suboptimal; identifying properties of alternative chassis organisms is needed for better circuit function.
method: Review of alternative microbial hosts analyzing desirable chassis properties: lifestyle, cell wall robustness, regulatory networks, whole-cell models, and tuneable metabolic fluxes.
finding: Pseudomonas putida is highlighted as a prominent alternative chassis due to metabolic diversity, robustness, and ease of manipulation; a modular chassis designed from first principles is envisioned.
baseline: E. coli as default synthetic biology chassis

problem: Existing deep learning models for object recognition are not directly applicable to microscopy images with populations of cells; whole-image classification and segmentation without single-cell annotations is needed.
method: CNN combined with multiple instance learning using a novel Noisy-AND pooling function, trained end-to-end on whole microscopy images with only image-level labels.
finding: Convolutional MIL models outperform previous methods on both mammalian breast cancer and yeast protein localization datasets without requiring any segmentation or single-cell labeling steps.
dataset: BBBC021v1 breast cancer screen (MCF-7, 12 MOA classes), yeast GFP localization (4144 strains, 17 classes), cluttered MNIST
metric: Mean average precision, treatment-level accuracy
baseline: Ensemble of 60 binary SVMs (yeast), factor analysis (breast cancer), CNN on cropped cells with bag-level labels
result: Outperforms all benchmarks on both datasets without pre/post-processing

problem: The relationship between HPV integration into host genomes and the resulting genomic structural variation in human cancers is poorly understood at high resolution.
method: Whole-genome sequencing (2×100bp paired-end, Illumina HiSeq 2000) of 10 cancer cell lines and 2 primary tumors, with breakpoint mapping and structural variation analysis at single-nucleotide resolution.
finding: HPV integrants flank and bridge extensive host genomic amplifications, deletions, inversions, and translocations; a 'looping' model explains HPV-mediated concatemer formation and focal genomic instability distinct from chromothripsis.
dataset: 10 cancer cell lines (2 cervical, 8 HNSCC) + 2 HPV+ primary HNSCC tumors
metric: Insertional breakpoint counts, viral copy number, CNV detection, PCR/Sanger verification rate
baseline: Previous SNP array-based studies of HPV integration sites
result: 111 unique insertional breakpoints in 9 HPV-positive cases; viral copy number 0-830 per cell

problem: Whether grid cell firing fields are generated by theta amplitude modulations (oscillatory interference models) or slow membrane potential ramps (attractor network models) is unresolved.
method: In vivo whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from MEC grid cells in head-fixed mice running on virtual reality linear tracks, with biocytin fills for morphological identification.
finding: Grid cells show large, reproducible membrane potential ramps tightly correlated with firing fields; theta oscillations influence spike timing but their amplitude modulations do not determine field locations, supporting attractor network models.
dataset: MEC grid cells in C57BL/6J mice, virtual 1D linear tracks
metric: Membrane potential depolarization amplitude, spatial information, correlation with firing rate, reliability
baseline: Oscillatory interference model predictions (theta amplitude modulation)
result: Ramps are larger, more reliable, and more informative about position than theta modulations

problem: Can a functional in-silico digital twin of cancer cell electrophysiology predict effects of ion channel modulation on cell cycle progression and serve as a tool for drug development?
method: Non-phenomenological digital twin of A549 lung adenocarcinoma cell electrophysiology, simulating membrane potential oscillations and effects of targeted ion channel activation/inactivation on cell cycle phases.
finding: The A549 digital twin predicts that TRPC6 blockade causes strong hyperpolarization suppressing S-G2/M transition, enabling possible cell cycle arrest; all simulated hypotheses were verified by experimental studies.
dataset: A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cell line
metric: Membrane potential (mV), cell cycle phase transition prediction
baseline: Experimental in-vivo and in-vitro verification studies
result: All simulated research hypotheses verified by experimental studies

problem: Medical digital twins for drug discovery in seasonal allergic rhinitis
method: scRNA-seq time-series multinomial network models

problem: Rheumatoid arthritis has unclear etiology due to complex dysregulated interactions between synoviocytes and immune cells, making it difficult to identify therapeutic targets.
method: Built a large-scale modular biochemical reaction map (2232 components, 1461 reactions) of intra- and intercellular interactions, converted to an executable Boolean model using CaSQ and BMA tools, validated with gene expression data.
finding: Created one of the largest executable Boolean models for biological systems; identified 118 intercellular interactions connecting four cell types; model reproduced known biological mechanisms and was used to explore treatments and identify new therapeutic targets.
dataset: RA synovial joint (M1/M2 macrophages, fibroblasts, CD4+ Th1 cells), transcriptomic data
metric: Similarity scores between model steady states and experimentally observed Boolean values
baseline: RA-Atlas cell-specific maps, existing knowledge of RA mechanisms
result: Fibroblast model: 275 nodes/73 inputs/446 interactions; Th1 model: 120 nodes/28 inputs/155 interactions; 118 intercellular interactions identified

problem: There is no clear way to understand how individual genetic variability affects susceptibility to drug-induced cardiac arrhythmias, requiring personalized safety pharmacology approaches.
method: Atomistic-scale molecular dynamics simulations predicted drug-hERG channel binding/unbinding rates, integrated into Kernik iPSC-CM computational model; developed multilayer 1D ring tissue model (1,019 cells) for arrhythmia prediction.
finding: The computational pipeline predicted different arrhythmia risks for dofetilide vs moxifloxacin, validated against experimental iPSC-CM data; some LQT1 digital twins displayed early afterdepolarizations matching experimental observations.
dataset: iCell iPSC-CMs (cell line 01434), LQT1 iPSC-CMs, 205 digital twin population
metric: APD90cF (Fridericia-corrected action potential duration at 90% repolarization), ΔAPD90
result: 205 digital twins generated; CL 500-2000ms; predictions matched experimental data for dofetilide (0.5-8 nM) and moxifloxacin (10-200 μM)

problem: It is unclear whether cellular effects of pulsed electrical stimulation on osteoblasts are caused by the electric field itself or by electrochemical by-products generated at the electrodes.
method: Applied 20 Hz biphasic pulses (6 mA, 10 ms) via platinum electrodes to MG-63 cells; compared direct stimulation vs AC-conditioned medium; used cyclic voltammetry, EIS, and digital twin modeling of the stimulation chamber.
finding: Hydrogen peroxide (electrochemical by-product) is not the main contributor to cellular effects; decreased adhesion is mediated through electrochemical products, but enhanced intracellular calcium and ROS require direct electric field stimulation.
dataset: MG-63 osteoblast-like cells
metric: Cell adhesion, spreading area, intracellular calcium, reactive oxygen species levels, H2O2 concentration
baseline: Control wells with electrodes but no applied current
result: 14.6 V peak-to-peak over 3 wells (4.87 V/well); electrolysis confirmed; direct stimulation enhanced Ca2+ and ROS while conditioned medium did not

problem: Identifying genetic variants associated with obesity-related quantitative traits (BMI, hip circumference, weight) to understand the genetic predisposition underlying the obesity epidemic.
method: Genome-wide association scan using Affymetrix 10K and 500K chips in a Sardinian cohort (N=6,148) with replication in the GenNet multi-ethnic study (N=3,467); variance component model with genomic control correction.
finding: Common genetic variants in the FTO gene are strongly associated with BMI, hip circumference, and weight; replicated in European Americans and Hispanic Americans; FTO had not been previously implicated in obesity.
dataset: Sardinian population (N=6,148), GenNet study (European Americans N=1,496, Hispanic Americans N=839, African Americans N=1,101)
metric: p-value, FDR, BMI units difference between genotype groups
baseline: Homozygotes for common allele at each SNP
result: rs9930506: BMI p=8.6×10⁻⁷, hip circumference p=3.4×10⁻⁸, weight p=9.1×10⁻⁷; 1.3 BMI units difference; replication meta-analysis p=0.001

problem: Porous nanofiber electrodes for CO2 electrolysis in solid oxide electrolysis cells suffer from insufficient interfacial bonding with the dense solid electrolyte, limiting reaction sites and performance.
method: Developed hybrid nanofiber electrode (H-LSCFP) incorporating crushed nanofibers into a nanofiber network via electrospinning; in situ exsolution of Co metal nanocatalysts after H2/CO2 treatment at 700°C; digital twinning with 3D microstructure reconstruction.
finding: The hybrid structure improved contact adhesion at the porous-solid interface and increased CO2 reduction reaction sites; achieved record current density among nanofiber-based SOEC electrodes.
dataset: La0.6Sr0.4Co0.15Fe0.8Pd0.05O3−δ (LSCFP) nanofibers on LSGM electrolyte
metric: Current density (A cm⁻²), Rietveld refinement goodness of fit (χ²)
result: 2.2 A cm⁻² at 800°C and 1.5 V in CO2 (new benchmark); χ²=0.94; exsolved Co nanoparticles ~15 nm

problem: It is unclear which normalization and differential abundance testing methods are appropriate for microbiome count data given varying characteristics such as uneven library sizes, sparsity, and compositionality.
method: Simulation studies evaluating seven differential abundance testing methods (rarefied and raw data) and multiple normalization methods for clustering/ordination; tested across varying sample sizes, library size ratios, and effect sizes.
finding: ANCOM is the only method with both good FDR control and high sensitivity (>20 samples/group); DESeq2 increases sensitivity for small datasets but has elevated FDR with uneven library sizes; rarefying lowers FDR when groups have ~10× library size differences.
dataset: Global Patterns dataset (ocean/feces microbiomes), simulated OTU count matrices
metric: False discovery rate (FDR), sensitivity, PERMANOVA R²
baseline: Rarefied data with nonparametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis, Mann-Whitney)
result: ANCOM: best FDR control across conditions; DESeq2: higher FDR with >20 samples/group and ~10× library size differences; rarefying: reduced FDR for uneven library sizes

problem: Predicting patient health trajectories from electronic health records is challenging due to data missingness, noise, and limited sample sizes, yet is critical for digital twin applications in clinical settings.
method: DT-GPT: fine-tuned LLM (BioMistral-7B) on text-encoded EHR data with channel-dependent input modeling; evaluated on short-term (24h ICU), medium-term (13-week NSCLC), and long-term (24-month Alzheimer's) forecasting tasks.
finding: DT-GPT outperformed state-of-the-art time series and LLM forecasting models across all three datasets; maintained inter-variable distributions and cross-correlations; demonstrated zero-shot forecasting capability.
dataset: NSCLC (Flatiron Health, N=16,496), MIMIC-IV ICU (N=35,131), ADNI Alzheimer's (N=1,140)
metric: Scaled mean absolute error (MAE)
result: Scaled MAE reduction: 3.4% (NSCLC), 1.3% (ICU), 1.8% (Alzheimer's) vs best baseline

problem: The broader rules governing how synaptic connectivity relates to functional properties of neurons across cortical layers and visual areas remain unknown beyond initial like-to-like findings in V1 layer 2/3.
method: Analyzed the millimetre-scale MICrONS EM dataset combined with two-photon calcium imaging; used a validated digital twin model to decompose neuronal tuning into feature and spatial components; trained recurrent neural networks on classification tasks for comparison.
finding: Like-to-like connectivity is universal across layers (L2-L5) and visual areas including feedback connections; only the feature component (not spatial/receptive field) predicts fine-scale connections beyond axon-dendrite proximity; discovered higher-order rule where postsynaptic cohorts show greater functional similarity than pairwise predictions.
dataset: MICrONS dataset (single mouse, V1 + higher visual areas AL/LM/RL, EM + calcium imaging)
metric: Signal correlations, axon-dendrite proximity (ADP) null distributions
baseline: Pairwise like-to-like connectivity rule, ADP-based null model for expected connectivity
result: Like-to-like connectivity across all layers and areas; disrupting like-to-like connections in RNNs impairs performance more than disrupting random connections

problem: Connecting cell-level gene expression patterns to organ-level physiological function through computational modeling requires integrating transcriptomic data with dynamical pathway simulations.
method: Developed a computational pipeline integrating gene expression timelines with systems of coupled differential equations to generate cell-type selective dynamical models; tested on macrophage eicosanoid biosynthesis network.
finding: Converting transcriptomic changes to a dynamical model predicted prostaglandin and thromboxane synthesis/secretion dynamics that matched published lipidomics data from the same experiments.
dataset: Macrophage eicosanoid biosynthesis network, published lipidomics data
metric: Agreement with published lipidomics measurements
baseline: Published lipidomics data from same experiments
result: Predicted prostaglandin and thromboxane dynamics matched published lipidomics data

problem: Reliable prediction of cell culture seed train behavior across consecutive bioreactor scales is challenging due to low sampling frequencies (every 24h), batch-to-batch variability, and large measurement errors.
method: Moving horizon estimation (MHE) with arrival cost and parameter penalty terms for dynamic model parameter updating; compared to weighted least squares estimation (WLSE) across three consecutive cultivation scales (40–2160 L).
finding: MHE outperformed WLSE by integrating prior knowledge, providing reliable predictions even with only 2 sampling points; an iterative learning workflow was developed for dynamic parameter estimation across seed trains.
dataset: Industrial CHO cell culture seed train (3 consecutive bioreactor scales: 40–2160 L)
metric: Relative prediction error (%) for viable cell density
result: MHE reliable with only 2 data points; outperformed WLSE across all estimation horizon scenarios

problem: Nanofibril alignment during assembly controls material properties
method: X-ray scattering-based digital twin for flow focusing
finding: Studied nanofibril orientation during assembly
dataset: cellulose nanofibrils
metric: accuracy

problem: Digital twin for continuous VLP production
method: Fed-batch and perfusion mode HEK293 cultivation
finding: Evaluated cell retention systems for continuous production
dataset: HEK293 cells
metric: productivity

problem: Digital twin for precision cardiology
method: Computational cardiac modeling for personalized therapy
finding: Framework for personalized cardiac treatment
dataset: patient cohort
metric: precision

problem: Germline chimerism in monochorionic dizygotic twins
method: Genetic analysis of twin chimerism
finding: Identified rare MCDZ twin occurrence
dataset: twin patients

problem: Integrated digital and biomimetic twins for precision medicine
method: Review of digital twin approaches for MASLD
finding: New paradigm for drug development needed

problem: Predicting benefit of treatment beyond progression in NSCLC
method: Virtual clinical trials with lesion-level response dynamics
finding: Virtual trials recapitulate heterogeneous clinical responses
dataset: NSCLC patients

problem: Ethical implications of digital twins in healthcare
method: Ethical analysis of data-driven health paradigm
finding: Explored ethical considerations of health digital twins

problem: Somatic UBA2 variant in concordant ALL of monozygotic twins
method: Genetic analysis of leukemic clones in twins
finding: Identified pre-leukemic somatic variant in twin ALL
dataset: twin patients

problem: Digital twin intervention outcomes for type 2 diabetes
method: Retrospective observational study of DT program
finding: 1985 participants enrolled, demonstrated DT efficacy for T2D
dataset: N=1985 T2D patients
metric: HbA1c

problem: AML consolidation therapy timing optimization
method: Simulation-based comparison of cytarabine dosing schedules
finding: AC-123 reduces WBC recovery time in 99.6% of simulated cycles
dataset: simulated cycles
metric: p-value

problem: Current prostate cancer diagnostics lack personalized predictive models; need a framework to simulate patient-specific tumour growth using clinical MRI data toward digital twins.
method: Finite Element Method coupling tumour growth dynamics and oxygen transport, initialized from patient-specific mpMRI biomarkers (ADC cellularity maps, K_Trans vasculature maps), with multi-objective optimization for parameter fitting across patients.
finding: Framework validated on 4 patients with multiple MRI follow-ups; model predicts prostate and tumour volume growth along with serum PSA levels, representing a preliminary step toward clinical digital twins for prostate cancer.
dataset: 6 prostate cancer patients under active surveillance (Gleason Score 6), Hospital La Fe Valencia, 2015-2020
metric: Prostate volume, tumour volume, serum PSA levels

problem: Personalized medicine requires integrating and processing vast amounts of patient data to find optimal drug treatments.
method: Proposes constructing high-resolution digital twin models of individual patients, computationally treated with thousands of drugs to identify optimal therapy.
finding: Clinical implementation of digital twins will require solving technical, medical, ethical, and theoretical challenges comparable in scale to the Human Genome Project, but could lead to greatly improved healthcare.

problem: PCOS is a common heterogeneous endocrine disorder requiring proper diagnosis and management to prevent metabolic, cardiovascular, and psychiatric complications.
method: Comprehensive review of epidemiology, diagnostic criteria, pathophysiology, comorbidities, and management strategies for PCOS.
finding: PCOS prevalence is up to 15-20% with ESHRE/ASRM criteria; insulin resistance affects 50-70% of PCOS women; treatment includes oral contraceptives, anti-androgens, and ovulation induction agents including letrozole and metformin.
result: prevalence 15-20%, insulin resistance 50-70%

problem: No single RNA-seq analysis pipeline applies to all cases; need a comprehensive review of best practices across all major analysis steps.
method: Survey and review of RNA-seq analysis steps including experimental design, QC, read alignment, quantification, visualization, differential expression, alternative splicing, functional analysis, gene fusion detection, and eQTL mapping.
