finding: Provides guidelines for each step of RNA-seq analysis with discussion of challenges; highlights that choices of library type, sequencing depth, and replicates must be tailored to the biological question and system under study.

problem: Health risks of sedentary behaviors are not well known despite widespread insufficient physical activity affecting one-third of the global population aged 15+.
method: Review of updated evidence on health risks and underlying biological mechanisms of sedentary lifestyle, including effects on lipid metabolism, insulin sensitivity, and vascular function.
finding: Sedentary behavior increases all-cause mortality, CVD mortality, cancer risk, metabolic disorders, musculoskeletal disorders, depression, and cognitive impairment; 150-300 min/week of moderate physical activity can offset adverse effects.
result: mean sedentary time 8.3h (Korea), 7.7h (US adults)

problem: Machine learning alone ignores fundamental physics laws leading to ill-posed problems, while multiscale modeling alone fails to efficiently combine large heterogeneous datasets.
method: Review of integrating machine learning with multiscale modeling across four topical areas: ODEs, PDEs, data-driven approaches, and theory-driven approaches, leveraging multidisciplinary expertise.
finding: ML and multiscale modeling naturally complement each other to create robust predictive models; integration can provide new insights into disease mechanisms, identify treatment targets, and inform clinical decision making.

problem: Digital twins for health (DT4H) hold great promise but are still in early stages; need to survey current applications, limitations, and emerging opportunities.
method: Scoping review of current DT applications in healthcare, consortium research centers, and emerging R&D opportunities.
finding: DT4H has potential to revolutionize personalized healthcare, predictive interventions, and remote monitoring, but faces challenges in technological innovation, ethics, societal impact, and legal guidance requiring global collaboration.

problem: Disease rarity, ethical issues regarding placebo arms, time, and cost impede clinical trial conduct for chronic graft-versus-host disease (cGvHD); need virtual control arms.
method: Constructed a digital twin cohort using Trial Accelerator™ platform from a database of 61M patients (232,909 cohorts); AI algorithm identified patient attributes matching protocol criteria; derived SOC arm for prednisone efficacy assessment.
finding: Feasible to construct a DT cohort from existing clinical trial data; prednisone ORR at 6 months (52.7%) agrees with literature, demonstrating that a DT SOC arm can potentially replace control arms in clinical trials.
dataset: cGvHD patients: 2,042 patients in 32 cohorts (flGvHD DT); 438 patients in 8 cohorts (SOC arm)
metric: Overall response rate (ORR) at 6 months (CR+PR)
result: ORR 52.7% at 6 months, disease onset 7.58 months post-HCT

problem: Cell identity is governed by complex gene-regulatory networks, but it remains challenging to interpret how transcription factor perturbations alter cell identity from unperturbed data alone.
method: CellOracle: ML-based GRN inference from single-cell multi-omics (scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq) with in silico TF perturbation simulation using custom network models and vector field visualization.
finding: Correctly models reported TF perturbation phenotypes in mouse/human hematopoiesis and zebrafish embryogenesis; predicted and experimentally validated a novel noto loss-of-function phenotype and identified lhx1a as an axial mesoderm regulator.
dataset: Mouse/human hematopoiesis, zebrafish embryogenesis (38,606-cell WT reference atlas at 6/8/10 hpf)
metric: AUROC, early precision ratio (EPR)
result: CellOracle with scATAC-seq base GRN outperformed all comparators in AUROC and EPR; robust down to 50 cells

problem: Accurate identification of B-cell epitopes remains a major challenge for developing epitope-based vaccines, therapeutic antibodies, and diagnostic tools.
method: Comprehensive review of experimental B-cell epitope mapping methods (X-ray crystallography, NMR, peptide arrays, phage display), epitope databases, and in silico prediction tools based on sequence and structural data.
finding: Advances in mapping and computational prediction yield molecular insights into antigen-antibody complex formation; however, real epitopes cannot be predicted by ignoring structural effects upon Ag-Ab complex formation, opening space for algorithm improvement.

problem: Predicting and designing effective cell penetrating peptides
method: SVM-based models using amino acid composition features
finding: High accuracy prediction of cell penetrating peptides
dataset: 708 peptides
metric: accuracy

problem: Drug-induced Torsade-de-Pointes prediction
method: Optimized IKr-dynamic ORd cardiac model
finding: Improved cardiac safety pharmacology predictions
metric: ROC

problem: Low observation numbers in biomedical research
method: scGAN for generating realistic scRNA-seq data
finding: GANs produce realistic single-cell RNA-seq data
dataset: 68,579 PBMCs
metric: correlation

problem: Modeling metabolic interactions between multiple cell types
method: Multi-cell type constraint-based metabolic reconstruction
finding: Omics-driven mechanistic models of brain metabolism
dataset: brain cell types
metric: accuracy

problem: Deconvolution of complex tissue cell type composition
method: Gene signature compendium from FANTOM5 and other sources
finding: Cell type gene signatures for tissue deconvolution
dataset: 39 cell types

problem: In silico phenotypic screening of anticancer agents
method: PASS activity spectrum prediction from chemical structure
finding: SAR models for cell line cytotoxicity prediction
dataset: 59,882 compounds
metric: accuracy

problem: Cell type identification from in vivo electrophysiology
method: EAP waveform analysis with biophysical models
finding: Cell type classification from extracellular recordings
dataset: 24 neurons
metric: specificity

problem: Modeling nanoscopic astroglial processes and calcium signaling
method: Stochastic generation of realistic astrocyte morphology
finding: Stem tree reconstruction of live astroglia
dataset: 13 cells
metric: correlation

problem: Predicting cell-type-specific chromatin organization
method: C.Origami multimodal deep learning architecture
finding: Accurate prediction of 3D chromatin organization
dataset: 90 cell types
metric: ROC

problem: Scaling hPSC expansion in stirred tank bioreactors
method: Instrumented bioreactor with in silico process modeling
finding: pH control improves hPSC yield 2-fold
dataset: hPSC cultures
metric: F1

problem: Sample heterogeneity in microarray data from mixed cell populations confounds identification of differentially expressed genes and gene expression-based classification.
method: Linear model-based computational framework using optimization to invert sample heterogeneity, estimating pure cell expression values and mixing percentages, with PCA for global performance evaluation.
finding: The method successfully reconstructs both sample and cell type-specific expression values from heterogeneous mixtures and estimates mixing percentages; a general-purpose model selection method determines the correct number of cell types.
dataset: cDNA microarray of 5 heterogeneous mixtures of lymph node and RKO colon cancer cells, 4704 human genes
metric: PCA-based global expression profile comparison
result: Successfully reconstructed pure cell profiles from 5 mixture ratios with known mixing percentages

problem: Understanding how DMSO enhances cell membrane permeability in cholesterol-containing membranes and whether MD-predicted pore formation regimes translate to living cells.
method: Molecular dynamics simulations of DOPC/20% cholesterol bilayers with varying DMSO concentrations, validated by DC-3F cell experiments measuring permeability to water, Ca2+ and Yo-Pro-1.
finding: Three DMSO concentration-dependent regimes (membrane loosening, stable pore formation, bilayer collapse) are reproduced in cholesterol-containing membranes and confirmed in living cells with size-dependent molecular permeability.
dataset: DOPC/20% cholesterol bilayer MD simulations, DC-3F cell cultures
metric: Membrane structural properties (area per lipid, order parameters), cell permeability to water/Ca2+/Yo-Pro-1
result: Three regimes confirmed: ≤10 mol% transient pores, ~15 mol% stable pores, ≥40 mol% bilayer collapse

problem: Understanding how metabolic allometric scaling emerges in cell aggregates when accounting for intrinsic fluctuations in mass and metabolic rate, which are typically neglected.
method: In silico pipeline using finite element simulations of reaction-diffusion (Fick's law + Michaelis-Menten kinetics) in randomly generated cell-laden spheroids with log-normal size distributions, combined with data collapse analysis.
finding: Scaling exponents change with increasing variability in both mass and metabolic rate; the pipeline identifies physiologically relevant size windows where cell cultures exhibit quantifiable similitudes with living organisms.
dataset: Computationally generated stem cell and hepatocyte spheroids, 17 size distributions (radii 31-5000 μm)
metric: Scaling exponent δ, data collapse functional minima, Anderson-Darling test (P > 0.05)
result: Minimum N=200 for marginal collapse, N=10^4 for joint collapse; nonisometric scaling (δ ≠ 1) in specific size windows

problem: The T cell epitope landscape of SARS-CoV-2 is largely unknown, and experimental identification is challenging, necessitating in silico prediction methods to aid vaccine design.
method: Review comparing in silico methods including SARS-CoV cross-reactivity screening, machine learning-based peptide-HLA binding prediction, immunogenicity prediction, and population coverage analysis.
finding: Multiple in silico approaches show varying performance in identifying experimentally verified immunogenic epitopes; methods exploiting SARS-CoV cross-reactivity and machine learning-based HLA binding prediction are most commonly used.
dataset: SARS-CoV-2 proteome, SARS-CoV epitope databases (ViPR, IEDB), GISAID sequences (>65,000 genomes)
metric: Ability to identify experimentally verified immunogenic epitopes from convalescent patients

problem: Nuclear envelope ruptures in laminopathy cells are stochastic, complicating quantitative analysis of their causes and regulators.
method: In silico synchronization of spontaneous nuclear rupture events using co-expressed H2B-GFP (stable reference) and mCherry-NLS (shuttling reporter) with automated tracking in LMNA knockout cells.
finding: Rupture frequency correlates inversely with lamin A/C levels; blebbistatin and NAT10 inhibitor remodelin reduce rupture frequency; ESCRT-III component CHMP4B knockdown prolongs nuclear signal recovery time.
dataset: HT-1080 LMNA knockout (HT-LKO) and MEF Lmna knockout (MEF-LKO) cells
metric: Rupture frequency, recovery halftime (Tr), nuclear circularity fluctuations
result: ~55% rupture frequency in MEF-LKO; blebbistatin reduced ruptures (p<0.05); remodelin improved circularity (p<0.001) and reduced ruptures (p<0.05); CHMP4B knockdown increased Tr (p<0.05)

problem: AI image translation for cell labeling lacks trustworthiness for mission-critical biomedical applications; prediction uncertainty and out-of-distribution detection are needed.
method: Ensemble of N=6 independently trained U-Net models for phase-contrast to fluorescence image translation, with pixel-wise standard deviation as uncertainty metric and FastEnsemble for computational efficiency.
finding: Ensemble prediction standard deviation correlates with prediction error and Pearson correlation coefficient; the method reliably detects out-of-distribution input images by reporting increased uncertainty.
dataset: Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs), LNCaP prostate cancer cells, mouse kidney tissue sections
metric: Prediction standard deviation, absolute error, Pearson correlation coefficient, sensitivity/specificity
result: Ensemble Std correlates with prediction error; FastEnsemble achieves comparable sensitivity/specificity to naive ensemble with reduced cost

problem: Identifying adult stem cell lineages in planarians without requiring transgenic lineage purification, and discovering novel neural stem cell populations.
method: Single-cell RNA sequencing (SmartSeq2) of 168 FACS-sorted cells from planarian heads, with hierarchical clustering, t-SNE, pseudotime analysis (Waterfall), and gene set enrichment analysis.
finding: Identified a molecularly distinct neural stem cell population (νNeoblasts) and a novel piwi-2+/piwi-1lo stem cell population; predicted neural lineage trajectory validated by in situ hybridization.
dataset: 168 single cells (96 X1 stem, 72 X2 progeny) from Schmidtea mediterranea planarian heads
metric: Hierarchical clustering, t-SNE, pseudotime ordering, gene set enrichment
result: 10 cell subgroups identified; 5150 transcripts/X1 cell; 64% average alignment rate; νNeoblast population validated in vivo

problem: Understanding doxorubicin's interaction with cholesterol-rich vs cholesterol-poor membrane domains to explain drug resistance mechanisms potentially involving P-gp efflux proteins.
method: UV-Vis derivative spectrophotometry (partition coefficients), fluorescence quenching (drug localization), steady-state anisotropy (membrane fluidity), and coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations of model liposomes.
finding: Cholesterol does not impair doxorubicin membrane partitioning but reduces its influence on membrane fluidity without direct drug-cholesterol interaction; resistance may stem from increased membrane density near efflux proteins.
dataset: DMPC:SM [8:2] and DMPC:SM:Chol [7:1.5:1.5] liposomes
metric: Partition coefficient (Kp, logD), Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV), fluorescence anisotropy
result: Kp=8195±406 (DMPC:SM), Kp=8707±523 (DMPC:SM:Chol), logD ~3.9; p<0.05

problem: Efficient simultaneous genome-scale identification of metabolic engineering targets for gene amplification and deletion is lacking; existing methods focus mainly on deletion targets.
method: Flux Design: elementary flux mode computation with flux correlation analysis to objective flux, identifying amplification (positive correlation) and deletion (negative correlation) targets with statistical ranking.
finding: For C. glutamicum lysine production, predicted targets matched known successful engineering strategies; for A. niger enzyme production, target relevance depended on metabolic state and carbon source.
dataset: Genome-scale metabolic models of Corynebacterium glutamicum and Aspergillus niger
metric: Target potential coefficient (α), Pearson correlation, p-value
baseline: Known validated metabolic engineering targets from literature
result: >20,000 elementary modes computed per organism; predicted targets match validated strategies (G6PDH amplification, pyruvate dehydrogenase deletion)

problem: Identifying novel human genes related to cilia by computational analysis of gene coexpression in tissues rich in ciliated cells, without requiring direct experimental isolation of ciliated cells.
method: Weighted Gene Coexpression Network Analysis (WGCNA) applied to 12 microarray datasets from brain, airways, and fallopian tubes, with consensus analysis across tissues and validation via GEO and Human Protein Atlas.
finding: Identified 297 known and 74 novel cilia-related genes; discovered MATE2 (SLC47A2) as brain-specific ciliary gene (confirmed by immunofluorescence) and dyslexia genes (DCDC2, DYX1C1, KIAA0319) as potentially cilia-related.
dataset: 12 microarray datasets from human brain (5), airways (5), fallopian tubes (2); ≥15,000 genes, ≥15 samples each
metric: Topological Overlap, module consensus gene composition, tissue-specificity expression validation
baseline: Known ciliary genes from CilDB database
result: Cilia module detected in 10/12 datasets; 297 known + 74 novel cilia genes; 82% of novel candidates validated

problem: Stem cell potency assessment lacks standardized methods; functional assays are cumbersome and molecular markers show discrepancies across cell types.
method: Review of functional assays (teratoma, blastocyst chimerism, tetraploid complementation) and in silico diagnostics including genomic profiling (DNA microarrays), bioinformatics algorithms (Stem Cell Matrix, PluriTest, ScoreCard), and epigenetic/metabolic marker evaluation.
finding: Pluripotency is a dynamic state regulated by many molecular factors; bioinformatics approaches (PluriTest, ScoreCard) show promise but functional assays remain the gold standard; no single molecular signature reliably predicts pluripotency across all cell types.
dataset: Human ESCs, iPSCs, multipotent and unipotent stem cells
baseline: Functional assays (teratoma formation, blastocyst chimerism, tetraploid complementation)

problem: Accurate modeling of blood flow requires cell-level simulation of red blood cells, but existing constitutive models for RBC membranes perform poorly at high shear rates.
method: HemoCell parallel computing framework using lattice Boltzmann method for plasma flow coupled with immersed boundary method for RBC membranes; a new constitutive material model based on strain energy formulation replacing WLC-based spectrin-link model.
finding: The new material model accurately reproduces apparent viscosity (Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect) across vessel diameters 20–128 μm and captures solid-like behavior of dense RBC suspensions at low shear rates; it outperforms the spectrin-link model at shear rates over 1,500 s⁻¹.
dataset: Simulated blood flow in straight vessels D=20–128 μm, hematocrit 20–45%, ~2×10⁴ cells
metric: Relative apparent viscosity vs vessel diameter
baseline: Coarse-grained spectrin-link model (Fedosov et al. 2010); experimental data from Pries et al. 1992
result: Good agreement with Pries et al. experimental viscosity curves; improved accuracy at shear rates >1,500 s⁻¹; scalable to 10⁶ cells on 8,192 cores

problem: The metabolic changes underlying clonal variation in monoclonal antibody-producing CHO cells are poorly understood, and the link between metabolic burden and recombinant protein productivity remains unclear.
method: Kinetic metabolic model for CHO cells with regulatory terms (hexokinase inhibition by G6P, PGI inhibition by PEP) calibrated against intracellular/extracellular metabolite profiles from shake flask batch cultures; simulation of intracellular fluxes and biomarker ratios.
finding: Clone-to-clone metabolic variation is more significant than the effect of recombinant protein expression; the high-producer clone shows efficient glucose utilization, increased pyruvate dehydrogenase flux, and higher TCA cycle flux with increased anaplerosis via pyruvate carboxylase.
dataset: CHO-Cum2 parental, high-producer, and low-producer clones (cumate-inducible anti-CD20 mAb), shake flask batch cultures over 6 days
metric: Intracellular flux distributions, ATP/ADP ratio, NADH/NAD ratio, extracellular metabolite concentrations
baseline: Parental (non-producing) CHO cell line; model without regulatory terms
result: mAb production accounts for at most 5% of total carbon uptake; regulatory terms (HK inhibition by G6P + PGI inhibition by PEP) significantly reduced simulation error for glucose and nucleotide ratios

problem: Linear B-cell epitope prediction methods show poor and inconsistent performance, and many existing tools are unavailable online, hindering vaccine design efforts particularly during COVID-19.
method: Benchmarking of 7 CLI-available B-cell epitope predictors (BcePred, BepiPred, ABCpred, COBEpro, SVMTriP, LBtope, LBEEP) on standardized datasets; development of consensus classifiers combining per-residue and per-peptide predictions.
finding: Individual predictors show modest performance (MCC 0.01–0.08); the consensus classifier achieved the best per-residue MCC and accuracy; BepiPred had the best per-peptide MCC; ~45% of published predictor web servers are no longer available.
dataset: Consensus_NR dataset (tens of thousands of epitope/non-epitope peptide sequences, 20-residue window)
metric: MCC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity
baseline: Individual predictors (BcePred, BepiPred, ABCpred, COBEpro, SVMTriP, LBtope, LBEEP)
result: Per-peptide best MCC=0.0778 (BepiPred); per-residue consensus MCC=0.0489; consensus accuracy=55.59% (per-peptide), 53.04% (per-residue)

problem: Bulk-tissue DNA methylomes average over many cell types, obscuring cell-type-specific contributions to disease; existing computational deconvolution methods are limited to blood.
method: EpiSCORE algorithm imputing cell-type-specific DNA methylation reference matrices from tissue-specific scRNA-seq atlases using promoter DNAm–mRNA anticorrelation; weighted robust partial correlation (wRPC) for cell-type fraction estimation in bulk methylomes.
finding: The DNAm-atlas covers 13 solid tissue types and 40 cell types with >90% validation accuracy in 8/13 tissues; it correctly predicts cancer cell of origin, discovers prognostic associations in olfactory neuroblastoma and stage 2 melanoma, and identifies neuronal origin for schizophrenia-associated differential methylation enriched for GWAS risk loci.
dataset: 13 human solid tissue types, 40 cell types; TCGA bulk DNAm data; independent single-nucleus DNAm datasets
metric: Validation accuracy, tumor purity correlation (vs ESTIMATE, ABSOLUTE, LUMP), prognostic association (survival)
baseline: ESTIMATE, ABSOLUTE, LUMP for tumor purity; existing blood-only deconvolution methods
result: Validation accuracy >90% for 8/13 tissues; tolerates up to 30–40% imputation error rate; novel prognostic subtypes identified in ONB and melanoma

problem: Designing efficient non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs) for organic solar cells with improved near-infrared absorption and photovoltaic properties beyond the Y21 reference molecule.
method: DFT and TDDFT at B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level to calculate band gap, exciton binding energy, transition density matrix, reorganization energy, absorption maxima, and open-circuit voltage of four novel Y-series NFAs (YA1–YA4) with modified end-capped acceptors.
finding: YA1 shows the best photovoltaic properties among designed molecules with the lowest energy gap, smallest reorganization energies, highest absorption maxima, and fine open-circuit voltage; all designed molecules outperform the Y21 reference.
dataset: Y21 reference molecule and 4 designed NFAs (YA1–YA4); PM6 donor polymer
metric: HOMO-LUMO gap, λmax, reorganization energy (λe, λh), open-circuit voltage (Voc), exciton binding energy
baseline: Y21 (experimentally synthesized, 15% efficient NFA, λmax=741 nm)
result: YA1: energy gap=1.924 eV, λe=0.0073 eV, λh=0.0083 eV, λmax=783.36 nm (gas)/715.20 nm (CHCl₃), Voc=1.17 V

problem: Whether anthocyanins can inhibit immune checkpoint proteins PD-1 and PD-L1 in colorectal cancer, thereby activating anti-tumor immune response in the tumor microenvironment.
method: In vitro cell viability assays (MTS) on HCT-116 and HT-29 colorectal cancer cells with pure anthocyanins; molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) for PD-1/PD-L1 binding; Western blot and immunofluorescence for PD-L1 expression; flow cytometry for PD-1 on PBMCs in mono- and co-culture.
finding: Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G) showed highest molecular docking affinity for PD-1 and PD-L1; C3G decreased PD-L1 fluorescence by 39% and PD-1 expression in PBMCs by 26–55% depending on culture conditions; D3G was the only effective anthocyanin for direct cancer cell inhibition.
dataset: HCT-116 (microsatellite instable), HT-29 (chromosomal instability) colorectal cancer cells; human PBMCs
metric: IC50, binding energy (kcal/mol), PD-L1 fluorescence intensity, PD-1 expression (%)
baseline: Atezolizumab and pembrolizumab (clinical immune checkpoint inhibitors); untreated controls
result: C3G PD-1 docking: −6.8 kcal/mol, PD-L1: −9.6 kcal/mol; D3G IC50: 396±23 (HCT-116), 329±17 μg/mL (HT-29); C3G decreased PD-L1 by 39%, PD-1 by 26–55%

problem: Identifying novel inhibitors of COP9 signalosome (CSN)-associated kinases CK2 and PKD that can stabilize tumor suppressor p53 and induce apoptosis in tumor cells.
method: In silico 2D/3D conformer screening of ~10⁶ compound database using curcumin and emodin as lead structures (3D RMSD superposition + 2D Tanimoto similarity); Lipinski's rule-of-five filtering; QSTR toxicity prediction; in vitro kinase assays and apoptosis assays (annexin V-FITC, DNA fragmentation, caspase activity).
finding: 35 compounds identified by in silico screening; 7 compounds (3 curcumin-group including piceatannol, 4 emodin-group including anthraquinone) potently inhibited CSN-associated kinases, increased p53 levels, and induced apoptosis in tumor cells.
dataset: In-house database of ~10⁶ compounds; HeLa tumor cells
metric: Kinase inhibition (% activity), Tanimoto coefficient, RMSD, p53 levels, annexin V binding, DNA fragmentation, caspase activity
baseline: Curcumin and emodin (known CSN kinase inhibitors)
result: 7/35 compounds confirmed as potent kinase inhibitors; 2D similarity threshold <85% (scaffold hoppers successfully identified); all compounds passed Lipinski and toxicity filters

problem: Predicting drug sensitivity of cancer cells to targeted therapies using genomic profiling, to enable personalized treatment stratification.
method: In silico tumor model (Cellworks v7.3) comprising >4,700 biological entities, ~6,500 reactions, ~25,000 kinetic parameters covering major signaling pathways; genomic profiles (copy number variation) used to create simulation avatars; predictions compared against empirical drug sensitivity data.
finding: The in silico model accurately predicted ~85% of gene mutation–drug sensitivity associations from the Garnett et al. study and achieved ~75% agreement with in vitro experimental findings on patient-derived GBM cell lines.
dataset: 45/639 cancer cell lines from Garnett et al. study; patient-derived GBM cell lines (Mayo Clinic); 70/130 drugs, 51/84 genes
metric: Agreement rate (% concordance between in silico predictions and experimental results)
baseline: Garnett et al. empirical drug sensitivity screening (hypothesis-independent); in vitro experimental data
result: ~85% retrospective agreement (22/25 testable associations); ~75% prospective agreement with in vitro GBM data

problem: Whether in silico hiPSC-derived cardiomyocyte models can reliably predict drug-induced ionic current block effects compared to adult ventricular cardiomyocyte models, for cardiac safety pharmacology.
method: In silico action potential models (Paci2013 for hiPSC-CMs, O'Hara-Rudy for hAdultV-CMs) simulating four degrees of selective block (0.1×IC50, IC50, 2×IC50, full) of eight major cardiac transmembrane currents (INa, ICaL, If, IK1, IKr, IKs, Ito, INaCa).
finding: hiPSC-CMs and hAdultV-CMs show qualitatively similar responses to ionic current block, but hiPSC-CMs are quantitatively more sensitive to ICaL block (up to 4× greater APD shortening) and IK1 block (repolarization failure) due to NCX overexpression and reduced repolarization reserve; IKr block effects are nearly identical at therapeutic concentrations.
dataset: In silico ventricular-like and atrial-like hiPSC-CM models; hAdultV-CM (O'Hara-Rudy endocardial) model
metric: APD30, APD50, APD70, APD90, MDP, AP amplitude, dV/dtmax
baseline: hAdultV-CM O'Hara-Rudy model; experimental data from Ma et al. 2011
result: IKr block: nearly identical APD prolongation at 0.1×IC50 and IC50; ICaL block: APD shortening up to 4× larger in hiPSC-CM; IK1 block: repolarization failure in hiPSC-CM but not hAdultV-CM

problem: The mechanisms driving variable invasive morphologies in Glioblastoma, ranging from individual to collective migration, and the role of cell-to-cell adhesion heterogeneity are not fully understood.
method: Combined 3D in vitro spheroid invasion assays (patient-derived and U87MG cells in BME substrate) with a discrete, individual cell-based mathematical model incorporating stochastic cell-to-cell adhesion heterogeneity.
finding: Primary GB spheroids exhibited a novel cohesive sheet-like invasive pattern mimicking perivascular invasion, while U87MG showed starburst non-cohesive migration. The mathematical model showed that cell-to-cell adhesive heterogeneity alone is sufficient to reproduce both invasive morphologies.
dataset: Patient-derived primary GB cells (76-year-old male) and U87MG cell line, 3D spheroid invasion assay over 288h
metric: Tumour expansion area, invasive pattern morphology, doubling time
baseline: U87MG cell line (established starburst invasion pattern)
result: Doubling times 30.8h (U87MG) and 25.4h (primary); distinct morphologies consistently reproduced in silico

problem: No licensed vaccines exist for Trichuris trichiura, which infects 500 million people, and current treatments have limited efficacy.
method: Systematic reverse vaccinology pipeline: in silico MHC-II T-cell epitope prediction (IEDB, NetMHC-II 2.2) from Trichuris genome ORFs, incorporation into Hepatitis B core antigen virus-like particles (VLPs), and in vivo challenge in mice.
finding: IEDB and NetMHC-II 2.2 showed ~78% sensitivity for epitope prediction. Mice vaccinated with VLPs+T-cell epitopes showed significantly reduced worm burden, mounted Trichuris-specific IgM and IgG2c responses, and produced Th2 cytokines with goblet cell hyperplasia.
dataset: T. muris genome (predicted ORFs), training set of 145 epitopes from 16 proteins, C57BL/6 mouse challenge model
metric: Sensitivity of epitope prediction, worm burden reduction, antibody titers (IgM, IgG2c), Th2 cytokines
baseline: VLPs without T-cell epitopes, Rankpep (10.61% sensitivity), SYFPEITHI (8.33% sensitivity)
result: IEDB/NetMHC-II sensitivity ~78%; significant worm burden reduction in VLP+epitope group

problem: Abnormal proliferation and differentiation can initiate colon cancer, but no calibrated computational model existed to simulate stochastic cell dynamics in normal human colon crypts using measured human biopsy data.
method: Agent-based model in NetLogo with position-dependent divide and die gradients along the crypt axis; calibrated via parameter sweeps against measured cell type counts from human biopsy specimens.
finding: The model reproduced measured average numbers and variation of stem, proliferating, and differentiated cells, as well as monoclonal conversion by neutral drift, adenoma formation from mutations, and crypt recovery from cytotoxic agents. Demonstrated utility for evaluating fractionated chemotherapy scheduling.
dataset: Normal human colon biopsy specimens (cell type counts from multiple crypts)
metric: Cell type counts (mean and variance), monoclonal conversion dynamics, adenoma formation, crypt recovery
baseline: Measured human biopsy cell type distributions
result: Reproduced measured cell type numbers and variation; simulated optimal chemotherapy duration with fractionated dosing

problem: Accurate patient-specific cancer prediction requires accounting for inter- and intratumoral proliferative heterogeneity, which is poorly characterized for primary Glioblastoma cell lines.
method: In vitro doubling time and cell size estimation for three patient-derived GB cell lines (GBP03, GBP06, GBP08) plus U87MG control, used to parametrize an individual cell-based mathematical model with sensitivity analysis of proliferation, cell size, and clonal heterogeneity.
finding: Intratumoral heterogeneity and differential proliferative capacity predict in vitro growth evolution. Multiclonal populations with the same mean proliferation exhibit greater tumor expansion than monoclonal ones, with variance increasing cumulatively over time.
dataset: Patient-derived GB cells: GBP03 (70y male), GBP06 (47y female, secondary GB), GBP08 (53y male), U87MG control
metric: Doubling time, cell diameter, tumor spheroid radius over time
result: GBP03 DT=49.7h (19μm), GBP06 DT=65.9h (16μm), GBP08 DT=31.2h (15μm); multiclonal variance reaches ~100μm radius difference at 14 days

problem: There is no effective vaccine against Ebola virus, and new approaches using in silico designed polyepitope T-cell immunogens need evaluation for immunogenicity.
method: Designed two artificial polyepitope antigens (EV.CTL for CD8+ and EV.Th for CD4+ epitopes) using TEpredict/PolyCTLDesigner software, cloned into pcDNA3.1, and evaluated immunogenicity in BALB/c mice via IFN-γ ELISpot and intracellular cytokine staining (ICS).
finding: Recombinant plasmids pEV.CTL and pEV.Th provided synthesis of corresponding mRNAs and proteins in transfected 293T cells and induced specific CD4+ and CD8+ T-lymphocyte responses in immunized mice.
dataset: Ebola virus proteins GP, VP24, VP30, VP35, L, VP40, NP; BALB/c mice
metric: IFN-γ ELISpot spot-forming cells, intracellular cytokine staining (ICS), mRNA/protein expression
result: Specific CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell responses detected in immunized mice by ELISpot and ICS

problem: In silico estimation of cytotoxicity against tumor and normal cell lines is needed to reduce time and costs of drug development, but existing tools have limited coverage of cell lines and mechanisms of action.
method: PASS approach using substructural atom-centric MNA descriptors and Bayesian algorithm to predict cytotoxicity against cell lines and molecular mechanisms of action, validated by leave-one-out and 20-fold cross-validation.
finding: CLC-Pred 2.0 predicts cytotoxicity for 391 tumor and 47 normal cell lines with mean AUC 0.925, NCI60 panel with AUC 0.870-0.945 depending on threshold, and 2170 mechanisms of action with AUC 0.979.
dataset: 128,545 compounds from ChEMBL/PubChem (391 tumor + 47 normal cell lines); 22,726 compounds for NCI60; 656,011 compounds for MoA
metric: AUC (LOO CV and 20-fold CV)
baseline: CLC-Pred v1.0 (278 tumor + 27 normal cell lines, 59,882 compounds)
result: AUC=0.925 (LOO CV) for cell lines; AUC=0.945 (NCI60, 1nM threshold); AUC=0.979 for MoA prediction

problem: Validating hypothesized combinatorial cis regulatory codes for metazoan gene expression requires extensive experimental work; computational methods to evaluate such codes before experimental verification are needed.
method: Developed CodeFinder framework integrating TFBS clustering (ModuleFinder) with gene expression data to evaluate enrichment of dTCF/Mad/Pnt/Twi/Tin motifs in non-coding sequences of Drosophila muscle founder cell genes, using evolutionary conservation across D. pseudoobscura and D. virilis.
finding: The full 5-TF regulatory code applies only to a subset of founder cell genes (those up-regulated in Pnt gain-of-function), not all FC genes. Experimentally confirmed 6 of 12 tested new CRMs driving mesodermal expression, 4 in founder cells.
dataset: 159 validated Drosophila muscle founder cell genes, D. melanogaster genome (dm2 assembly)
metric: Group specificity score, CodeFinder enrichment (p-value with Bonferroni correction), CRM validation rate
baseline: Non-FC gene promoter regions (1,590 background regions)
result: 6/12 (50%) predicted CRMs experimentally validated; 4 drive founder cell expression

problem: Understanding how developmental systems evolve over time is key, but experimental hurdles limit our whole-system understanding of stem cell and organ development.
method: Constructed in-silico agent-based models using software engineering tools (statecharts, activity diagrams) for three developmental systems, integrating known mechanisms with simplified assumptions where knowledge is limited.
finding: Models of mouse pancreas, C. elegans gonad, and partial rodent brain recapitulated organ development, anticipated known experimental results, and generated novel testable predictions, some experimentally validated.
dataset: Mouse pancreas, C. elegans gonad, rodent brain development
metric: Qualitative recapitulation of developmental dynamics, prediction of experimental outcomes
result: Models recapitulated development of 3 organs; novel predictions generated and some experimentally validated

problem: Fish nucleated red blood cells have been implicated in immune functions, but comprehensive functional pathway profiling of their transcriptome and proteome has not been performed.
method: RNA-seq de novo assembly and label-free shotgun proteomics (cytosolic/membrane fractionation, hemoglobin removal, pH reversed-phase peptide fractionation, LC ESI-MS/MS) of rainbow trout RBCs, followed by Cytoscape pathway enrichment analysis.
finding: Identified 13,937 expressed genes and 1,770 proteins; in silico functional analysis revealed immune networks including MHCII antigen presentation, leukocyte activation/differentiation, and hematopoietic stem cell differentiation regulation.
dataset: Rainbow trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) peripheral blood nucleated RBCs, 8 fish pooled
metric: Gene read counts, PSM counts, PPI enrichment p-value, FDR
result: 14,008 genes, 1,770 proteins identified; MHCII network FDR p=1.37×10⁻²⁷, PPI enrichment p<1.0×10⁻¹⁶

problem: Integration of single-cell multi-omics data is challenging due to modality-specific noise, data scarcity for less established modalities, and alignment difficulties across different omics layers.
method: scCross uses variational autoencoders (VAE) coupled with generative adversarial networks (GAN) and mutual nearest neighbors (MNN) for modality alignment, enabling cross-modal data generation, multi-omics simulation, and in silico perturbation.
finding: scCross achieves comparable or superior performance to state-of-the-art methods in multi-omics integration, excelling in cell clustering (ARI, NMI) and cell mixing metrics, while also enabling cross-modal generation and in silico perturbation analysis.
dataset: Matched mouse cortex, matched mouse lymph nodes, matched mouse atherosclerotic plaque immune cells (GSE240753), unmatched mouse cortex (scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq + snmC-seq)
metric: ARI, NMI, cell type average silhouette width (ASW), graph connectivity, ASW for omics layers
baseline: Seurat v4, scglue, uniPort, sciCAN, scDART, Harmony
result: Comparable or better ARI/NMI/ASW across all benchmark datasets vs. 6 state-of-the-art methods

problem: Tumor cell radiosensitivity varies by cell cycle phase
method: 3D agent-based tumor spheroid growth model
finding: Simulated tumor growth and radiation response
metric: sensitivity

problem: Taste receptor cell marker expression during development
method: Expression analysis in circumvallate papillae
finding: Characterized TRCSM expression during embryogenesis
dataset: mouse

problem: Ion channel targeting for cancer therapy
method: Calcium dynamics simulation with SOCE mechanisms
finding: Model reproduces calcium imaging data and SOCE dynamics
dataset: A549 cells

problem: OSCC molecular pathogenesis from dysplastic lesions
method: Microarray gene expression analysis from GEO database
finding: Signaling network dysregulation in early OSCC tumorigenesis
dataset: leukoplakia cohort

problem: Inventory of human pancreas cell types and markers
method: SORT-Seq deep single-cell sequencing
finding: Transcriptomic profiling of pancreatic cell types
dataset: human pancreas

problem: Tissue engineering scaffold design and fabrication
method: SolidWorks 3D modeling for scaffold design
finding: Computational scaffold design for tissue regeneration

problem: CAZy enzyme diversity in F. virguliforme
method: CAZy identification and annotation
finding: F. virguliforme produces diverse cellulose and pectin degrading enzymes
dataset: F. virguliforme

problem: Intronic lncRNA regulation in renal cell carcinoma
method: Custom microarray with intronic lncRNA probes
finding: 29 intronic lncRNAs differentially expressed in RCC
dataset: RCC patients
metric: FDR

problem: Anti-cancer potential of Phoenix dactylifera extract
method: Cell viability and apoptosis assays
finding: PDSE inhibits breast and liver cancer cell proliferation
dataset: MDA-MB-231
metric: IC50
result: 85 ug/mL

problem: Colon cancer treatment resistance and natural compounds
method: Antiproliferative assays with hydroxyphenylacetic acids
finding: Compounds reduce colon cancer cell viability
dataset: colon cancer cells

problem: ECM protein characterization in tissues
method: Enrichment and MS analysis of extracellular matrix proteins
finding: ECM protein composition analysis across tissues
dataset: mouse tissues
metric: specificity

problem: Cold atmospheric plasma effects on melanoma
method: Softjet CAP treatment of cancer cell lines
finding: Physical parameter effects of CAP on cancer cells
dataset: melanoma cells

problem: In silico vaccine design for leishmaniasis
method: Linear epitope prediction from Leishmania proteome
finding: Feasibility of in silico peptide vaccine approach
dataset: Leishmania spp.
metric: accuracy

problem: NSCLC prognostic biomarker discovery
method: Integrated lung cancer microarray database analysis
finding: Identified prognostic genes from 1,715 patients
dataset: N=1715 NSCLC
metric: accuracy

problem: NF-kB regulated gene identification in pancreatic beta-cells
method: TFBS discriminant analysis in promoter sequences
finding: Over-represented TFBS in NF-kB regulated genes
dataset: beta-cell genes
metric: accuracy

problem: Detecting functional cell assemblies from neural recordings
method: Large-scale cortical microcircuit simulation (211K neurons)
finding: Diverse assemblies detected from network simulations
dataset: cortical model

problem: 3D cell culture simulation in polymeric scaffolds
method: SALSA hybrid discrete/continuous cellular automaton
finding: Validated simulation matches in vitro cell density
dataset: cell culture
metric: accuracy

problem: Genotype-to-phenotype variation for personalized medicine
method: LNCS cardiac cell model with detailed parameterization
finding: Design patterns for computational genotype-phenotype studies
dataset: mouse cardiac
metric: sensitivity

problem: MSC-derived EVs therapeutic potential for COVID-19
method: In silico analysis of miRNA/mRNA in MSC-EVs
finding: MSC-EVs show potential against COVID-19 inflammation
dataset: MSC exosomes

problem: Post-operative radiotherapy optimization for NSCLC
method: IMRT, PSPT, and IMPT plan comparison
finding: Proton therapy spares lung dose vs IMRT
dataset: N=10 patients
metric: dose

problem: Understanding phenotypic variability in LQT3 hiPSC-CMs and evaluating antiarrhythmic drug responses (mexiletine, ranolazine) using in silico population models.
method: Populations of in silico hiPSC-CM models (modified Paci2015) calibrated with experimental data; simulated mexiletine and ranolazine multichannel effects at 5/10/20 μM using single pore block model.
finding: LQT3 models reproduce APD prolongation (+34.3%) matching experiments; symptomatic phenotypes show overexpression of ICaL, IK1, INaL and underexpression of IKr; mexiletine terminates spontaneous APs in far more models than ranolazine (784 vs 53 out of 1,401).
dataset: LQT3 V1763M mutation hiPSC-CMs, control n=1,463 models, mutant n=1,401 models
metric: APD90, APD prolongation (%), AP termination count
baseline: Control hiPSC-CM population, experimental data from Ma et al.
result: ΔAPD90=+34.3% (sim) vs +48% (exp); mexiletine 784/1401 vs ranolazine 53/1401 AP termination

problem: Determining the tissue origins of circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) in health and disease using an unbiased methylation deconvolution approach.
method: Reference methylation atlas of 25 human tissues/cell types from Illumina 450K/EPIC arrays; quadratic programming deconvolution of genome-wide cfDNA methylomes; validated with in silico simulations and in vitro DNA mixes at known proportions.
finding: Healthy plasma cfDNA originates from WBCs (55%), erythrocyte progenitors (30%), vascular endothelial cells (10%), hepatocytes (1%); disease-specific tissue contributions detected in sepsis, islet transplantation, and multiple cancer types agreeing with clinical findings.
dataset: 25 human tissues/cell types methylation atlas, plasma cfDNA from healthy donors and patients (sepsis, islet transplant, colon/lung/breast/prostate cancer)
metric: Tissue contribution percentage, deconvolution accuracy
baseline: In vitro DNA mixes at known proportions, in silico simulations
result: WBCs 55%, erythrocyte progenitors 30%, endothelial 10%, hepatocytes 1% in healthy plasma

problem: Identifying effective anti-cancer stem cell (CSC) agents to overcome treatment resistance and disease progression in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).
method: Connectivity Map (CMap) analysis with CSC-associated gene signatures querying 11,641 L1000 profiles and LINCS database (1.3M profiles); validated with in vitro CSC growth/EMT assays and in vivo xenograft models.
finding: Thiostrepton identified as top anti-CSC candidate; inhibits CSC growth, reduces CD133/Nanog/Oct4A, suppresses EMT and Src signaling, elevates tumor suppressor miR-98; synergistic with gemcitabine in suppressing NSCLC growth.
dataset: NSCLC cell lines (A549, H460, H1299, CL141, CL152), 11,641 L1000 profiles, CMap 2.0 (6,100 profiles), LINCS (1.3M profiles)
metric: Normalized enrichment score (NES), connectivity score, P-value
baseline: CMap database ranking, LINCS database ranking, anti-CSC gene signature (GSE18150)
result: NES=1.914 (P<0.0001), CMap enrichment=0.797 (P<0.001), LINCS rank 22nd (score_best4=97.368)

problem: Bridging the gap between in vitro cell culture data and clinical outcomes in drug development by integrating novel cell culture technologies with computational in silico models.
method: Review of hiPSCs, 3D cells, organoids, and microphysiological systems combined with PBPK, PK/PD, QSP models and virtual clinical trials for in vitro to in vivo translation.
finding: Synergizing high-quality in vitro data from novel cell culture platforms with mathematical models (PBPK, QSP) can better guide drug development, predict clinical outcomes, and optimize dose regimens.

problem: Automating detection of leukemic cell populations in multi-parameter flow cytometry readouts, replacing labor-intensive and subjective manual gating of low-dimensional projections.
method: Support Vector Machines (SVM) with RBF kernel for multivariate classification of 4-color flow cytometry data (CD10, CD20, CD34, CD19); trained on manually gated data with sample-wise normalization.
finding: SVM classification recovers manually gated leukemic cells with high accuracy; multivariate classification captures higher-order marker dependencies missed by 1D/2D gating and enables reliable detection of small cell populations.
dataset: 37 leukemic samples from childhood precursor B-cell ALL (d0/d8/d15) + 6 artificial dilution mixes, 4-color flow cytometry
metric: Sensitivity, specificity
result: 99.78% sensitivity, 98.87% specificity

problem: Elucidating the molecular bases of how V1/V2 and V3 loops on HIV-1 gp120 modulate viral receptor recognition (CD4, CCR5) and contribute to viral adaptation in growth-restrictive cells.
method: Homology modeling of full-length glycosylated gp120 in CD4-free and -bound states; 50 ns molecular dynamics simulation (AMBER 10, ff99SB-ILDN + GLYCAM06); cell-based adaptation experiments identifying enhancing substitutions.
finding: V1/V2 loop settles near V3 for conformational masking of neutralization epitope in CD4-free state and rearranges near CD4 upon binding; adaptation substitutions on receptor binding surfaces in V1/V2 and V3 enhance viral entry with reduced sensitivity to CCR5 antagonist TAK-779.
dataset: HIV-1 JRFL strain gp120 (GenBank U63632), PDB structures (3JWD, 3IDX, 3U4E, 2QAD, 2B4C, 1MEQ)
metric: RMSD, inter-residue distances, viral entry/growth efficiency, TAK-779 sensitivity
result: RMSD plateau after 20 ns; V1/V2-V3 thermodynamically stable arrangement confirmed

problem: Predicting and optimizing post-printing cellular behavior in 3D bioprinted cancer cell constructs without extensive trial-and-error experimentation.
method: Cellular automata (CA) model simulating post-printing cell behavior; validated with 3D bioprinted MDA-MB-231 cell-laden gelatin/alginate hydrogel constructs monitored over 11 days (MTT, live/dead, Ki-67).
finding: CA model successfully simulated 3D bioprinted structure and captured in vitro observations including viability and proliferation dynamics; can predict behavior for different initial cell numbers and bioink formulations without replicating experiments.
dataset: MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells in 4% gelatin/4% alginate hydrogel, 2-2.5×10^6 cells
metric: Cell viability (%), proliferation fold-change, Ki-67 index, doubling time
baseline: 2D cell culture (3× faster doubling time), in vitro measurements
result: Viability 76±2% (day 0) → 99±1% (day 7) → 96±2% (day 11); 2.8-fold proliferation by day 11

problem: Evaluating the potential of platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) as radiation dose enhancers in breast cancer cell lines and understanding cell-type-dependent efficacy.
method: In vitro experiments (clonogenicity, cell-cycle, oxidative stress, DNA DSBs) and Monte Carlo in silico simulation of ionization processes with 3.2 nm PEGylated PtNPs in T47D and MDA-MB-231 cells irradiated with 137Cs gamma rays.
finding: No significant radio-enhancement effect of PtNPs in breast cancer cells, unlike previously observed in HeLa cells; simulation predicted only ~3% dose increase near NPs; PtNPs exhibited antioxidant properties that may counteract the radio-enhancement effect.
dataset: T47D and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell lines, 0.5 mM PtNPs, 0-6 Gy gamma irradiation
metric: Clonogenic survival fraction, dose enhancement ratio, intracellular Pt mass (pg/cell)
baseline: Radiation alone (without PtNPs), previous HeLa cell study under same conditions
result: ~3% simulated dose increase near NPs; no significant biological enhancement; Pt uptake 0.5 pg/cell (MDA-MB-231), 0.2 pg/cell (T47D)

problem: Developing non-invasive DNA methylation biomarkers for detecting NSCLC and other common carcinomas from circulating cell-free DNA in liquid biopsies.
method: In silico identification of cancer-specific hypermethylated CpG loci from TCGA data; validated with methylation-specific qPCR on plasma cfDNA from NSCLC patients and healthy controls; cross-validated on 8 independent GEO cancer cohorts.
finding: 10 DNA methylation biomarkers distinguish NSCLC from controls with high accuracy; signal correlates with tumor size and decreases after surgical resection; validated across 8 additional carcinoma types from independent GEO data.
dataset: NSCLC patient plasma cfDNA, healthy controls, 8 GEO cancer cohorts (n=1,471 tumors), normal blood (n=310, GSE72773), normal tissues (n=571)
metric: AUC (ROC analysis)
baseline: Normal blood cohort (n=310), respective normal tissue cohorts
result: AUC=0.956 (clinical cfDNA), AUC=0.987-1.0 (GEO blood ref), AUC=0.972-1.0 (GEO tissue ref), 5-marker subset AUC=0.97

problem: Reconstructing the core gene regulatory network of OCT4 in human embryonic stem cells by integrating heterogeneous high-throughput functional data.
method: Integrated analysis of ChIP-on-chip (OCT4/SOX2/NANOG binding), RNAi-mediated OCT4 silencing, and promoter sequence analysis (octamer/SOX-OCT motif scanning) with de novo motif discovery in hESCs.
finding: Identified a core OCT4 network of 33 target genes important for pluripotency and differentiation; integration increased functional information 1.3-4.7× over individual methods; de novo motif analysis revealed known regulators and novel candidates including C-MYC/MAX heterodimer.
dataset: Human ESCs (WA09, H1), ChIP-on-chip (308 targets), RNAi expression (1,104 targets)
metric: Functional enrichment fold (vs individual methods), overlap percentage between methods
baseline: Individual experimental methods alone (ChIP-on-chip, RNAi, motif analysis separately)
result: 33 core target genes identified; functional enrichment 1.3-4.7× vs individual methods; <4% overlap between ChIP-on-chip and RNAi

problem: Much of the genetic contribution to breast cancer risk remains unknown, with rare coding variants and common non-coding variants only partially explaining heritability.
method: Genome-wide association study (GWAS) using the OncoArray and iCOGS platforms with imputation, combined with in silico functional annotation of risk loci in breast cells.
finding: Identified 65 new breast cancer risk loci at genome-wide significance; credible risk SNPs fall predominantly in distal regulatory elements with strong overlap between candidate target genes and somatic driver genes in breast tumours.
dataset: 122,977 cases and 105,974 controls of European ancestry; 14,068 cases and 13,104 controls of East Asian ancestry (BCAC consortium)
metric: P-value for association, heritability enrichment fold-change
baseline: Previously known breast cancer risk loci from prior GWAS
result: 65 new loci at P < 5e-8; heritability in regulatory features 2-5-fold enriched vs genome-wide average

problem: Breast cancer stem cells (BCSCs) express high levels of anti-apoptotic survivin, and a natural compound targeting survivin to induce apoptosis in BCSCs is needed.
method: In silico molecular docking (MOE 2010.10, MMFF94x, London dG scoring) of 7 natural compounds with survivin/caspase-9/caspase-3, followed by in vitro validation using qRT-PCR, ELISA, immunoblotting, and Annexin-V/PI flow cytometry on CD24-/CD44+ BCSCs.
finding: Andrographolide was selected as lead compound due to specific interaction with survivin Thr34 phosphorylation site; it decreased survivin and phospho-survivin levels, increased caspase-3, and induced apoptosis in BCSCs with no cytotoxicity to MSCs.
dataset: Human CD24-/CD44+ breast cancer stem cells and human mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs)
metric: CC50, ΔG binding (kcal/mol), pKi, mRNA fold change, apoptosis percentage
baseline: Rocaglamide (highest overall affinity but non-specific to Thr34); MSCs as selectivity control
result: CC50 = 0.32 mM in BCSCs; andrographolide ΔG = -8.2617 kcal/mol at Thr34 vs rocaglamide ΔG = -5.3851 at Ile19

problem: It is difficult to apply multiple sequencing technologies to the same single cell, requiring computational methods to integrate disjoint single-cell multi-omics measurements without correspondence information.
method: Unsupervised manifold alignment algorithm (MMD-MA) combining maximum mean discrepancy, distortion preservation, and penalty terms to embed cells from different modalities into a shared latent space using kernel methods.
finding: MMD-MA correctly aligned simulated multi-domain data and real single-cell gene expression and methylation data without requiring any correspondence information between cells or features.
dataset: 3 simulated manifolds (n=300 each, p1=1000, p2=2000); real scRNA-seq and methylation data from mouse embryonic stem cells
metric: Average fraction of samples closer than the true match
baseline: No direct comparison possible — other algorithms (e.g. UnionCom, MATCHER) require correspondence or were unavailable at time of study
result: Fraction of samples closer than true match approaches zero across all three simulations

problem: The bioactive metabolite profile, antibacterial, antioxidant, and anticancer potential of Eruca sativa leaves against colorectal carcinoma cells are not fully characterized.
method: HR-LC/MS phytochemical profiling, agar well diffusion antibacterial assay, DPPH/H2O2 antioxidant assays, MTT cytotoxicity assay on HCT-116 and Caco-2 cells, and in silico ADME/Tox prediction (SwissADME, pkCSM).
finding: E. sativa contains diverse bioactive compounds (vitamins, fatty acids, alkaloids, flavonoids, terpenoids, phenols) with antibacterial activity and promising anticancer activity against colorectal cancer cells in a dose/time-dependent manner.
dataset: HCT-116 and Caco-2 colorectal carcinoma cell lines; P. aeruginosa, B. subtilis, E. coli, S. aureus bacterial strains
metric: IC50, zone of inhibition
result: DPPH IC50 = 66.16 μg/mL; H2O2 IC50 = 76.05 μg/mL; HCT-116 IC50 = 64.91 μg/mL; Caco-2 IC50 = 83.98 μg/mL

problem: Cell-based therapies for heart failure using hCICs and hMSCs are limited by incomplete understanding of their effects on host cardiomyocyte electrophysiology and calcium cycling.
method: Developed a novel mathematical ODE model of hCIC electrophysiology fitted to patch-clamp data, coupled to existing hCM and hMSC models; ran population-of-models simulations (2,500 randomized models) of heterocellular coupling and paracrine signaling in healthy, failing, and fibrotic conditions.
finding: Paracrine signaling was necessary and sufficient to correct abnormal electrophysiology and calcium cycling of heart failure cardiomyocytes in fibrotic (but not non-fibrotic) myocardium, counteracting fibrotic effects while restoring arrhythmia-related metrics.
dataset: Digitized experimental hCIC patch-clamp data from Zhang et al. 2014; human cardiomyocyte model (O'Hara-Rudy)
metric: Action potential duration (APD), calcium transient amplitude (CaT), upstroke velocity, resting membrane potential
baseline: Healthy hCM electrophysiology; individual hCIC or hMSC coupling vs combined therapy vs fused CardioChimeras
result: hCICs produced greater calcium cycling increases with lesser APD reduction than hMSCs; permutation of 2,500 models predicted correction in fibrotic but not non-fibrotic HF

problem: How endothelial cells make rapid collective decisions during tip cell selection in angiogenesis, and whether filopodia play a role beyond simple sensing.
method: MemAgent-spring model (MSM) agent-based simulations of VEGF/Notch lateral inhibition with filopodia dynamics, validated with in vivo live imaging of zebrafish intersegmental vessel growth; information-theoretic measures from computational neuroscience applied to assess active perception.
finding: Filopodia provide coupled positive feedback between movement and sensing that confers bistable switch-like properties to Notch lateral inhibition, speeding up tip cell selection; GOF filopodia can rescue patterning speed in LOF VEGF conditions.
dataset: In silico: 10-cell MSM simulations; in vivo: zebrafish intersegmental vessel (ISV) live imaging
metric: Time to pattern (salt-and-pepper selection), transfer entropy, active information storage
baseline: Reduced filopodia (F=0.5 LOF) vs normal (F=2) vs enhanced (F=3 GOF); LOF VEGF gradient conditions
result: LOF filopodia significantly delayed tip selection; GOF filopodia rescued patterning speed under LOF VEGF

problem: The mechanism of action of algal-derived polysaccharides (fucoidan, alginate) on inflammatory biomarkers involved in early atherosclerosis — particularly selectins, MCP-1, and ICAM-1 — is poorly understood.
method: Molecular docking (AutoDock 4.2.6) of fucoidan and alginate with L-selectin, E-selectin, MCP-1, and ICAM-1, followed by in vitro validation using THP-1 monocyte migration assay, LDH/crystal violet cytotoxicity, and qRT-PCR for MCP-1/ICAM-1 expression.
finding: Fucoidan had greater binding affinity than alginate for all four inflammatory targets; it inhibited THP-1 monocyte migration and significantly suppressed MCP-1 and ICAM-1 expression in IFN-γ-stimulated THP-1 macrophages with no cytotoxicity up to 200 μg/mL.
dataset: THP-1 human monocytic leukemia cells; L-selectin (5VC1), E-selectin (1G1T), MCP-1 (1DOK), ICAM-1 (1IAM) crystal structures
metric: Binding energy (kcal/mol), inhibition constant (Ki), fold change in gene expression
baseline: Alginate as comparison ligand; IFN-γ-only treated cells as positive control
result: Fucoidan-L-selectin ΔG = -5.82 kcal/mol vs alginate -4.3 kcal/mol; MCP-1 suppressed 8-fold, ICAM-1 suppressed 4-fold vs IFN-γ only

problem: Identifying natural inhibitors of anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 protein from Wedelia trilobata secondary metabolites to promote cancer cell apoptosis.
method: In silico molecular docking (AutoDock Vina/PyRx) of 52 W. trilobata metabolites against Bcl-2 protein (PDB: 2W3L), molecular dynamics simulation (100 ns), and ADMET profiling (pkCSM, SwissADME).
finding: Friedelin showed the best binding affinity (-10.1 kcal/mol) to Bcl-2, superior to the standard drug Obatoclax (-8.4 kcal/mol), with stable MD simulation over 100 ns and favorable ADMET properties including no AMES toxicity, hepatotoxicity, or skin sensitization.
dataset: 52 bioactive secondary metabolites from W. trilobata; Bcl-2 protein crystal structure (PDB: 2W3L)
metric: Binding energy (kcal/mol), RMSD, ADMET parameters
result: Friedelin ΔG = -10.1 kcal/mol vs Obatoclax -8.4 kcal/mol; stable complex over 100 ns MD simulation

problem: The role of biomechanical processes (as opposed to molecular signaling alone) in driving early tooth morphogenesis from bud to cap stage is poorly understood.
method: 3D computational model (EmbryoMaker) using overdamped Langevin dynamics with differential tissue growth and cell adhesion, validated against ex vivo cell-tracking, mechanical relaxation experiments, and observed tooth shape changes (mouse E13-E15).
finding: Two biomechanical processes — differential tissue growth and differential cell adhesion — were sufficient to explain 3D cervical loop formation, their direction, and buccal-lingual vs anterior-posterior shape asymmetry; mesenchymal tensile stresses restrict lateral growth forcing downward cervical loop extension.
dataset: Mouse molar tooth germs (E13-E15, bud to cap stage); ex vivo organ cultures with mechanical perturbations
metric: Morphological shape comparison, cervical loop orientation, tissue relaxation response
baseline: Alternative growth distribution hypotheses (uniform vs enamel-knot-restricted vs gradient)
result: Accelerated epithelial growth + specific differential adhesion between 3 tissues reproduced observed 3D cap-stage morphology

problem: Traditional single-cell adhesion force experiments may not accurately reflect cell-cell interactions within colonies; understanding how adhesion forces scale in multicellular contexts is crucial for modeling cancer invasion.
method: Multi-scale multi-compartment mathematical model coupling intracellular E-cadherin–β-catenin dynamics with cell-level mechanics, comparing static vs dynamic adhesion hypotheses and fitting to dual-pipette separation force data.
finding: Only the dynamic adhesion model (where E-cadherin complexes redistribute among neighbors) fit experimental data; adhesion forces between cells in colonies are lower than single-cell experiments suggest; pressure-related cell-cycle arrest enables protrusive invasive structures independent of pro-invasion molecule expression.
dataset: E-cadherin separation force data from Chu et al. dual-pipette assays; L929 cell colony experiments
metric: Cell-cell separation force (nN), E-cadherin expression level correlation
baseline: Static adhesion model (Model 1: fixed bond number per cell pair) vs dynamic adhesion model (Model 2: redistributed complexes)
result: Dynamic model fit data well while static model failed; colony adhesion forces predicted lower than isolated two-cell measurements

problem: Existing in silico CPP prediction methods produce a large number of false-positive results due to insufficient and non-diverse negative training data.
method: Ensemble deep learning (5 LSTM+attention models) trained on 9-mer sliding window fragments, using 11M human reference protein-derived peptides as negative set to reduce false positives.
finding: AiCPP outperformed MLCPP, CellPPD, and CPPred with higher specificity, and identified novel CPPs from amyloid precursor protein that were experimentally validated and optimized.
dataset: 2,798 unique CPPs (5-38 aa) from CellPPD/MLCPP/CPPsite 2.0; 11,046,343 negative 9-mers from 113,620 human reference proteins; test set of 150 CPP + 150 non-CPP
metric: AUC, MCC, ACC, SEN, SPE, PRE
result: AUC=0.927, MCC=0.722, ACC=0.860, SPE=0.893 (vs MLCPP AUC=0.882, CellPPD AUC=0.724, CPPred AUC=0.845)

problem: Computational tissue models either describe few cells in high detail or larger ensembles coarsely; bridging these two scales for large-scale 3D tissue simulation is computationally prohibitive.
method: Highly parallel cellular Potts model (CPM) on the NAStJA HPC framework with domain decomposition, halo exchange, and agent-based cellular events driving multi-scale simulation.
finding: The parallelized CPM introduces no directional bias from domain subdivision and scales approximately linearly beyond 10,000 cores, enabling 1000^3 voxel cancerous tissue simulation at sub-cellular resolution.
dataset: Synthetic 3D tissue simulations (2-cell verification, cancerous tissue 1000^3 voxels)
metric: Volume/surface ratio deviation, cell center displacement, parallel scaling efficiency
result: ~3% temporal variation in surface/volume, 0.4 voxel center drift (vs 10 voxel cell extent), linear scaling beyond 10,000 cores

problem: The interplay between genetic risk variants and epigenetic regulatory elements in primary Sjogren's syndrome (SS) is poorly understood, particularly which cell types are most affected.
method: In silico analysis of 72 SS-associated SNPs using VEP, RegulomeDB, and HaploReg v2 for regulatory motif enrichment, linkage disequilibrium screening, and histone marker analysis across cell types (B cells, monocytes, epithelial cells).
finding: 94.4% of SS risk variants showed regulatory function enrichment; transcriptionally active motifs were most enriched in B cells (> monocytes > T cells); active histone markers at promoters and enhancers predominated in B cells.
dataset: 72 SNPs from 43 SS gene risk factors; ENCODE/Roadmap Epigenome ChIP data for GM12878 (B cells), CD14+ (monocytes), A549 (epithelial)
metric: Pearson's Chi-squared test (P<0.05), regulatory motif enrichment percentage, LD r²≥0.8
result: 94.4% regulatory enrichment; 645 LD variants identified; B cell-specific active histone markers at promoters and enhancers

problem: Very few Leishmania major CD8+ T cell epitopes have been mapped for human HLA types, hindering polytope vaccine development against leishmaniasis.
method: In silico epitope prediction using 7 algorithms (SYFPEITHI, BIMAS, EpiJen, Rankpep, nHLApred, NetCTL, Multipred) for HLA-A*0201-restricted 9-mer peptides from 6 L. major antigens, validated by in vitro PBMC stimulation with intracellular cytokine assay, ELISpot, and ELISA.
finding: LmsTI-1 (pool II) and LPG-3 (pool IV) peptides elicited specific CD8+/IFN-γ+ T cell responses in HLA-A2+ recovered individuals, confirmed by ELISpot and ELISA.
dataset: 6 L. major antigens (CPB, CPC, LmsTI-1, TSA, LeIF, LPG-3); PBMCs from HLA-A2+ and HLA-A2- L. major-recovered individuals
metric: % CD8+/IFN-γ+ T cells, cutoff-based responder rate, ELISpot, ELISA
baseline: HLA-A2- recovered individuals (control), HLA-A2+ healthy donors (control)
result: 31.6% and 13.3% of HLA-A2+ recovered persons responded above cutoff for pools II and IV respectively

problem: No specific antiviral therapeutics exist for MERS-CoV; in silico prediction of siRNAs targeting conserved viral regions could accelerate oligonucleotide-based therapeutic development.
method: Integrated bioinformatics approach (i-SCORE, RNAxs, RNAfold) for siRNA prediction targeting MERS-CoV orf1ab gene, followed by in vitro validation via reverse transfection in Vero cells and quantitative real-time PCR.
finding: 21 functional off-target-reduced siRNAs were selected from 462 candidates; siRNA-1 and siRNA-4 showed the best inhibition of viral replication in Vero cells with no cytotoxicity.
dataset: 17 full-genome MERS-CoV sequences (human and camel isolates, 92.3-99.9% identity); Vero cell line
metric: Ct values (qRT-PCR), cytotoxicity, free energy folding, seed-duplex stability
result: siRNA-1 best at 0.1 nM, siRNA-4 best at 5.0 and 10 nM in cell lysate; 21/462 siRNAs passed stringent filtering

problem: Active anti-leukemia components in Traditional Chinese Medicine herbs like Indigo naturalis and their precise molecular targets remain unidentified.
method: Comprehensive 2D K562/cell membrane chromatography (CMC) for active component screening, combined with in silico target identification (PharmMapper, TargetHunter), validated by cell viability (CCK-8), apoptosis (Annexin V-FITC), and Western blot.
finding: Three active components (indirubin, tryptanthrin, isorhamnetin) were identified; Src kinase was identified as isorhamnetin's membrane target (Kd=3.81 μM), mediating anti-leukemia effects through G2/M cell cycle arrest.
dataset: K562 leukemia cell line; Indigo naturalis (Qingdai) extract; 24 chemical components identified by Q-TOFMS
metric: Cell viability (CCK-8), apoptosis rate, dissociation constant (Kd), retention time on CMC
baseline: Imatinib (membrane receptor drug) and dexamethasone (intracellular receptor drug) as CMC controls
result: Kd(Src-isorhamnetin)=3.81 μM; isorhamnetin induces dose-dependent apoptosis and G2/M arrest in K562 cells

problem: How mechanical confinement and hypoxia jointly regulate tumor cell aggregate growth dynamics is poorly understood, limiting predictive modeling of early tumor growth.
method: Dual in vitro-in silico approach: CT26 tumor spheroids grown in alginate capsules (spherical and tubular) with tunable confinement, combined with a continuum mathematical model coupling cell mass fraction, oxygen diffusion, and internal pressure.
finding: Two distinct confinement regimes were identified: strong confinement (spherical capsules) causes homogeneous pressure rise above inhibitory threshold, while weak confinement (tubes) allows continued tip proliferation with central pressure buildup and hypoxia-induced necrosis.
dataset: CT26 colon carcinoma cells in alginate spherical capsules (90 μm inner radius) and tubular capsules
metric: Aggregate radius vs time, internal pressure, oxygen mass fraction thresholds
result: Growth inhibition pressure threshold 800-2000 Pa; confluence at tc=66h (spherical); oxygen inhibition below 8×10⁻⁶ mass fraction

problem: Whether noncoding RNAs (miRNAs, lncRNAs) regulate signaling pathways involved in SARS-CoV infection is not fully investigated, limiting identification of novel therapeutic targets.
method: Bioinformatic network analysis using STRING (PPI), GSEA/KEGG (pathway enrichment), MiRTarBase (miRNA-mRNA interactions), miRWalk2.0 (miRNA-lncRNA interactions), and cytoHubba (hub node identification).
finding: TGF-beta signaling pathway was identified as the most enriched pathway (11 genes); hub miRNAs (hsa-let-7a, miR-181a, miR-20b, miR-17, miR-27a) and hub lncRNAs (MALAT1, XIST) were identified as potential therapeutic targets.
dataset: SARS-CoV proteome (15 viral proteins) vs human proteome (40 host proteins); 293 protein-protein interactions
metric: P-value, gene count per pathway, hub degree (cytoHubba)
result: TGF-beta pathway P=6.99E-21 (11 genes); 5 hub miRNAs and 2 hub lncRNAs identified; 293 virus-host PPI interactions

problem: The molecular mechanism of quercetin's inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and its neuroprotective potential against Aβ25-35-induced oxidative stress are not fully elucidated.
method: Multispectroscopic analysis (fluorescence quenching, CD, UV-vis), enzyme kinetics (Ellman's method, Lineweaver-Burk), molecular docking, MD simulation, and PC12 cell neuroprotection assay (MTT, MDA, GSH, ROS).
finding: Quercetin inhibits AChE in a reversible mixed manner by binding at the peripheral aromatic site and extending into the active center; it synergizes with galantamine and protects PC12 cells from Aβ25-35-induced oxidative stress.
dataset: AChE from Electrophorus electricus; PC12 cells with Aβ25-35-induced oxidative stress model
metric: IC50, binding constant (Ka), fluorescence quenching, MDA/GSH/ROS levels
baseline: Galantamine hydrobromide (IC50=0.29 μM); taxifolin (IC50=54.01 μM); quercitrin (IC50=94.5 μM)
result: IC50=4.59±0.27 μM; Ka=(5.52±0.05)×10⁴ L/mol at 25°C; synergistic inhibition with galantamine

problem: Whether BCG vaccine can generate cross-reactive T cells against SARS-CoV-2 through shared epitopes is unknown, despite epidemiological associations between BCG vaccination and reduced COVID-19 severity.
method: Protein BLAST for similar 9-amino acid sequences between SARS-CoV-2 and M. bovis BCG, followed by HLA class I binding affinity prediction using IEDB and NetMHCpan 4.1 for 15 common HLA alleles.
finding: Seven similar 9-amino acid sequences (1 aa substitution) were found between SARS-CoV-2 and M. bovis; 6 of 7 peptide pairs showed moderate to high HLA class I binding affinity, supporting possible cross-reactive T cell generation.
dataset: SARS-CoV-2 proteome (MN908947.3) vs M. bovis BCG str. Tokyo 172; 15 common HLA-A/-B/-C alleles (Japan, USA)
metric: HLA binding affinity (Percentile Rank <10), number of shared amino acid sequences
result: 7 similar 9-mer sequences found; 6/7 pairs bind common HLA class I with Percentile Rank <10; >40 identical heptamer sequences

problem: Identifying a reliable miRNA signature for diagnosis and prognosis of renal clear cell carcinoma (KIRC), as individual miRNA markers suffer from limited reproducibility across studies.
method: Comprehensive analysis of miRNA expression profiles from TCGA (516 KIRC patients) using limma differential expression, ROC curve analysis, Kaplan-Meier survival analysis, and univariate/multivariate Cox regression.
finding: A three-miRNA signature was identified that effectively diagnoses KIRC with high sensitivity/specificity and independently predicts overall survival, classifying patients into high- and low-risk groups with significantly different outcomes.
dataset: TCGA KIRC cohort, N=516 patients
metric: sensitivity, specificity, AUC, Kaplan-Meier OS/DFS, Cox regression HR
result: sensitivity=98.3%, specificity=97.2%, 63 DE miRNAs (34 up, 29 down), independent prognostic factor for OS

problem: Improving human embryonic stem cell differentiation into definitive endoderm (DE) remains challenging and costly due to reliance on expensive growth factors like activin A.
method: Three orthogonal drug repurposing approaches (ssCMAP/LINCS, correlation-based, pathway/TF-based) to identify candidate DE inducers, validated using a SOX17-mNeonGreen CRISPR reporter hESC line with FACS and RNA-seq.
finding: Identified BRD-K42644990 as a novel compound that induces DE formation without activin A, likely via MYC pathway inhibition, achieving equivalent differentiation efficiency with 90% cost reduction when added to the standard protocol.
dataset: H1 hESC SOX17-mNG reporter line, GEO: GSE75748 (H1), GSE109658 (H9)
metric: % CXCR4+ cells, % SOX17-mNG+ cells (FACS), DE-induction efficiency
baseline: standard AA+CHIR protocol (100 ng/mL activin A + 3 µM CHIR)
result: 90% cost reduction with equivalent efficiency, 999 candidate inducers identified, 11/14 compounds induced DE

problem: The molecular mechanism by which citral suppresses human prostate cancer is unknown, and identifying drug targets that kill metastatic prostate cancer cells is critically needed.
method: Citral isolated from lemongrass oil via MPLC/HPLC, evaluated by MTT assay, flow cytometry, DNA fragmentation, Hoechst/PI staining, qPCR, Western blotting, molecular docking, and in silico ADMET/PASS prediction.
finding: Citral suppresses prostate cancer cell lipogenesis through AMPK phosphorylation activation and downregulation of FASN, ACC, HMGR, and SREBP1, and induces apoptosis by upregulating BAX and downregulating Bcl-2.
dataset: PC-3 and PC3M prostate cancer cell lines
metric: IC50, cell viability (MTT), apoptosis rate, protein expression levels
result: ADMET predicted safe drug-like properties, Pa>0.7 for apoptosis-related activities

problem: Adequate protective vaccines against Klebsiella pneumoniae are lacking, and identifying B cell epitopes of virulence-associated fimbriae antigens is needed for rational vaccine design.
method: Immunoinformatics approach using IEDB (7 tools), ABCpred, and conformational epitope prediction (DiscoTope, ElliPro) on type 1 fimbriae antigens (FimA, FimF, FimG, FimH), followed by antigenicity, toxicity, allergenicity, and human similarity screening.
finding: Four final B cell epitopes (one per Fim protein) were identified that are immunogenic, antigenic, non-toxic, non-allergenic, and dissimilar to human peptides, with suitable physicochemical properties for vaccine candidates.
dataset: K. pneumoniae type 1 fimbriae proteins FimA (CDO12578.1), FimF (CDO12574.1), FimG (CDO12573.1), FimH (CDO12572.1)
metric: epitope prediction scores, antigenicity, allergenicity, toxicity, human similarity
result: 4 final epitopes selected from 5+3+5+4 linear epitopes across four Fim proteins

problem: Developing more efficient isatin-based antitumor candidates by optimizing N-alkylindole-isatin conjugates for improved growth inhibitory profiles against cancer cells.
method: Synthesis of N-alkylindole-isatin conjugates, SRB cytotoxicity assay, NCI-60 panel screening, flow cytometric cell cycle and Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis assays, CDK2 and Bcl-2 inhibition assays, and molecular docking.
finding: N-propylindole-5-methylisatin hybrid 8a displayed broad-spectrum antiproliferative activity, induced cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in T-47D breast cancer cells, and potently inhibited CDK2 and Bcl-2, with N-propyl group contributing significant hydrophobic interactions.
dataset: A-549, MDA-MB-231, HCT-116, NCI-60 panel, T-47D breast cancer cells
metric: IC50, GI50 (MG-MID), cell cycle distribution, apoptosis %

problem: Discovering anti-pancreatic cancer agents is complex and expensive; computational approaches are needed to predict molecules that simultaneously inhibit multiple PANC cell lines and PANC-related proteins.
method: Perturbation theory combined with multilayer perceptron machine learning (PTML-MLP) trained on ChEMBL protein inhibition data and GDSC cell line sensitivity data, using molecular descriptors to predict multi-target inhibitors.
finding: Two PTML-MLP models with >78% accuracy were built and used to design three new molecules predicted as versatile anti-PANC agents capable of simultaneously inhibiting caspase-1, TNF-alpha, IGF1R, and multiple PANC cell lines.
dataset: 3,833 chemicals, 9,705 cases from ChEMBL and GDSC v7.0, 34 biomolecular/cellular targets, 12 PANC cell lines
metric: accuracy, IC50 cutoffs for activity classification
result: accuracy >78%, 3 new molecules designed as multi-target anti-PANC candidates

problem: Predicting cell-penetrating anticancer peptides (Cp-ACPs) is challenging as no existing model simultaneously evaluates both anticancer and cell-penetrating properties of peptide sequences.
method: Parallel two-layer ML framework using RF, SVM, and XGBoost classifiers with hybrid compositional/physicochemical features and multilayered recursive feature elimination (RFE), fused via consensus hard-fusion strategy.
finding: CpACpP achieved high prediction accuracy for both ACP and CPP subclassifiers, and the consensus fusion method effectively predicted Cp-ACPs, outperforming existing tools ACPred, mACPpred, MLCPP, and CPPred-RF.
dataset: experimentally validated ACP and CPP datasets, independent Cp-ACP validation set
metric: accuracy, AUC, MCC, precision
result: ACP ACC=0.98, AUC≈0.98; CPP ACC≈0.94, AUC≈0.95; Cp-ACP fusion AUC=0.94, SVM MCC=0.68

problem: It is largely unknown whether long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) are deregulated in SARS-CoV-2 infection and what transcription factors might regulate them during COVID-19.
method: In silico analysis of RNA-seq data (GSE147507) using edgeR differential expression, NPInter v4.0 for lncRNA interaction partners, KEGG/GO pathway analysis, and ChIPBase v2.0 for transcription factor binding at lncRNA promoters.
