finding: 20 lncRNAs (including MALAT1, NEAT1) were upregulated and 4 downregulated in SARS-CoV-2 infected cells; these interact with >2800 genes and 422 miRNAs involved in viral infection and immune pathways, regulated by IRF1, IRF4, STAT1, and STAT3.
dataset: GSE147507 (A549, Calu-3 cell lines infected with SARS-CoV-2, COVID-19 patient lung tissue)
metric: FDR, fold change, logFC, log(cpm)
result: 20 upregulated + 4 downregulated lncRNAs, EGOT increased 3.0-4.6 fold, 2788 interacting genes/proteins

problem: Physapubenolide (PB) shows anticancer cytotoxicity but its molecular targets and mechanisms in melanoma are unknown, limiting clinical development.
method: In silico target prediction combining chemical similarity searching, QSAR, and molecular docking, validated with CCK8 viability assay, EdU proliferation, colony formation, Annexin V/PI apoptosis, Western blotting, and melanoma xenograft model.
finding: PB targets HMGCR, inhibiting the HMGCR/YAP pathway to suppress melanoma proliferation and migration, induce apoptosis, and restore vemurafenib sensitivity in resistant A-375 cells.
dataset: A-375, SK-MEL-5 melanoma cells, vemurafenib-resistant A-375, HaCaT normal cells, melanoma xenograft model
metric: IC50, cell viability, EdU-positive cells, apoptosis rate, protein expression
baseline: vemurafenib-sensitive A-375 cells, HaCaT normal keratinocytes
result: IC50=1.225 µM (A-375), 1.416 µM (SK-MEL-5) at 72h, no significant cytotoxicity in HaCaT at low concentrations

problem: Multiple in silico hSC-CM models exist but their drug response predictions have not been systematically compared against each other and against in vitro data.
method: Simulated 15 drug actions using simple pore-block model with experimental IC50 data for seven ion channels on three hSC-CM models (Koivumäki2018, Kernik2019, Paci2020), with sensitivity analysis of INa, ICaL, and IKr blockade.
finding: The three models show qualitatively consistent CTD90 changes but smaller magnitudes than in vitro; they differ in susceptibility to pro-arrhythmic events and automaticity suppression, reflecting variability in underlying experimental calibration data.
dataset: 15 drugs tested on 3 in silico hSC-CM models (Koivumäki2018, Kernik2019, Paci2020), in vitro hSC-CM data
metric: CTD90 (% change), APD90, arrhythmic event occurrence, MDP
result: sparfloxacin 10µM: +42% CTD90 in vitro vs +17%/+6%/+9% in silico; qualitative agreement but quantitative underestimation

problem: Discovery of new inhibitors against HCT116 human colon cancer cell line by developing QSAR models using both molecular descriptors and NMR descriptors.
method: Two QSAR approaches: (A) regression models using molecular descriptors from 7,339 compounds with machine learning (SOM clustering, ward clustering); (B) classification models using 1D NMR data (1H and 13C) from 110 marine actinobacteria samples.
finding: Approach A achieved strong predictive regression models; Approach B demonstrated that 1D NMR descriptors can predict anticancer activity of natural product samples prior to bioactivity screening, enabling a frontloading drug discovery strategy.
dataset: 7,339 molecules from ChEMBL/ZINC (Approach A); 50 crude extracts, 55 fractions, 5 pure compounds from marine actinobacteria (Approach B)
metric: R², RMSE, classification accuracy
result: Approach A: R²=0.75/0.73, RMSE=0.66/0.69 (train/test); Approach B: accuracy >63% (train and test)

problem: Estimating the fitness effects of chromosome number alterations (aneuploidy) and testing whether cancer cells have elevated tolerance to aneuploidy compared to normal cells.
method: Discrete time branching process simulation (Chiron software) modeling cellular growth with chromosome mis-segregation and selection, integrated with FISH experimental data from fibroblasts and cancer cell lines.
finding: Cancer cells show significantly reduced fitness penalty from aneuploidy (~6%) compared to normal cells (~30%), and combined aneuploidy tolerance plus mis-segregation better reproduces cancer genomic landscapes than mis-segregation alone.
dataset: 12,433 interphase fibroblast nuclei (FISH), >400 cancer cases from Mitelman Database
metric: Selection parameter s (fitness reduction per aneusomy), chromosomal mis-segregation rate
baseline: Models based on chromosomal mis-segregation alone
result: Normal cells s≈0.30 vs cancer cells s≈0.06 (p=0.0008)

problem: Existing computational cell type proportion estimation methods suffer from performance degradation due to mismatch between training data and real-world bulk RNA-seq data (batch effects).
method: DAISM-DNN pipeline: in silico data augmentation (DAISM) mixing calibration samples with single-cell reference to generate dataset-specific training data, then training a deep neural network for deconvolution.
finding: DAISM-DNN consistently and substantially outperforms nine state-of-the-art methods across all evaluated cell types in real-world datasets by building dataset-specific models that overcome cross-platform batch effects.
dataset: 11 independent real-world datasets (n=685 total samples), 36 human PBMC samples with CyTOF ground truth
metric: Friedman test with post hoc Nemenyi test, correlation with ground truth proportions
baseline: CIBERSORT, CIBERSORTx, EPIC, quanTIseq, MCP-counter, xCell, ABIS, MuSiC, Scaden
result: Outperforms all 9 methods consistently across all cell types; cross-batch validation with 30 calibration + 6 test samples

problem: Functional T-cell epitope discovery for cancer immunotherapy suffers from high false-negative rates in in silico prediction and lacks systematic validation with high-throughput experimental platforms.
method: Combined in silico epitope prediction (binding affinity, uniqueness, natural processing filters) with iTopia Epitope Discovery System measuring MHC Class I binding and stability, followed by immunogenicity testing in HLA-A2 transgenic mice.
finding: The iTopia stability assay is a valuable complement to in silico prediction for epitope prioritization; 13 novel T-cell epitopes and analogs were identified from CEA, HER2, and TERT tumor antigens.
dataset: 225 candidate peptides from CEA, HER2, and TERT tumor-associated antigens; 5 HLA alleles (A1, A2, A3, A24, B7)
metric: MHC complex half-life (T1/2), binding fluorescence, IFN-γ ELISPOT
baseline: BIMAS, ANN prediction algorithms; random (non-filtered) peptide selection
result: 5- to 50-fold enrichment in MHC-I binders vs reference; 13 novel epitopes identified; 31% CEA and 8% HER2 epitopes shared with other human proteins

problem: Understanding how macromolecular crowding (MMC) in the intracellular environment stabilizes DNA/DNA and DNA/RNA hybrids, and why PCR efficiency is compromised by the disparity between primer-annealing and extension temperature optima.
method: Atomistic molecular dynamics simulations (CHARMm force field, 1 ns) of nucleic acid hybrids under macromolecular confinement, validated by real-time PCR and SYBR Green I melting curve analysis under crowded conditions.
finding: MMC stabilizes hydrogen bonding between complementary nucleotides, increases melting temperatures by up to 8°C, enhances duplex helicity, and drives specificity of duplex formation preferring matched over mismatched sequences.
dataset: DNA oligomers (A20:T20, A15:T15, mixed sequences) and DNA:RNA hybrids (T20:rA20)
metric: H-bond count, RMSD, melting temperature (Tm), duplex preservation percentage
result: Tm increase up to 8°C; duplex preservation 71% (crowded) vs 37% (non-crowded); 11/40 H-bonds retained under confinement vs 0/40 non-crowded at 34°C

problem: Investigating whether resveratrol exerts anti-neoplastic activity in ovarian cancer through epigenetic modulation of non-coding RNAs (miRNAs and lncRNAs).
method: Microarray profiling of miRNAs and lncRNAs in OVCAR-3 ovarian cancer cells treated with 100 μM resveratrol for 24h, followed by DIANA tools pathway analysis, Gene Ontology analysis, and PubMed literature validation.
finding: Resveratrol modulates multiple miRNAs and lncRNAs involved in cancer-relevant pathways including cell homeostasis, proliferation, death, and motility, supporting its anti-neoplastic potential via non-coding RNA epigenetic mechanisms.
dataset: OVCAR-3 ovarian cancer cells, Human Whole Genome Oligo Microarrays 860K v2 (27,958 genes + 7,419 lincRNAs)
metric: Log2 fold-change, adjusted p-value
result: 44 up-regulated + 54 down-regulated miRNAs (FC>0.2, adj.p<0.01); 6 up + 1 down miRNAs (FC>0.585, adj.p<0.01)

problem: Identifying novel antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) and cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs) from venomous fish natterin toxins as alternatives to antibiotic therapies and drug delivery systems.
method: In silico prediction pipeline using CAMP, AMPA, AmpGram, C2Pred, and CellPPD web servers, with physicochemical analysis (ProtParam, PepCalc, DispHred), membrane-binding (Boman index), immunogenicity/toxicity screening, and PEP-FOLD3 structure prediction.
finding: 57 bioactive peptides (19 AMPs, 8 CPPs, 30 dual AMP/CPPs) were identified from five natterin toxins; 15 peptides selected as lead compounds for experimental validation. 54 peptides localized to plasma membrane.
dataset: 5 natterin toxin sequences from Thalassophryne nattereri (natterin 1-4 and P)
metric: SVM score, RF probability, Boman index, instability index, GRAVY
result: 57 BAPs identified; 15 lead compounds selected; 16 antiviral + 37 anticancer peptides predicted

problem: Reviewing and advancing mathematical and computational (in silico) models of tumour growth and treatment-induced cell kill to improve radiotherapy and chemotherapy planning.
method: Review of mathematical and stochastic computer models including Monte Carlo methods, cellular automata, agent-based models, and deterministic ODE/PDE approaches for simulating fractionated radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and combined therapies.
finding: Greater depth of tumour biology knowledge at cellular, genetic, and chemical levels is required for model advancement; computational sensitivity studies within biologically plausible limits remain valuable despite data-gathering challenges.

problem: Characterizing the pharmacologic properties and cytotoxicity mechanisms of indolylquinone analogues of perezone against cancer cells using computational methods.
method: DFT calculations (B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p)) for reactivity parameters and frontier molecular orbitals, OSIRIS for toxicological/physicochemical predictions, and molecular docking against apoptosis-related proteins (caspase-3, caspase-9, Apaf-1).
finding: Indolylmenadione showed the highest cytotoxicity correlating with smallest HOMO-LUMO gap; docking scores correlated with IC50 ranking. No mutagenic, tumorigenic, or reproductive toxicity predicted for the four compounds.
dataset: 4 indolylquinone analogues tested on MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cells
metric: IC50, HOMO-LUMO gap (Egap), docking score, hydrogen bonding
result: IC50 ranking: indolylmenadione (25.06 μg/mL) > indolylperezone > indolylplumbagine > indolylisoperezone; Egap correlation with cytotoxicity confirmed

problem: Overcoming drug resistance in non-small cell lung cancer by discovering dual-targeting compounds against MEK and PIM1 kinases through drug repurposing.
method: High-throughput virtual screening of 11,808 DrugBank molecules using Glide XP docking, RF-Score-VS (machine learning scoring function), and Prime MM/GBSA binding energy calculations against MEK1 and PIM1 targets.
finding: Two compounds (DB012661 and DB07642) showed significant dual binding capability against both MEK and PIM1, with van der Waals interactions as the major contributor to binding affinity.
dataset: 11,808 DrugBank molecules (3,085 FDA-approved + 5,689 experimental + 3,034 investigational); MEK1 (PDB:3W8Q), PIM1 (PDB:5KZI)
metric: Glide XP docking score, RF-Score-VS, Prime MM/GBSA ΔGbind, ROC-AUC, SIE free energy
baseline: 25 active + 75 decoy compounds from DUD-E for validation; known MEK inhibitors
result: ROC-AUC: 0.86 (Glide XP), 0.90 (RF-Score-VS); 2 lead compounds identified with dual MEK/PIM1 binding

problem: Prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets associated with cancer stem cell (CSC) characteristics have not been fully identified in glioma.
method: OCLR machine learning estimated mRNAsi from TCGA RNA-seq data; EdgeR identified DEGs between high/low stemness groups across grades; LASSO-regularized multivariate Cox regression built a prognostic nomogram from 11 stemness-related genes.
finding: mRNAsi is a reliable index for IDH1 mutation status, histologic grade, and overall survival; three novel prognostic biomarkers (F2RL2, CLCNKA, LOXL4) were identified for glioma.
dataset: TCGA glioma cohort (705 primary brain gliomas: 532 LGG + 173 GBM, 657 with clinical data)
metric: AUC-ROC, Kaplan-Meier survival, univariate/multivariate Cox p-value
result: Nomogram AUC=0.832; 340 SRGs identified; risk score independently prognostic

problem: Multidrug resistance (MDR) mediated by MRP1 is a major cause of chemotherapy failure in retinoblastoma, and the potential of curcumin to inhibit MRP1 function in RB cells is unknown.
method: Western blot and RT-PCR assessed MRP1 expression; flow cytometry with calcein-AM measured MRP1 transport function; ATPase activity and 8-azido-ATP-biotin photoaffinity labeling characterized binding mechanism; homology modeling and docking simulation studied molecular interactions.
finding: Curcumin did not affect MRP1 expression but inhibited MRP1 transport function by increasing calcein-AM accumulation (~30-50%) and interacted with the substrate binding site, as confirmed by ATPase inhibition and docking studies.
dataset: Y79 retinoblastoma cell line
metric: Fluorescent substrate accumulation (calcein-AM), ATPase activity (Pi release), p-value
baseline: DMSO-treated control; MRP1 inhibitors probenecid (1mM) and indomethacin (100µM)
result: ~30-50% increase in calcein-AM accumulation; p<0.05 vs control

problem: The functional consequences of BAP1 somatic mutations in clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) and their potential therapeutic implications are not well understood.
method: Kaplan-Meier survival analysis compared BAP1-mutated vs wild-type ccRCC from TCGA; DEGs identified by t-test with FDR correction; GO/KEGG enrichment and PPI network analysis with MCODE identified hub genes and modules; CpG methylation analysis assessed CIMP correlation.
finding: BAP1-mutated ccRCC patients had significantly worse OS and DFS; 1,799 DEGs were identified; CAD, TSPO, CTNNB1, and MAPK3 were top hub genes; BAP1 mutation strongly correlated with CIMP.
dataset: TCGA ccRCC cohort (445 cases: 39 BAP1-mutated, 406 wild-type)
metric: Kaplan-Meier OS/DFS p-value, adjusted P<0.01, FDR<0.01, Z-score difference>0.5
result: OS p=0.035, DFS p=0.036; mutation frequency 8.76% (39/445); 583 up-regulated + 1,216 down-regulated DEGs

problem: The mechanisms by which cellular senescence is stably maintained after DNA damage response (DDR) initiation, and particularly the role of ROS feedback, are poorly understood.
method: Combined in-silico interactome analysis with functional siRNA target gene inhibition, stochastic mathematical modeling, and live cell microscopy (confocal, FACS, electron microscopy) in primary human and mouse fibroblasts undergoing stress-induced or telomere-dependent senescence.
finding: A positive feedback loop exists where p21 (CDKN1A) induces mitochondrial dysfunction and ROS production through GADD45-p38MAPK-GRB2-TGFBR2-TGFβ signaling, and these ROS replenish DNA damage foci to maintain DDR; this loop is both necessary and sufficient for stable senescence.
dataset: MRC5 human embryonic lung fibroblasts, p21-null MEFs, TRF2ΔBΔM transgenic mice (brain/gut sections, 12-15 months)
metric: DNA damage foci frequency, MitoSOX/DHR fluorescence intensity, mitochondrial membrane potential (JC-1/TMRM), BrdU/Ki67 labeling index
baseline: Untreated proliferating fibroblasts; siRNA knockdown controls (scrambled siRNA)
result: ROS elevated from day 1 post-IR and sustained; antioxidant treatment reduced but did not abolish DDR foci; p21 siRNA abolished ROS feedback

problem: Suboptimal nitrogen availability is a primary constraint to plant growth, and the utility of root cortical cell size (CCS) and cortical cell file number (CCFN) as adaptations for nitrogen acquisition is untested.
method: OpenSimRoot functional-structural plant/soil model simulated maize root anatomy variants (CCS, CCFN, RCA, LRBD) under varying soil nitrogen in silt loam and loamy sand; multiple linear regression predicted tissue construction costs from empirical measurements of 12 maize genotypes.
finding: Reduced CCFN increased shoot dry weight over 80% and large CCS increased shoot biomass by 24% under nitrogen stress; integrated phenotypes with reduced CCFN, large CCS, and high RCA improved biomass up to 145% in loamy sand; phene synergisms exist between CCS, CCFN, and LRBD.
dataset: 12 maize (Zea mays) genotypes with phenotypic variation for CCFN and CCS; simulated silt loam and loamy sand soils
metric: Shoot dry weight (% change), root respiration, nitrogen content, root diameter
baseline: Increased CCFN (18 files) and small CCS (200 µm²)
result: Reduced CCFN: +82% shoot growth; large CCS: +24%; integrated phenotype: +44% (silt loam), +145% (loamy sand)

problem: Predicting the response of specific cancer cell lines to drug treatment requires integrating both genomic features of cancer cells and chemical properties of drugs, which prior approaches addressed separately.
method: Neural network and random forest models were trained on GDSC data using 138 genomic features (mutations, CNVs, microsatellite status) of 608 cell lines combined with 689 chemical descriptors of 111 drugs to predict IC50 values; 8-fold cross-validation and an independent blind test of 13,565 newly generated IC50 values were used for evaluation.
finding: Integrating genomic and chemical features into multi-drug models significantly outperformed single-drug genomic-only models; the approach can impute missing IC50 values and generalize to unseen tissue types.
dataset: GDSC (Genomics of Drug Sensitivity in Cancer): 608 cell lines × 111 drugs, 38,930 training IC50 values, 13,565 blind test IC50 values
metric: Coefficient of determination (R²), RMSE, Pearson correlation
baseline: Single-drug neural network models using genomic features only
result: R²=0.72 (8-fold CV), R²=0.64 (blind test), R²=0.61 (unseen tissue)

problem: Doxorubicin's severe side effects at high effective doses limit its clinical use in liver cancer, and strategies to potentiate low-dose doxorubicin efficacy via chemosensitizers and metronomic scheduling need investigation.
method: MTT cytotoxicity assays under long-term and metronomic (short repeated 2h) exposure schedules in HepG2 cells; flow cytometry measured doxorubicin/rhodamine-123 intracellular accumulation; Western blot assessed P-gp expression; molecular docking and MM-PBSA dynamic simulations characterized caryophyllane-P-gp interactions.
finding: Metronomic scheduling enhanced low-dose doxorubicin cytotoxicity (IC50 reduced ~2.5-fold with triple exposure); caryophyllane sesquiterpenes further potentiated this effect by inhibiting P-gp efflux function and reducing P-gp expression; β-caryophyllene showed stronger P-gp binding affinity by MM-PBSA.
dataset: HepG2 human hepatoma cell line
metric: IC50, combination index, intracellular fluorescence accumulation, P-gp expression (Western blot), MM-PBSA binding energy
baseline: Doxorubicin alone (single 24h exposure); DMSO vehicle control
result: Dox IC50: 14.4µM(24h)→1.6µM(72h); metronomic triple 2h IC50=5.8µM; ~9-fold potency increase with long-term exposure

problem: The mechanisms and prognostic implications of inflammatory cell infiltration patterns in non-malignant breast lobular tissue, and whether physiological inflammation can be distinguished from pathological lymphocytic lobulitis (LLO), are poorly understood.
method: Agent-based model of immune-epithelial cell interactions in breast lobular epithelium calibrated from immunohistochemistry (CD8, CD4, CD163) quantification of ~900 lobular structures from 22 healthy women; automated image analysis on whole-slide images; simulated 12 menstrual cycles with varying hormone levels and damage rates.
finding: A critical epithelial damage rate threshold separates physiological from pathological inflammatory regimes; the immunological context (immune cell density, functional orientation, spatial distribution) contains prognostic information not captured by conventional diagnostics; menstrual cycle length and hormone status influence inflammatory responses.
dataset: Breast tissue from 22 healthy premenopausal women (reduction mammoplasty), ~650 lobular structures (luteal) + ~250 (follicular); 7 BRCA1/2 mutation carriers for validation
metric: Relative immune cell number (per epithelial cell), damaged epithelial cell fraction, killing rate (k_kill), damage rate (k_dge)
baseline: Physiological homeostasis (linear damage-response regime) in healthy women
result: Non-linear immune response above critical damage threshold; immunosuppression and effector exhaustion in pathological regime

problem: Acellular pertussis vaccines show waning immunity and resurgence of whooping cough; tolerance-inducing epitopes in vaccine antigens that cross-react with human sequences may reduce vaccine efficacy.
method: EpiMatrix screened 49 Bordetella pertussis proteins for HLA class II epitope content across 8 supertype alleles; JanusMatrix identified TCR-face cross-reactive peptides homologous to human proteome; OptiMatrix detolerized 4 peptides by disrupting HLA/MHC binding; in vitro competitive binding assays validated reduced HLA class II affinity of detolerized variants.
finding: Seven proteins were identified as good vaccine candidates; 19 cross-reactive peptides were found; detolerized variants of 4 peptides showed reduced HLA class II binding affinity in vitro, supporting epitope engineering as a strategy for improved pertussis vaccines.
dataset: 49 Bordetella pertussis proteins (Tohama I strain); 8 HLA class II supertype alleles covering >95% human population
metric: EpiMatrix Protein Score, JanusMatrix Homology Score, HLA class II binding affinity (competitive binding assay IC50)
result: 7/49 proteins selected; 19 cross-reactive peptides identified; detolerized variants showed reduced HLA binding vs wild-type

problem: The complex interplay of ion channel expression and its consequences on cancer cell bioelectric properties and proliferation is insufficiently understood, and no computational whole-cell model of a cancer cell existed.
method: Hidden Markov model-based whole-cell ion current model of A549 lung adenocarcinoma incorporating 11 functionally expressed ion channels; particle swarm optimization fitted channel numbers to whole-cell patch-clamp data; simulated membrane potential oscillations across cell cycle phases (G0/G1/S/G2/M).
finding: The model reproduces membrane potential oscillations driving cell cycle transitions and predicts effects of targeted ion channel modulation; PSO showed highest stability over 1,000 simulation runs; the model identified key channels (KCa3.1, TASK-1, Kv7.1) governing cell cycle-dependent membrane potential changes.
dataset: A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cell line; Single Cell Expression Atlas (113 ion channel genes); whole-cell patch-clamp recordings
metric: Whole-cell current fit (least squares), membrane potential (mV), channel number estimation
baseline: Experimental whole-cell patch-clamp recordings of A549 cells
result: 11 ion channels modeled from 113 expressed; resting membrane potential oscillation from hyperpolarized to depolarized states across cell cycle

problem: Identifying novel lncRNA-mRNA regulatory axes in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) to understand mechanisms of disease progression.
method: Batch Cox regression screening, random forest hub lncRNA selection, Spearman correlation for target prediction, GO/KEGG enrichment, GSEA, and expression validation using TCGA, GEO, and CCLE databases.
finding: LINC00654 identified as hub lncRNA with NINL as downstream target; high NINL expression associated with therapy response but is a risk factor for OS/DSS in older and advanced-stage patients; NINL involved in neutrophil-mediated immunity and NF-κB signaling.
dataset: TCGA DLBCL (N=48), GSE32018 (22 DLBCL + 7 normal), GSE18376 (23 DLBCL), 6 CCLE cell lines
metric: HR, p-value, Spearman correlation, random forest importance (>0.3)
result: LINC00654 HR=5.53 (p=0.009), NINL therapy response p=0.036, 1443 target mRNAs identified

problem: Investigating whether routinely administered ALL chemotherapeutic agents can bind and inhibit deregulated cell cycle proteins (Cyclins E1, D1, D3, A1 and CDK2, CDK6).
method: Schrödinger Glide XP molecular docking of chemotherapeutic drugs (Doxorubicin, Daunorubicin, Clofarabine, Nelarabine, Flavopiridol) to PDB crystal structures of cell cycle proteins.
finding: Drugs successfully docked to Cyclins E1, A1 and CDK2, CDK6 but failed for Cyclins D1 and D3; Doxorubicin showed highest binding affinity to CCNE1, outperforming known inhibitors.
dataset: PDB crystal structures (1W98, 3NUP, 2W96, 3G33, 1FIN), drugs from NCI and BindingDB
metric: Glide GScore (kcal/mol), docking energy (kcal/mol)
baseline: Known cyclin E1 inhibitors Baicalein (−6.693 kcal/mol) and Silymarin (−5.573 kcal/mol)
result: Doxorubicin GScore −8.978 kcal/mol (CCNE1), Daunorubicin −7.769 kcal/mol; range −8.978 to −2.868 kcal/mol

problem: Understanding the role of nitric oxide synthase (NOS) structural and functional features in the extreme radioresistance of Lepidopteran insect cells compared to mammalian cells.
method: In silico analysis including PSI-BLAST homology search across 22 organisms, SWISS-MODEL tertiary structure prediction, NetPhos 2.0 phosphorylation prediction, and WoLFPSORT subcellular localization.
finding: Lepidopteran NOS has significantly higher phosphorylation scores and primarily cytoplasmic localization compared to mammalian NOS, which may reduce NOS activation by radiation and limit oxidative damage amplification.
dataset: NOS protein sequences from 22 organisms (Bombyx mori, Drosophila, rat nNOS)
metric: Phosphorylation score (NetPhos 2.0), WoLFPSORT localization score, sequence identity (%)
result: Lepidopteran phosphorylation 0.885±0.02 vs mammalian 0.694±0.094; cytoplasmic score 23 vs 10

problem: Identifying natural compounds that can bypass or overcome multidrug resistance (MDR) mechanisms in cancer, including P-gp, BCRP, p53 knockout, and ΔEGFR.
method: Resazurin cytotoxicity assays on drug-resistant cell panels, Schrödinger molecular docking and dynamics on Akt/EGFR pathway proteins, Western blot for Akt/STAT3, and NCI-60 pharmacogenomics correlation.
finding: Oridonin bypasses major drug resistance mechanisms with much lower cross-resistance than doxorubicin; dose-dependently downregulates Akt and STAT3; ΔEGFR cells were even more sensitive to oridonin.
dataset: CCRF-CEM/CEM-ADR5000, MDA-MB-231/BCRP, HCT116 p53+/−, U87MG/ΔEGFR, HepG2/AML12, NCI 60 cell lines
metric: IC50 (μM), degree of resistance (fold), Glide docking score
result: Oridonin P-gp resistance 5.17-fold vs doxorubicin 975.60-fold; IC50 1.65 μM (CCRF-CEM); ΔEGFR resistance 0.88-fold

problem: Improving plasma half-life and fibrin affinity of tissue plasminogen activator (t-PA) for thrombolytic therapy by designing a novel chimeric truncated form.
method: In silico modeling (MODELLER 9v1, Swiss-PDB, GROMOS96 energy minimization, Ramachandran analysis), SOEing-PCR gene construction, CHO DG44 cell expression, amidolytic and fibrin binding assays.
finding: Novel chimeric GHRP-SYQ-K2S truncated t-PA successfully expressed in CHO cells, preserving thrombolytic activity with 86% fibrin binding of full-length t-PA and favorable structural integrity.
dataset: CHO DG44 cell line, recombinant t-PA (PDB: 1tpk, 1bda, 1fzv, 2flh)
metric: Expression level (IU/ml), specific activity (IU/mg), fibrin binding (%), RMSD
result: 752 IU/ml expression, 566,917 IU/mg specific activity, 86% fibrin binding, RMSD 0.53 (serine protease domain)

problem: Building a comprehensive database of miRNA:lncRNA interactions to understand the sponge/decoy regulatory function of lncRNAs in the miRNA interactome.
method: Semi-automated text mining of publications, in-house analysis of 153 AGO CLIP-Seq libraries (PAR-CLIP, HITS-CLIP), DIANA-microT algorithm for in silico target prediction, and RNA-Seq expression analysis.
finding: LncBase v2 provides >70,000 experimentally supported miRNA:lncRNA interactions (14-fold increase over v1), covering 66 cell types across 36 tissues; identified ~400 viral-miRNA:lncRNA pairs and tissue-specific regulation patterns.
dataset: 153 AGO CLIP-Seq libraries, >6 billion RNA-Seq reads, human and mouse transcriptomes
metric: Number of interactions, MRE conservation scores, binding affinity scores
result: 14-fold increase in experimental interactions, >70,000 supported interactions, ~12,900 lncRNA transcripts with MREs

problem: Understanding the dual protective and pathogenic roles of glutathione (GSH) in cancer initiation, progression, and treatment resistance.
method: Comprehensive literature review of GSH mechanisms in redox regulation, signal transduction, and therapeutic approaches including direct/indirect GSH targeting and GSH-based prodrugs.
finding: Elevated GSH in tumor cells drives progression and drug resistance; novel therapies targeting the GSH antioxidant system show promise; computational methods enable in silico testing of GSH-targeted treatments.

problem: Designing novel imidazol-5-one derivatives with improved cytotoxicity against HCT116 colorectal cancer cells and potent CDK2 inhibition.
method: QSAR modeling via genetic function algorithm with DFT-optimized descriptors, ligand-based drug design, and AutoDock Vina molecular docking to CDK2 (PDB: 6GUE).
finding: Four designed compounds (e, h, j, l) showed significantly better cytotoxicity than template with IC50 < 5.0 μM and better CDK2 inhibition than standard drugs sorafenib and kenpaullone.
dataset: 36 imidazole-5-ones, HCT116 colorectal cancer cell line, CDK2 (PDB: 6GUE)
metric: R², R²adj, Q²cv, R²ext, IC50 (μM), binding affinity (kcal/mol), VIF
result: QSAR R²=0.7397, Q²cv=0.5547, R²ext=0.7202; binding affinity −10.8 to −11.0 kcal/mol; IC50 <5.0 μM

problem: Developing robust, efficient bioprocesses for mass production of human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs) in stirred tank bioreactors for therapeutic and industrial applications.
method: In silico process modeling with computable upscaling, fully controlled hPSC expansion in stirred tank bioreactors with perfusion feeding strategy and optimized E8 medium formulations.
finding: Protocol enables high-density hPSC culture at 35×10⁶ cells/mL while reducing culture medium consumption by 75%, providing a systematic blueprint for scalable hPSC bioprocessing.
dataset: Human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs) in stirred tank bioreactors
metric: Cell density (cells/mL), medium consumption reduction (%)
result: 35×10⁶ cells/mL density, 75% medium reduction

problem: Quantifying the HLA-specific alloreactivity potential from minor histocompatibility antigens (mHA) in stem-cell transplant donor-recipient pairs using whole exome sequencing.
method: Whole exome sequencing of 9 SCT donor-recipient pairs, ANNOVAR-based nonameric peptide generation from nsSNPs, and HLA class I binding prediction using IEDB SMM and NetMHCpan algorithms.
finding: A very large library of potentially immunogenic peptides exists even in HLA-matched pairs; unrelated donor pairs have significantly more presented peptides than matched related donors, explaining higher GVHD incidence.
dataset: 9 SCT donor-recipient pairs (4 MRD, 5 URD), whole exome sequences
metric: Peptide binding affinity (IC50 nM), number of presented peptides, Mann-Whitney U test p-value
baseline: MRD vs URD comparison, NCBI dbSNP reference
result: Median 18,396 presented peptides (IC50<500nM), 2,254 strongly presented (IC50<50nM); URD>MRD p=0.016

problem: Campylobacter jejuni causes bacterial diarrheal illness worldwide with limited vaccine options. This study aims to design epitope-based peptide vaccines by targeting invasive, virulent and membrane-associated proteins.
method: Immunoinformatics approaches including Bepipred, ABCpred, IEDB tools for B- and T-cell epitope prediction, VaxiJen for antigenicity, TMHMM for transmembrane topology, and AutoDock Vina for in silico docking to HLA alleles.
finding: Identified B-cell epitopes (EINKN, TGSRLN, KSNPDI, LDENGCE) and T-cell epitopes from six C. jejuni proteins showing 80-100% conservancy across strains with 22-60% population coverage, validated by molecular docking to HLA alleles.
dataset: C. jejuni strain 11168 proteins (FlaA, CadF, Cia, PEB1, PEB3, MOMP) from UniProt
metric: VaxiJen antigenicity score, IC50 binding affinity, epitope conservancy %, population coverage %
baseline: VaxiJen threshold >0.5, IC50 <100 nM, Bepipred threshold 0.35, ABCpred cutoff 0.51
result: 80-100% epitope conservancy, 22-60% population coverage, VaxiJen scores 0.69-0.88

problem: Rapid generation of serological diagnostics for COVID-19 requires identifying SARS-CoV-2-specific B-cell epitopes that do not cross-react with endemic human coronaviruses.
method: Systematic computational survey of 27 SARS-CoV-2 proteins using BepiPred2 for linear epitope prediction, DiscoTope2 for conformational epitopes, sequence conservation analysis against endemic HCoVs, validated by ELISA with patient sera.
finding: N-terminal nucleoprotein fragment and spike RBD showed highest sensitivity and specificity for COVID-19 serodiagnosis. Mixing both antigens for ELISA coating achieved 94% sensitivity and 97.2% specificity.
dataset: 80 RT-PCR confirmed SARS-CoV-2 patient samples, 106 control samples, 2,234 endemic HCoV protein sequences
metric: sensitivity, specificity, signal-to-noise ratio
baseline: BepiPred2 threshold (80% specificity/30% sensitivity), endemic HCoV cross-reactivity
result: sensitivity 94% (N=80), specificity 97.2% (N=106)

problem: Global identification of APC/C ubiquitin ligase substrates has remained challenging despite the enzyme's critical role in cell cycle regulation.
method: In silico substrate prediction by cross-referencing KEN-box motif-containing proteins (2,206) with cell cycle-regulated mRNAs (651 genes from 4 independent studies), followed by experimental validation with FLAG-Cdh1 immunoprecipitation, mass spectrometry, and cell cycle degradation assays.
finding: Identified 145 putative APC/C substrates enriched for chromatin regulators. UHRF1 is a direct APC/C substrate whose degradation at mitotic exit regulates G1 progression and global DNA methylation patterning.
dataset: Human proteome (HeLa, U2OS, RPE-1, HEK-293T, HCT116 cells), 4 cell cycle transcriptional studies
metric: enrichment over random expectation, KEN-motif overlap, protein oscillation during cell cycle
baseline: 33 well-validated KEN-containing APC/C substrates, FLAG-IP mass spectrometry (15/53 known substrates recovered)
result: 145 putative substrates identified, 24/33 known substrates captured (73%), chromatin regulators enriched

problem: Understanding how spatial organization of membrane-less organelles (nuclear speckles) and organelle heterogeneity affect RNA splicing particle biogenesis in mammalian cells.
method: Data-driven spatially-resolved HeLa cell model with 11 compartment types, chemical reaction networks for spliceosomal assembly, stochastic reaction-diffusion simulations using Lattice Microbes (LM v2.3) for up to 15 minutes of biological time.
finding: Nuclear pore complex number directly affects splicing particle formation; compartmentalization is critical for correct particle assembly yield; slight increase in splicing particle localization to speckles disproportionately enhances mRNA splicing and reduces noise; gene-speckle distance significantly affects mRNA production rate.
dataset: HeLa cell structural data (volume 3000 μm³, nuclear radii 3.7-5.3 μm), 20 nuclear speckles, 4 Cajal bodies
metric: mRNA production rate, splicing particle count, mRNA noise (coefficient of variation)
baseline: Well-mixed (non-spatial) model, varying nuclear pore complex numbers and speckle configurations
result: 15 min biological time simulated (longest for eukaryotic cell), genes closer to speckles produce mRNA at higher levels

problem: The biochemical interactions underlying host-microbiome behavior regulation are poorly understood. This study explores inter-kingdom ecological relationships using an in silico model.
method: Analytical modeling and computational simulation of engineered gene networks (toggle switches, repressilators) in a commensal microbiome interfaced with a biomimetic robotic host, implemented in MATLAB/Simulink using ode5 (Dormand-Prince/Runge-Kutta) integration.
finding: Robot movements depended on programmed biochemical network dynamics within the microbiome; balanced toggle switch produced bistable depot-seeking behavior; RBS strength served as a tunable parameter for modifying host nutrient affinity; host-to-microbiome feedback enabled predation-like behavior.
dataset: Simulated 20m × 20m 2D arena with carbon depots (lactose/arabinose), engineered gene circuits (toggle switch, repressilator)
metric: behavioral trajectory, bistability maintenance, GFP/mCherry expression levels
baseline: Balanced toggle switch as proof-of-concept, varied RBS strengths
result: average runtime 2.78 min per simulation, bistability maintained under stochastic conditions

problem: CD44 is a cancer stem cell marker with pleiotropic roles in tumorigenesis, but its differential expression and prognostic value in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) require investigation.
method: In silico analysis using TCGA-HNSCC dataset (520 tumors, 44 normals) via UALCAN portal for mRNA expression and CPTAC dataset (108 tumors, 71 normals) for protein expression, correlated with clinicopathological parameters and Kaplan-Meier survival analysis.
finding: CD44 mRNA and protein expression were significantly upregulated in HNSCC vs normal tissues. High CD44 correlated with poor survival, was overexpressed in HPV-negative and TP53-mutant tumors, and upregulated in Stage 1 and Grade 2 HNSCC.
dataset: TCGA-HNSCC (520 primary HNSCC, 44 normal), CPTAC (108 HNSCC, 71 normal)
metric: median transcript level, p-value, overall survival (Kaplan-Meier)
baseline: normal tissue controls (mRNA median 148.7, protein median -1.3)

problem: Linear B-cell epitopes within P. vivax malaria vaccine candidate PvMSP9 as targets of functional antibodies remain undefined, limiting synthetic peptide vaccine development.
method: In silico epitope prediction using BepiPred (cutoff 0.9), IUPred for disorder prediction, ANCHOR for binding region prediction, and Robetta for 3D modeling, validated by seroepidemiological assessment with 545 naturally exposed individuals using ELISA.
finding: The tandem repeat EAAPENAEPVHENA (PvMSP9 E795-A808) was identified as a highly immunogenic linear B-cell epitope in a disordered binding region. 56% of PvMSP9-RIRII responders also recognized the synthetic peptide; IgG1 prevalence correlated with time since last malaria episode.
dataset: 545 naturally P. vivax-exposed individuals from malaria-endemic regions of western Brazil, PvMSP9 Belem strain (AAL78897.1)
metric: BepiPred prediction score, IUPred disorder score, ANCHOR binding probability, IgG reactivity index, seroprevalence %
result: 58% responders to RIRII (316/545), 56% cross-reactive to peptide (177/316), epitope BepiPred score 1.59, disorder 0.97

problem: Agricultural crop residue cell walls are highly resistant to saccharification due to recalcitrant polysaccharides and variable linkage structures, limiting second-generation biofuel production efficiency.
method: Review of complementary glycomic methods (chromatography, NMR, antibody glycome profiling) for cell wall characterization combined with -omics-based approaches (metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, metaproteomics) for CAZyme discovery and functional prediction, including metagenome-assembled genomes.
finding: Combining high-resolution cell wall structural analysis with meta-omic characterization of lignocellulose-metabolizing microbial communities provides promising strategies to maximize biofuel yield from crop waste. Tailored approaches for individual plant sources and novel CAZyme deployment can improve bioconversion.
dataset: Agricultural crop residues (review of multiple plant sources)

problem: miR-21 is the most abundant miRNA in colorectal cancer but its role in drug resistance mechanisms, particularly to topoisomerase inhibitors, is poorly characterized.
method: Stable miR-21-overexpressing DLD-1 CRC cell line with MTT viability assays, Western blot, qPCR, Kaplan-Meier survival analysis (TCGA-COAD), and bioinformatics gene signature analysis (Connectivity Map/CMap, GSEA).
finding: miR-21 overexpression induced resistance to topoisomerase inhibitors (SN-38, doxorubicin, VP-16) by reducing apoptosis and enhancing pro-survival autophagy without altering topoisomerase expression. Bioinformatics revealed miR-21 induced gene reprogramming mimicking topoisomerase inhibitor signatures and downregulated proteasome pathway genes.
dataset: DLD-1 human CRC cell line, TCGA-COAD cohort (181 high/180 low miR-21 expression)
metric: IC50, cell viability %, hazard ratio, metastasis-free survival p-value
result: miR-21 high expression: poor metastasis-free survival (p<0.05), resistance to SN-38/doxorubicin/VP-16 but not 5-FU

problem: Effective therapeutics targeting SARS-CoV-2 cell entry proteins (ACE2, TMPRSS2, spike protein) are needed to inhibit the first stage of COVID-19 infection.
method: Molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) of 106 bioactive terpenoids from African medicinal plants against hACE2, TMPRSS2, and spike proteins, followed by ADMET prediction, 100 ns molecular dynamics simulation, and MM-GBSA binding free energy calculations.
finding: Eight terpenoids showed high binding to catalytic residues: 24-methylene cycloartenol and isoiguesterin targeted ACE2 hotspot 31, abietane diterpenes bound TMPRSS2 S1-specificity pocket, 3-benzoylhosloppone bound spike RBD, and cucurbitacin B bound S2 subunit. Interactions were stable in MD simulations with favorable drug-likeness.
dataset: 106 bioactive terpenoids from African medicinal plants, PDB structures (1R42, 2OQ5, 6VW1, 6VSB, 5X5B, 5X5C)
metric: binding energy (kcal/mol), MM-GBSA binding free energy, RMSD, drug-likeness descriptors
baseline: MLN-4760 (-7.7 kcal/mol for ACE2), camostat (-7.6 kcal/mol for TMPRSS2), nelfinavir mesylates
result: >10 terpenoids exceeded reference inhibitor binding affinities, interactions preserved in 100 ns MD simulations

problem: Oncogenic miRNA-21 is upregulated in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), and targeted delivery of siRNA to inhibit miRNA-21 in EGFR-overexpressing TNBC cells remains a challenge.
method: In-silico molecular docking (Autodock Vina, I-TASSER) for siRNA-miRNA-21 and GE11-EGFR binding, combined with in vitro studies (MTT, flow cytometry, qPCR, western blot) on chitosan nanoparticle formulations.
finding: GE11-siRNA-CSNPs showed enhanced cytotoxicity against TNBC cells, significantly inhibited miRNA-21 expression, cell migration, and colony formation, and induced apoptosis by blocking the PI3K/AKT and ERK1/2 signaling axis.
dataset: MDA-MB-231 (TNBC) and MCF7 breast cancer cell lines
metric: Binding energy (kcal/mol), cell viability %, gene expression levels, particle size/PDI/zeta potential
baseline: Untreated cells, naked siRNA-CSNPs (without GE11 peptide conjugation)
result: siRNA-miRNA-21 binding energy −18.98 kcal/mol, GE11-EGFR binding energy −52.62 kcal/mol, nanoparticle size 122 nm

problem: Experimentally validated miRNA:gene interactions are fragmented across thousands of manuscripts and raw NGS datasets, making comprehensive access difficult for researchers.
method: Text-mining pipeline for article selection combined with manual curation of published experiments and analysis of >150 raw NGS datasets (HITS-CLIP, PAR-CLIP, CLASH).
finding: DIANA-TarBase v7.0 indexed over half a million experimentally validated miRNA:gene interactions across 24 species and 356 cell types, achieving 9- to 250-fold more entries than any other relevant database.
dataset: MedLine/PubMed Central articles, 150+ raw NGS datasets, 24 species, 356 cell types
metric: Number of curated interactions, species coverage, cell type coverage
baseline: DIANA-TarBase v6.0 and other miRNA target databases (miRTarBase, miRecords, StarBase)
result: >500,000 interactions, 9-250x more entries than competing databases

problem: During noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT), cell-free fetal DNA fraction can only be accurately quantified from Y-chromosomal reads for male fetuses, requiring advanced methods for gender-independent prediction.
method: PREFACE combines principal component analysis with neural networks to model copy number profiles from shallow whole-genome sequencing (sWGS) data, enabling semi-supervised training with both male and unlabeled female samples.
finding: PREFACE achieved Pearson r=0.94 between predicted and Y-based fetal fractions using the full cohort; the method can also predict mixed multiple pregnancies and sex chromosomal aneuploidies.
dataset: NIPT cohort (n_female=2468, n_male=2723, total 5572 samples) from Center for Medical Genetics Ghent
metric: Pearson correlation, mean absolute error (MAE)
baseline: Elastic net (supervised), SeqFF, FFY (Y-chromosomal read count-based fraction)
result: Pearson r=0.94 (full cohort), r=0.9 (~1100 males only), superior to elastic net

problem: No vaccine exists for Chlamydia trachomatis despite it being the most common bacterial STI globally; the conserved PmpD protein is a promising immunogen but its large size and complex structure hinder recombinant approaches.
method: In silico epitope prediction using NetMHC/NetMHCII (T-cell), BepiPred (linear B-cell), RaptorX (3D homology modeling), and DiscoTope (conformational B-cell epitopes).
finding: Six regions containing overlapping B-cell and T-cell epitopes were identified by multiple predictors, representing valuable candidates for subunit vaccine design against C. trachomatis.
dataset: C. trachomatis serovar L2 PmpD amino acid sequence (Genbank AAK69391.2, 1530 residues)
metric: Binding affinity (strong/weak), P-value, uGDT, Ramachandran plot quality
result: 70 strong MHC-I epitopes, 742 strong MHC-II epitopes, 24 linear B-cell epitopes, 91% structure modeled, 6 multi-epitope consensus regions

problem: Gene expression profiling of bulk breast tumors conflates mRNA from tumor epithelial and stromal cells, making it difficult to ascribe expression differences to specific cell types for metastasis prediction.
method: Weighted t-statistics using histopathological area fraction estimates of tumor and stromal components from H&E-stained sections of 198 fresh-frozen breast tumors, validated against laser capture microdissected tissue.
finding: Gene expression differences between distant metastasis and no-metastasis groups were mostly ascribed to tumor epithelial cells; PIP5K2A was specifically elevated in stroma of the metastasis group, confirmed in microdissected tissue.
dataset: 198 fresh-frozen breast tumors (DBCG82b&c cohort: 118 DM, 80 NoDM)
metric: FDR (5%), p-value, Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
result: 182 DEGs (unweighted), 30 gene-probes ascribed solely to carcinoma cells, 38 mostly tumor-associated, 5 stromal-associated

problem: Metastatic breast cancer remains a leading cause of death in women, necessitating the discovery of effective natural-product-based cytotoxic and anti-migration agents.
method: Ethanol/water extraction, MTT assay for cytotoxicity, scratch wound healing assay for migration inhibition, UHPLC-MS phytochemical profiling, and in silico molecular docking against Bcl-2, Bcl-XL, ERK2, and FAK.
finding: Ethanol extract of K. parviflora (EEKP) showed dose-dependent cytotoxicity on 4T1 and MCF-7 cells with no toxicity on NIH-3T3; HTMF and denbinobin were identified as responsible bioactive compounds via docking.
dataset: 4T1 (metastatic breast cancer), MCF-7, NIH-3T3 (non-cancerous) cell lines
metric: IC50 (µg/mL), cell viability %, binding energy (kcal/mol)
result: IC50 128.33 µg/mL (24h) and 115.09 µg/mL (48h) on 4T1, 138.43/124.81 µg/mL on MCF-7, no cytotoxicity on NIH-3T3 up to 250 µg/mL

problem: Amyloid-β misfolds into toxic oligomers causing neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease, and preventing toxic oligomer formation is a potential therapeutic strategy.
method: Molecular dynamics simulations for inhibitor design and optimization, single molecule force spectroscopy (SMFS) for binding validation, and MTT cell viability assays on HT22 mouse hippocampal cells.
finding: SGA1 and SGA3 (anti-parallel binding pseudopeptides) demonstrated neuroprotection against Aβ(1-42) oligomers, while SGC1 (parallel binding) was not protective; myristoylated variants intended for BBB crossing were cytotoxic.
dataset: HT22 mouse hippocampal cells, Aβ(1-42) oligomers
metric: Cell viability %, IC50, one-way ANOVA with Dunnett's test
baseline: Aβ(1-42) oligomer-treated cells (50±5% viability), untreated control (100%)
result: SGA1 improved viability to 65±6%, SGA3 to 68±3% (vs 50±5% Aβ alone, p<0.05/0.01), Myr-SGA IC50 ~7.5 µM (cytotoxic)

problem: In silico prediction of discontinuous B-cell epitopes has shown moderate performance, partly due to improper benchmark definitions that create artificial false positives from incomplete epitope annotations.
method: DiscoTope-2.0 with novel spatial neighborhood scoring function and half-sphere exposure (HSE) as surface measure, evaluated with proper benchmarks incorporating biological unit and multiple epitope information.
finding: Proper benchmark definitions significantly improved reported prediction performance; DiscoTope-2.0 outperformed other state-of-the-art methods on both cross-validation and independent evaluation datasets.
dataset: 75 antigen structures (DiscoTope training set), 52 independent evaluation structures from IEDB-3D (clustered into 33 homology groups)
metric: AUC
baseline: DiscoTope-1.2, PEPITO, ElliPro, SEPPA, Epitopia, EPCES, EPSVR
result: AUC 0.748 (cross-validation), improved to 0.824 with proper benchmarks; significantly better than Epitopia (p=0.033) and EPSVR (p=0.006)

problem: Growing cancer incidence and resistance to conventional anti-cancer drugs necessitate the discovery of new chemotherapeutic agents from natural sources, particularly Amaryllidaceae alkaloids.
method: In vitro cytotoxicity (MTT assay) of 10 alkaloid fractions against 6 cancer cell lines, molecular docking against Bcl-2 family (Mcl-1, Bcl-xL) and HK2, and micropropagation of endangered species via tissue culture.
finding: C. jagus and E. bonplandii showed strongest activity on AGS gastric cancer cells; lycorine and haemanthamine were the most active pure alkaloids; powelline and buphanidrine identified as promising candidates via combined in vitro/in silico analysis.
dataset: AGS, BT-549, HEC-1B, MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, PC3 cancer cell lines, HaCat control; 10 Amaryllidaceae species
metric: Cell viability %, IC50 (µg/mL and µM), binding energy
baseline: Doxorubicin (positive control, IC50 5.73±0.80 µg/mL on AGS)
result: Lycorine IC50 4.17±0.18 µg/mL (14.51 µM), haemanthamine IC50 13.18±0.47 µg/mL (43.74 µM) on AGS; E. bonplandii 45.79±3.05% viability at 30 µg/mL

problem: Vernonia amygdalina is used in traditional African medicine as an antidiabetic remedy, but the specific roles of its bioactive compounds luteolin and vernodalol in antidiabetic and anticancer activity are unclear.
method: HPLC quantification of luteolin and vernodalol in leaf/root extracts, α-glucosidase inhibition assay, BSA glycation assay, ORAC antioxidant assay, MTT cell viability on HT-29 cells, and in silico ADMET prediction.
finding: Luteolin (prevalent in leaves) is responsible for antioxidant and antidiabetic effects, while vernodalol (prevalent in roots) drives antiproliferative activity; both compounds have suitable ADMET profiles as drug candidates.
dataset: HT-29 colon cancer cells, Vernonia amygdalina leaf and root extracts (6 extract types)
metric: IC50 (µM), ORAC (µmol TE/g), α-glucosidase inhibition %, cell viability %
result: Luteolin IC50 22.2 µM, vernodalol IC50 5.7 µM on HT-29; luteolin in EthLE 0.54±0.04 mg/g, vernodalol in EthRE 336.79±15.60 mg/g

problem: Understanding how benzothiadiazine drug derivatives interact with phospholipid cell membranes to inform drug design for hypertension, hypoglycemia, and HIV treatments.
method: Microsecond all-atom molecular dynamics simulations using CHARMM36m force field with DOPC/DOPS/cholesterol bilayer models constructed via CHARMM-GUI; four in silico-designed DBD derivatives with electron-donating and electron-accepting substituents.
finding: All benzothiadiazine derivatives show strong affinity for the membrane interface and form single/double hydrogen bonds (1.6–2.1 Å) with lipids and cholesterol, though solvation characteristics vary significantly between derivatives (fully water-solvated vs. continuously membrane-attached for 10–70 ns).
dataset: DOPC/DOPS-K/cholesterol bilayer model (112 DOPC, 28 DOPS, 60 cholesterol, 10,000 water molecules), 5 DBD derivative systems
metric: area per lipid, membrane thickness, radial distribution functions, hydrogen bond length and occupancy
baseline: previously simulated DBD1 reference; experimental values from Nagle et al.
result: A=52.2 Å² (exp 54.4 Å²), Δz≈43.0 Å (exp 40 Å), H-bond lengths 1.6–2.1 Å, <5% fluctuation in membrane properties

problem: Lack of standardized terminology and systematic criteria for interpreting sequence variants identified through increasingly complex clinical genetic testing including NGS panels, exomes, and genomes.
method: Expert workgroup consensus (ACMG/AMP/CAP) with two rounds of surveys to >100 sequencing laboratories, open forums, workshops, and iterative testing of classification criteria against known variants.
finding: Established a 5-tier classification system (pathogenic, likely pathogenic, uncertain significance, likely benign, benign) with 28 evidence-based criteria combining population, computational, functional, and segregation data for Mendelian disorder variant interpretation.
dataset: surveys from >45 laboratories in North America, protocols from 11 labs
result: 5-tier system with 28 criteria adopted by majority of surveyed labs; 'likely pathogenic/benign' defined as >90% certainty

problem: Drug-tolerant CD44v9-positive cancer cell subpopulations in gastric tumors cause early relapse after cytotoxic chemotherapy, and no established treatment strategy exists to eliminate them.
method: Gene expression signature-based in silico screening using JFCR_LinCAGE anticancer compound database on patient-derived gastric cancer cells, validated in BALB/c-nu/nu mouse xenograft models with flow cytometry, MTT assays, and Western blotting.
finding: EGFR inhibitors (afatinib, erlotinib) selectively target CD44v9-positive cells at clinically relevant concentrations by downregulating AKT/ERK pathways, significantly reducing residual cancer cells and enhancing irinotecan's antitumor effect in vivo.
dataset: gastric cancer patient-derived cells (JSC15-3, JSC17-2, JSC17-7), 66 clinical tissue microarray samples, BALB/c-nu/nu xenograft
metric: cell viability (MTT IC50), CD44v9+ fraction (flow cytometry), colony formation, tumor volume
baseline: SN-38 or 5-FU treatment alone, DMSO control
result: CD44v9 in 34.8% of gastric cancers (66 cases), EGFR inhibitors significantly reduced residual cells after cytotoxic treatment

problem: Bacterial biofilm formation on food matrices and processing equipment causes persistent contamination, and traditional sanitizers have inadequate efficacy against biofilms.
method: Agar well diffusion, MIC/MBC determination, biofilm disruption assays (crystal violet, XTT, LDH), light/scanning electron microscopy, and molecular docking of identified phytochemicals against bacterial adhesion proteins (Sortase A, EspA, OprD, type IV b pilin).
finding: E. sativa ethanolic crude extract effectively inhibits planktonic growth and disrupts established biofilms of all tested food-borne pathogens; molecular docking suggests the mechanism involves targeting cell adhesion proteins.
dataset: S. aureus MTCC 96, E. coli MTCC 9537, P. aeruginosa MTCC 741, S. enterica ser. typhi MTCC 8767
metric: zone of inhibition, MIC, MBC, biofilm inhibition %, EPS reduction %, XTT cell viability
baseline: chloramphenicol (1 mg/mL positive control), 10% DMSO (negative control)
result: MIC 125–500 µg/mL, MBC 250–1000 µg/mL, biofilm inhibition 58.68–73.45%, EPS reduction 59.73–82.77%

problem: Need for rationally designed dual kinase inhibitors that simultaneously target angiogenesis (VEGFR2) and cell cycle progression (CDK1) for anti-cancer therapy.
method: In silico molecular modeling (SPROUT, Glide) for dual-target compound design, validated by recombinant kinase assays ([γ-³³P]-ATP), endothelial-fibroblast co-culture angiogenesis model, and murine ex vivo aortic ring assay.
finding: JK-31 potently inhibits both VEGFR2 and CDK1, blocks VEGF-A-mediated signaling and mitotic entry, displays anti-angiogenic activity in co-culture and ex vivo models, and reduces growth of endothelial and MCF-7 breast cancer cells.
dataset: human primary endothelial cells, MCF-7 breast cancer cells, murine aortic ring ex vivo model
metric: IC50, predicted pKi (binding affinity), cell proliferation, kinase inhibition %
baseline: vatalanib, sunitinib (VEGFR2 inhibitors), AT7519, JNJ-7706621 (CDK1 inhibitors), staurosporine
result: predicted pKi ≤ -7 (nanomolar affinity) for both targets, greater VEGFR2 than CDK1 potency, greater CDK1 inhibition than AT7519

problem: Molecular mechanisms of caffeine's anticancer effects on melanoma are not fully elucidated, particularly regarding effects at defined differentiation levels of cancer cells.
method: In silico screening of caffeine molecular targets in GEO GDS1375 melanoma dataset (ROC analysis, cross-validation via IST Online and GEPIA2), followed by in vitro proliferation, melanin production, and cytokine profiling on BRAF-mutated melanoma-initiating cells (MICs).
finding: Tyrosinase (TYR) is a key mediator of caffeine's action on melanoma; caffeine promotes melanin production and reduces secretion of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IP-10, MIP-1α, MIP-1β, RANTES) in MICs. Low TYR expression correlates with poor prognosis in BRAF-mutated melanoma.
dataset: GEO GDS1375 (45 primary melanomas, 18 nevi), BRAF-mutated MICs (Mel1, Mel3)
metric: AUC-ROC, cell count, melanin content, cytokine concentration (Luminex), overall survival (Kaplan-Meier)
result: 8 genes with AUC≥0.85 (TYR AUC=0.94, PARP1 AUC=0.98), low TYR significantly associated with unfavorable prognosis in BRAF-mutated melanoma

problem: Need for effective natural PTP1B and α-glucosidase dual inhibitors for T2DM treatment, as existing synthetic inhibitors have dose-dependent side effects and have failed clinical trials.
method: Enzyme inhibition assays (PTP1B with p-NPP, α-glucosidase with p-NPG), Lineweaver-Burk and Dixon plot kinetic analysis, molecular docking (AutoDock 4.2), and glucose uptake measurement (2-NBDG) in insulin-resistant HepG2 cells.
finding: Mulberrofuran G, albanol B, and kuwanon G from M. alba root bark show potent mixed-type dual inhibition of PTP1B and α-glucosidase, enhance glucose uptake, and decrease PTP1B expression dose-dependently in insulin-resistant HepG2 cells.
dataset: insulin-resistant HepG2 cells, recombinant human PTP1B, α-glucosidase
metric: IC50, Ki, binding energy (kcal/mol), glucose uptake (2-NBDG fluorescence), PTP1B expression (Western blot)
baseline: ursolic acid (PTP1B IC50 3.54 µM), acarbose (α-glucosidase IC50 119.16 µM), rosiglitazone
result: PTP1B IC50: 0.57 µM (mulberrofuran G), 0.80 µM (albanol B), 2.26 µM (kuwanon G); α-glucosidase IC50: 1.31–2.35 µM; binding energy -8.24 kcal/mol (allosteric PTP1B)

problem: Whipple's disease caused by T. whipplei is rare but fatal, and current treatments (hydroxychloroquine/doxycycline for 12–18 months) are ineffective with lifetime follow-up required; no vaccine exists.
method: Immuno-informatics pipeline: allergenicity screening (AllergenFP), T-cell epitope prediction (NetMHCII PAN 3.2), antigenicity scoring (VaxiJen ≥0.7), 3D modeling (PEP-FOLD 3), molecular docking (PatchDock), and molecular dynamics simulation.
finding: Two epitopes predicted — VLMVSAFPL (from RNA polymerase β subunit, 4 HLA DRB1 alleles) and IRYLAALHL (from membrane protein insertase YidC, 6 HLA DRB1 alleles) — with satisfactory docking interactions and population coverage.
dataset: T. whipplei proteome (5 proteins from NCBI/UniProtKB)
metric: atomic contact energy (ACE), binding score, RMSD, VaxiJen antigenicity score, 1-log50k affinity, population coverage
baseline: reference HLA-peptide crystal structures from RCSB-PDB (4AH2, 4IS6)
result: RMSD 0–1.5 Å, VaxiJen ≥0.94 for both epitopes, ACE -443.3 kcal/mol, satisfactory world population coverage

problem: Need to characterize B and T cell epitopes of Der f 25, a major allergen from house dust mite D. farinae, to support allergen immunotherapy development.
method: Bioinformatics-based epitope prediction using DNAStar, BPAP, BepiPred 1.0 (B cell), NetMHCIIpan-3.0 and NetMHCII-2.2 (T cell), with homology modeling (SWISS-MODEL) and structural validation (Ramachandran, ERRAT, VERIFY 3D, ProSA).
finding: Eight B cell epitopes and five T cell epitopes were predicted for Der f 25, which belongs to the triosephosphate isomerase family with a TPI pattern (PS001371). The homology model was validated as high quality.
dataset: Der f 25 protein from D. farinae (NCBI KC305500.1), template T. molitor TPI (PDB: 2I9E, 74% identity)
metric: Ramachandran plot (% favored), ERRAT quality factor, VERIFY 3D, ProSA Z-score, RMSD
result: 93% Ramachandran favored, ERRAT 97.034, 8 B cell + 5 T cell epitopes predicted

problem: mRNA levels predict no more than 40% of protein abundance in human cells, and the contribution of translation efficiency to this discrepancy is poorly characterized across cell lines.
method: Bioinformatic integration of genome-wide mRNA (NGS/FPKM via tophat/cufflinks) and protein (mass spectrometry/IBAQ) levels with protein stability data (pulsed SILAC from HeLa) across five human cell lines to estimate translation efficiency.
finding: Estimated translation efficiency shows considerable consistency between cell lines for many genes; deviations are attributable to regulated protein degradation or known translational control mechanisms, suggesting improved models for interpreting mRNA expression data are feasible.
dataset: 5 human cell lines (MCF-7, HeLa, A-431, U-2 OS, U-251 MG), 8,170 genes, 24,920 mRNA-protein pairs
metric: Spearman correlation, R², translation efficiency (proteins/mRNA/h)
result: mRNA predicts 24–34% (R²) of protein levels, TE estimated for 3,990 genes, median ~43.6 proteins/mRNA/h

problem: Most known MMP9 inhibitors have failed in clinical trials for metastatic cancer. This study aims to identify natural plant-derived inhibitors targeting the hemopexin-like domain of MMP9 (PEX9) as anti-breast cancer agents.
method: In silico virtual screening of 200 natural compounds via AutoDock Vina molecular docking against PEX9, followed by bioguided fractionation with FRET-based MMP9 inhibition assay and MTT cytotoxicity assay on breast cancer cell lines.
finding: Six of eight plant crude extracts inhibited MMP9; Ageratum conyzoides and Ixora coccinea fractions showed strong MMP9 inhibition, and Ageratum crude extract demonstrated potent anti-proliferative activity against 4T1 breast cancer cells with a high safety index.
dataset: 200 natural compounds from Indonesian herbal databases; 8 local plant extracts; 4T1 and T47D breast cancer cell lines; HFL1 noncancerous cells
metric: binding affinity (kcal/mol), IC50, inhibition %, True Positive Rate (TPR), RMSD, safety index
baseline: NNGH (hydroxamate MMP9 inhibitor) as positive control; sulfate ion native ligand for control docking; doxorubicin for cytotoxicity
result: Top hits binding affinity −11.2 to −8.1 kcal/mol; Ageratum fractions IC50=43 µg/mL (MMP9), IC50=22 µg/mL (4T1); TPR=65%; RMSD=1.99 Å

problem: Lignin accumulation in pear fruit drives stone cell formation that negatively impacts fruit quality. The role of the RBOH gene family, key enzymes in ROS metabolism required for lignin polymerization, is unknown in most fruit trees.
method: Genome-wide in silico identification of RBOH genes across five fruit tree genomes using BLASTp, followed by phylogenetic analysis, chromosomal mapping, cis-acting element analysis, qRT-PCR expression profiling, and subcellular localization in pear fruit.
finding: 40 RBOHs were identified across five fruit trees; PbRBOHA and PbRBOHD are plasma membrane-localized candidate genes whose expression correlates with stone cell content during pear fruit development, implicating them in ROS-mediated lignification.
dataset: Genomes of Pyrus bretschneideri, Prunus persica, Citrus sinensis, Vitis vinifera, Prunus mume; pear fruit developmental stages (15-145 DAF)
metric: transcript abundance (qRT-PCR), apoplastic O2.− content, phylogenetic bootstrap (1000 replications)
baseline: Arabidopsis AtRBOH family (10 members) as query reference; known lignification-related RBOHs for phylogenetic comparison
result: 10 PbRBOHs on 8 chromosomes; 3 PbRBOHs closely related to lignification RBOHs; PbRBOHA/PbRBOHD expression consistent with stone cell content changes

problem: P-glycoprotein (P-gp) overexpression causes multidrug resistance in cancer, and prior P-gp inhibitors have failed clinically because they are themselves transport substrates of the pump.
method: Ultrahigh throughput in silico screening for drug-like molecules targeting P-gp nucleotide-binding domains (not drug-binding sites), followed by in vitro MTT cell viability assays on paired DU145/DU145TXR prostate cancer cells and noncancerous HFL1 cells.
finding: Three of four computationally identified P-gp inhibitors reversed multidrug resistance in DU145TXR cells, restoring paclitaxel sensitivity comparable to naive cells at low potentiation concentrations without significant toxicity to noncancerous cells.
dataset: DU145 (naïve) and DU145TXR (MDR, P-gp overexpressing) human prostate cancer cells; HFL1 noncancerous human fetal lung cells
metric: IC50, fold resistance, percent cell survival (MTT assay)
baseline: Verapamil (known P-gp inhibitor); DU145 naïve cells (drug-sensitive parental line)
result: DU145TXR >2,400-fold resistant to paclitaxel; inhibitor potentiation concentrations <3 µmol/L; sensitivity restored to naïve cell levels

problem: Constructing pseudo-temporal orderings of single cells from scRNA-seq data is useful for studying gene expression dynamics, but limited computational tools exist and quantitative methods for comparing them are lacking.
method: TSCAN uses cluster-based minimum spanning tree (MST) construction: cells are first clustered, MST connects cluster centers, and pseudo-time is obtained by projecting cells onto the tree. PCA is applied for dimensionality reduction after gene-level clustering to mitigate dropout effects.
finding: Cell clustering before MST construction reduces tree space complexity, improving accuracy and robustness of pseudo-time reconstruction. TSCAN provides competitive performance across multiple datasets and includes quantitative evaluation measures (POS score) for method comparison.
dataset: HSMM (human skeletal muscle myoblasts), LPS (dendritic cells), qNSC (quiescent neural stem cells) single-cell RNA-seq datasets
metric: POS score (pseudo-time ordering accuracy), cell ordering similarity (robustness), detection of known differentially expressed genes
baseline: Monocle, Waterfall (existing pseudo-time reconstruction methods); 518 a priori differentiation genes from Monocle analysis
result: TSCAN detected same two-branch topology as Monocle on HSMM; clustering improved ordering accuracy vs non-clustering approaches

problem: Whether CD8Treg-mediated killing of encephalitogenic CD4Th1 cells requires both cell types to be primed by the same dendritic cell is unknown, and the factors controlling the magnitude of the cytotoxic T lymphocyte response in murine EAE are unclear.
method: Agent-based simulation (ARTIMMUS) of experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis in Java/MASON framework, with 1000 simulation runs per experiment analyzed using non-parametric A-Tests for effect magnitude.
finding: Effective CD4Th1 regulation does not require co-priming on the same DC; only ~1% of DCs present both peptide types. Spatial competition between CD4Treg and CD8Treg cells and timing of Qa-1 expression critically determine CTL response magnitude.
dataset: Simulated murine EAE immune system (ARTIMMUS); 1000 simulation runs per condition
metric: A-Test effect magnitude score (thresholds: >0.71 or <0.29 = large/significant), cell population counts, % CD4Th1 apoptosis
baseline: Control simulations (normal DC antigen presentation) vs mutually exclusive peptide presentation experiments
result: ~90% DCs present single peptide type; mutually exclusive presentation: ~10% reduction in CD8Treg priming, ~5% reduction in CD4Th1 killing; A-Test=0.415 (not significant)

problem: Standard microarray normalization and analysis methods require many replicates and may miss biologically meaningful gene expression changes. An integrated approach is needed to analyze how the extracellular matrix modulates cancer cell phenotype.
method: Noise-level normalization of microarrays, robust linear regression for global adjustment, hypervariable (HV) gene identification via global F-test, hierarchical and K-means clustering, pathway analysis (IPA), TRE analysis (PAINT), and experimental TF validation using Panomics arrays.
finding: Over 2,000 HV genes were identified when papilloma cells were cultured on cancer-remodeled ECM. A subset of 'beacon genes' representing largest changes captured essential biology and predicted transcriptional regulatory networks that were confirmed experimentally.
dataset: RT4 bladder transitional cell papilloma cells on plastic vs Matrigel (crECM); 22,464 long-oligo spotted arrays; 15,287 expressed genes; GEO GSE9291
metric: standard deviations above noise (expression threshold), relative SD (HV threshold >3.8 SD, p<6.7×10⁻⁵), p-value for false positives
baseline: Cells cultured on plastic (2 arrays) vs cells on cancer-remodeled ECM (9 arrays over time course)
result: HV threshold >3.8 SD (p<6.7×10⁻⁵); beacon genes >5 SD (p<2.87×10⁻⁷); >2,000 genes of interest; predicted TRE patterns confirmed by Panomics TF arrays

problem: Current neoantigen vaccine design focuses only on MHC-peptide binding affinity, ignoring other antigen presentation steps, leading to a gap between high prediction accuracy and low clinical effectiveness (~20% validated).
method: Neo-intline integrates six in-silico steps simulating the full T-cell epitope presentation pathway: mutation expression filtering, proteasome cleavage prediction, TAP binding, MHC-I/II binding affinity, and TCR recognition scoring. Validated with TESLA benchmark and in vivo mouse melanoma experiments.
finding: Neo-intline outperformed MuPeXi, Neopepsee, and pTuneos on melanoma samples. Both MHC-I and MHC-II predicted peptides recruited corresponding T cells in vivo, and combination with GM-CSF/poly(I:C) or PD-1 antibodies showed significant anticancer effects.
dataset: 7 clinical samples (3 melanoma, 3 GI, 1 BRCA); TESLA benchmark; IEDB validated peptides; B16F10 mouse melanoma model (C57BL/6)
metric: Rank Coverage Score (RCS), Rank Score (RS), AUC-ROC for drug classification, CD4+/CD8+ T cell recruitment
baseline: MuPeXi, Neopepsee, pTuneos (state-of-the-art neoantigen prediction pipelines)
result: Neo-intline best in 5/7 samples; RS=0.43 (vs 0.45 pTuneos, 0.48 MuPeXi, 0.74 Neopepsee) for positive samples

problem: Transcriptional reprogramming of proliferative melanoma cells into an invasive subpopulation drives metastasis, but the regulatory mechanisms and key transcription factors underlying this phenotypic switch are poorly understood.
method: Integration of transcriptome (RNA-seq), open chromatin (FAIRE-seq), histone modification (ChIP-seq), and DNA methylation data from melanoma cultures and TCGA biopsies, with NMF clustering, GSEA, and regulatory motif analysis to identify master regulators.
finding: Thousands of genomic regulatory regions distinguish proliferative (SOX10/MITF-driven) and invasive (AP-1/TEAD-driven) states. TEAD knockdown established a causal role in the invasive gene network and linked TEADs to cell invasion and MAPK inhibitor sensitivity.
dataset: TCGA-SKCM (375 samples RNA-seq, 345 exome), two microarray compendia (135 and 263 melanoma samples), 11 melanoma cultures (FAIRE-seq, ChIP-seq, RNA-seq)
metric: adjusted p-value (limma/DESeq2), GSEA enrichment, NMF clustering, Fisher's exact test, Kruskal-Wallis test, Bonferroni correction
baseline: Hoek gene signatures (97 genes) for proliferative/invasive states; known melanocyte lineage markers (SOX10, MITF, PAX3)
result: 643 invasive + 772 proliferative signature genes; 3 NMF clusters in each compendium; no mutation enrichment between states; TEAD knockdown reduced invasion

problem: There is urgent need to identify therapeutic targets and repurpose existing drugs for treating severe COVID-19 patients, particularly by understanding the immune system's protein interactions with SARS-CoV-2.
method: Immune system protein-protein interactome network construction (1,584 nodes, Cytoscape/STRING, confidence cutoff 0.9), single-cell RNA sequencing analysis of ACE2/TMPRSS2 co-expressing cells, and fully connected deep neural networks for multitask drug activity classification.
finding: 25 potential therapeutic targets were identified as significantly overexpressed in nasal goblet cells, lung type II pneumocytes, and ileal enterocytes. Deep neural networks predicted activity of 10,672 drugs, identifying approved drugs and investigational compounds for COVID-19 treatment.
dataset: 332 SARS-CoV-2-associated human proteins; 3,885 immune system proteins (IMGT, InnateDB, DAVID); scRNA-seq of human tissues; 10,672 drugs from ChEMBL
metric: degree centrality, AUC-ROC (deep neural network classification), gene expression significance in scRNA-seq
baseline: Gordon et al. SARS-CoV-2 protein interaction map; Overmyer et al. severe COVID-19 proteomics (146 proteins); Bouhaddou et al. phosphoproteomics (97 proteins)
result: PPi network: 1,584 nodes, 332,968 edges; 25 overexpressed targets in ACE2+/TMPRSS2+ cells; degree centrality mean 44.5 (immune) vs 23.6 (SARS-CoV-2 associated)

problem: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) has low treatment success rates with conventional therapies, necessitating identification of new drug candidates targeting NSCLC-associated receptor molecules.
method: DFT quantum chemical calculations (B3LYP/LANL2DZ) for molecular characterization of lochnericine, molecular docking (AutoDock Vina/PyRx) against EGFR, KRAS, Ntrk, ALK, and TRIM11, molecular dynamics simulation, ADME prediction, and MTT assay on A549 cells.
finding: Lochnericine showed favorable binding affinity to NSCLC-associated target proteins, stable protein-ligand complexes in MD simulation, and demonstrated remarkable anti-proliferative and apoptotic activity against A549 lung cancer cells.
dataset: A549 lung cancer cell line; NSCLC target proteins: EGFR (2ITY), KRAS (7LGI), Ntrk (7VKO), ALK (2XP2), TRIM11 (Q96F44)
metric: binding affinity (kcal/mol), FMO band gap energy, RMSD (MD simulation), ADME parameters (Lipinski's rule), MTT cell viability
baseline: Known co-crystallized ligands for each target protein; Lipinski's Rule of Five for drug-likeness
result: Optimized energy 1,111.43 a.u.; lochnericine inhibits all five NSCLC targets; stable MD trajectory; anti-proliferative against A549 confirmed

problem: Mutation-targeted therapy in cystic fibrosis is not available for patients carrying ultra-rare CFTR variants like W57G/A234D; efficacy of CFTR modulators on this genotype is unknown.
method: In silico structural modeling using cryo-EM CFTR structures (5UAK, 6O1V) for stability prediction, combined with functional characterization via western blotting, forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) assay in colonoids, and Ussing chamber analysis in reprogrammed nasal epithelial cells.
finding: Both mutations destabilize CFTR but W57G is ~10 kcal/mol more destabilizing than A234D; residual CFTR function increases with VX661±VX445±VX770 treatment; in vivo VX770 reduced sweat chloride to non-CF levels and increased FEV1% by 6% after 27 weeks.
dataset: Patient-derived colonoids and nasal epithelial cells (W57G/A234D genotype), FRT and CFBE cell lines, F508del/F508del and non-CF controls
metric: Sweat chloride concentration, predicted FEV1%, forskolin-induced swelling, CFTR protein band C expression, ΔΔG (kcal/mol)
baseline: Untreated (DMSO) conditions, non-CF controls, F508del/F508del genotype
result: 6% absolute FEV1% increase after 27 weeks VX770, sweat chloride reduced to non-CF levels, W57G destabilization ~10 kcal/mol greater than A234D

problem: The biochemistry of multi-cell systems (hepatocyte-endothelial-adipocyte) involving homeostatic balance in glucose and lipid metabolism is too complex to understand without computational models.
method: Modular ODE-based model using Michaelis-Menten kinetics implemented in Simulink, merging three validated single-cell metabolic models with physiological interconnections under static and dynamic (flow) conditions.
finding: Good agreement between simulated and experimental metabolic profiles for monocultures and connected three-culture system; heuristic correction factors identified differential pathway activation under flow vs static conditions.
dataset: In vitro connected three-culture system: 250K hepatocytes, 80K endothelial cells, 300K adipocytes in MCmB 2.0 bioreactor
metric: Glucose, fatty acid, lactate, glycerol concentration profiles over time
result: Good agreement between simulated and experimental metabolic profiles in both static and dynamic conditions

problem: Reliable personalized prediction of NSCLC response to chemotherapy requires estimation of in vivo cell-killing efficacy, which cannot be directly measured or adequately reproduced by in vitro experiments.
method: Multiscale mechanistic model incorporating uncontrolled proliferation, population heterogeneity, hypoxia, and treatment resistance; sensitivity analysis via OFAT and Latin hypercube sampling/partial rank correlation coefficient (LHS/PRCC) approaches.
finding: Volume growth rate and growth fraction at diagnosis are key determinants for accurate cell kill rate estimation; the framework provides quantitative CKR estimates for cisplatin-based doublet regimens applicable to personalized predictions.
dataset: 13 NSCLC patients (9 SCC, 4 ADC, stage III-IV) treated with cisplatin + gemcitabine/vinorelbine/docetaxel
metric: Cell kill rate (CKR), tumor volume change from CT imaging, partial rank correlation coefficients
baseline: Pre-treatment tumor volume (CT at two time points), exponential growth model
result: CKR estimates provided for 13 patients; volume growth rate and growth fraction identified as highest-sensitivity parameters via PRCC
