problem: Genome-scale metabolic reconstructions include all reactions implied by genome annotation, but many are inactive in specific contexts; methods are needed to create context-specific networks for accurate in silico modeling.
method: GIMME (Gene Inactivity Moderated by Metabolism and Expression) algorithm using quantitative gene expression data and required metabolic functionalities with linear optimization to produce context-specific reconstructions and quantitative inconsistency scores.
finding: GIMME produces context-specific metabolic networks consistent with experimental results for E. coli adaptive evolution, metabolic engineering strain design, and human skeletal muscle cells; inconsistency scores quantify data-objective agreement.
dataset: E. coli adaptive evolution strains, E. coli metabolic engineering strains, human skeletal muscle cells (GEO expression data)
metric: Normalized consistency score (NCS), inconsistency score (IS), permutation test p-value
baseline: Genome-scale metabolic reconstruction (full network), random permutations (10,000 randomizations)
result: Consistency scores statistically significant (p<0.05 permutation test); context-specific predictions match known experimental phenotypes

problem: T-cell epitope prediction algorithms used for SARS-CoV-2 vaccine design have inherent limitations that may cause vaccine candidates to miss critically important immune responses.
method: Perspective/review analysis of limitations in HLA class I-restricted epitope prediction algorithms, including biases toward 9-mer/10-mer peptides and incomplete HLA allele coverage.
finding: Approaches exclusively based on 9-mer/10-mer epitope prediction will miss important T-cell responses; unbiased overlapping peptide panels should complement prediction-based approaches to define correlates of protective immunity.
dataset: SARS-CoV-2 viral proteome
baseline: 9-mer/10-mer HLA class I epitope prediction algorithms

problem: Metastatic melanoma resists TRAIL-induced apoptosis via pro-inflammatory NF-κB and STAT3 pathways, limiting therapeutic options that selectively kill cancer cells.
method: Boolean network modeling of melanoma signaling pathways with stuck-at fault analysis to simulate gene mutations and drug interventions; experimental validation using A375 melanoma cell viability assays.
finding: Cryptotanshinone increases TRAIL sensitivity through dual action on DR5 upregulation and STAT3 inhibition; it induces apoptosis even without TRAIL by downregulating anti-apoptotic factors, validated experimentally in A375 cells.
dataset: A375 metastatic melanoma cell line, Boolean network of TRAIL/NF-κB/STAT3 signaling pathways
metric: Apoptosis ratio (sum pro-apoptotic / sum anti-apoptotic factors), cell viability
result: CT induces apoptosis even without TRAIL input; enhanced cell death in A375 cells experimentally validated

problem: Gemcitabine has limited cellular uptake; pharmacokinetic properties of novel gemcitabine-cell-penetrating peptide conjugates need evaluation to assess therapeutic viability.
method: PCA-derived z-scale from 12 physicochemical descriptors of 20 amino acids for QSAR CPP prediction models; GastroPlus pharmacokinetic simulation of plasma concentration profiles for conjugates.
finding: CPP prediction models achieved ~80% accuracy; Gem-Cys-pVEC conjugate showed best in vitro bioactivity; GastroPlus simulations confirmed conjugates reach therapeutic plasma levels with bioavailability comparable to parental gemcitabine.
dataset: CellPPD database (900 CPP, 1148 non-CPP), MKN-28 and HT-29 cell lines
metric: Sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, MCC, Q², AUC (pharmacokinetic)
result: 79% sensitivity, 80% specificity, 81% accuracy, 61% MCC, Q²=0.406; conjugate AUC comparable to GEMZAR

problem: SMYD3 lysine methyltransferase is overexpressed in breast carcinomas and drives uncontrolled proliferation; a fast, affordable hit-to-lead methodology for identifying SMYD3 inhibitors is needed.
method: In silico docking screening of 137,990 ZINC15 molecules using Schrödinger software against SMYD3 binding pocket (PDB: 5EX3), followed by in vitro methyltransferase assays and breast cancer cell line experiments.
finding: Inhibitor-4 reduces SMYD3-mediated H3 methylation by 70%, restores normal proliferation rates, arrests cell cycle, and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells without affecting wildtype cells.
dataset: MCF7 and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell lines, MCF10A normal cells, ZINC15 library (137,990 molecules)
metric: SMYD3 methyltransferase activity (% reduction), cell proliferation rate, Glide docking score (kJ/mol)
baseline: BCI-121 (known SMYD3 inhibitor), untreated controls, wildtype MCF10A cells
result: 70% reduction in H3 methylation, binding energy -7.2 to -9.1 kJ/mol, SMYD3 overexpressed 1.8-fold (MCF7) and 2.6-fold (MDA-MB-231) vs normal

problem: Preclinical identification of drug-induced cardiotoxicity and its translation to human proarrhythmic risk remain major challenges; animal models have species-specific limitations.
method: In silico drug trials on a population of 530 human cardiac Purkinje cell models for 14 reference compounds at multiple concentrations and pacing frequencies; comparison with in vitro rabbit Purkinje fiber recordings and clinical TdP risk from CredibleMeds.
finding: In silico human Purkinje EAD-based predictions achieved 100% accuracy for TdP risk classification, outperforming rabbit experiments (79%); high consistency between in silico and in vitro results with discrepancies explained by species differences.
dataset: 14 reference compounds, 530 in silico human Purkinje models, rabbit Purkinje fibers (n=4-6 per compound), CredibleMeds clinical TdP reports
metric: TdP risk classification accuracy, APD90, APD50, dV/dtMax, EAD occurrence, APA
baseline: In vitro rabbit Purkinje fiber experiments (79% accuracy), APD-based prediction (86% accuracy)
result: 100% accuracy (in silico EAD-based) vs 79% (rabbit in vitro) vs 86% (APD-based, both platforms) for proarrhythmic risk prediction

problem: Stone cells negatively affect pear fruit quality through secondary cell wall thickening and lignification; the KNOX gene family regulating these processes has not been characterized in pear.
method: Genome-wide identification of KNOX family in Pyrus bretschneideri using BLASTp and domain analysis; phylogenetic and synteny analysis across four Rosaceae species; overexpression of PbKNOX1 in Arabidopsis with lignin content and cell wall thickness measurements.
finding: 18 PbKNOX genes identified across 10 chromosomes; PbKNOX1 expression inversely correlates with stone cell and lignin accumulation; PbKNOX1 overexpression decreased secondary cell wall thickness by ~19% and lignin content by ~13% with downregulation of lignin biosynthesis genes.
dataset: Pyrus bretschneideri (Dangshan Su pear) at 8 developmental stages, Arabidopsis thaliana Columbia-0, grape/mei/strawberry genomes
metric: Secondary cell wall thickness, lignin content (%), qRT-PCR gene expression levels
result: ~19% decrease in vessel cell wall thickness, ~13% reduction in lignin content in PbKNOX1-overexpressing Arabidopsis lines

problem: Whether B cells can activate CD4+ T cells in the CNS of MS patients by presenting idiotopes from their own immunoglobulin variable regions on HLA class II molecules, and whether prerequisites for such idiotope-driven T-B cell collaboration exist.
method: High-throughput sequencing of IGHV transcripts from CSF and blood of MS/OIND patients combined with in silico prediction of cathepsin cleavage, HLA-peptide binding affinity (NetMHCIIpan), and T cell-exposed motif (TCEM) rarity analysis.
finding: CDR3 idiotopes from MS patients have high predicted affinity for HLA-DRB1*15:01, are predicted to be endosomally processed by cathepsins S and L in positions allowing HLA binding, and CSF B cells from MS patients contain more rare TCEMs than OIND controls, associated with preferential IGHV4 gene family usage.
dataset: 11 relapsing-remitting MS patients and 6 OIND controls, 1,812,920 IGHV amino acid sequences from CSF and blood
metric: predicted HLA binding affinity (IC50 nM), cathepsin cleavage probability (>0.8), TCEM rarity frequency, p-value
baseline: OIND patient CSF B cells, blood B cells from same patients
result: CDR3 lengths MS CSF 15.55aa vs OIND 15.25aa (p<0.001), cathepsin cleavage variation across IGHV families p<0.0001, IGHV4 preferentially cleaved by cathepsin S

problem: Understanding the factors limiting predictive accuracy of allogeneic HSCT-related non-relapse mortality, including statistical methodology, number of features, and population size.
method: In-silico iterative simulations using 6 machine learning algorithms (AdaBoost, ADT, logistic regression, MLP, naive Bayes, random forest) with varying sample sizes, subpopulations, and feature selection on EBMT registry data.
finding: Predictive performance plateaus at ~6,000-8,800 patients reaching max AUC of 0.67; only 3-5 variables are needed for near-maximal AUC; top 3 variables across all algorithms are disease stage, donor type, and conditioning regimen. Traditional HSCT data has been 'worn out'.
dataset: 25,923 adult acute leukemia patients from EBMT registry (2005-2013), 326 European centers
metric: AUC-ROC
baseline: Comparison across 6 ML algorithms and varying sample sizes/subpopulations
result: Max AUC=0.67 (LR and AdaBoost), plateau at 6,611-8,814 patients, CR2 subpopulation AUC=0.55-0.59, NRM prevalence 9.2% at day 100

problem: Determining the biological effect of chlorogenic acid (CGA) on colorectal cancer cells HT-29 and SW480, and its potential modulatory mechanism on the Wnt/β-catenin pathway via interactions with β-catenin and LRP6.
method: In vitro flow cytometry assays (PI/DiOC6 viability, MitoTracker Red ROS, DNA content cell cycle) and in silico molecular docking against β-catenin and LRP6 protein targets.
finding: CGA at 2000 µM significantly reduces viability and causes DNA fragmentation in SW480 cells but not HT-29; induces dose-dependent ROS production in both lines. CGA shows similar docking affinity to LRP6 as niclosamide and higher affinity for β-catenin than reference inhibitors C2 and iCRT14.
dataset: SW480 and HT-29 colorectal cancer cell lines
metric: mean fluorescence intensity (MFI), cell cycle phase distribution (%), docking binding affinity (kcal/mol), p-value
baseline: Untreated cells; reference ligands niclosamide (LRP6), C2 and iCRT14 (β-catenin)
result: CGA 2000µM: significant DiOC6 decrease and PI increase in SW480 (p≤0.001), dose-dependent ROS increase (p≤0.0001), sub-G1 increase at ≥500µM

problem: Ranking natural phenolic compounds for their therapeutic effectiveness against cancer stem cells (CSCs) to identify potential CSC-targeted therapy candidates.
method: Bipartite graph construction of 21 phenolics and 1118 CSC genes weighted by interaction strength, Disease Specificity Index (DSI), and Disease Pleiotropy Index (DPI), ranked using a modified PageRank algorithm. Top candidates evaluated for drug-likeness via Lipinski's Rule of Five.
finding: Top 5 ranked phenolics are Resveratrol, Curcumin, Quercetin, Epigallocatechin Gallate, and Genistein; all except EGCG satisfy Lipinski's Rule of Five with favorable oral bioavailability and pharmacokinetic properties.
dataset: 21 phenolics from literature, 1118 CSC genes from CSCdb database, PCIDB interaction database
metric: PageRank score, Lipinski Rule of Five compliance, molecular weight, logP, H-bond donors/acceptors, polar surface area
result: Top 5: Resveratrol, Curcumin, Quercetin, EGCG, Genistein; MW range 228-458, logP 2-5, ≤7 rotatable bonds

problem: Understanding the mechanism of action underlying mechanical conditioning of cardiac adipose tissue-derived progenitor cells (ATDPCs) within the physiological range for cardiac regeneration applications.
method: Custom magnet-based mechanical stimulator applying ~10% stretch at 1 Hz for 7 days to cardiac ATDPCs on 3 patterned PDMS surfaces (vertical, horizontal, smooth); gene/protein expression analysis (RT-qPCR, western blot) and in silico artificial neural network analysis of secretome.
finding: Mechanical stretching upregulated structural cardiac genes and showed rising trends in cardiac transcription factors, with modulations dependent on surface pattern. Secretome analysis revealed upregulation of proteins associated with myocardial infarction and cardiac regeneration (immune response, angiogenesis, cell adhesion regulators).
dataset: Cardiac and subcutaneous ATDPCs from human patients, 3 surface patterns × mechanical stimulation conditions
metric: gene expression fold change (RT-qPCR), protein expression levels, artificial neural network disease association scores
baseline: Unstimulated cells on same surface patterns; subcutaneous ATDPCs as tissue control
result: ~10% longitudinal elongation achieved, PDMS Young's modulus 1.3 MPa, trapezoidal waveform 1 Hz with 100ms rise/fall times mimicking cardiac cycle

problem: Developing comprehensive in silico, in vitro, and in cellulo approaches to specifically monitor the SARS-CoV-2 spike/ACE2 interaction for characterizing viral entry and identifying potential antiviral inhibitors.
method: 400 ns molecular dynamics simulation (AMBER18/ff14SB) of S-RBD/ACE2 complex, pulldown assays, AlphaLISA and biolayer interferometry (BLI) binding assays, pseudotyped lentiviral transduction assays, cell imaging, and flow cytometry.
finding: Identified transient druggable pockets at the S/ACE2 interface via MD clustering; determined kinetic binding parameters by BLI; established pseudotyped lentiviral system recapitulating S-mediated entry and cell fusion; developed cell-surface S/ACE2 interaction monitoring by imaging and flow cytometry.
dataset: SARS-CoV-2 S-RBD (PDB 6M0J, 2.45Å), recombinant RBD(438-516)/ACE2 proteins, HEK293T-ACE2 cell lines
metric: RMSD, hydrogen bond occupancy (%), AlphaLISA signal (AS units), BLI kinetic parameters (kon, koff, KD), transduction efficiency
baseline: X-ray crystal structure (PDB 6M0J) as reference; VSV-G pseudotyped vectors as positive control
result: 400ns MD simulation, 8 structural clusters identified, AlphaLISA up to 1,000,000 AS units at nM protein concentrations, DMSO tolerance confirmed

problem: Addressing the small sample size problem in high-dimensional scRNA-seq data where the number of cell samples is far smaller than the feature (gene) size, limiting downstream analysis.
method: LSH-GAN: a modified GAN using locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to subsample real data for augmenting generator input, training with 2-layer MLP (16,16) architecture; evaluated on gene selection and cell clustering tasks.
finding: LSH-GAN generates more realistic cell samples than benchmarks (cscGAN, SUGAR, Splatter) with lower Wasserstein distance; generated samples improve downstream gene selection and cell clustering performance across multiple datasets.
dataset: 5 scRNA-seq datasets: Yan (90 cells, 20214 genes), Klein (2717 cells), Darmanis (466 cells), Pollen (299 cells), Melanoma (67,461 cells)
metric: Wasserstein distance, adjusted Rand index (ARI), normalized mutual information (NMI)
baseline: cscGAN, SUGAR, Splatter; gene selection methods: GLM-PCA, CV2 Index, M3Drop, Fano Factor, Seurat HVG
result: LSH-GAN achieves lowest Wasserstein distance across datasets; sample:feature ratio as low as 0.012; k=5, t=1-2 optimal LSH parameters

problem: Investigating whether MDSC depletion combined with tumor cell vaccination can elicit effective anti-tumor immunity and sustain tumor dormancy in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC).
method: Deterministic ODE mathematical model with 3 variables (cancer cells, MDSCs, effector immune cells) using Gompertz tumor growth, calibrated against experimental 4T1 murine TNBC data; simulations of MDSC depletion schedules and vaccination protocols.
finding: MDSC depletion every 48h controls tumor growth; vaccination with small number of tumor cells combined with MDSC depletion elicits effective immune response suppressing subsequent aggressive tumor challenge, resulting in sustained tumor dormancy.
dataset: Murine 4T1 TNBC-like cell line and MR20 dormant derivative in BALB/c mice (calibrated to published experimental data by Lan et al.)
metric: tumor cell count over time, immune cell dynamics, MDSC population size, dormancy threshold
baseline: Tumor growth without MDSC depletion; published experimental data from Lan et al.
result: MDSC depletion every 48h starting day 7 halts tumor growth for up to 70,000 initially injected cells; vaccination + MDSC depletion achieves sustained dormancy

problem: Investigating the role of viscoelastic cell-ECM mechanical interactions in avascular glioma tumor spheroid growth and invasion patterns.
method: Continuous two-component in silico model simulating oxygen transport, three cell phenotype transitions (proliferative, hypoxic, necrotic), hydrostatic forces in cell-ECM composite, cell motility and adhesion, with visco-elastic cell aggregation and elastic ECM scaffold.
finding: ECM stiffness, mechanical deformations, and cell-cell adhesion strongly affect tumor progression; a Transition Layer on the spheroid surface facilitates invasion; at certain high ECM stiffness, distant multicellular clusters form, while at further increased stiffness subtumors do not form.
dataset: U87WT and U87ΔEGFR glioma cell line experimental data from Stein et al.
metric: cell density profiles (cells/mm³), spheroid radius, invasive zone width, core-IZ boundary densities
baseline: Experimental MTS data from Stein et al. for U87WT (C_IZ=10⁴ cells/mm³) and U87ΔEGFR (C_IZ=2.5×10³ cells/mm³)
result: Simulated densities match experiment in major spheroid part; center density ~60% of measured value; ECM stiffness threshold determines cluster formation vs. no subtumor formation

problem: Evaluating graphyne as a drug carrier for hesperetin delivery to cancer cells and characterizing the supramolecular interactions governing the complex formation.
method: DFT calculations (B3LYP/Lanl2DZ) with Gaussian 09 for geometry optimization, TDDFT for excited states, NBO analysis, charge decomposition analysis (CDA), noncovalent interaction (NCI) analysis, and IEF-PCM solvation model in water.
finding: Hesperetin adsorbs onto graphyne via two C-H noncovalent interactions (2.54 and 3.68 Å bond distances); complex formation increases dipole moment to 2.02 Debye enhancing hydrophilicity; HOMO-LUMO analysis confirms charge transfer from hesperetin to graphyne carrier, enabling controlled drug delivery.
dataset: Hesperetin (HPT) and graphyne (GRP) molecular structures
metric: adsorption energy (Ead), HOMO-LUMO gap, dipole moment (Debye), bond distance (Å), solvation energy
baseline: Isolated GRP and HPT molecules; gas phase vs. water solvent phase
result: Dipole moment increased to 2.02 Debye upon complexation, C-H bond distances 2.54 and 3.68 Å, charge transfer from HPT HOMO to GRP LUMO confirmed

problem: Cellular migration mechanics characterization
method: Multidimensional microscopy with computational analysis
finding: Characterized cell migration protrusions
dataset: fibroblasts
metric: Bhattacharyya

problem: Bioprocess optimization under process-model mismatch
method: Mathematical modeling of IPA production
finding: Cell growth and IPA production compete for acetyl-CoA
dataset: E. coli

problem: Antiproliferative and anti-obesity activity of Nyctanthes extract
method: Cell viability and phenolic content analysis
finding: Phenolic compounds contribute to biological activity
dataset: leukemia cells
metric: specificity

problem: Cell-free gene expression with linear DNA templates
method: sfGFP fluorescence-based expression quantification
finding: PCR-based linear DNA for cell-free expression

problem: Tumor heterogeneity in cancer progression and treatment resistance
method: scRNA-seq profiling of tumor and adjacent tissues
finding: Single-cell analysis reveals tumor heterogeneity
dataset: patient tissues
metric: R2

problem: Silver nanoparticle hazard assessment
method: Nanosight characterization of Ag NPs
finding: Mean effective diameter 120nm with monomodal distribution
result: 120nm

problem: 3D cell organization with extracellular matrix interactions
method: Pseudospectral 2D and 3D simulations
finding: Identified three solution states for cell organization
metric: accuracy

problem: Autonomous glycolysis oscillations in central carbon metabolism
method: Mathematical modeling of glycolytic oscillations
finding: Self-control mechanisms in E. coli glycolysis
dataset: E. coli
metric: sensitivity

problem: Tissue cellular composition predicts tumor response
method: Seg-SOM method for cell feature transformation
finding: Spatial cell analysis predicts therapy response
dataset: tumor tissue

problem: TCR binding to peptide-MHC complexes in T-cell immunity
method: TCR expression, refolding and binding analysis
finding: TCRs show diverse conformational strategies during binding
metric: R2

problem: Understanding how single-cell behaviors lead to tissue-level dynamics in cancer systems, including hypoxia, angiogenesis, invasion, and immunosurveillance.
method: Survey of cell-based computational modeling approaches including cellular automata (CA), lattice gas CA, cellular Potts models (CPM), center-based models (CBM), and other off-lattice methods.
finding: Cell-based models are well-suited for problems where single-cell effects drive clinical progression; open source platforms and cloud/supercomputing resources are lowering barriers to large-scale simulation studies.
dataset: Cancer biology systems (hypoxia, angiogenesis, invasion, stem cells, immunosurveillance)

problem: Directly measuring cellular dose of nanoparticles in vitro is impractical, leading to reliance on mass-based exposure metrics that can introduce errors of 3-6 orders of magnitude.
method: Computational model (ISDD) solving Stokes Law (sedimentation) and Stokes-Einstein equation (diffusion) simultaneously to calculate particle transport to cells in monolayer culture systems.
finding: Without adjusting any parameters, ISDD predictions matched experimental cellular doses within ~2-fold; target cell doses can differ from nominal media concentrations by 3-6 orders of magnitude on a particle number or surface area basis.
dataset: Polystyrene spheres (20-1100 nm), 35 nm amorphous silica, 30 nm iron oxide agglomerates
metric: Predicted vs measured cellular dose (fold difference)
result: Predictions within 5% to 3-fold of experimental data, typically ~2-fold; dose discrepancy up to 3-6 orders of magnitude

problem: Understanding how fluid shear stress from blood flow and interstitial flows affects cancer cell behavior during metastasis in vascular and tumor microenvironments.
method: Review of computational models (Darcy/Brinkman equations for interstitial flow, adhesive dynamics for selectin-mediated adhesion) and experimental models of fluid shear stress effects on tumor cells.
finding: Fluid shear stress modulates cancer cell viability, adhesion to endothelium, migration, invasion, and chemotherapeutic sensitivity; computational models coupling mechanical forces with chemical signaling can predict cell behavior in dynamic microenvironments.
dataset: Cancer cells in vascular and tumor microenvironments
metric: Shear stress (dyn/cm²), interstitial flow velocity
result: Interstitial flow-generated shear stress on smooth muscle cells ~1.0 dyn/cm²

problem: Understanding how stem cell, trophectoderm, and endoderm lineages arise from gene regulatory network dynamics, and identifying strategies for reprogramming differentiated cells to stem cell state.
method: ODE-based dynamical model of a gene regulatory network (Oct4, Sox2, Nanog, Cdx2, Gata-6, Gcnf) using thermodynamic framework of gene regulation, with assumptions of Oct4 activation of Gata-6 and Nanog repression by Oct4-Gata-6 heterodimer.
finding: The model successfully describes biphasic behavior and lineage commitment; reprogramming from endoderm to stem cell state is best achieved by over-expressing Nanog rather than suppressing differentiation genes like Gata-6.
dataset: Mouse embryonic stem cell regulatory network (ChIP-on-chip and microarray data)
metric: Bifurcation analysis, steady-state concentrations of transcription factors
result: Nanog overexpression optimal for endoderm-to-stem-cell reprogramming

problem: Testing whether a pseudopod-based mechanism, where chemoattractants modulate an intrinsic pseudopod cycle rather than directly causing actin polymerization, can predict complex chemotactic cell behavior.
method: Computational model based on pseudopod autocatalysis with coupled positive and negative feedback loops, using Arbitrary Lagrangian Eulerian surface finite element method and level set method in MATLAB.
finding: The model reproduces cell polarization, persistence, pseudopod selection in gradients, and predicts that the angle between successive pseudopods increases proportionally to the difference between cell direction and gradient direction—confirmed experimentally in Dictyostelium.
dataset: Dictyostelium AX3 cells (developed 4 hr, imaged with Quimp3)
metric: Pseudopod angles, cell speed, persistence, directional accuracy
result: Predicted pseudopod angle relationship confirmed experimentally

problem: Understanding how signaling molecules, cytoskeleton, cell membrane, and mechanics interact in space and time to produce eukaryotic cell shape change and motility.
method: Review comparing computational approaches for cell motility: reaction-diffusion equations, viscoelastic flow equations, with boundary methods including level set, phase field, immersed boundary, and Cellular Potts models.
finding: No single computational approach is optimal for all motility problems; simpler models enable complete parameter studies while detailed 2D/3D models provide better biological comparison; the free-boundary problem remains a major technical challenge.
dataset: Eukaryotic motile cells (keratocytes, Dictyostelium, neutrophils, nematode sperm)

problem: Determining the relative contributions of tip cell migration rate and stalk cell proliferation rate on tumor vasculature formation during sprouting angiogenesis.
method: Hybrid 3D agent-based model on a 500×500×500 μm grid where endothelial cells (tip, stalk, phalanx) are modeled as physical agents responding to VEGF gradients over 20-day simulations.
finding: Proliferation rate has a greater effect on the spread and extent of vascular growth compared to migration rate, with strong implications for designing anti-angiogenic therapies that differentially target these processes.
dataset: In silico tumor vasculature (500³ μm grid, 10 trials per condition, 20-day simulations)
metric: VLD (mm/mm³), bifurcation density (1/mm³), vascular segment length (mm), vascular segment tortuosity (mm/mm), fractal dimension
baseline: Variable proliferation rates (0.0147-0.083 1/hr) and migration rates (5-30 μm/hr)
result: VLD=16.9-130.6 mm/mm³, VST=1.37-2.60 across proliferation rates at fixed MR=10 μm/hr

problem: Modeling realistically crawling and deforming cells in tissues is computationally challenging due to multiple levels of complexity in cell morphology and migration.
method: Extended Cellular Potts Model (CPM) with actin-inspired local feedback mechanism (Act model) using geometric mean of local activity values to bias membrane fluctuations into stable protrusions.
finding: The Act model produces realistic amoeboid crawling and keratocyte-like gliding cells that can migrate randomly or follow cues, squeeze between cells, or migrate collectively, while adding minimal computational overhead to the basic CPM.
dataset: Simulated amoeboid and keratocyte-like cells in 2D lattice environments
metric: Cell speed, persistence, morphological realism, computational load
baseline: Standard Cellular Potts Model (CPM) without Act extension
result: Computationally light extension enabling large dense heterogeneous tissue simulations

problem: Understanding how actin protrusion dynamics regulates 3D cell migration through degradable viscoelastic extracellular matrix, which is difficult to investigate experimentally.
method: Hybrid 2D model combining agent-based deformable cell model (viscoelastic actin cortex) with meshless Lagrangian smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) for degradable viscoelastic ECM; 6-hour migration simulations with 12 replicates per condition.
finding: Stochastic variability in protrusion lifetime alone explains differences in cell migration velocity; directly changing protrusion number, lifetime, or length does not affect migration. Optimal number of simultaneous protrusions is 1 (isotropic ECM) or 2 (anisotropic ECM).
dataset: Simulated cells in viscoelastic ECM (50-400 Pa stiffness, 6-hour migration, 12 replicates × 5 values per set)
metric: Absolute migration velocity (μm/hr), protrusion number, lifetime, length
baseline: Varying ECM stiffness (50-400 Pa) and cell contractile strength
result: Mean absolute migration velocity 3.7-10.2 μm/hr; optimal 1-2 simultaneous protrusions

problem: Understanding the complex interactions among mTOR, AMPK, and sirtuin signaling pathways in cell energy balance, metabolism, aging, and disease.
method: Large system of coupled ODEs and algebraic equations modeling insulin/IGF-1 pathway, mTORC1 with amino acid sensors (sestrin2), Preiss-Handler and salvage pathways (NAD+/SIRT1), AMPK, FOXO, and PGC-1α; parameters fitted to experimental data using interior point optimization.
finding: PRAS40 plays a clinically important role in mTORC1 inhibition especially at low insulin levels; SIRT1-activating compounds may cause premature autophagy; the model captures leucine-sestrin2-arginine interplay and its effect on mTORC1.
dataset: Mouse C2C12 myoblast cell line (supplemented with mouse kidney, rat, and human cell data)
metric: Protein phosphorylation levels, steady-state concentrations, dose-response to rapamycin/wortmannin
baseline: Control (no drug treatment) vs rapamycin and wortmannin interventions
result: PRAS40 lowers steady-state phosphorylated mTORC1 by 18% with sufficient insulin; substantially greater inhibition at low insulin

problem: Computational model coupling cell mechanics and gene regulation in embryogenesis
method: MecaGen discrete-element method for ellipsoidal cells
finding: Model couples mechanical behavior and GRN in 3D space
dataset: zebrafish

problem: Representational similarity analysis connecting branches of systems neuroscience
method: RSA correlation-distance matrix analysis
finding: FFA but not EVC shows category-specific representations
dataset: human fMRI
metric: correlation

problem: Adult stem cell therapies for wound healing with biomaterials
method: Biomaterial-based scaffolds for wound healing
finding: Review of computational models for skin repair

problem: Cardiac conduction in cell-based computational model
method: EMI model for cardiac tissue electrophysiology
finding: Non-uniform sodium channel distribution affects conduction

problem: Cell migration mechanism in intestinal crypt
method: 2D cylindrical and 3D models of crypt dynamics
finding: Passive mechanism sufficient for crypt cell migration
dataset: mouse

problem: Macrophage-tumor cell crosstalk computational modeling
method: Mathematical models of cancer-macrophage interaction
finding: Crosstalk leads to two distinct steady states

problem: Cell sorting pattern recapitulation using computational model
method: Hexagonal lattice cell model with autonomous movement
finding: Model reproduces inside-outside cell sorting pattern

problem: Autophagic vesicle dynamics computational modeling
method: Live-cell microscopy with LC3-fluorescent tagging
finding: Single cell autophagy analysis system developed
dataset: U2OS cells

problem: Visual recognition memory via grid cells
method: Computational model using grid cell associations
finding: Model performs one-shot learning for recognition

problem: Cell rigidity sensing dynamic mechanisms
method: Actomyosin network tethered to 3D domains
finding: Molecular motors act as rigidity sensors

problem: Computational modeling of hematopoietic differentiation network
method: Computational simulation of differentiation pathways

problem: Molecular markers in RCC progression steps
method: In vivo passaging of RENCA cells
finding: Increased aggressiveness through passaging
dataset: mouse

problem: VEGF isoform transport in skeletal muscle microenvironment
method: Finite difference biophysical model
finding: Characterized VEGF transport in rat EDL muscle
dataset: rat EDL muscle

problem: Mechanical homeostasis in soft biological tissues
method: Finite element computational framework
finding: Physical foundations of tissue mechanical homeostasis

problem: Cellular cytoskeletal mechanics role in health
method: Review of cytoskeletal mechanics models

problem: Plk1-p53-G2/M checkpoint crosstalk in cell cycle
method: Computational model of G2/M checkpoint regulation
finding: Differential cell cycle regulation mechanisms
metric: sensitivity

problem: Cell-centred modeling of vascular adaptation
method: Cell-centred ABM for stent-vessel interaction
finding: Predicted injury and ECM degradation under stent struts

problem: Low-frequency LFP enhancement prediction in cortical models
method: Network model of cortical layer II/III
finding: FS-RS model replicated gamma but not low-frequency enhancement

problem: Angiopoietin-Tie signaling pathway modeling
method: Computational signaling pathway model validated experimentally
finding: Model captures and reproduces experimental results

problem: Cell-biomaterial interaction simulation at multiple scales
method: Multi-scale computational modeling review

problem: Current Alzheimer's disease therapeutics (AChE inhibitors) provide only symptomatic relief; natural compounds with dual AChE inhibition and anti-amyloid activity are needed.
method: Computational molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) to screen four natural compounds against AChE, followed by in vitro AChE inhibition assay, MTT cell viability, ThT amyloid aggregation assay, and oxidative stress biochemical analysis in SH-SY5Y neuronal cells.
finding: Combined curcumin and piperine showed synergistic AChE inhibition (IC50 comparable to donepezil), preserved cell viability up to 85% against Aβ-induced damage, inhibited Aβ42 fibril aggregation and disaggregation, and reversed Aβ-induced oxidative stress; bacoside A and chebulinic acid were unsuitable.
dataset: SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cell line, Aβ42 peptide
metric: IC50, binding affinity (kcal/mol), synergism quotient (SQ), cell viability (%), ThT fluorescence intensity (a.u.), p-value
baseline: Donepezil (IC50 54.79 μg/ml), individual curcumin and piperine treatments
result: Combined IC50=62.81 μg/ml (vs donepezil 54.79), SQ=1.824 at 35μM, cell viability 85%, ThT aggregation 0.432 a.u., disaggregation 0.532 a.u. (p<0.01)

problem: The standard theory of metastatic colonization assumes metastases grow independently, but this has not been rigorously tested against quantitative multi-modal data.
method: Mathematical modeling (ODE-based tumor growth and metastatic dissemination) calibrated on GFP-tracking data of primary kidney tumors and lung metastases in orthotopic murine renal cell carcinoma, validated against MRI size distributions; spatial tumor growth model for merging metastases.
finding: The standard theory of independent metastatic growth was rejected—predicted size distributions did not match MRI observations, and proliferation alone could not explain metastatic volume increases. Spatial interactions (merging) between metastatic foci significantly impact global metastatic burden dynamics.
dataset: Orthotopic RENCA-GFP murine renal cell carcinoma model (n=31 GFP-tracked mice, n=6 MRI mice), Balb/c mice
metric: Tumor volume (mm³), metastatic size distribution, doubling time (hours), predicted vs observed macro-metastasis count
baseline: Standard theory of independent metastatic growth (single-cell seeding, autonomous expansion)
result: Required doubling times of 5-8 hours (impossible, minimum mammalian cell cycle ~10h); predicted size distributions inconsistent with MRI at days 19 and 26

problem: Understanding how cell biomechanical effects impact tissue regeneration requires quantitative 3D models, but existing center-based models oversimplify cell shape and mechanics.
method: 3D high-resolution deformable cell model (DCM) with triangulated cell surfaces, viscoelastic elements (Kelvin-Voigt/Maxwell), calibrated against optical stretcher experiments on MDA-MB-231 cells; applied to liver lesion closure, monolayer growth, and multicellular spheroid simulations.
finding: The DCM predicts that migration forces needed to close tissue lesions are much smaller than those predicted by center-based models. Maxwell rheological elements better capture cell relaxation behavior. The model quantitatively reproduces optical stretcher deformation data and liver regeneration dynamics.
dataset: MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells (optical stretcher, N=100), CCl4-induced liver lesion regeneration in mice
metric: Cell deformation (long/short axis lengths), migration force, cortex thickness, cell radius
baseline: Center-based models (CBM) with rigid cell shapes
result: DCM migration forces much smaller than CBM predictions; cell radius 8.8±1.3μm, cortex ~500nm; Maxwell model superior for relaxation

problem: The root cause of progressive dopaminergic cell loss in Parkinson's disease SNc is unknown; the role of STN-induced glutamate excitotoxicity needs computational investigation.
method: Spiking neuron network model (Izhikevich 2D neurons) of SNc-STN-GPe system with stress accumulation and cell death mechanisms, simulated in MATLAB with 0.1s timestep; tested conventional therapies (glutamate inhibition, dopamine restoration, subthalamotomy, DBS).
finding: A positive-feedback loop between SNc cell loss and STN disinhibition creates a runaway neurodegeneration effect once a threshold is crossed. Excitotoxicity initiates via weak excitotoxicity (energy deficit) then transitions to strong excitotoxicity (disinhibited STN). Glutamate inhibition, dopamine restoration, subthalamotomy, and DBS showed superior neuroprotective effects.
dataset: Rat basal ganglia neuronal population model (SNc-STN-GPe), published firing rate data
metric: Firing rate (Hz), synchrony, burst index, SNc cell loss progression (%)
baseline: Healthy basal ganglia state (100% SNc cells), published firing rate data from Modolo et al. 2007 and Tripathy et al. 2014
result: PD symptoms onset after >50% SNc cell loss; threshold-like relationship between SNc loss and STN synchrony

problem: No reliable biomarkers can separate responders from non-responders before anti-PD-1 immunotherapy in NSCLC; quantitative understanding of the anti-tumor immune response is needed.
method: Quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model with 55 ODEs and 53 algebraic equations integrating tumor growth, antigen processing/presentation, T cell dynamics, antibody PK, and immune checkpoint dynamics; calibrated with clinical data; simulated 12 patients from NCT02259621 neoadjuvant nivolumab trial.
finding: In addition to TMB, effector and regulatory T cell counts in tumor and blood predict responders. The model predicted augmented durable response with adjuvant nivolumab after neoadjuvant treatment plus resection. The model captures the variety of clinical responses including fast responders.
dataset: 12 NSCLC patients from NCT02259621 neoadjuvant nivolumab trial, human clinical and experimental data
metric: Tumor size change, T cell counts (Teff/Treg), TMB, MHC/antigen-binding affinity
baseline: Neoadjuvant nivolumab + resection alone (clinical trial protocol NCT02259621)
result: Predicted augmented durable response with added adjuvant nivolumab; TMB + Teff/Treg as combined biomarker

problem: Computational cell cycle models vary widely in paradigm and application; a comprehensive comparative analysis is needed to guide their use in cancer treatment strategy optimization.
method: Systematic review comparing deterministic vs stochastic, single-cell vs population, and mechanistic vs abstract computational cell cycle models; analysis of applications to drug efficacy, drug discovery, resistance, and dose optimization.
finding: Different modeling paradigms have distinct strengths: deterministic models for well-characterized pathways, stochastic for cellular heterogeneity, agent-based for tumor microenvironment interactions. Integration of computational models with clinical data enhances precision oncology and personalized treatment design.
dataset: Published computational cell cycle models and cancer therapy literature (review)

problem: Whether diminished synaptic gain in schizophrenia affects pyramidal cells or interneurons is unclear, and the relationship between synaptic deficits and symptoms is not established across paradigms.
method: Dynamic causal modeling (DCM) of 3 EEG paradigms (resting state, mismatch negativity, 40-Hz ASSR) and resting-state fMRI in PScz (n=108), relatives (n=57), and controls (n=107); parametric empirical Bayes for group-level inference on cortical microcircuit parameters.
finding: All characteristic EEG changes in schizophrenia across 4 paradigms are explained by increased self-inhibition (decreased synaptic gain) in pyramidal cells. Psychotic symptoms relate to disinhibition (decreased self-inhibition) in auditory cortical areas, supporting a primary pyramidal cell deficit compensated by interneuron downregulation.
dataset: PScz (n=108), first-degree relatives (n=57), healthy controls (n=107); EEG + fMRI
metric: EEG spectral power (θ/α/β/γ), MMN amplitude, 40-Hz ASSR power, DCM self-inhibition parameters, BPRS positive/negative symptom scores, p-value (Bonferroni-corrected)
result: PScz: increased θ (Z=2.63, pCorr=.035), decreased β (Z=−2.77, pCorr=.022), increased γ (Z=2.58, pCorr=.040); all explained by pyramidal self-inhibition increase

problem: No whole-brain spiking network model exists for Drosophila despite abundant connectome data; understanding fundamental neural network properties requires such a model.
method: Flysim platform using FlyCircuit database: SPIN machine-learning for arbor polarity prediction, overlap-based synapse prediction, morphology-to-physiology translation, whole-brain spiking network reconstruction (20,089 neurons, 1,044,020 synapses) with synaptic short-term depression; resting state simulation.
finding: The reconstructed brain network was easily stabilized with synaptic short-term depression while a randomized network exhibited seizure-like activity. The reconstructed brain showed structurally and dynamically more diverse activity including both Poisson-like and patterned firing, with broadly distributed E-I indices.
dataset: FlyCircuit database (Drosophila melanogaster), 20,089 neurons, 1,044,020 synapses
metric: Polarity prediction accuracy, firing rate, synchrony, hyperactivity prevalence, E-I balance index distribution
baseline: Randomized brain network (shuffled connections preserving degree)
result: Polarity prediction ~84-92% accuracy; reconstructed brain stable vs randomized seizure-like; two distinct neuron size peaks (local ~858μm vs projection ~1753μm)

problem: CD4+ T cell differentiation produces complex hybrid phenotypes beyond classical lineages, but the environmental conditions driving these mixed phenotypes are poorly characterized.
method: Mechanistic logic-based (probabilistic Boolean control network) computational model of 38 signaling components and 96 interactions regulating Th1/Th2/Th17/iTreg differentiation; state space analysis under 511 environmental conditions with varying cytokine compositions and dosages via Cell Collective platform.
finding: The model predicts classical plus novel mixed T cell phenotypes (Th1-Th2, Th1-iTreg, Th17-iTreg, etc.) that match experimentally observed hybrid states. Lineage decisions are regulated by both composition and dosage of extracellular signals, with specific input patterns enabling transitions between canonical and complex phenotypes.
dataset: In silico CD4+ T cell differentiation model (38 components, 9 inputs, 511 conditions)
metric: Transcription factor activity levels (Tbet, GATA3, RORγt, Foxp3), ergodic set analysis, dose-response curves
baseline: Classical single-lineage T cell phenotypes (Th0, Th1, Th2, Th17, iTreg)
result: 511 conditions yielded classical + 6 novel mixed phenotypes; dose-dependent transitions between canonical and hybrid states

problem: ATF filtration modeling relies on empirically derived formulas with oversimplified lumped parameters, failing to capture localized fluid behavior and fouling dynamics in perfusion cell culture.
method: 2D axisymmetric porous CFD model validated against experimental ATF pressure profiles using water and CHO cell culture supernatant at multiple cross-flow velocities (0.11-0.70 m/s); analysis of localized flux, Starling flow, and fouling resistance distribution.
finding: CFD provided first numerical evidence of Starling flow in ATF systems. Higher feed cross-flow velocity increases localized permeate flux but also increases reversible and irreversible fouling. At typical 2 L/m²/h flux, only ~50% of membrane length is utilized per phase, achieving full utilization over one complete ATF cycle.
dataset: CHO cell line perfusion bioreactor (2L, 29 days, ~30M cells/ml), polysulfone hollow fiber membrane (0.2μm, 470cm²)
metric: RMSE (inlet/permeate pressure), localized flux (L/m²/h), fouling resistance, membrane utilization (%)
baseline: Empirically derived ATF models with lumped average parameters (Kelly et al. 2014)
result: RMSE 0.02-0.19 psi (water validation); ~50% membrane utilization per phase; Starling flow numerically confirmed

problem: Tip cell overtaking during angiogenic sprouting
method: Cellular Potts models of angiogenesis
finding: Tip cell overtaking occurs spontaneously from stochastic motion

problem: ABMs for mechano-chemo-biological cellular responses
method: Agent-based model with literature-derived rules
finding: Rule-set confidence scoring for 19 rules

problem: Stem cell aging decline in tissue function
method: Artificial genome multiscale model of transcriptional regulation
finding: In silico stem cell aging modeling

problem: Reproducibility of published neuroscience research
method: Computational reproduction methods
metric: MSE

problem: Computational model for cell flow in microchannels
method: PyOIF software for cell flow modeling
finding: Demonstrated cell flow simulation in bifurcations

problem: Intratumoral hypoxia fate mapping
method: In vivo fate mapping of hypoxic cells
finding: Characterized hypoxic/post-hypoxic tumor phenotype
dataset: mouse tumors

problem: Pulmonary fibrosis pathologic remodeling
method: In vitro fibroblast proliferation studies with TGF-β1/PGE2
finding: TGF-β1 and PGE2 dose-dependent fibroblast modulation
dataset: IMR-90 fibroblasts
metric: correlation

problem: Eukaryotic cell membrane and lipid raft study
method: Advanced analytical methods for membrane characterization
finding: Resolution improved 5000x from 1μm to 0.2nm

problem: Computational cardiac electrophysiology modeling
method: Cardiac electrophysiology computational methods

problem: CD19 CAR T-cell therapy response prediction
method: Computational model of CAR T-cell dynamics
finding: Critical determinants of remission, resistance, relapse
dataset: patient data
metric: correlation

problem: How does heterogeneous mitochondrial arrangement in cardiomyocytes affect metabolite distributions and force dynamics, given that cardiac mitochondria are not arranged in crystalline columns as previously assumed?
method: 2D finite element modeling of cardiac bioenergetics on computational meshes derived from serial block face scanning electron microscopy (SBF-SEM) images, coupling ODE oxidative phosphorylation models with PDEs for metabolite diffusion.
finding: Heterogeneous mitochondrial distributions cause significant spatial variation in Pi, Cr, and PCr concentrations, but the creatine kinase shuttle maintains near-uniform ATP/ADP ratios under normoxia. Under hypoxia, this buffering fails, leading to non-uniform force generation.
dataset: SBF-SEM images of one complete rat cardiac cell, 3 cross-sections
metric: spatial concentration gradients of phosphagens (ATP, ADP, Pi, Cr, PCr), twitch force variation
baseline: idealized crystal lattice-like mitochondrial geometries from previous computational studies
result: mitochondrial area fraction 45-55%, near-uniform ATP/ADP in normoxia, significant gradients under hypoxia

problem: To what extent can internal representations of computational object-vision models explain the representational geometry of inferior temporal (IT) cortex, and does supervised vs unsupervised training matter?
method: Compared representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) of 37 computational models (HMAX, VisNet, SIFT, GIST, deep CNN) against human IT (fMRI) and monkey IT (cell recordings) for 96 stimuli using Kendall tau-A correlation.
finding: A deep supervised convolutional network (trained on 1.2M labeled images) best explained IT representation. Unsupervised models failed to explain categorical clustering in IT. Remixing deep network features with category-discriminating linear combinations fully explained the IT data.
dataset: 96 object images, human IT fMRI (N=4), monkey IT cell recordings (674 neurons, 2 monkeys)
metric: Kendall tau-A RDM correlation, categorization performance, noise ceiling
baseline: 27 not-strongly-supervised models (HMAX, VisNet, SIFT, GIST, unsupervised CNN, etc.)
result: deep supervised CNN highest RDM correlation; remixed deep features fully reached noise ceiling

problem: How do fluctuations in epigenetic cofactors (metabolites used by histone-modifying enzymes) affect epigenetic landscapes and cell state transitions during aging and disease?
method: Stochastic model of epigenetic regulation using Chemical Master Equation with Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) to generate ensemble of ER system configurations reflecting cofactor variability. Bifurcation analysis of mean-field QSSA equations.
finding: Heterogeneity in epigenetic metabolites (HDM/HDAC levels) stochastically drives phenotypic variability, producing resilient states (large epigenetic barriers) and plastic states (lowered barriers sensitive to reprogramming). Fine-tuning cofactor levels can redirect between these states.
dataset: computational ensemble of epigenetic regulation configurations (differentiation- and pluripotency-promoting genes)
metric: epigenetic barrier height, bistability region boundaries, cell state transition probability
baseline: previous stochastic epigenetic models by Dodd et al. and Menéndez et al.
result: distinct resilient/plastic epi-states emerge from cofactor variability; metabolism-responsive loss of epigenetic resilience as aging mechanism

problem: How to develop a robust computational method for solving coupled bulk-surface reaction-diffusion equations on evolving 2D domains, applicable to cell migration and chemotaxis?
method: Moving mesh finite element method using a novel moving mesh PDE (MMPDE) approach for adaptive grid generation, with a conservative ALE scheme for bulk-surface coupled reaction-diffusion equations.
finding: The method achieves second-order spatial and temporal accuracy on model problems with known solutions. Applied to cell migration, it predicts a novel mechanism where cells increase persistence and polarity by generating their own local chemoattractant gradients.
dataset: model problems with known analytical solutions; simulated eukaryotic cell migration
metric: spatial and temporal convergence order
result: second-order spatial and temporal accuracy

problem: What mechanism underlies the lasting aftereffects of transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS), and how do stimulation parameters affect network remodeling?
method: Recurrent network model of excitatory and inhibitory leaky integrate-and-fire neurons with homeostatically regulated structural plasticity, simulated in NEST. Systematic testing of electrode size, montage, intensity, and duration.
finding: Weak subthreshold tDCS perturbs homeostatic equilibrium, triggering synaptic growth response and cell assembly formation among stimulated neurons. Strong focal stimulation enhances connectivity within assemblies, and repetitive stimulation with well-chosen duty cycles further increases impact.
dataset: simulated cortical network (excitatory and inhibitory neurons)
metric: firing rate modulation, synaptic connectivity changes, cell assembly strength
result: ±0.1 mV polarization causes >±10% firing rate change; repetitive stimulation with alternating polarization induces highest connectivity changes

problem: Can an artificial neural network predict the output power of mono-crystalline, multi-crystalline, and amorphous photovoltaic cells under varying light intensity and temperature conditions?
method: Three-layer ANN (input: light intensity and temperature; output: maximum output power) using Tansig/Purelin transfer functions and Traingdm training, with 62 training and 10 validation data points per cell type.
finding: ANN predictions closely match experimental data. Optimal hidden neuron count varies by cell type: 3-4 for multi- and amorphous, 8 for mono-crystalline. Amorphous cells are least susceptible to external conditions but have lowest efficiency.
dataset: 3 PV cell types (mono-, multi-, amorphous crystalline), 72 data points each (6 light intensities × 12 temperatures)
metric: correlation coefficient (R), mean squared error (MSE)
result: R=0.99988 for mono (n=8), R=0.99924 for multi (n=3), R=0.99928 for amorphous (n=4)

problem: How do cell-matrix adhesion dynamics explain the diversity of lymphocyte motility patterns (floating, stepping, pivoting, persistent vs Brownian walks) observed on different extracellular matrix substrates?
method: Cellular Potts model extended with Act model for actin-driven propulsion and dynamic cell-matrix adhesions (de novo formation, growth, shrinkage, rupture, and feedback onto propulsion).
finding: Increasing attachment area and adhesion strength decreases cell speed and persistence. The model recapitulates floating, stepping, and pivoting behaviors. Strongly adherent cells show short-term persistent but long-term subdiffusive motility. Small adhesions at the front allow more persistent motility than larger clusters at the back.
dataset: simulated lymphocyte motility on ECM substrates (fibronectin, ICAM, casein analogs)
metric: cell speed, persistence, mean squared displacement (MSD) exponent, autocorrelation of velocity
result: subdiffusive MSD exponent <1 for strongly adherent cells; recapitulates B cell floating/stepping/pivoting and T cell walking/gliding

problem: What is the functional significance of the phasic firing pattern (long bursts and silences lasting tens of seconds) in vasopressin neurons for encoding afferent input information?
method: Integrate-and-fire model with activity-dependent slow depolarising afterpotential (DAP) via calcium-inactivated potassium leak current, modulated by dynorphin release. Compared phasic and non-phasic neuron populations' encoding properties.
finding: Phasic firing population responds linearly to increases in tonic synaptic input (0-10 spikes/s range), while non-phasic populations show nonlinear responses. Phasic cells encode transient input changes independently of background activity, providing functional advantages for information coding.
dataset: in vivo recordings from rat supraoptic nucleus vasopressin neurons
metric: ISI distribution, hazard function, burst duration, silence duration, mean firing rate, population response linearity
baseline: non-phasic neuron population (identical cells without phasic firing mechanism)
result: linear response in 0-10 spikes/s range for phasic cells; mean burst 154s, silence 16s, intraburst rate 8.91 Hz

problem: Do the two proposed lumen formation mechanisms in angiogenesis — vacuolation and cell-cell repulsion — work independently in different vessel types, or can they act synergistically?
method: Agent-based computational model using compartmental Cellular Potts model (CPM) with modules for cell surface polarization, pinocytotic vesicle/vacuole formation, and cell-cell repulsion. Extensive sensitivity analysis across parameter space.
finding: Each mechanism alone produces lumens only in a narrow parameter region, but combining both mechanisms makes lumen formation much more robust, suggesting they work synergistically in parallel rather than in different vessel types.
dataset: simulated branched cord of 12 endothelial cells in ECM (2D cross-section)
metric: lumen formation success across parameter space, lumen continuity
baseline: each mechanism tested independently (vacuolation alone, cell-cell repulsion alone)
result: combined mechanisms produce lumens across much wider parameter space than either alone

problem: How does WNT/CTNNB1 signaling regulate the dynamic distribution and complex state of endogenous CTNNB1 across subcellular compartments, and what are the key regulatory nodes?
method: CRISPR/Cas9 knock-in of SGFP2 tag at endogenous CTNNB1 locus in haploid HAP1 cells, combined with quantitative live-cell microscopy (FCS, N&B analysis, FRAP), and ODE-based computational modeling of WNT signaling.
finding: A substantial fraction of CTNNB1 resides in slow-diffusing cytoplasmic complexes regardless of pathway activation status, but the complex undergoes major size reduction upon WNT activation. Three regulatory nodes control CTNNB1 distribution: the destruction complex, nucleocytoplasmic shuttling, and nuclear retention.
dataset: HAP1 haploid human cell line with SGFP2-CTNNB1 and SGFP2-CTNNB1(S45F) knock-in
metric: absolute CTNNB1 concentration (nM), diffusion coefficient, molecular brightness, nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio
baseline: unstimulated (physiological) vs WNT3A-treated and CHIR99021-treated (hyperactivated) conditions
result: 1.7-fold cytoplasmic and 3.0-fold nuclear increase after WNT3A; ~160 nM cytoplasmic CTNNB1 concentration

problem: Hematopoietic lineage commitment regulatory circuitry
method: Commitment-switch computational model of gene interactions
finding: Model reproduces bistable B-cell lineage commitment
dataset: human CD34+ cells

problem: Scalable iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte production
method: 3D spinner flask differentiation process
finding: Established baseline for scalable cardiomyocyte production
dataset: human iPSC LiPSC-18R

problem: Somatic mutation processes in cancer genomes
method: Mathematical model of mutational signature classification
finding: Four classes of somatic mutations with distinct signatures
dataset: cancer genomes

problem: Theory of biological tissue organization
method: Cellular automaton for tissue expansion
finding: Demonstrated tissue formation from single cells

problem: Intermediate band solar cell material discovery
method: Computational screening of 2100 compounds
finding: Novel IB materials identified for solar cells
dataset: 2100 compounds
metric: correlation
result: 63.2% theoretical

problem: Mathematical models of mammalian circadian clock
method: Review of cell-autonomous circadian oscillator models

problem: O-LM cell biophysical modeling in hippocampus
method: Multi-compartment models with 9 ion channels
finding: Examined spiking differences with different synaptic inputs
dataset: rat hippocampal cells

problem: Cardiac progenitor cell exosomes for RVHF repair
method: 2D vs 3D CPC culture with exosome analysis
finding: Donor-specific differential responses to exosome treatment
dataset: 3 human CPC donors
metric: correlation

problem: Cerebellar stellate cell functional properties
method: Electrophysiology with detailed model generation
finding: Validated computational stellate cell models
dataset: mouse cerebellum
metric: correlation

problem: HSC lineage specification biological inducers
method: Primary mouse cell culture growth profiling
finding: Complex feedback mechanisms in HSC differentiation
dataset: mouse HSCs
metric: sensitivity

problem: Predicting and controlling embryonic stem cell differentiation within 3D embryoid bodies is poorly understood, limiting regenerative medicine applications.
method: Rules-based agent modeling with physics-based aggregation simulation of embryoid bodies, where cells are modeled as incompressible rigid spheres with stochastic binary Oct4 state transitions and intercellular communication rules.
finding: Loss of Oct4 pluripotency can be modeled as a binary stochastic process with intercellular communication rules, and the emergent spatial patterns are independent of EB structure, size, or cell division.
dataset: Mouse embryonic stem cells (D3 cell line), embryoid bodies of 250 and 1000 cells
metric: Radius, circularity, connection count, connection length, Oct4 spatial pattern classification
baseline: Experimental confocal microscopy Oct4 expression patterns over 7 days
result: Surface area-to-volume ratio ~8% error vs sphere, simulated aggregate properties match experimental EBs

problem: Computational models of cellular electroporation are difficult to validate against quantitative molecular uptake data, limiting understanding of membrane permeabilization dynamics.
method: Two-stage ordinary differential equation model coupling reversible membrane defect formation with irreversible solute transport, fit using Nelder-Mead minimization against fluorescence microscopy time-series data.
finding: HEPES buffer enables 3- to 5-fold faster molecular transport than PBS or SFDF; interpulse delay up to 100 μs does not appreciably affect uptake in multipulse schemes; per-pulse permeability enhancement decreases with pulse number.
dataset: Single adherent cells in microfluidic chamber, 3 buffers (PBS, SFDF, HEPES), 4 field strengths (170-400 kV/m), 4 pulse durations (1-1000 μs)
metric: Sum of squared residuals (SSR), molecular uptake time-series fit
baseline: Experimental quantitative propidium uptake measurements from Part I
result: max(SSR_PBS)=0.025, max(SSR_SFDF)=0.0070, max(SSR_HEPES)=0.0066; δ 3-5x larger for HEPES

problem: As biological network models grow in size and complexity, efficient methods for validating and analyzing their dynamical behavior are lacking.
method: Model verification (automated unitary testing of logical model specifications) combined with value propagation for analytical computation of environmental/genetic perturbation effects, applied to a Boolean logical model of T cell activation with >200 components and >450 interactions.
finding: Model verification enables continuous testing during model development; value propagation predicts differential potential of CTLA4 vs PD-1 checkpoint immunotherapies on T cell activation.
dataset: T cell activation signaling network including CTLA4 and PD-1 checkpoint inhibitory pathways
metric: Dynamical specification compliance, stable state reachability
baseline: Manual iterative trial simulations for model validation
result: Comprehensive model with >200 components, >450 interactions; differential CTLA4 vs PD-1 predictions confirmed by Jupyter notebooks

problem: Understanding mechanisms of action of endocrine-active chemicals on steroidogenesis requires computational models that can predict steroid concentration responses in vitro.
method: Mechanistic ODE model of adrenal steroidogenesis incorporating enzymatic reactions and transport pathways, with iterative parameter estimation using MATLAB, validated against time-course LC/MS measurements of 14 steroids in H295R cells.
finding: The model accurately predicts steroid concentrations under control and metyrapone exposure conditions; metyrapone inhibits CYP11B1 causing cortisol decrease and precursor accumulation in a dose-dependent manner.
dataset: Human H295R adrenocortical carcinoma cells, 14 steroids measured, metyrapone at 1 and 10 μM
metric: R-squared for transport equilibrium fit, visual correspondence of time-course predictions
baseline: Experimental time-course steroid concentrations (control cells, 5 time points)
result: Transport R² = 0.70-0.99 across 14 steroids; convergence in ~24 min on Intel Core 2 Duo

problem: Understanding collective cell migration in 3D remains elusive, particularly how microscopic forces and cell-cell/cell-matrix receptor interactions influence group motility and stability.
method: 3D computational framework modeling cells as overlapping spheres with coarse-grained surface receptor sites for cell-cell adhesion and cell-matrix traction, incorporating ECM viscous resistance and stochastic force components.
finding: Cell pair stability depends on traction force magnitude and receptor dynamics; mobile surface receptors promote stronger intercellular stability; matrix resistance, cellular stiffness, and adhesion intensity contribute differently to migration with memory effects.
dataset: Simulated cell pairs and 50-cell clusters with varying receptor dynamics (4 cases of static/dynamic C-C and C-M sites)
metric: Center-to-center distance, bound vs detached state classification, pair stability over simulation time
baseline: Four receptor dynamics cases (static/dynamic combinations of cell-cell and cell-matrix sites)
result: Simulation time scale of days; 50-cell cluster motility demonstrated; memory effects alter pair motility

problem: The mechanobiological cascade leading to medical device-related pressure ulcers at the cell scale is not well understood, particularly how deformation-inflicted damage and inflammatory edema interact.
method: Cell-scale finite element modeling of 30 adipocytes near a bony prominence (nasal bridge) using Neo-Hookean hyperelastic material, with a strain-time cell death prediction model and sequential simulation of deformation and edema-induced interstitial pressure.
finding: Cells adjacent to bony surfaces die first due to adipose-bone stiffness gradients; initial cell death triggers inflammatory edema that escalates further cell damage in a nonlinear, cumulative vicious cycle pattern.
dataset: 30 mature adipocytes (70 μm diameter) near nasal bone, CPAP ventilation mask scenario
metric: Effective stress distribution, plasma membrane strain, volumetric PM stress exposure histograms
baseline: Deformation-only loading vs combined deformation + edema pressure loading
result: Cell death onset within minutes at high strain; nonlinear escalation demonstrated across 4 simulation steps

problem: The regulatory mechanisms by which Langerhans cells orchestrate adaptive immune responses in the skin, particularly through interferon regulatory factors, are not established.
method: Boolean Petri net-based modeling of IRF gene regulatory network, parameterized with transcription factor expression values from whole transcriptome microarray analysis (Affymetrix), with in vitro validation using T cell activation assays.
finding: IRF1, IRF4, and IRF8 form a key regulatory network in LCs; TNFα and TSLP differentially regulate IRF expression leading to differential CD8 T cell activation; PI3Kγ inhibition shifts LC function from Th2 toward Th1 responses.
dataset: Primary human Langerhans cells stimulated with TNFα or TSLP, EBV-specific CD8 T cell lines
metric: IFN-γ ELISpot counts, transcriptome fold change (BETR p<0.05), Pearson correlation (r>0.8)
baseline: Unstimulated migratory LCs (time 0), DMSO control for PI3Kγ inhibition
result: 527 differentially regulated genes, 18 clusters identified; in silico predictions confirmed by IFN-γ T cell assays

problem: The role of periglomerular cells in controlling mitral cell spiking in the olfactory bulb, relative to granule cells, is not fully understood.
method: Reduced multicompartmental conductance-based model (6 compartments) of the periglomerular cell in NEURON simulator, integrated into a cell triad model with mitral and granule cells to study inhibitory interactions.
finding: Periglomerular cells are more effective than granule cells at inhibiting mitral cells and are the only ones capable of complete inhibition; granule cells facilitate PG cell inhibition by enlarging the parameter range for complete mitral cell suppression.
dataset: Computational model of olfactory bulb cell triad (periglomerular, mitral, granule cells)
metric: Firing rate, spike latency, inhibition completeness, parameter sensitivity (conductance densities)
baseline: Granule cell inhibition alone vs combined PG + granule cell inhibition
result: Input resistance 1190 MΩ (matching experimental ~1000 MΩ); stepwise firing rate control demonstrated

problem: Cell characteristics evolve with increasing passage number in vitro, but the mechanisms driving these changes and their impact on experimental reproducibility are poorly understood.
method: Random walk lattice-based model simulating in vitro cell culture passaging (passage 0-30) with heterogeneous proliferation rates and passage-induced damage, combined with in silico scratch assays to assess functional consequences.
finding: Average proliferation rate can either increase or decrease with passaging depending on competition between initial heterogeneity and passage-induced damage; scratch assay closure rates differ significantly between passage numbers, suggesting passage number should always be reported.
dataset: Simulated near-homogeneous and heterogeneous cell lines, passages 0-30, lattice size 4168×3610
metric: Mean proliferation rate, standard deviation of proliferation rate, scratch assay closure rate
baseline: Passage 0 (initial state) vs passage 30; near-homogeneous vs heterogeneous cell lines; no damage vs varying damage levels
result: Dramatic proliferation rate shift by passage 30 in heterogeneous lines; significantly different scratch closure rates across passage numbers

problem: Discovering transcription factors for cell reprogramming is largely trial and error; computational models that account for cellular heterogeneity and predict long-term reprogramming outcomes are lacking.
method: scREMOTE integrates single-cell multiomics (scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq) to compute regulation potential from CRE accessibility, TF motifs, and chromatin conformation, then uses linear regression to iteratively predict long-term TF perturbation effects.
finding: scREMOTE successfully predicts that Gata3 overexpression reprograms transit-amplifying cells toward inner root sheath and Runx1 toward hair shaft lineages, capturing higher-order gene regulations in hair follicle development.
dataset: Hair follicle developmental system (simultaneous scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq), 35 highly expressed TFs
metric: Empirical p-value against random TF-gene pairs, regulation potential enrichment across 5 databases
baseline: Random subsets of TF-gene pairs (1 million resamples per database); known regulations from TRRUST, hTFtarget, TFBSDB, RegNetwork, MSigDB
result: All 5 databases significantly enriched (p<0.05 to p<1e-6); successful prediction of Gata3→IRS and Runx1→Hair Shaft reprogramming

problem: Abstract T cell recognition of a cognate peptide–major histocompatibility complex (pMHC) presented on the surface of infected or malignant cells is of
method: Methods Models selected for analysis First, we gathered all publicly available models and excluded those which we were unable to perform comparative a
finding: Results Evaluating model performance in predicting peptide immunogenicity To perform an unbiased evaluation of existing models in predicting immunogen

problem: BioNumbers (http://www.bionumbers.hms.harvard.edu) is a database of key numbers in molecular and cell biology--the quantitative properties of biologic
method: METHODS The software is implemented as a Microsoft ASP.NET 2.0 web application with a Microsoft SQL Server 2005 database. The search is performed with
finding: FUTURE PLANS AND CONCLUSIONS BioNumbers is a dynamic database that is being continuously expanded and advanced. The support of the scientific communit

problem: We propose a three-dimensional computational model to simulate the transient deformation of suspended cancer cells flowing through a constricted micro
method: Problem statement and computational method Problem description We consider an initially spherical cancer cell flowing through a constricted channel, a
finding: Results and discussion In this section, we conduct extensive parametric studies of the effects of mechanical properties of the cell membrane, cytoplas

method: 2 HIGH THROUGHPUT SINGLE‐CELL RNA SEQUENCING TECHNOLOGY: EXPERIMENTAL PROCEDURES The throughput of scRNA‐seq increases from a few cells per experiment
finding: 5 CONCLUSION REMARKS AND FUTURE PERSPECTIVES Single‐cell RNA sequencing has proven as one of the transforming technologies in life sciences over the p

problem: Ectopic heartbeats can trigger reentrant arrhythmias, leading to ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death. Such events have been attributed t
method: Methods Ventricular myocyte model We have developed a three-dimensional (3D) spatial model of a single myocyte based on the (non-spatial) Greenstein-W
finding: Results Model properties In order to reproduce protocols designed to measure ECC properties, membrane potential was stepped to varying test potentials

problem: The epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) signaling pathway is one of the most important pathways that regulate growth, survival, proliferation, and
method: Supplementary Material Supplementary PDF 1 Supplementary PDF 2 Supplementary SBML 1 --- ---

problem: The use of genetic tools, imaging technologies and ex vivo culture systems has provided significant insights into the role of tissue inducer cells and
method: MATERIALS AND METHODS MODEL DEVELOPMENT To develop a computer simulation of lymphoid tissue formation, an agent-based approach to complex systems mode
finding: REDUCING UNCERTAINTY IN SIMULATION RESULTS Prior to performing any analysis of simulation results, it is important to establish the number of replicat

problem: We develop a parameter estimation method based on approximate Bayesian computation (ABC) for a stochastic cell invasion model using fluorescent cell c
method: 3. Methods 3.1. Simulation model We adopt the discrete random walk model developed by Simpson et al. [ 7 ] on a 2D hexagonal lattice. Each lattice sit

problem: Our quantitative predictions of absolute molecular concentrations and reaction fluxes have major biological implications: Model comparison provides ev
method: Electronic supplementary material The online version of this article (doi:10.1186/s12918-015-0147-1) contains supplementary material, which is availab
finding: Results Here we present a novel quantitative kinetic model for the interactions of the key players PKD, phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase III beta (PI4KII

problem: Unipolar Brush Cells (UBCs) have been suggested to play a critical role in cerebellar functioning, yet the corresponding cellular mechanisms remain po
method: Methods This work presents a combined modeling (I) and experimental analysis (II) of UBC electroresponsiveness, in which the models was accurately mat
finding: Results UBC multi-compartmental model: channel distribution and passive properties UBCs (Figure 1A ) were recorded from the granular layer of lamina X

problem: Developing a computational model to predict mucin-type O-linked glycan biosynthesis pathways in CHO cells transfected with various glycosyltransferase combinations.
method: ODE-based kinetic modeling using GlycoSim (XML) and COPASI simulator with Evolution Strategy (SRES) parameter estimation and LSODA deterministic time-course simulations.
finding: The optimized model accurately predicted highly processed glycan abundances but was less accurate for early-pathway structures; a combined multi-experiment model could not accurately predict all glycome profiles, suggesting additional factors (Golgi pH, hypoxia) need incorporation.
dataset: CHO-K1 cells, 25 glycosyltransferase combinations, PSGL-1/mIgG2b with 53+ O-glycosylation sites
metric: Simulated vs observed glycan abundance (concentration at TGN compartment), standard deviation weighting
baseline: Experimental glycan profiles from mass spectrometry (intensity-adjusted for charged glycans: 0.3x singly, 0.4x doubly charged)
result: Per-experiment model closely matched experimental data; combined 4-experiment model showed discrepancies across conditions

problem: Understanding the dynamic interplay between cadherin-mediated cell adhesion and Wnt signaling pathway through the balance of β-catenin distributions.
method: Network model decomposed into three modules (cell adhesion, degradation circle, transcriptional regulation) using stochastic simulations with calibrated kinetic parameters from experimental measurements.
finding: Cell adhesion affects Wnt signaling both negatively (sequestering β-catenins, slowing release from membrane destruction complexes) and positively (cadherin clustering increases β-catenin phosphorylation rate promoting nuclear signaling); cadherin switch and Axin oscillation phenomena were reproduced.
dataset: Parameterized from multiple cell lines, subcellular system at 2.5% cell volume (~10^-12 L), 30 initial free ABC molecules
metric: Semi-quantitative accuracy reproducing experimental kinetic responses (β-catenin fold changes, phosphorylation dynamics)
baseline: Experimental measurements: β-catenin 6-fold increase under Wnt, GSK3-phosphorylated β-catenin recovery in 2hr, CK1-phosphorylated 3-fold increase
result: Qualitatively reproduced 5 key experimental kinetic phenomena with semi-quantitative accuracy

problem: Investigating whether exploratory adaptation explains how glioblastoma cells from different tumor anatomic regions cope with micro-environmental pressures and achieve phenotypic diversity.
method: Dynamical model simulating gene regulatory network perturbations (4121 nodes, 78,686 edges) with pathway activity-based phenotype definitions, using TCGA RNA-Seq (672 gliomas) and Ivy-GAP spatial transcriptomics (270 samples, 41 tumors).
finding: Stochastic exploration of regulatory network structure confers adaptive capacity; some phenotypes are inherently plastic while others are rigid; phenotypic paths are constrained to pass through specific spatial-phenotypic ranges even with exploratory capacity.
dataset: TCGA LGG+GBM (516 LGG, 156 GBM), Ivy-GAP (122 samples from 10 tumors, 5 anatomic regions), 186 KEGG pathways
metric: Phenotype distance (pathway activity vectors), fold change ≥2, -log10 FDR ≥5
baseline: Intrinsic phenotypic drift without environmental challenge (control simulation)
result: 2975 differentially expressed genes identified; demonstrated CT→CTpan, CTpan→LE, CT→CTmvp, CTmvp→LE, CTmvp→IT, CT→LE phenotypic transitions

problem: Reviewing how cell-based computational modeling can integrate single-cell transcriptomic data with morphogenetic models in plants, particularly addressing spatial localization reconstruction.
method: Review of cell-based computational modeling approaches (vertex models, cell-centered models) combined with single-cell RNA-seq and cell-resolution imaging techniques for plant morphogenesis.
finding: Cell-based computational models can help solve the inverse problem of restoring individual cells' spatial localization from single-cell transcriptomic data; plasmodesmata-mediated cell-to-cell communication topology must be considered in plant scRNA-seq analysis.
dataset: Plant tissues (Arabidopsis root tip, various plant organs)

problem: Reviewing computational modeling approaches to investigate the abnormal biomechanics and biorheology of diabetic red blood cells and their adverse effects on microcirculation in type 2 diabetes.
