method: Review of multiscale numerical frameworks including dissipative particle dynamics (DPD), transport DPD, coarse-grained RBC models, and coagulation cascade modeling (23 factors, 21 coupled advection-diffusion reaction equations).
finding: Diabetic RBCs show reduced deformability, enhanced aggregation, and altered tank-treading dynamics; the area-to-volume (A/V) ratio is a key determinant of RBC traversal across small capillaries; increased flow resistance in diabetic blood exceeds normal blood.
dataset: Normal and diabetic RBC models (N-RBC, D-RBC1-3), 3D rectangular microchannel (110×20×20 μm), patient-specific microfluidic experiments
metric: Axial/transverse diameter, membrane fluctuation height, cell transition speed, tank-treading frequency
result: D-RBC1 and D-RBC2 show faster tank-treading than N-RBC; D-RBC3 exhibits slower rotation; decreased A/V ratio leads to decreased cell transition speed

problem: Understanding how single cells locally modulate chemoattractant concentrations through membrane-bound enzyme breakdown, enabling chemotaxis in otherwise saturating gradient conditions.
method: Bulk-surface computational model extending a pseudopod-centred cell polarization model with extracellular attractant breakdown by membrane-bound phosphodiesterase, parametrized using Dictyostelium/cAMP data.
finding: Individual cells can substantially modulate their local attractant field under physiological conditions; membrane-bound enzyme breakdown reduces receptor occupancy from saturating levels to near-Kd concentrations, enabling efficient chemotaxis in otherwise saturating fields.
dataset: Dictyostelium cells chemotaxing toward cyclic AMP, Kd=30 nM
metric: Receptor occupancy, ligand concentration field, chemotactic efficiency
baseline: No-breakdown condition (receptor interaction alone has limited effect on linear ligand field)
result: At Vmax=1000 nmoles/10^7 cells/min, receptor occupancy reduced from 95% saturation to Kd-comparable levels (~30 nM)

problem: Quantifying the mechanism by which neuronal growth cones sense and align with nanograting topography through filopodial mechanosensing.
method: Synergistic approach combining finite element (FE) stress analysis, closed-form analytic equations, and in silico simulations (CX3D Java environment) to model filopodial interaction with nanogratings.
finding: Neuritic terminal geometry and adhesion constraint distribution are key for cell/substrate coupling; analytic equations accurately predicted alignment as a function of ridge width and depth; in silico simulations matched biological experiments.
dataset: PC12 cells on nanogratings (periods 1, 1.5, 2 μm) and flat substrate, 323 terminals analyzed
metric: R², alignment angle, tortuosity, p-value (Mann-Whitney U test)
baseline: Biological experiments on PC12 cells growing on nanogratings and flat substrates
result: R²=0.96 (ridge width vs alignment), R²=0.97 (ridge depth vs alignment); no significant difference from biology (alignment p=0.3571, tortuosity p=0.2236)

problem: Finding exact traveling wave solutions and validating numerical schemes for the fractional mathematical model of HIV-1 infection of CD4+ T-cells with antiviral drug therapy.
method: Three analytical methods (modified Khater, extended simplest equation, sech-tanh) through Atangana-Baleanu fractional derivative, validated numerically via septic-B-spline scheme.
finding: The modified Khater method produced the most accurate solutions among the three analytical approaches; septic B-spline is the only B-spline family member applicable to equations with higher order derivative terms.
dataset: HIV-1 infection of CD4+ T-cells fractional mathematical model
metric: Absolute error between exact and numerical solutions
baseline: Exact analytical solutions from modified Khater, extended simplest equation, and sech-tanh methods
result: Modified Khater: absolute error ~1.24E-06; extended simplest equation: ~1.25E-09; sech-tanh: ~2.15E-04

problem: Creating and validating multi-cell computational models that predict collective chemokine, cytokine, and cellular biomarker profiles in inflamed or cancerous tissue microenvironments.
method: ODE-based single-cell computational models (using Michaelis-Menten and mass action kinetics) combined into multi-cell models incorporating autocrine/paracrine signaling, with machine learning for parameter estimation.
finding: Multi-cell computational models accurately predicted collective biomarker profiles; models became personalized when cell line-specific genomic data were included, correctly predicting MM cell line-specific immunosuppressive effects on dendritic cells.
dataset: GE keratinocytes + dendritic cells + helper T lymphocytes; MM cell lines MM.1S and U266B1; 18+ cell type models
metric: Prediction accuracy (% matched chemokine/cytokine responses), Fisher's exact test p-value
baseline: Observed chemokine/cytokine responses from laboratory multi-cell cultures
result: LPS: 75% accuracy (15/20, p=0.02069); Pam3CSK4: 80% (16/20, p=0.005909); MM co-culture: 75% (3/4) cytokine + 100% (6/6) DC marker inhibition

problem: Understanding how the BR-induced fast response pathway components at the plasma membrane are sufficient to initiate cellular elongation processes in Arabidopsis root epidermis cells.
method: ODE-based mathematical model of BR perception and signaling (4 compartments: cytosol, cell wall, vacuole, PM) combined with HPTS pH measurements and MIFE electrophysiology in Arabidopsis root tips.
finding: BR response competence predominantly depends on H+-ATPase amount and activity in the PM; the model predicted a required cation influx channel to compensate apoplastic acidification, which was subsequently identified and experimentally validated as a potassium channel.
dataset: Arabidopsis thaliana root tip epidermis (Col-0 wild type, bri1-301 mutant), meristematic and elongation zones
metric: Apoplastic pH (HPTS ratio 458/405), H+ flux (MIFE), membrane potential hyperpolarization, p-value
result: Statistically significant BR-induced apoplastic acidification in EZ (p≤0.001); model correctly predicted potassium channel requirement confirmed experimentally

problem: Which model of NK cell education—two-step selection or sequential—best explains the impact of MHC class I on the Ly49 receptor repertoire?
method: Mathematical modeling of two NK cell education schemes (sequential and two-step selection) fitted to flow cytometry data of Ly49 receptor expression in single MHC class I mice, with parameter sweeps over binding strengths and maturation thresholds.
finding: The two-step selection model fits biological data better than the sequential model; MHC class I favors maturation of NK cells with one or few self-receptors (positive selection), and Ly49 receptors are more promiscuous in MHC class I binding than previously thought.
dataset: Single MHC class I mice (H2Kb, H2Db, H2Dd, H2Ld) and MHC-deficient C57BL/6 splenocyte NK cells
metric: Model fit to observed Ly49 receptor co-expression frequencies (singletons, pairs, triplets)
baseline: Sequential model of NK cell education
result: Two-step selection model provides superior fit across all four single MHC class I backgrounds

problem: How do stress, strain, and deformation behave in single plant cells during drying at different moisture levels, and can this be computationally simulated in 3D?
method: A 3D coupled Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) and Coarse-Grained (CG) meshfree computational model simulating cell fluid and cell wall mechanics under compression at varying dryness levels, turgor pressures, strain rates, and cell sizes.
finding: The model effectively simulates cell shrinkage and compression mechanics during drying; fresh cells show higher stiffness and stress under compression while dried cells exhibit lower stiffness, and turgor pressure is the dominant factor controlling mechanical response.
dataset: Simulated single apple parenchyma cells (100 µm diameter)
metric: Stress-strain relationships, force-deformation curves, turgor pressure (200 kPa normal)
result: 3D model reproduces expected stiffness reduction with drying (X/X0 from 1.0 to 0.1)

problem: How do immune system parameters and stochastic processes influence the early stages of tumor initiation at the cellular level?
method: A semi-stochastic cell-level computational model using Green's function solutions for chemokine diffusion, stochastic differential equations for cell migration (random walk, chemotaxis, haptotaxis), and individual-based simulation of epithelial cells, tumor cells, and T-cells in 2D and 3D.
finding: A 'good' immune system response (higher T-cell mobility and appearance rate) can completely eliminate tumor cells in early stages, while a 'bad' immune response leads to tumor cell fractions reaching ~30%; stochastic variation between runs is considerable but qualitatively consistent.
dataset: Simulated tissue domain (radius 40 µm) with epithelial cells, tumor cells, T-cells
metric: Tumor cell fraction, T-cell counts, epithelial cell counts over time
result: Good immune system: tumor fraction ~0%; bad immune system: tumor fraction ~30%

problem: What mechanisms underlie the occasional onset and spontaneous termination of non-fatal cardiac arrhythmias, and what role do gap junction alterations in ischemic tissue play?
method: A 2D computational model of 128×256 cardiac cells using the Beeler-Reuter electrophysiology model, simulated in the NEURON environment on a BlueGene/Q supercomputer, with ischemic areas modeled via reduced and randomly fluctuating gap junction conductance and rectifying properties.
finding: Post-ischemic alteration of gap junction conductance in a small cardiac area can reproduce all main types of non-fatal arrhythmia (PVBs, bigeminy, trigeminy, couplets, triplets, VT); three potential treatments identified: surgical ablation, pharmacological reduction of rectifying properties, and decreased gap junction conductance.
dataset: Simulated 2D cardiac tissue (12.8×4.1 mm, 32,768 cells) validated against ECG recordings from Physionet Database
metric: Qualitative comparison to experimental ECG patterns (PVB, bigeminy, trigeminy, couplets, triplets, VT)
baseline: Control conditions (normal gap junction conductance 30 nS, no ischemic area)
result: Model reproduces all major non-fatal arrhythmia types; 130 sec simulation in ~3 hours on 1024 processors

problem: How does Hedgehog (Hh) signaling maintain compartment boundaries in the Drosophila wing disc, and why do perturbations of Hh signaling affect cell sorting far from the boundary?
method: A cellular vertex model of tissue mechanics coupled to an explicit Hh signaling pathway model (diffusion + transduction PDEs/ODEs), simulating cell growth, division, and sorting in the wing primordium with smo mutant clone experiments for validation.
finding: Hh signaling effects on cell sorting extend surprisingly far from the compartment boundary; the best simulation fit assumes Hh signaling redistributes tension locally within a cell (relative to neighbors' signaling status) rather than altering overall cell tension, suggesting homotypic interaction of a putative Hh target molecule at the cell surface.
dataset: Drosophila melanogaster wing imaginal discs with smo3 mutant clones
metric: Clone roundness (4πA/L²), clone position relative to A/P boundary
baseline: Previous heterotypic interaction model (Landsberg et al.)
result: Homotypic redistribution model recapitulates non-local sorting effects of smo- clones observed experimentally

problem: How have single-cell multi-omics technologies evolved over the past decade, and what are their current capabilities, applications, and limitations?
method: Comprehensive review of established and cutting-edge single-cell multi-omics technologies, evaluated across throughput, resolution, modality integration, uniqueness, and accuracy dimensions.
finding: Single-cell multi-omics have advanced cell lineage tracing, tissue/cell atlas production, tumor immunology, and spatial mapping; key remaining challenges include benchmarking computational integration tools, high sequencing costs, limited single-cell coverage, and low throughput for spatial technologies.

problem: What is the current state of genome-scale metabolic model (GEM) reconstruction across organisms, and what are their key applications in systems-level metabolic studies?
method: Review of GEM reconstruction pipelines and simulation approaches including flux balance analysis, with case studies spanning strain engineering, drug targeting, enzyme function prediction, pan-reactome analysis, and multi-organism interaction modeling.
finding: GEMs have been successfully applied to strain development (e.g., E. coli d-phenyllactic acid production increased from 0.55 to 1.62 g/L), drug targeting in pathogens like P. falciparum and A. baumannii, enzyme function discovery, and modeling multi-cell interactions; future integration of protein allocation and structural information will expand capabilities.
dataset: GEMs across bacteria, archaea, and eukarya (e.g., E. coli iJO1366, Y. lipolytica iYLI647, P. falciparum, A. baumannii)
metric: Production titer (g/L), gene essentiality prediction accuracy
baseline: E. coli base strain XB201T (0.55 g/L d-phenyllactic acid)
result: Engineered strain: 1.62 g/L d-phenyllactic acid; fed-batch: 13.9 g/L aromatic polymer

problem: Can energy deficiency at cellular and network levels explain the excitotoxic loss of dopaminergic SNc neurons in Parkinson's disease, and which neuroprotective interventions are most effective?
method: A multi-scale hybrid excitotoxicity model (HEM) combining Izhikevich spiking neuron models for STN/GPe with a detailed biophysical conductance-based model of SNc neurons including calcium dynamics, energy metabolism (glycolysis, oxidative phosphorylation), and apoptotic pathways, simulated in MATLAB.
finding: Energy deficiency causes SNc neurons to shift from tonic to bursting firing and leads to calcium accumulation and apoptosis; glutamate inhibition and apoptotic signal blocker therapies can halt SNc cell loss, while other interventions (dopamine restoration, DBS, calcium channel blockers) only slow progression.
dataset: Simulated rat basal ganglia network (STN-GPe-SNc) based on anatomical data
metric: Percentage of surviving SNc cells, firing frequency (Hz), intracellular calcium concentration (mM), extracellular dopamine concentration
result: Glutamate inhibition and apoptotic signal blockers halt SNc cell loss; SNc tonic firing 3-8 Hz, bursting ~20 Hz

problem: Existing hash table-based short read aligners like MAQ are too slow for large-scale resequencing and do not support gapped alignment for single-end reads.
method: Burrows-Wheeler Alignment tool (BWA) using backward search with Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT) for efficient short read alignment against large reference genomes, supporting mismatches, gaps, base space (Illumina) and color space (SOLiD) reads.
finding: BWA is ~10-20x faster than MAQ while achieving similar accuracy; it supports gapped alignment for single-end reads and outputs in SAM format compatible with SAMtools for downstream variant calling.
dataset: Simulated reads from human genome; real Illumina and SOLiD sequencing data
metric: Alignment speed (CPU seconds), mapping accuracy (error rate of confident mappings), number of mapped reads, memory usage
result: 10-20x faster than MAQ with similar accuracy; requires ~2.3 GB memory for human genome

problem: Existing propensity scale methods for predicting linear B-cell epitopes have limited accuracy, and improved prediction methods are needed for vaccine and diagnostics design.
method: Hidden Markov model (HMM) trained on AntiJen B-cell epitope data combined with Parker propensity scale as a weighted sum (BepiPred: 0.60 HMM + 0.40 Parker), evaluated via ROC curves on independent datasets.
finding: The BepiPred method (HMM + Parker combination) significantly outperforms individual propensity scale methods and the standalone HMM on the independent HIV validation dataset.
dataset: Pellequer dataset (literature B-cell epitopes), AntiJen database, HIV epitopes from Los Alamos HIV database
metric: AUC-ROC (Aroc), sensitivity at fixed specificity, paired t-test P-values
baseline: Parker propensity scale (Aroc=0.586), Levitt scale (Aroc=0.572), standalone HMM (Aroc=0.586)
result: BepiPred Aroc=0.600±0.011 on HIV validation set; sensitivity 53.8% at 60% specificity

problem: Whether the unprimed antigen-specific CD8+ T cell repertoire contains memory phenotype cells, and how such cells arise in the absence of prior immunization.
method: MHC class I tetramer staining with anti-PE magnetic bead enrichment to isolate antigen-specific CD8+ T cells from unprimed mice, flow cytometry phenotyping, adoptive transfer into RAG-/- hosts, and analysis in germ-free mice.
finding: The unprimed CD8+ T cell repertoire contains 'virtual memory' (VM) CD44hi cells specific for foreign antigens, arising via homeostatic proliferation rather than microbial exposure, with rapid proliferation and IFN-γ production capacity distinct from naive CD44lo counterparts.
dataset: C57BL/6J mice, germ-free C57BL/6J mice, RAG-/- OT-1 and gBT1 TCR transgenic mice; OVA-Kb, B8R-Kb, gB-Kb specificities
metric: percentage of tetramer+ cells that are CD44hi, absolute cell counts per mouse
baseline: CD44lo naive phenotype counterparts within the same antigen-specific population
result: ~10-25% of antigen-specific CD8+ T cells in unprimed mice are CD44hi VM phenotype (varies by specificity)

problem: Need for freely accessible, rapid computational prediction of ADME properties, drug-likeness, and medicinal chemistry friendliness of small molecules during early drug discovery when physical samples are limited.
method: Web tool integrating predictive models including BOILED-Egg (GI absorption and BBB permeation), iLOGP (lipophilicity), Bioavailability Radar, ESOL solubility, and drug-likeness filters (Lipinski, Ghose, Veber, Egan, Muegge), built with Python 2.7 backend and OpenBabel/JChem.
finding: SwissADME provides free, login-free access to a comprehensive suite of validated ADME prediction models with user-friendly interface, interoperable with other SwissDrugDesign web tools for end-to-end drug discovery support.
dataset: user-submitted small molecules (SMILES input)

problem: Existing single-cell gene expression analysis tools lack scalability to datasets exceeding one million cells and a unified Python-based framework integrating preprocessing through trajectory inference.
method: Python toolkit built on AnnData (HDF5-backed annotated data matrices) integrating preprocessing, t-SNE/graph-drawing/diffusion map visualization, Louvain clustering, differential expression testing, and diffusion pseudotime for trajectory inference.
finding: Scanpy achieves 5-16x speedup over Cell Ranger R kit and 5-90x over Seurat per analysis step, enabling analysis of 1.3 million cells without subsampling in a few hours on 8 cores, with interactive runtimes for ~100k cells.
dataset: 68,579 PBMCs, 2,700 PBMCs, 1.3 million mouse brain cells (E18, 10x Genomics)
metric: runtime speedup per analysis step, scalability (max cells analyzed)
result: 5-16x speedup vs Cell Ranger, 5-90x vs Seurat; 1.3M cells analyzed in hours on 8 cores

problem: Existing single-cell RNA-seq methods lack throughput, speed, and efficiency for profiling tens of thousands of cells per sample, and cannot resolve host-donor chimerism at single-cell resolution without prior genotype information.
method: GemCode droplet-based scRNA-seq with gel bead barcoding in 8-channel microfluidic chip (~100k GEMs per channel in ~6 min), UMI-based 3' mRNA counting, and de novo SNV calling from transcriptome data for genotype-based cell classification.
finding: System achieves ~50% cell capture efficiency, profiled ~250k cells across 29 samples; SNV-based genotype method detects minor populations as low as 3% from mixed transcriptome data without prior genotype knowledge.
dataset: 293T and 3T3 cell lines, synthetic ERCC RNAs, 68k PBMCs, bone marrow mononuclear cells from HSCT patients
metric: cell capture efficiency, sensitivity, positive predictive value (PPV)
result: ~50% cell capture, ~80% GEM fill rate, >95% sensitivity and PPV for minor genotypes ≥3%

problem: No existing human ventricular action potential model accurately uses undiseased human data and reproduces the full range of physiological behaviors including early afterdepolarizations (EADs) and alternans critical for arrhythmia studies.
method: Hodgkin-Huxley style ionic model formulated with new undiseased human ventricular data separating Ca2+-dependent from voltage-dependent ICaL inactivation, validated against AP morphology, APD rate dependence, restitution, and Ca2+ transients, with CaMK modulation of Ca2+ cycling.
finding: Model reproduces experimental AP morphology, APD rate dependence, and restitution; generates EADs under IKr block during slow pacing and AP/Ca2+ alternans at >200 bpm. CaMK suppression or SERCA upregulation eliminates alternans; INaCa links Ca2+ alternation to AP alternans.
dataset: undiseased human ventricular myocytes and tissue from 140 donor hearts (56% male, age 41±12 years)
metric: APD rate dependence, restitution curves, Ca2+ transient peak and decay, [Na+]i rate dependence
baseline: TP model, GB model, Priebe-Beuckelmann model, Iyer model
result: First human model to reproduce both EADs and alternans (>200 bpm); predecessors failed on one or both

problem: The genome-scale metabolic reconstruction of E. coli K-12 MG1655 needed updating with new genomic and biochemical knowledge and experimental validation of gene essentiality predictions.
method: Constraint-based modeling (FBA) with experimental phenotypic screening of 1,075 Keio Collection gene knockout strains on glucose/lactate/succinate under aerobic/anaerobic conditions, GapFind gap analysis, and comparative genomic mapping to all sequenced E. coli strains.
finding: iJO1366 accounts for 1,366 genes, 2,251 reactions, and 1,136 unique metabolites; accurately predicts growth phenotypes for knockout strains; mapping to sequenced E. coli genomes identified hundreds of unannotated genes in pathogenic strains.
dataset: E. coli K-12 MG1655, Keio Collection (1,075 knockouts), all available sequenced E. coli genomes
metric: growth/no-growth prediction accuracy (gene essentiality), growth threshold <5% of wild-type
result: 1,366 genes (107 new), 2,251 reactions, 1,136 metabolites; accurate essentiality predictions across 4 conditions

problem: Whether macromolecular behavior observed in vitro accurately reflects in vivo behavior, and whether simple excluded-volume crowding models are sufficient to explain intracellular effects on protein stability and diffusion.
method: Atomically detailed Brownian dynamics simulation of E. coli cytoplasm containing 50 most abundant macromolecule types (1,008 molecules, 275 g/L), calibrated to in vivo GFP diffusion (FRAP data), with steric, electrostatic, and short-range hydrophobic interaction models.
finding: Steric-only crowding models overestimate GFP diffusion 3-6x relative to experiment; short-range hydrophobic (quinary) interactions are essential for reproducing in vivo diffusion and protein thermodynamic stabilities. Effects beyond simple crowding must be considered.
dataset: E. coli cytoplasmic proteome (Link et al., 50 most abundant macromolecules), in vivo GFP FRAP data
metric: translational diffusion coefficient (DLtrans), thermodynamic stability (ΔΔG of folding/association)
result: Steric-only model: DLtrans 3-6x too high; full model with ε≈0.1 kcal/mol reproduces experimental GFP diffusion

problem: Researchers in computational physiology and biology repeatedly re-implement common components (meshes, ODE/PDE solvers), slowing progress; need for a reliable, open-source, tested simulation framework.
method: Open source C++ library (BSD licence) with modules for cell-based simulations (on-lattice cellular automata/Potts, off-lattice centre/vertex), cardiac electrophysiology (monodomain/bidomain), ODE/PDE solvers, developed using test-driven development and continuous integration.
finding: Chaste has enabled high-performance defibrillation studies on realistic human cardiac geometries and novel insights into stem cell dynamics in colorectal crypts; its modular design accelerates new applications across physiology and biology.
dataset: human cardiac geometries, colorectal crypt models, tumour spheroid models

problem: Metabolic network reconstructions do not explicitly account for macromolecular synthesis; a genome-scale reconstruction of E. coli's transcriptional and translational machinery is needed to enable quantitative omics data integration.
method: Constraint-based modeling of the 'E-matrix' (12,766 reactions, 423 gene products) reconstructed from >500 publications and 3 databases using template-based reaction formulation in Perl, with gene expression data integrated as flux constraints via FBA.
finding: Model accurately predicted ribosome production without parameterization; in silico rRNA operon deletion showed high RNA polymerase density is needed on remaining operons to maintain ribosome numbers; functional protein modules span multiple subsystems.
dataset: E. coli K-12 MG1655 (genome m56, EcoCyc v10.6)
metric: ribosome production rate, functional module composition, gene expression constraint satisfaction
result: Predicted ribosome numbers match experimental data without parameterization; 303 components across 26 subsystems

problem: Need for a generic, compact, and efficient alignment format supporting diverse sequencing platforms and read lengths, with universal post-processing tools for downstream analysis.
method: SAM/BAM format specification with extended CIGAR notation (supporting splicing, clipping, padding), positional sorting, BGZF-based indexing for random access; SAMtools implements indexing, variant calling, pileup generation, and alignment viewing.
finding: SAM/BAM format with SAMtools enables modular, stream-based processing of genomic alignments without loading entire files into memory; adopted as the standard format for the 1000 Genomes Project.
dataset: 1000 Genomes Project read alignments
result: supports reads up to 128 Mbp, random access via BAM indexing

problem: Characterizing cell composition of complex tissues from gene expression profiles is challenging due to noise, unknown mixture content, and closely related cell types.
method: CIBERSORT uses nu-support vector regression (SVR) for deconvolution with a signature matrix of 22 immune cell types (LM22), validated against flow cytometry on PBMCs, tonsils, lung tissue, and follicular lymphoma biopsies using HGU133 Plus 2.0 microarrays.
finding: CIBERSORT outperformed other deconvolution methods with respect to noise, unknown mixture content, and closely related cell types across fresh, frozen, and fixed tissues including solid tumors.
dataset: PBMCs, tonsils, lung tissue, follicular lymphoma biopsies (Phase III trial NCT00017290), influenza vaccination cohort (N=20)
metric: Correlation with flow cytometry, noise robustness, sensitivity to unknown content
baseline: LLSR, QP, PERT, Abbas et al. deconvolution methods
result: Outperformed all other methods across multiple tissue types and conditions

problem: Simultaneous gene regulatory network reconstruction and cell-state identification from single-cell RNA-seq data remains challenging due to the need to link transcription factor activity to cell heterogeneity.
method: SCENIC combines GENIE3 (random forest co-expression network inference), RcisTarget (TF-motif enrichment for regulon identification), and AUCell (regulon activity scoring per cell), with GRNboost as a scalable Spark-based alternative for large datasets.
finding: Cis-regulatory analysis can guide identification of transcription factors and cell states; SCENIC provides critical biological insights into mechanisms driving cellular heterogeneity in tumors and brain.
dataset: Single-cell RNA-seq compendium from tumors and brain (including Zeisel's mouse brain dataset)
metric: ARI (Adjusted Rand Index), DREAM4/DREAM5 network inference challenge scores
baseline: GENIE3 was top-performing method in DREAM4 and DREAM5 challenges
result: Successfully identified known cell types and regulatory networks; highly comparable results across normalization methods

problem: Completing the 150-year-old effort to identify all human cell types by defining them in terms of distinctive molecular profiles and connecting this with classical cellular descriptions.
method: International collaborative effort proposing high-throughput single-cell molecular profiling (scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics) to create a comprehensive open reference map of cells in healthy human tissues.
finding: An open comprehensive reference map of molecular states of cells would propel systematic study of physiological states, developmental trajectories, regulatory circuitry, and cellular dysregulation in disease.
dataset: All human cell types across healthy tissues (proposed)

problem: Reliable prediction of antibody (B-cell) epitopes, particularly discontinuous epitopes, remains challenging yet highly desirable for vaccine and immunodiagnostic design.
method: ElliPro implements Thornton's method by approximating protein shape as an ellipsoid, calculating residue protrusion index (PI) using center of mass, and clustering neighboring residues based on PI values and distance thresholds (R=6A, S=0.5).
finding: ElliPro performed best among six structure-based methods for discontinuous epitope prediction; best prediction ranked in top three for >70% of proteins and never exceeded rank five.
dataset: Benchmark dataset of 39 discontinuous epitopes from 3D structures of antibody-protein complexes
metric: AUC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, accuracy, Fisher's exact test p-value
baseline: Six structure-based methods (DiscoTope, CEP, and others)
result: AUC=0.732 for most significant prediction per protein

problem: The previous E. coli metabolic reconstruction (iJR904) needed updating to align with latest genome annotation, incorporate thermodynamic information, and expand metabolic coverage.
method: Genome-scale metabolic reconstruction (iAF1260) using constraint-based modeling and flux balance analysis (FBA), integrating EcoCyc/MetaCyc databases, thermodynamic consistency analysis, and gene-protein-reaction (GPR) assignments via SimPheny platform.
finding: iAF1260 is the largest metabolic reconstruction of any unicellular organism, accounts for 1260 ORFs, and can predict high-throughput experimental growth and gene deletion phenotypic screens.
dataset: E. coli K-12 MG1655 genome, EcoCyc/MetaCyc databases
metric: Growth rate predictions, gene essentiality prediction accuracy
result: 1260 ORFs (356 additional), 2077 reactions (1146 additional), 92% experimentally verified ORFs

problem: Understanding how intrinsic noise, spatial heterogeneity, and molecular crowding contribute to stochastic behavior in the inducible lac genetic switch of E. coli.
method: Well-stirred and spatially resolved whole-cell stochastic simulations using kinetic parameters from in vivo single-molecule fluorescence experiments; cryoelectron tomography for 3D spatial cell models; maximum likelihood estimation for parameter extraction from population distributions.
finding: Noise peaks near the switching threshold; spatial heterogeneity in fast-growth cells slightly decreases overall noise due to anomalous subdiffusion and increases repressor rebinding; slow-growth cells show increased mRNA localization and transcriptional burst frequency.
dataset: E. coli lac operon, cryoelectron tomography cell structures (fast-growth and slow-growth conditions)
metric: Noise (coefficient of variation), burst frequency, burst size, repressor rebinding rate, mRNA-protein probability landscapes
result: Slight decrease in noise and increased repressor rebinding for spatially-resolved fast-growth model; linear relationship between burst size and inducer concentration confirmed

problem: Differential analysis of RNA-seq count data is challenged by small replicate numbers, discreteness, large dynamic range, and outliers requiring suitable statistical approaches.
method: DESeq2 uses negative binomial GLM with empirical Bayes shrinkage estimation for dispersions and log fold changes, Cook's distance for outlier detection, and independent filtering for multiple testing adjustment.
finding: Shrinkage estimation improves stability and interpretability of fold change and dispersion estimates, enabling more quantitative analysis focused on the strength rather than mere presence of differential expression.
dataset: RNA-seq count data (Bottomly et al. mouse strains, Pickrell et al. human samples, and other benchmarking datasets)
metric: Sensitivity, precision, FDR control, adjusted p-value (Benjamini-Hochberg)
baseline: DESeq, edgeR, and other differential expression methods
result: Improved fold change stability and FDR control, especially with small sample sizes

problem: Predicting three-dimensional protein structure from amino acid sequence alone—the protein folding problem—especially when no homologous structure is available, has been an open problem for over 50 years.
method: AlphaFold uses a novel deep learning architecture with invariant point attention (IPA), multi-sequence alignments, template structures, and physical/geometric inductive biases; end-to-end differentiable training on PDB structures.
finding: AlphaFold regularly predicts protein structures with atomic accuracy competitive with experimental structures in the majority of CASP14 cases, greatly outperforming all other methods.
dataset: CASP14 benchmark targets, PDB training data, BFD (2.2 billion protein sequences)
metric: GDT_TS (Global Distance Test), lDDT, TM-score
baseline: Other CASP14 participants, previous computational structure prediction methods
result: Median GDT_TS=92.4 on CASP14; accuracy competitive with experiment in majority of cases

problem: The proliferation of multiple chemical file formats creates interoperability challenges across chemistry software and research domains.
method: Open Babel is an open-source chemical toolbox implementing cheminformatics algorithms including format interconversion, conformer searching, 2D depiction, substructure/similarity searching, partial charge assignment, and aromaticity detection.
finding: Open Babel provides a comprehensive solution for chemical file format interoperability, applicable across organic chemistry, drug design, materials science, and computational chemistry, with over 400 citations.
dataset: 110+ chemical file formats
result: Interconverts 110+ chemical file formats; >400 citations over 10 years

problem: Single-cell RNA sequencing captures only static snapshots, posing a fundamental challenge for analyzing time-resolved phenomena such as embryogenesis or tissue regeneration.
method: RNA velocity estimates the time derivative of gene expression state by distinguishing unspliced and spliced mRNAs from standard scRNA-seq protocols; implemented as velocyto.R and velocyto.py pipelines using steady-state and dynamical modeling.
finding: RNA velocity is a high-dimensional vector that predicts future cell states on a timescale of hours; validated in neural crest lineage and reveals the branching lineage tree of developing mouse hippocampus.
dataset: Mouse hippocampus development, human embryonic brain, neural crest lineage, multiple published scRNA-seq datasets (10x Genomics, inDrop, SMART-seq2)
metric: Velocity vector accuracy, lineage prediction concordance with known biology
result: Accurately predicted future cell states on hours timescale; revealed branching lineage tree of mouse hippocampus

problem: Why do unicellular organisms shift to energetically inefficient metabolism at higher growth rates, and how do cell size and ribosomal content co-vary with growth rate?
method: Mathematical models of self-replicating systems integrating metabolism, ribosomes, and enzyme synthesis, with growth rate maximization as the optimization objective.
finding: The shift in metabolic efficiency originates from a tradeoff between investments in enzyme synthesis and metabolic yields for alternative catabolic pathways; these patterns emerge from first principles when optimizing the entire self-replicating system rather than individual subsystems.
dataset: Theoretical models of unicellular organisms
metric: Growth rate
baseline: Models considering only partial physiology (e.g., metabolism alone)

problem: Modeling intracellular processes that depend on spatial locations and stochastic collisions of individual protein molecules requires accurate and efficient spatial simulation tools.
method: Smoldyn 2.1, a particle-based simulator using Smoluchowski-framework algorithms for diffusion, membrane interactions, and reactions of individual molecules in 3D space.
finding: Smoldyn was more accurate and computationally efficient than MCell and ChemCell; a yeast pheromone model showed that secreted Bar1 protease sharpens the concentration gradient to help identify the fittest mating partner.
dataset: Yeast pheromone response system (~200,000 molecules, ~7,000 cubic microns)
metric: Computational efficiency, simulation accuracy
result: 75 min simulated time in ~10 hours runtime

problem: Previous flux balance analyses addressed metabolism or metabolism-regulation coupling independently, without accounting for extracellular signaling cues and their interplay with metabolic and regulatory networks.
method: Integrated dynamic FBA (idFBA) combining stoichiometric reconstructions of signaling, metabolic, and transcriptional regulatory networks with quasi-steady-state assumptions and time-delayed inclusion of slow reactions.
finding: idFBA produced dynamic profiles of biomass and substrate consumption comparable to an equivalent kinetic model, while requiring only linear programming and no exhaustive kinetic parameterization.
dataset: Prototypic integrated system; S. cerevisiae osmoregulation module
metric: Dynamic profiles of biomass production, substrate consumption
baseline: Equivalent detailed kinetic model of the same system
result: Comparable time-course predictions to kinetic model

problem: Determining differential expression in replicated count data from digital gene expression technologies, accounting for both biological and technical variability.
method: edgeR uses an overdispersed Poisson (negative binomial) model with empirical Bayes moderation of dispersion across transcripts to improve inference reliability.
finding: edgeR enables differential expression analysis even with minimal replication (one or two replicates per condition), and is applicable beyond sequencing to other count data such as proteome peptide counts.
dataset: Digital gene expression (DGE) / RNA-Seq count data
metric: p-value, FDR

problem: A pneumonia epidemic of unknown etiology emerged in Wuhan, China in December 2019, requiring identification and characterization of the causative pathogen.
method: Full-length genome sequencing from patient samples, phylogenetic analysis, virus isolation on Vero E6 and Huh7 cells, receptor binding studies, and serum neutralization assays.
finding: The novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV/SARS-CoV-2) shares 79.6% sequence identity with SARS-CoV and 96% with bat coronavirus RaTG13, and uses the ACE2 receptor for cell entry; patient sera could neutralize the isolated virus.
dataset: 5 patient BALF/swab samples, bat coronavirus RaTG13, 2,794 confirmed cases
metric: Genome sequence identity (%)
result: 79.6% identity to SARS-CoV, 96% to bat coronavirus RaTG13

problem: Inferring differential signal in high-throughput sequencing count data requires proper estimation of data variability across the dynamic range and a suitable error model.
method: DESeq models count data with a negative binomial distribution, linking variance and mean via local regression, with variance estimates pooled across genes of similar expression strength.
finding: The method provides robust differential expression analysis for count data with few replicates by sharing information across genes for variance estimation.
dataset: RNA-Seq, ChIP-Seq count data
metric: p-value, adjusted p-value

problem: Simultaneous measurement of multiple modalities in single cells necessitates computational methods that can integrate data types of varying quality to define cellular states.
method: Weighted-nearest neighbor (WNN) analysis, an unsupervised framework that learns cell-specific modality weights to generate an integrated multimodal representation; applied within Seurat v4.
finding: Integrative multimodal analysis substantially improves cell state resolution compared to unimodal analysis, identifying previously unreported lymphoid subpopulations and enabling rapid mapping of new datasets including vaccination and COVID-19 immune responses.
dataset: CITE-seq human PBMCs with 228 antibodies (hundreds of thousands of cells), cord blood 8,617 cells
metric: Cell state resolution, clustering accuracy
baseline: Independent unimodal analysis of RNA or protein separately
result: Improved cell state resolution over unimodal analysis

problem: Translating cancer genomic diversity into therapeutic predictions requires large-scale preclinical models with detailed genetic and pharmacological annotation.
method: Cancer Cell Line Encyclopedia (CCLE): gene expression, copy number, and massively parallel sequencing of 947 cell lines combined with 8-point dose-response pharmacological profiling for 24 drugs across 479 lines; predictive modeling via naive Bayes and elastic net regression.
finding: Identified genetic, lineage, and expression-based predictors of drug sensitivity, including novel associations: AHR expression with MEK inhibitor efficacy in NRAS-mutant lines, SLFN11 with topoisomerase inhibitor sensitivity, and plasma cell lineage with IGF1R inhibitor response.
dataset: 947 human cancer cell lines, 24 anticancer drugs
metric: Drug sensitivity (IC50, 8-point dose response)

problem: High-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing does not capture phenotypic information such as cell-surface protein levels.
method: CITE-seq: oligonucleotide-labeled antibodies enable simultaneous measurement of surface protein and transcriptome in single cells, leveraging DNA-dependent DNA polymerase activity of reverse transcriptase.
finding: CITE-seq successfully integrates cellular protein and transcriptome measurements into a single-cell readout, is compatible with existing scRNA-seq approaches, and scales with throughput increases.
dataset: Single cells with surface protein markers

problem: The fundamental mechanisms generating cell-to-cell variability from identical DNA sequences remain elusive, limiting understanding of developmental plasticity and tumor heterogeneity.
method: Single-cell ATAC-seq (scATAC-seq) integrated into a programmable microfluidics platform to map the accessible genome of individual cells across multiple cell types.
finding: Accessibility variance is systematically associated with specific trans-factors and cis-elements; combinations of trans-factors either induce or suppress variability; single-cell accessibility patterns recapitulate chromosome compartments de novo.
dataset: Hundreds of single cells across 8 mammalian cell types
metric: Accessibility variance scores
baseline: Bulk ATAC-seq (tens of millions of cells)
result: Single-cell aggregate maps closely resemble bulk accessibility profiles

problem: Multicellular systems problems require simulating both biochemical microenvironment and many mechanically/biochemically interacting cells, but existing tools lack scalability or accessibility.
method: Open-source agent-based simulator (PhysiCell) in C++ with multi-substrate biotransport solver, OpenMP parallelization, and built-in sub-models for cell cycling, death, mechanics, and motility.
finding: PhysiCell achieves linear performance scaling and simulates 10^5-10^6 cells on desktop workstations; demonstrated emergent tissue-scale dynamics in tumor spheroids, DCIS, biorobots, and cancer immunology.
dataset: Hanging drop tumor spheroids, ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), synthetic biorobots, cancer heterogeneity/immunology models
metric: Cell count scaling, tumor diameter, computational wall time, linear speedup with threads
baseline: Other 3-D multicellular platforms (CompuCell3D, Chaste, Biocellion, Timothy)
result: ~10^6 cells in 18-day HDS simulation, linear scaling with cell number, feasible on quad-core desktop

problem: Understanding how tumor-induced angiogenesis affects the growth rate, size, and morphology of solid tumors in 3D over long time periods.
method: Glazier-Graner-Hogeweg (Cellular Potts) multi-cell model coupled with PDE-based oxygen and pro-angiogenic factor fields, simulating tumor cell proliferation, hypoxia, necrosis, and endothelial cell chemotaxis.
finding: Vascularized tumors grow faster and transition through distinct morphological phases (spherical → cylindrical → paddle-shaped); avascular tumors form cylinders following blood vessels rather than remaining spherical.
dataset: Simulated 3D vascular tumor with pre-existing vasculature in 850×850×850 µm domain
metric: Tumor volume, growth rate, morphology (diameter), number of cells over time
result: Avascular tumor max diameter ~200 µm; vascularized tumor ~300 µm diameter; 75-day simulation

problem: Several gene-deficiency models produce innate CD8+ T cells with memory phenotype, but the underlying mechanism driving this cell-extrinsic differentiation was unknown.
method: Mixed bone marrow chimeras, flow cytometry for surface markers and transcription factors, Listeria monocytogenes infection model, and genetic crosses with IL-4Rα-/-, PLZF-/- mice.
finding: PLZF+ T cells produce IL-4 that drives memory-like CD8+ T cell development; this mechanism operates in KLF2-, ITK-, and CBP-deficient mice and underlies innate CD8+ T cells in BALB/c mice.
dataset: KLF2-deficient, ITK-deficient, CBP-deficient, and BALB/c mouse models
metric: Flow cytometry (CD44, CXCR3, Eomes, T-bet expression), IFN-γ intracellular staining, OT-I competitive transfer ratios
baseline: Wild-type bone marrow chimeras, all-WT thymic environment
result: Bystander CD8+ T cells show elevated CD44, CD122, Eomes, rapid IFN-γ; IL-4Rα/PLZF double-deficiency abolishes phenotype

problem: As biological network models grow large and revisions frequent, it becomes difficult to manage equations directly or combine previously developed models; no existing approach fully meets transparency, extensibility, and reusability needs.
method: PySB framework embedding rule-based modeling (BioNetGen/Kappa) within Python, using macros for biochemical actions (bind, catalyze, assemble) and ODE integration via scipy/VODE.
finding: PySB enables modular, reusable, and version-controlled biological models as full Python programs; demonstrated by generating 18 EARM apoptosis model variants from shared reusable components.
dataset: Extrinsic apoptosis reaction models (EARM 2.0), apoptosis signaling networks
metric: Model expressiveness, code reusability, number of variant models generated
result: 18 apoptosis model variants from reusable modules; models publicly available on GitHub

problem: Drug development suffers high attrition rates due to poor pharmacokinetic and safety properties; rapid computational ADMET prediction is needed early in the pipeline.
method: pkCSM uses distance-based graph signatures derived from molecular graphs combined with machine learning to build 30 ADMET predictors (14 regression + 16 classification) across absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity.
finding: pkCSM achieves performance as good as or better than existing methods, with significant improvement on 11 datasets; scalable to large screening datasets (>10,000 compounds).
dataset: Multiple ADMET datasets: up to 18,000 compounds (metabolism), 10,000+ (rat toxicity), Caco2, T. pyriformis, minnow toxicity
metric: Correlation coefficient, accuracy, AUC-ROC, RMSE
baseline: Existing ADMET prediction methods (various published benchmarks)
result: Significant improvement on 11 datasets (water solubility, Caco2, rat/T. pyriformis/minnow toxicity, P-gp, CYP450 1A2/C19/2C9)

problem: Predicting toxicity of therapeutic peptides/proteins before synthesis is critical for peptide-based drug discovery, but no prior method used experimentally validated non-toxic peptides.
method: SVM and KNN models using amino acid composition, dipeptide composition, and AAP (amino acid pair) profiles, trained on experimentally validated toxic and non-toxic peptides from multiple databases.
finding: Achieved 54-86% accuracy depending on features; dipeptide composition was the best discriminator; first method using experimentally validated non-B-cell epitopes as negative examples.
dataset: 1805 toxic peptides (ATDB, ArachnoServer, ConoServer, DBETH, SwissProt) + 3893 non-toxic (SwissProt) / 13541 non-toxic (TrEMBL)
metric: Accuracy, MCC, AUC
baseline: Random peptides as non-toxic (previous methods like BTXpred, NTXpred)
result: Dipeptide SVM: accuracy 81.24%, MCC=0.61, AUC=0.88; confirmed dataset accuracy up to 86%

problem: Predicting MHC class II peptide binding affinity and identifying the peptide binding core is critical for understanding adaptive immune responses but existing methods have limited accuracy.
method: NN-align: artificial neural network trained with novel algorithm correcting binding core redundancy bias, incorporating peptide flanking residue information for simultaneous core identification and affinity prediction.
finding: NN-align consistently outperforms TEPITOPE, SMM-align, and NetMHCIIpan across six independent benchmark datasets covering 14 HLA-DR alleles; flanking residues significantly improve accuracy.
dataset: IEDB HLA-DR benchmark (14 alleles, 14,000+ peptide binding values), SYFPEITHI ligands, Lin et al. and Wang et al. benchmarks
metric: AUC-ROC (500 nM binding threshold)
result: Average AUC=0.798 (NN-W) vs TEPITOPE 0.718, SMM-align 0.778; improved on 12/14 alleles

problem: Existing methods for predicting linear B-cell epitopes use random peptides as negatives and small datasets, leading to unreliable performance; improved methods with experimentally validated data are needed.
method: SVM and KNN models using binary profiles, dipeptide composition, and AAP profiles, trained on experimentally validated B-cell epitopes and non-epitopes from IEDB database.
finding: First method using experimentally validated non-B-cell epitopes; achieved 54-86% accuracy; dipeptide composition is the most discriminative feature; models perform differently on validated vs random negatives.
dataset: Lbtope_Fixed (12063 epitopes, 20589 non-epitopes), Lbtope_Variable (14876 epitopes, 23321 non-epitopes), Lbtope_Confirm (1042/1795)
metric: Accuracy, MCC, AUC, sensitivity, specificity
baseline: ABCpred, BepiPred, BCPred (using random peptide negatives)
result: Dipeptide SVM on Lbtope_Fixed: accuracy 81.24%, MCC=0.61, AUC=0.88

problem: Predicting conformational B-cell epitopes currently requires 3D antigen structure; no method exists to predict them from amino acid sequence alone.
method: SVM models using composition profile of patterns (CPP) — encoding amino acid composition within sliding windows — trained on 187 non-redundant antigen chains from IEDB and benchmark datasets.
finding: Sequence-based CPP model achieves performance comparable to structure-based methods (DiscoTope, CEP, PEPITO), demonstrating conformational B-cell epitope prediction is feasible from primary sequence.
dataset: 187 non-redundant protein chains (2261 antibody-interacting residues), benchmark 52 antigens
metric: MCC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity
result: CPP model: MCC=0.73, accuracy=86.59%; comparable to structure-based DiscoTope (MCC=0.20) and CEP (MCC=0.10)

problem: Genome-scale metabolic models predict maximal biomass yield but not actual growth rate, because they rely only on stoichiometry without enzyme kinetic considerations.
method: MOMENT (MetabOlic Modeling with ENzyme kineTics) integrates enzyme turnover rates and molecular weights into constraint-based metabolic modeling, accounting for isozymes, protein complexes, and a physiological bound on total enzyme concentration.
finding: Enzymes catalyzing high-flux reactions have higher turnover numbers (design principle); MOMENT predicts E. coli growth rates correlated with experimental measurements across diverse media, improving upon FBA.
dataset: E. coli K-12 BW25113 grown on diverse carbon sources in M9 minimal medium
metric: Pearson correlation (predicted vs measured growth rate and intracellular fluxes)
result: Pearson r=0.45-0.46 (p<0.001) between turnover rates and fluxes; significant growth rate prediction improvement over FBA

problem: Existing short-read aligners are inefficient or inapplicable for reads >200 bp, and hashing-based tools like BLAT and SSAHA2 are substantially slower in aligned bases per unit time.
method: BWA-SW algorithm using FM-indices for both reference and query sequences, with dynamic programming between a prefix trie and prefix DAWG, accelerated by heuristic pruning of low-scoring matches and seed containment.
finding: BWA-SW is as accurate as SSAHA2, more accurate than BLAT, and several to tens of times faster than both, with dramatically lower alignment error rates especially for longer reads.
dataset: Simulated reads from human genome (100 bp–10,000 bp at 2–10% error rates, ~10 Mbp total per condition)
metric: CPU time (seconds), Q20% (fraction confidently aligned), errAln% (alignment error rate)
result: BWA-SW errAln 0.00–0.17% vs BLAT 0.00–5.47%; 3–40x faster than BLAT, 10–80x faster than SSAHA2

problem: Understanding the biological processes generating somatic mutations in cancer is limited despite all cancers being caused by somatic mutations.
method: Extracted mutational signatures from a 96-class substitution classification (6 base substitution types × 16 trinucleotide contexts) across 7,042 cancers using computational decomposition, validated by orthogonal sequencing and visual curation.
finding: Identified 21 validated distinct mutational signatures; some are ubiquitous (Signatures 1A/1B in 25/30 cancer types), others are cancer-type specific; discovered localized hypermutation (kataegis) in many cancer types.
dataset: 4,938,362 mutations from 7,042 cancers of 30 classes (507 whole-genome, 6,535 exome sequences)
metric: Signature validation rate, mutation prevalence per Mb
result: 21 of 27 signatures validated; mutation prevalence ranges from ~0.001/Mb to >400/Mb across cancer types

problem: Accurate prediction of MHC class I-peptide binding affinity across multiple alleles and peptide lengths (8–11 residues) for immunological applications.
method: Artificial neural networks trained on quantitative affinity data from IEDB and eluted peptide data from SYFPEITHI for 55 MHC alleles, supplemented by position-specific scoring matrices for 67 additional HLA alleles, with a length approximation algorithm.
finding: NetMHC-3.0 achieves high prediction accuracy validated on a large independent dataset, with 75–80% confirmed MHC binders in pathogen proteome predictions.
dataset: IEDB + SYFPEITHI training data; 6,452 non-redundant 9-mer affinity measurements covering 32 HLA alleles for evaluation
metric: AUC, Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC), sensitivity, positive predictive value (PPV)
baseline: Previous NetMHC versions, directly trained 10-mer ANNs
result: AUC=0.86, PCC=0.71 for 9-mers; 76% sensitivity, 78% PPV at IC50<500 nM threshold

problem: Early prediction of drug-induced pro-arrhythmic cardiotoxicity is critical, but current animal models have limited accuracy and raise ethical and translational concerns.
method: In silico drug trials in a population of 1,213 human ventricular action potential models (ORd-based) for 62 reference compounds using pore-block IC50/Hill coefficient drug models, with comparison against rabbit wedge ECGs and hiPS-CM recordings.
finding: Repolarization abnormalities in the population of models predict clinical TdP risk with higher accuracy than single-model biomarkers or animal models; drug-induced changes are consistent across in silico, ex vivo, and in vitro assays.
dataset: 62 reference compounds, 1,213 human ventricular AP models, rabbit wedge ECGs, hiPS-CM Ca2+-transient recordings
metric: Accuracy, sensitivity, specificity for clinical TdP risk classification
baseline: Single ORd model (17% sensitivity), APD prolongation-based classification (80% accuracy, 64% specificity)
result: 89% accuracy overall; 96% accuracy, 100% sensitivity, 92% specificity for high-risk vs safe drug classification

problem: Genome-scale kinetic models of metabolism that faithfully recapitulate effects of multiple genetic interventions are needed for reliable design of overproducing microbial strains.
method: Ensemble modeling approach with elementary kinetic representations, parameterized by a genetic algorithm simultaneously fitting fluxomic data from wild-type and 25 mutant strains across multiple growth conditions; 457 reactions, 337 metabolites, 295 regulatory interactions.
finding: k-ecoli457 achieves substantially higher correlation with experimental product yields than FBA, MOMA, or maximum yield approaches, with 61% of predicted fluxes within experimental error ranges.
dataset: E. coli wild-type and 25 mutant strains (4 growth conditions), 320 engineered strains spanning 24 product metabolites
metric: Pearson correlation coefficient, fraction of predicted fluxes within experimental ranges
baseline: FBA (r=0.18), MOMA (r=0.37), maximization of product yield (r=0.47)
result: r=0.84 for product yields across 320 strains; 61% of fluxes within 1 SD of experimental data

problem: Quantifying how metabolic constraints and transcriptional regulation each contribute to determining metabolic gene activity in an integrated genome-scale model.
method: Steady-state regulatory flux balance analysis (SR-FBA) using mixed integer linear programming to identify metabolic-regulatory steady states, with flux variability analysis to explore alternative solutions.
finding: Metabolic constraints determine 45–51% of gene flux activity states while transcriptional regulation determines 13–20%; 36 genes are redundantly expressed; ~30% remain undetermined, suggesting metabolic variability.
dataset: E. coli integrated metabolic-regulatory model under 110 minimal growth media (66 aerobic, 44 anaerobic)
metric: Fraction of genes with determined expression/activity states, gene expression prediction accuracy
baseline: Standard FBA (metabolic constraints only), Boolean regulatory model alone
result: 45–51% determined by metabolism, 13–20% by regulation, 36 redundantly expressed genes, ~30% undetermined

problem: Testing whether contractile myocytes sense their mechanical microenvironment and alter differentiation, organization, and morphology accordingly.
method: C2C12 myoblasts cultured on collagen-coated polyacrylamide gels of varied elasticity (1–100+ kPa); AFM indentation of excised normal (C57) and dystrophic (mdx) mouse EDL muscle; immunofluorescence for myosin/actin striations.
finding: Myosin/actin striations emerge only on substrates matching normal muscle stiffness (~12 kPa); not on glass, much softer, or stiffer gels including dystrophic-stiffness gels; adhesion strength increases monotonically with substrate stiffness.
dataset: C2C12 myoblasts, C57 normal and mdx dystrophic mouse EDL muscle (n=387 AFM indentations)
metric: Young's modulus (E, kPa), striation presence/absence, cell area, adhesion strength
result: E_normal=12±4 kPa, E_mdx=18±6 kPa; striations only at ~12 kPa matching tissue stiffness

problem: Predicting B-cell epitopes from antigen sequences alone has seen limited accuracy improvement over the past decade, especially for sequence-only methods.
method: Random forest regression trained on epitopes annotated from 160 non-redundant antibody-antigen crystal structures, using residue physicochemical properties and NetSurfP-predicted structural features in a sliding window.
finding: BepiPred-2.0 significantly outperforms BepiPred-1.0 and LBtope on both structural epitope benchmarks and IEDB linear epitope data; training on structurally defined epitopes improves performance even for linear epitope prediction.
dataset: 160 non-redundant antigen-antibody crystal structures (PDB), IEDB linear epitope collection
metric: AUC, AUC10%, positive predictive value (PPV), true positive rate (TPR)
result: AUC=0.62, AUC10%=0.121 on structural epitopes (p<1e-6 vs all baselines)

problem: Invasive tumor dissemination is only incompletely attenuated by protease inhibitors, suggesting unknown compensatory migration mechanisms.
method: 3D collagen matrix migration assays with broad-spectrum protease inhibitor cocktail (MMP, serine, cathepsin inhibitors); time-lapse video and confocal microscopy in vitro; intravital multiphoton microscopy in mouse dermis in vivo.
finding: Near-total protease inhibition induces a mesenchymal-to-amoeboid transition (MAT) with near-undiminished migration rates; cells switch from path-generating proteolytic to path-finding amoeboid squeezing through matrix gaps.
dataset: HT-1080/MT1 fibrosarcoma cells, MDA-MB-231 breast carcinoma cells, mouse dermis (in vivo)
metric: Migration speed, morphology transition percentage (mesenchymal vs amoeboid), collagen degradation
baseline: Untreated proteolytic mesenchymal migration (no protease inhibitors)
result: Near-undiminished migration rates under protease inhibition; conversion from spindle to amoeboid morphology

problem: Providing a comprehensive, publicly accessible catalog of experimentally characterized immune epitopes to support immunology research and vaccine development.
method: Manual curation by PhD-level curators from published literature following standardized guidelines, combined with direct investigator data submissions; complete redesign of database structure and querying interface for IEDB 2.0.
finding: IEDB 2.0 covers ~99% of publicly available peptide epitope data for infectious agents (excluding HIV) and 93% for allergens, with both positive and negative experimental results captured.
dataset: 180,978 literature-curated experiments + 129,186 directly submitted experiments across all tested species
metric: Coverage percentage of publicly available epitope data
result: ~99% coverage for infectious agent epitopes, 93% for allergen epitopes

problem: Understanding how endothelial cells collectively organize into vascular networks and sprouts in vitro through mechanical cell-ECM interactions, bridging single-cell behavior to collective morphogenesis.
method: Hybrid cellular Potts model (CPM) coupled with finite element method (FEM) to simulate cell motility, ECM mechanics, and mechanosensing (durotaxis, strain stiffening, cell traction).
finding: Three biologically plausible assumptions — cell traction, strain stiffening, and durotaxis — suffice to reproduce endothelial cell behavior across three spatial scales: single cells, pairwise interactions, and collective network formation/sprouting.
dataset: In vitro endothelial cells on compliant matrices (H5V murine embryonic heart endothelial cells, bovine aortic endothelial cells on acrylamide gels)
metric: Cell length, cell orientation, spreading area, qualitative comparison to experimental morphology
baseline: Published experimental observations of endothelial cells on compliant substrates
result: Single parameter set reproduces behavior across three spatial scales with isotropic orientation response to applied strain

problem: Exact stochastic simulation of large biochemical networks is computationally prohibitive, limiting the study of single-cell dynamics in realistic biological systems.
method: Hybrid jump/continuous Markov stochastic simulation (HyJCMSS) with adaptive Euler-Maruyama and Milstein integrators, parallelized via MPI for supercomputers.
finding: Hy3S enables efficient and accurate stochastic simulation of large biochemical networks with thousands of reactions, including bistable networks with spontaneous state transitions.
dataset: Nonlinear cycle test, large-scale benchmark (up to 20,000 reactions), bistable multiscale biochemical network with positive feedback
metric: Error in probability distribution, mean, and variance vs system size; computational time
result: Scales to 20,000 reactions; accuracy improves with system size; open-source GPL-licensed

problem: The mechanisms by which unconventional 'virtual memory' CD8+ T cells develop in the absence of antigen-driven activation, and the role of Eomesodermin (Eomes) regulation in this process.
method: Flow cytometry, adoptive transfer, bone marrow chimeras, and in vivo infection models using knockout mice (IFNAR−/−, IRF9−/−, STAT1−/−, Eomes conditional KO) with polyI:C stimulation and Listeria infection.
finding: Type I interferon signaling directly activates Eomes expression in CD8+ T cells through the ISGF3 complex (STAT1/2/IRF9), driving the development, function, and age-dependent expansion of virtual memory CD8+ T cells.
dataset: C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice, IFNAR−/−, IRF9−/−, STAT1−/− knockout strains, 6-10 week old
metric: Cell frequency, absolute counts, Eomes MFI, mRNA expression levels
result: Significant reduction of VM CD8+ T cells and Eomes expression in IFNAR−/− mice; IFNβ-induced Eomes upregulation is IRF9- and STAT1-dependent

problem: HTS analysis projects frequently deviate from standard workflows, requiring custom scripts, but no unified Python framework existed for rapid development across common HTS data formats.
method: Python library (HTSeq) providing parsers for common HTS formats (SAM/BAM, FASTQ, GFF, VCF, BED), genomic interval data structures, and the htseq-count tool for RNA-Seq read counting.
finding: HTSeq enables rapid development of custom HTS analysis scripts by integrating parsers for many common formats; htseq-count correctly handles ambiguous read-gene overlaps for differential expression preprocessing.
dataset: General-purpose tool for high-throughput sequencing data (RNA-Seq demonstrated)
metric: Processing speed (reads/minute), RAM usage
result: 1.2 million reads/minute (~0.6M read pairs), ~250 MB RAM on standard laptop

problem: The gap between known protein sequences and experimentally determined structures remains large, and existing homology modelling tools needed improved accuracy and support for protein complexes.
method: Automated homology modelling with ProMod3 engine using BLAST/HHblits template search, fragment-based loop modelling, TreePack side-chain optimization, CHARMM27 energy minimization via OpenMM, and QMEANDisCo local quality estimation.
finding: ProMod3 significantly improves model accuracy over the previous ProMod-II engine; SWISS-MODEL now supports homo- and heteromeric complex modelling with stoichiometry inference.
dataset: 250 CAMEO target proteins (Oct 2017–Jan 2018 benchmark)
metric: lDDT (Local Distance Difference Test), GDT-HA, TM-score
result: Significantly improved lDDT, GDT-HA, and TM-score vs ProMod-II on 250 CAMEO targets

problem: Mammalian organogenesis involves rapid transformation of germ layers into organs, but a comprehensive single-cell transcriptional atlas of this process was lacking.
method: sci-RNA-seq3 (single-cell combinatorial indexing) profiling ~2 million cells from 61 mouse embryos E9.5–E13.5, with trajectory inference via Monocle 3.
finding: Generated the Mouse Organogenesis Cell Atlas (MOCA) identifying >500 cell subtypes and 56 developmental trajectories spanning essentially every organ system, with thousands of marker genes.
dataset: ~2 million cells from 61 C57BL/6 mouse embryos, E9.5–E13.5
metric: Number of cell subtypes, trajectories, and marker genes; UMIs per cell
baseline: Previous single-cell studies of mid-gestational mouse development
result: 2,058,652 cells profiled, 500+ cell subtypes, 56 trajectories, primordial germ cells detected at 0.01% frequency

problem: Clinical responses to cancer drugs are strongly influenced by genomic alterations, but no large-scale public resource existed to systematically link drug sensitivity to molecular markers for biomarker discovery.
method: High-throughput drug sensitivity screening across cancer cell lines integrated with genomic data (somatic mutations, copy number, transcriptomics) from the COSMIC database, with statistical association analysis.
finding: GDSC provides the largest public resource for cancer drug sensitivity data integrated with genomic markers, facilitating preclinical identification of therapeutic biomarkers.
dataset: ~700 cancer cell lines, 138 anticancer drugs, ~75,000 drug sensitivity experiments
metric: Drug sensitivity (IC50), statistical associations between genomic features and drug response
result: 75,000 experiments across 700 cell lines and 138 drugs; freely available at cancerRxgene.org

problem: Single-cell RNA-seq data manifolds contain both discrete cell types and continuous transitions, but existing methods cannot robustly represent both in a scalable, interpretable map.
method: Partition-based graph abstraction (PAGA) using kNN graph construction, Louvain clustering, and a statistical connectivity measure between partitions to generate topology-preserving maps at multiple resolutions.
finding: PAGA reconciles clustering with trajectory inference, producing consistent topology across multiple datasets and scaling to millions of cells with much higher computational efficiency than single-cell-level analysis.
dataset: 4 hematopoietic datasets, adult planaria, zebrafish embryo, 1.3 million mouse brain neurons
metric: Topology preservation, computational efficiency, cross-dataset consistency
baseline: Existing manifold learning and trajectory inference methods (diffusion maps, force-directed graphs)
result: Consistent topology across 4 hematopoietic datasets; benchmarked on 1 million neurons; much higher computational efficiency

problem: Mechanisms controlling lung progenitor differentiation into mature alveolar cell types are incompletely known due to limited lineage markers and ensemble averaging in bulk transcriptomics.
method: Microfluidic single-cell RNA-seq on 198 cells from mouse distal lung epithelium at 4 developmental stages (E14.5, E16.5, E18.5, adult), with unbiased genome-wide cell classification.
finding: Confirmed the classical model of distal lung epithelial diversity, discovered many novel cell-type markers including transcriptional regulators, and reconstructed the full life cycle of the AT2 cell lineage from bipotential progenitors.
dataset: 198 cells from mouse distal lung epithelium (C57BL/6, E14.5–adult)
metric: Genome-wide transcriptional profiles, marker gene specificity
baseline: Classical model of epithelial cell-type diversity in distal lung based on marker expression and fate-mapping
result: Identified distinct cell populations and novel markers; reconstructed bipotential progenitor maturation along both alveolar lineages

problem: Pooled CRISPR screens are restricted to simple readouts (proliferation, sortable markers), while arrayed screens with transcriptome profiling have much lower throughput.
method: CROP-seq re-engineers the lentiviral LentiGuide-Puro vector to place the gRNA cassette in the 3' LTR, making it part of a polyadenylated mRNA detectable by droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq.
finding: CROP-seq enables pooled CRISPR screens with single-cell transcriptome resolution, maintaining high genome editing efficiency and compatibility with standard cloning protocols.
dataset: K562, Jurkat, HEK293T cell lines; T cell stimulation screen targeting TCR signaling regulators
metric: Genome editing efficiency, functional viral titer, out-of-frame indel frequency
baseline: LentiGuide-Puro (standard pooled CRISPR vector); Perturb-seq, CRISP-seq
result: 90.5% genome editing efficiency (vs 95.5% LentiGuide-Puro), 62.4% out-of-frame indels, comparable functional titers

problem: Conventional splicing-based RNA velocity analyses have fundamental limitations in accurately estimating velocities and predicting cell fate transitions, lacking a framework to extract governing regulatory functions from single-cell transcriptomic data.
method: Dynamo infers absolute RNA velocity from metabolic labeling scRNA-seq, reconstructs continuous vector field functions, applies differential geometry (Jacobian, acceleration, curvature) to extract gene regulations, and uses the least-action-path (LAP) method for transition path prediction and in silico perturbation.
finding: Dynamo overcomes intrinsic limitations of conventional RNA velocity, reveals mechanisms driving early megakaryocyte appearance, elucidates asymmetrical regulation in the PU.1-GATA1 circuit, accurately predicts drivers of hematopoietic transitions, and predicts cell-fate diversions from gene perturbations.
dataset: Human CD34+ hematopoietic stem/progenitor cells (scNT-seq), HL60 cell differentiation (10x scRNA-seq), hematopoiesis clone tracing, scSLAM-seq, sci-fate, scEU-seq
metric: Vector field topology (fixed points, separatrices), Jacobian regulatory network recovery, LAP transition path accuracy
result: Absolute RNA velocity from metabolic labeling overcomes splicing-based velocity limitations; correctly identifies self-activation and mutual inhibition in toggle-switch; accurately predicts hematopoietic transition drivers

problem: Histological staining of tissue samples for pathology is time-consuming, costly, and destructive to the specimen, limiting downstream analyses of the same tissue section.
method: PhaseStain uses a generative adversarial network (GAN) trained on paired quantitative phase images (QPI) from lensfree holographic microscopy and corresponding brightfield images of histologically stained tissue to perform virtual digital staining.
finding: PhaseStain produces virtually stained images from label-free QPI that closely match histologically stained brightfield images across three tissue/stain combinations, with high structural similarity and robustness to phase noise.
dataset: Human skin tissue (H&E), kidney tissue (Jones' stain), liver tissue (Masson's trichrome), ~10 megapixel images per sample (~1.47 mm² FOV)
metric: Structural Similarity Index (SSIM)
baseline: Histochemically stained brightfield microscopy images (gold standard)
result: SSIM = 0.8113 (skin/H&E), 0.8141 (kidney/Jones'), 0.8905 (liver/Masson's trichrome); inference time 0.617s per ~0.45 mm² FOV

problem: Existing human genome-scale metabolic reconstructions lacked consensus, had incomplete metabolite annotations, mass/charge imbalances, and failed to correctly predict energy metabolism such as ATP yield on different carbon sources.
method: Recon 2.2 was developed through manual curation (fatty acid beta-oxidation, oxidative phosphorylation, electron transport chain coupling) combined with automated error checking via linear programming for mass/charge balancing, and semi-automated curation using the SuBliMinaL Toolbox.
finding: Recon 2.2 correctly predicts ATP yield on different carbon sources, integrates all prior Recon 2 versions into a new consensus, and provides the most complete and best-annotated human metabolic reconstruction with improved gene-protein-reaction relationships.
dataset: Human metabolism (genome-scale), E. coli iJR904 reconstruction for validation context
metric: ATP yield prediction accuracy on carbon sources, mass/charge balance completeness, reconstruction size
result: 5324 metabolites, 7785 reactions, 1675 genes; correct ATP yield predictions on multiple carbon sources; all reactions mass- and charge-balanced

problem: Standard Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) cannot explain the switch from respiration to fermentation with carbon overflow at fast growth rates, as it lacks proteome allocation constraints linking metabolism to protein biosynthetic costs.
method: CAFBA extends FBA with a single genome-wide proteome allocation constraint derived from empirical bacterial growth laws, solved efficiently as a linear program (LP) with only 3 empirically determined parameters, using ensemble averaging over unknown protein costs.
finding: CAFBA solutions cross over from respiratory, growth-yield maximizing states at slow growth to fermentative states with carbon overflow at fast growth, quantitatively predicting acetate excretion rate and growth yield in E. coli.
dataset: E. coli K-12 MG1655, iJR904 GSM/GPR reconstruction, glucose-limited growth
metric: Acetate excretion rate, growth yield, overflow onset ratio (λ_ac/λ_max)
baseline: Standard FBA (growth-rate maximization without proteome constraints)
result: Quantitatively accurate acetate excretion and growth yield predictions from only 3 parameters (w_R,0=0.169 h, φ_max=48.4%, λ_max); λ_ac/λ_max ratio robust across conditions

problem: How can systems biology advances support S. cerevisiae as both a model organism for eukaryal biology and a cell factory for industrial production?
method: Review of systems biology approaches applied to S. cerevisiae, covering genomics, metabolic engineering, and computational modeling.
finding: Systems biology underpins advances in both understanding eukaryal biology and engineering yeast as a versatile cell factory for fuels, chemicals, and pharmaceuticals.
dataset: Saccharomyces cerevisiae

problem: Traditional models fall short of modeling and simulating cell function and behavior across scales; how can AI be leveraged to create virtual cells?
method: Perspective article proposing multi-scale, multi-modal large neural network-based AI virtual cell (AIVC) design principles.
finding: AIVCs can transform biological research by enabling high-fidelity simulations of molecules, cells, and tissues, accelerating discoveries and guiding experiments.
dataset: multi-omics cellular data (conceptual)

problem: The mechanism of vacuole biogenesis in plant cells is debated between endosome fusion and ER-derived models.
method: Whole-cell 3D electron tomography at nanometre resolution on Arabidopsis root cells, including wild-type and MVB/fusion mutants.
finding: Central vacuoles are derived from multivesicular body (MVB)-to-small vacuole transition and subsequent SV fusions, requiring functional MVBs and membrane fusion machineries.
dataset: Arabidopsis thaliana root cortical cells
metric: vacuole diameter distribution, statistical analysis of developmental stages
result: SVs 400-1000 nm identified as nascent vacuoles

problem: Whole-cell models require realistic parameters for thousands of molecular reaction rates, but no suitable framework exists for reliable estimation at this scale.
method: Review and comparative analysis of parameter estimation and inference approaches for large-scale dynamical systems.
finding: Much cellular behaviour may be explained by smaller parameter sets; identifying and assessing such sets is becoming possible even for very large systems of equations.
dataset: whole-cell dynamical systems (conceptual)

problem: Continuous isolation and purification of circulating tumour cells (CTCs) from blood based on size characteristics.
method: Optically-induced-dielectrophoresis (ODEP)-based virtual cell filters in a microfluidic system.
dataset: circulating tumour cells (CTCs)

problem: Achieving whole-cell computational models that predict cellular phenotypes from genotype remains a major challenge.
method: Review of emerging principles including rule-based modeling, multi-algorithmic simulation, bioinformatics, and data sharing approaches.
finding: Ongoing efforts in scalable modeling tools are enabling more comprehensive and accurate whole-cell models, including potential models of human cells.
