metric: Survival rate, tumour regression, rechallenge protection
result: 80% survival >5 months with allogeneic vaccine; protected at 5-month rechallenge

problem: Quantifying surface complexation of Np(V) onto whole cells and cell components of Shewanella alga for understanding actinide-microbiological interactions.
method: Acid-base titrations with FITEQL modeling for surface functional groups; batch Np(V) sorption experiments; CCBATCH biogeochemical model for stability constant computation.
finding: Stability constants were similar across whole cells, cell walls, and EPS; sorption patterns explained by combining surface complexation with aqueous Np(V) speciation; log K values similar to aqueous carboxyl and N-containing ligands.
dataset: Shewanella alga strain BrY (whole cells, cell wall, EPS), Np(V)
metric: Stability constants (log K), deprotonation constants (pKa)
baseline: Aqueous carboxyl and N-containing carboxyl ligands
result: log K 1.8-3.9 for various surface functional groups

problem: Data-dependent acquisition incompletely samples peptide ions in complex protein mixtures like whole cell lysates in shotgun proteomics.
method: Statistical model to predict sampling levels for mixtures of specific complexity, relating spectral sampling to relative protein abundance.
finding: 10 analyses required for 95% saturation of protein identifications in yeast lysate; spectral sampling shows a linear relationship with relative protein abundance over 2 orders of magnitude.
dataset: Yeast soluble cell lysate
metric: Sampling saturation level, spectral count, linear dynamic range
result: 95% saturation at 10 analyses; 2 orders of magnitude linear dynamic range

problem: Determining whether the switch from whole-cell to acellular pertussis vaccines will affect protection against B. parapertussis infections.
method: Murine respiratory model comparing Dutch whole-cell vaccine and B. pertussis fimbriae (serotype-2 and -3) against B. pertussis and B. parapertussis infections.
finding: The whole-cell vaccine and B. pertussis fimbriae protected against both B. pertussis and B. parapertussis, though less efficiently against B. parapertussis; fimbriae were as efficacious as WCV against B. pertussis, indicating antigenic distinction between species' fimbriae.
dataset: Murine respiratory model, B. pertussis, B. parapertussis
metric: Protection efficacy (colonization reduction)
result: Fimbriae as efficacious as WCV against B. pertussis; lower efficacy against B. parapertussis

problem: Understanding the mechanism by which cytochrome P-450 reacts with polyhalomethanes in biodehalogenation, including products, stoichiometry, and kinetics.
method: Kinetic and stoichiometric analysis of cytochrome P-450 cam oxidation by polyhalomethanes, comparing isolated enzyme, deuteroheme, and whole-cell Pseudomonas putida (PpG-786) reactivity.
finding: All reactions proceed via hydrogenolysis of the carbon-halogen bond. P. putida is a valid whole-cell model for P-450 reductase activity. Iron-bonded carbenes are not intermediates.
dataset: Pseudomonas putida PpG-786, cytochrome P-450 cam
metric: reaction rate, stoichiometry
result: trichloronitromethane fastest, chloroform slowest substrate

problem: Evaluating whether inactivated whole-virus or Tween-ether-extracted FIV vaccines can protect against systemic and mucosal challenge in a feline AIDS model.
method: Immunization with whole inactivated virus (WIV) or Tween-ether viral protein (TEV) vaccines, followed by intravenous cell-free virus or intravaginal FIV-infected cell challenge in cats.
finding: Neither vaccine conferred resistance to systemic IV challenge, but WIV vaccination significantly reduced viral burden following intravaginal mucosal challenge despite similar infection rates.
dataset: feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) cat model
metric: viraemia levels, infection rate
result: significantly lower viral burdens in WIV vaccinees after intravaginal challenge

problem: Understanding the role of secreted microRNAs as a novel form of intercellular communication in multicellular organisms.
method: Literature review and synthesis of studies on microvesicle-dependent and RNA-binding protein-associated miRNA trafficking between cells.
finding: Secreted miRNAs are actively trafficked between cells via microvesicles or RNA-binding proteins, functioning as endogenous miRNAs in recipient cells to regulate multiple target genes simultaneously.
dataset: multicellular organism intercellular communication

problem: Modeling skin heating and injury mechanisms from prolonged millimeter-wave electromagnetic radiation exposure, including both direct thermal and indirect biochemical injury.
method: Transport-lattice system model coupling a layered skin model with a whole-body model to estimate heat and chemical transport in rat skin in vivo at 94 GHz.
finding: The model's thermal response matches experimental measurements. Prolonged low-intensity exposure (75 mW/cm², 40 min) causes significant direct thermal injury, while short high-intensity exposure (1 W/cm², 10 s) causes less. Biochemical release may cause additional indirect injury at distant sites.
dataset: anesthetized rat skin in vivo, 94 GHz MMW exposure
metric: subcutaneous and colonic temperature, thermal-injury indicator
baseline: experimental temperature measurements from prior studies
result: model consistent with measured temperatures at 75 mW/cm² for 40-min exposure

problem: Addressing the shortage of donor organs for transplantation by developing whole-organ tissue engineering approaches using decellularized scaffolds.
method: Review of decellularization methods for vascular organs (heart, liver, lung) to produce acellular 3D biologic scaffolds, followed by recellularization with selected cell populations and bioreactor culture.
finding: Decellularized organ scaffolds provide naturally occurring 3D matrices that can be reseeded with cells. Preliminary animal model studies show proof of concept, but significant challenges remain.
dataset: heart, liver, lung donor organs; animal models
result: encouraging proof-of-concept results in animal models

problem: Understanding how catecholamine-induced changes in individual neuron responsivity translate to improved signal detection at the network level.
method: Neural network model of catecholamine effects combined with parallel distributed processing computer simulation of signal detection performance.
finding: Changes in individual neuron responsivity do not improve single-element signal detection, but the same changes in a network of elements do improve whole-network signal detection, accounting for CNS stimulant effects on human performance.
dataset: simulated neural network, human signal detection task
metric: signal detection performance, signal-to-noise ratio
result: network-level signal detection improvement from responsivity changes

problem: Developing a model to estimate leukocyte composition from whole-blood DNA methylation data using cell counts as reference.
method: Training a predictive model using whole-blood DNA methylation profiles with cell counts as reference.
finding: The model provided precise estimates for common cell types.
dataset: whole-blood DNA methylation, leukocyte cell counts
metric: estimation precision
result: precise estimates for common cell types

problem: Comparing the efficacy of syngeneic vs allogeneic cytokine-expressing whole-cell tumor vaccines and determining the influence of irradiation in a murine melanoma model.
method: Prophylactic vaccination of syngeneic C57/BL6 and allogeneic C3H mice with B16-F10 melanoma cells transfected with GM-CSF, IL-4, or IL-7, followed by tumor challenge. CTL assays and fluorescent dye tracking of vaccine cells.
finding: GM-CSF and IL-7 significantly protected syngeneic mice but not allogeneic mice. Irradiated vaccines of either tissue-type are rapidly processed, suggesting cytokine transfection effects are transient and of limited long-term use in situ.
dataset: B16-F10 murine melanoma, C57/BL6 and C3H mice
metric: survival, CTL activity, p-value
result: GM-CSF and IL-7 significant protection in syngeneic (P<0.05), not in allogeneic (P>0.05)

problem: Developing a general mathematical framework for simulating both single-channel and whole-cell ionic currents from arbitrary first-order kinetic schemes of ion channel conformational states.
method: Solution of the master equation (Markov process) expressed in terms of eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the kinetic system, with numerical implementation for complex schemes.
finding: The derived expression provides a general recipe for whole-cell current calculation and conditional probabilities for single-channel simulation, demonstrated with an activation-inactivation model.
dataset: ion channel kinetic models

problem: Characterizing the full spectrum of microglial activation states in neurodegeneration and identifying disease-relevant expression signatures not captured by mouse models alone.
method: Co-regulated gene module derivation from CNS myeloid transcriptional profiles across diverse mouse models, single-cell RNA-seq analysis, and whole-tissue RNA profiling from human neurodegenerative disease with cellular composition correction.
finding: Identified microglial subsets expressing interferon-related or proliferation modules distinct from previously reported disease-associated microglia. Human AD tissue showed elevated neurodegeneration modules plus modules not implicated by mouse models alone.
dataset: mouse tauopathy and AD models, human AD brain tissue
metric: gene module expression, single-cell clustering
result: identified novel microglial activation states and AD-specific modules absent in mouse models

problem: Proposing a model for how the number of repeated structures (e.g., somites) is controlled during animal morphogenesis.
dataset: animal morphogenesis, somitogenesis

problem: Determining the synaptic mechanisms underlying frequency and intensity tuning in auditory cortex neurons, particularly the role of inhibition.
method: In vivo whole-cell recordings from single neurons in rat auditory cortex to measure excitatory and inhibitory receptive fields and their temporal dynamics.
finding: Excitatory and inhibitory receptive fields overlap almost exactly, contradicting lateral inhibition models. Balanced inhibition rapidly truncates excitatory responses within 1-4 ms, increasing temporal precision rather than establishing tuning.
dataset: rat primary auditory cortex neurons
metric: receptive field overlap, spike timing precision
result: inhibition truncates spiking within 1-4 ms

problem: Characterizing endothelial cell gene expression changes in an animal model of early-onset Fuchs' endothelial corneal dystrophy (FECD).
method: Whole genome expression analysis of corneal endothelial cells in a mouse model of FECD.
finding: First endothelial whole genome expression analysis in an animal FECD model; identified COX2 up-regulation as warranting further investigation for its role in disease.
dataset: mouse model of early-onset FECD, corneal endothelial cells
metric: gene expression levels
result: endothelial COX2 up-regulation identified

problem: Developing dye-sensitized solar cells optimized for efficient power generation under ambient indoor lighting conditions.
dataset: dye-sensitized solar cells, ambient lighting

problem: Developing and characterizing a thin nano-porous bi-layer latex coating system to preserve Gluconobacter oxydans as a high-intensity whole-cell biocatalyst.
method: Entrapping G. oxydans in acrylate/vinyl acetate co-polymer coatings (<50 µm), measuring intrinsic Michaelis-Menten kinetics in microbioreactors, determining diffusivities via diffusion cell, and validating with a diffusion-reaction model.
finding: High reaction rates (80-100 g/L·h) achieved with cellcoat effectiveness factors of 0.22-0.49, which are 20-fold greater than previously reported. About 50% viability lost during drying.
dataset: Gluconobacter oxydans, D-sorbitol to L-sorbose oxidation
metric: reaction rate (g/L·h), effectiveness factor, viable cell density
baseline: previously reported G. oxydans whole-cell oxidation systems
result: 80-100 g/L·h reaction rate, 20-fold higher effectiveness factor

problem: Determining the detailed atomic structure of the membrane-associated Torpedo nicotinic acetylcholine receptor in the closed-channel form.
method: Cryo-electron microscopy of helical tubes (342 images) with crystallographic refinement of the density map placed in an artificial unit cell.
finding: Refined 4Å structure reveals the complete receptor including ligand-binding and intracellular domains. ACh-coordinating residues are far apart in the closed channel, and comparison with AChBP suggests how ligand binding initiates channel opening via loop closure and rotational movements.
dataset: Torpedo nicotinic acetylcholine receptor
metric: R-factor, R-free, resolution
baseline: previous lower-resolution structures, AChBP crystal structure
result: R-factor 36.7%, R-free 37.9% at 4Å resolution

problem: Characterizing the energy savings achievable by dynamically reducing active cells in cellular access networks during low-traffic periods.
method: Analytical models optimizing energy savings by switching off cells while maintaining coverage through remaining active cells, considering both continuous and discrete cell layout constraints.
finding: Significant energy savings are possible by reducing active cells during low-traffic periods while maintaining whole-area coverage through the remaining cells.
dataset: cellular access network models
metric: energy savings

problem: Determining whether the microtrabecular lattice observed in the cytoplasmic ground substance of cultured cells is a genuine structure or a specimen preparation artifact.
method: High voltage transmission electron microscopy of cultured cells (WI-38, NRK, chick embryo fibroblasts) with various fixation methods (chemical and freezing), critical-point drying validation, and comparison with plastic-embedded specimens.
finding: The microtrabecular lattice (3-6 nm strands forming a 3D network) is preserved under diverse fixation and drying methods and closely represents the nonrandom structure of living cytoplasmic ground substance, not an artifact.
dataset: WI-38, NRK, chick embryo fibroblast cultured cells; erythrocyte and protein solution models
metric: structural preservation across fixation methods
result: lattice preserved by chemical and nonchemical fixation; 3-6 nm diameter strands

problem: Validating Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH) as a drug target for novel anti-malarial chemotherapy.
method: High-throughput screening for PfDHODH inhibitors, selectivity profiling against human DHODH, whole-cell parasite assays, and lead optimization of triazolopyrimidine series with in vivo mouse model testing.
finding: Multiple chemical scaffolds show potent and selective PfDHODH inhibition with strong enzyme-to-parasite activity correlation. An optimized triazolopyrimidine analog is orally bioavailable with good in vivo efficacy, validating PfDHODH as a drug target.
dataset: Plasmodium falciparum, in vivo mouse malaria model
metric: enzyme inhibition potency, selectivity ratio, oral bioavailability, in vivo efficacy
result: strong selectivity for malarial over human enzyme; oral bioavailability and in vivo efficacy demonstrated

problem: Understanding how tumour cell heterogeneity from genetic mutations affects the geometry and invasive structure of solid tumours during early vascular growth.
method: Hybrid mathematical model combining discrete individual tumour cells with continuous concentrations of extracellular matrix, matrix-degradative enzymes, and oxygen, examining cell-cell and cell-matrix adhesion interactions.
finding: Individual cell interactions, particularly cell adhesion properties, critically influence tumour shape and invasive structure, with heterogeneous cell populations producing distinct geometric patterns.
dataset: simulated solid tumour invasion model
metric: tumour geometry, invasion patterns

problem: Resolving the inconsistency between discrete biome-based vegetation representation in climate models and the physiological parameterizations needed for biogeochemical coupling.
method: Simulations with NCAR LSM using satellite-derived (1 km) plant functional type (PFT) distributions as continuous patches within grid cells, compared at 3°, 2°, and 1° resolutions.
finding: Continuous PFT representation produces direct responses in ground temperature, evaporation, and albedo, reduces model sensitivity to spatial resolution, and provides a necessary bridge between climate and ecosystem models.
dataset: 1-km satellite vegetation data, NCAR LSM global simulations
metric: ground temperature, evaporation, albedo, sensible/latent heat flux
result: reduced model sensitivity to resolution with continuous PFT representation

problem: Proposing a mechanosensory transduction mechanism by which osteocytes sense small in vivo bone strains through fluid shear stresses on their cell processes.
method: Biot's porous media theory combined with fiber-filled channel flow theory to model fluid flow through proteoglycan-filled canalicular annuli surrounding osteocytic processes, relating whole-bone loads to local fluid shear.
finding: Predicted pore relaxation time (1-2 s) matches experimental measurements, and predicted fluid shear stresses (8-30 dyn/cm²) match levels known to elicit intracellular Ca²⁺ responses. The canalicular pore space fiber spacing is ~7 nm.
dataset: osteocyte canalicular system, bone loading models
metric: pore pressure relaxation time, fluid shear stress
baseline: previous predictions (100-fold too short relaxation times)
result: relaxation time 1-2 s, shear stress 8-30 dyn/cm², fiber spacing ~7 nm

problem: Testing whether polyploidy (whole-genome duplication) can accelerate the rate of evolutionary adaptation.
method: In vitro evolution experiments comparing haploid, diploid, and tetraploid yeast, with mathematical modeling, whole-genome sequencing, and phenotypic analysis of evolved clones.
finding: Tetraploids adapt significantly faster than haploids and diploids, driven by higher rates of beneficial mutations with stronger fitness effects including aneuploidy, chromosome loss, and point mutations.
dataset: Saccharomyces cerevisiae haploid/diploid/tetraploid strains
metric: adaptation rate, fitness effects of mutations
result: tetraploids significantly faster adaptation

problem: Extending whole-powder-pattern fitting without a structural model (Pawley method) from neutron to X-ray powder diffraction data.
method: Individual profile fitting to establish angle-dependent profile-shape model, then whole-powder-pattern fitting with unit-cell-constrained peak positions and independent integrated intensities, applied to α-SiO₂, α-Al₂O₃, CaCO₃, and mixed ZrO₂.
finding: Peak shift correction has the largest influence on fit quality. Asymmetry and angular-dependent intensity decay must be modeled. The method was successfully applied to mixed monoclinic-tetragonal ZrO₂.
dataset: α-SiO₂, α-Al₂O₃, CaCO₃, mixed monoclinic/tetragonal ZrO₂
metric: overall pattern fit quality, refined parameter accuracy
result: successful whole-pattern fitting of X-ray data including mixed-phase ZrO₂

problem: Developing a theory for how cellular organization evolved, particularly the transition from communal horizontal gene transfer-dominated evolution to vertically inherited lineages.
method: Theoretical model based on the conjecture that horizontal gene transfer (HGT) dynamics are determined by recipient cell organization, with analysis of the transition from communal to lineage-based evolution.
finding: Aboriginal cells were simple enough for HGT to alter all componentry, making communal evolution the primary driver. As cell designs became more complex, a 'Darwinian Threshold' was crossed where vertical inheritance became dominant.
dataset: theoretical model of early cellular evolution

problem: Testing whether hydrostatic pressure can be locally regulated in animal cells, contradicting models that predict instantaneous pressure equilibration across the whole cell.
method: Using cell blebs as local pressure reporters, locally perfusing cortex-relaxing drugs on one side of blebbing cells, and modeling cytoplasm as a poroelastic contractile network.
finding: Pressure does not equilibrate across the cell on scales of ~10 µm and ~10 s. A poroelastic model where cytosol flows through a contractile elastic network accounts for spatially heterogeneous pressure transients.
dataset: blebbing animal cells
metric: pressure equilibration length and timescale
baseline: incompressible viscoelastic continuum / viscous liquid models
result: non-equilibration on scales of ~10 µm and ~10 s

problem: Developing a tractable analytical framework for downlink SINR analysis in heterogeneous cellular networks with flexible, biased cell association policies.
method: Multi-tier stochastic geometry model with randomly located base stations characterized by transmit power, path loss exponent, spatial density, and user admission bias; Cox proportional hazards-like derivations for outage and rate.
finding: Neither the number of BSs nor tiers changes outage probability or average ergodic rate in interference-limited full-loaded HCNs with unbiased association. Biasing alters performance metrics in predictable ways.
dataset: heterogeneous cellular network model (macro/pico/femtocells)
metric: outage probability, SINR CDF, average ergodic rate, minimum average user throughput
result: closed-form SINR distributions; outage invariant to BS count without biasing

problem: Elucidating the cellular and synaptic mechanisms by which patient antibodies cause anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.
method: Application of patient NMDAR antibodies and Fab fragments to cultured rat hippocampal neurons with surface receptor quantification, whole-cell patch-clamp recordings, and in vivo antibody infusion in Lewis rats.
finding: Patient antibodies cause selective, titer-dependent, reversible decrease in NMDAR surface density via antibody-mediated capping and internalization, specifically reducing NMDAR-mediated synaptic currents without affecting AMPA receptors or other synaptic proteins.
dataset: patient NMDAR antibodies, cultured rat hippocampal neurons, female Lewis rats
metric: NMDAR surface density, miniature EPSC amplitude, antibody titer correlation
baseline: Fab fragments (no cross-linking), AMPA receptor currents
result: selective NMDAR decrease correlating with antibody titer; no effect on AMPA currents

problem: Reviewing the molecular genetics of T-cell antigen receptor genes and understanding the mechanisms of MHC-restricted antigen recognition.
method: Review of cloning and structural analysis of TCR alpha, beta, and gamma chain genes, including organization, ontogeny, rearrangement patterns, and diversification mechanisms.
finding: TCR genes follow the immunoglobulin paradigm in structure and diversification, with important differences. The rules governing T- and B-cell activation differ, and TCR ligands remain poorly characterized.
dataset: T-cell receptor alpha, beta, gamma genes

problem: Minimizing current redistribution inequality in gate-commutated thyristors (GCTs) caused by uneven impedances along the current path from GCT circles to the gate contact.
method: Whole GCT wafer hybrid model combining physics-based cell models with lumped parameter models, optimized using Tabu search heuristic algorithm with physical concentric contact rings for impedance measurement.
finding: An optimized set of GCT cell numbers for a 4-inch wafer was obtained, reducing current redistribution inequality by 20%.
dataset: 4-inch GCT wafer
metric: current redistribution inequality
result: 20% reduction in current redistribution inequality

problem: Determining the synaptic locus (presynaptic vs postsynaptic) of long-term potentiation (LTP) expression in hippocampal CA1.
method: Whole-cell voltage clamp recordings in hippocampal slices with statistical analysis of synaptic variability, including failure rate analysis and postsynaptic dialysis experiments.
finding: LTP involves presynaptic enhancement of transmitter release, as evidenced by decreased trial-to-trial variability and reduced proportion of synaptic failures. LTP triggering is postsynaptic but expression is presynaptic.
dataset: rat hippocampal CA1 slices, MGN-LA pathway
metric: synaptic variability, failure rate, EPSC amplitude
result: significant decrease in synaptic failures during LTP

problem: Evaluating the mouse intranasal/nasopharyngeal challenge model as a potency and release assay for whole-cell pertussis (wP) vaccines.
method: Mouse nasopharyngeal challenge model for testing whole-cell pertussis vaccine potency.
finding: The mouse nasopharyngeal challenge model could serve as a potency and release assay for wP vaccines, though direct correlation with clinical vaccine efficacy requires further investigation.
dataset: mouse nasopharyngeal challenge model, whole-cell pertussis vaccines
metric: potency

problem: Understanding the molecular and cellular mechanisms of the epithelial to mesenchymal transition (EMT) and its relevance to cancer metastasis and organ fibrosis.
method: Review of transcription factor regulatory networks, posttranscriptional and epigenetic programs, and developmental EMT studies as models for pathological EMT.
finding: EMT is now established as critical in the metastatic cascade, regulated by complex transcriptional loops coupled to epigenetic programs, and linked to cell survival, stemness, inflammation, and immunity. It constitutes a promising therapeutic target.
dataset: epithelial tumors, organ fibrosis models, embryonic development

problem: Sequencing the genome of moso bamboo, the first species in the Bambusoideae subfamily, to understand its biology and rapid growth.
method: Whole-genome shotgun sequencing and assembly, gene prediction with cDNA and deep RNA-seq support, clustered gene family analysis, and collinearity analysis for whole-genome duplication detection.
finding: The 2.05-Gb assembly (95% coverage) contains 31,987 genes. Bamboo underwent whole-genome duplication 7-12 MYA, generating gene duplicates involved in shoot development. A potential connection between drought-responsive and flowering genes was identified.
dataset: Phyllostachys heterocycla var. pubescens (moso bamboo)
metric: genome coverage, gene count
result: 2.05-Gb assembly, 95% coverage, 31,987 genes, WGD 7-12 MYA

problem: Understanding how unconnected glioblastoma cells, lacking tumor microtube network connections, contribute to whole-brain colonization and invasion.
method: In vivo imaging, single-cell transcriptomics, calcium imaging, and analysis of tumor cell migration patterns (Lévy-like movement) in mouse models and human glioblastoma samples.
finding: Unconnected glioblastoma cells in neuronal/neural-progenitor-like states receive synaptic input from neurons, use neuronal migration mechanisms, and are driven by neuronal activity to form new tumor microtubes and increase invasion speed.
dataset: mouse glioblastoma models, human glioblastoma tissue, single-cell transcriptomics
metric: invasion speed, calcium signal complexity, Lévy-like movement patterns
result: neuronal activity increases invasion speed and de novo TM formation

problem: Quantifying memory CD8 T cell number, distribution, and recovery biases across tissues to reassess immunosurveillance models.
method: Parabiosis and quantitative immunofluorescence microscopy of mouse memory CD8 T cells, comparing results with standard lymphocyte isolation methods.
finding: Standard isolation methods fail to recover most cells and bias against certain subsets. Resident memory T cells greatly outnumber recirculating cells in non-lymphoid tissues, and homing patterns do not conform to hypothesized migration models.
dataset: mouse memory CD8 T cell population across tissues
metric: cell recovery rate, resident vs recirculating cell ratio
result: residents greatly outnumber recirculating cells in non-lymphoid tissues

problem: Determining whether lower vascular-normalizing doses of anti-VEGFR2 therapy can reprogram the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment to enhance cancer vaccine immunotherapy.
method: Comparison of low (normalizing) vs high (antivascular) doses of anti-VEGFR2 antibody in combination with whole cancer cell vaccine in murine breast cancer models, with macrophage polarization and T-cell infiltration analysis.
finding: Lower normalizing doses are superior to high doses in promoting M2-to-M1 macrophage polarization, CD4+/CD8+ T-cell infiltration, and enhancing vaccine efficacy in a CD8+ T-cell-dependent manner.
dataset: immune-tolerant and immunogenic murine breast cancer models
metric: macrophage M1/M2 polarization, T-cell infiltration, anticancer efficacy
result: lower doses superior in M1 polarization, T-cell infiltration, and vaccine efficacy

problem: Designing a self-organizing multilayered neural network (Cognitron) capable of unsupervised pattern recognition.
Testing the cancer multi-hit model prediction that normal individuals carry stable populations of cancer-prone mutant cells by searching for p53-mutated keratinocyte clones in normal human skin.
method: Whole-mount preparations of human skin with immunohistochemical detection of p53-mutated keratinocyte clones, comparing sun-exposed and sun-shielded skin.
finding: Normal skin contains frequent clonal patches of p53-mutated keratinocytes (60-3000 cells) at >40 cells/cm², covering up to 4% of epidermis. Sun-exposed skin has more frequent and larger clones, showing sunlight acts as both mutagen and tumor promoter.
dataset: normal human skin (sun-exposed and sun-shielded)
metric: clone frequency (cells/cm²), clone size, epidermal coverage (%)
result: >40 clones/cm², up to 4% epidermis, clones 60-3000 cells

problem: Identifying prognostic factors for overall survival in metastatic renal cell carcinoma patients treated with VEGF-targeted agents.
method: Retrospective analysis of 645 anti-VEGF therapy-naïve mRCC patients from 7 centers using Cox proportional hazards regression with bootstrap validation.
finding: Six independent prognostic factors identified (4 MSKCC factors + neutrophils + platelets > ULN). Three risk groups: favorable (mOS not reached, 2y OS 75%), intermediate (mOS 27 mo, 2y OS 53%), poor (mOS 8.8 mo, 2y OS 7%).
dataset: 645 metastatic RCC patients from 7 US/Canadian centers
metric: overall survival, C-index, hazard ratio
result: C-index 0.73; mOS: favorable NR, intermediate 27 mo, poor 8.8 mo

problem: Resolving the debate over whether long-term potentiation (LTP) expression in hippocampal CA1 involves presynaptic or postsynaptic modification.
method: Whole-cell voltage clamp recordings in hippocampal slices with analysis of synaptic variability, failure rates, and effects of prolonged postsynaptic dialysis on LTP triggering vs expression.
finding: LTP is robustly recorded with improved signal resolution. Postsynaptic dialysis blocks LTP triggering but not expression. Changes in synaptic variability and decreased failures during LTP indicate significant presynaptic enhancement of transmitter release.
dataset: rat hippocampal CA1 slices
metric: synaptic variability, failure proportion, signal-to-noise ratio
baseline: pre-LTP baseline responses, conventional intracellular recordings
result: decreased failure proportion and variability during LTP

problem: Providing a comprehensive reference on the theory and applications of dislocations in materials science.
Understanding how phosphatidylcholine (PC) and phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) metabolism regulates lipid, lipoprotein, and whole-body energy metabolism and their roles in disease.
method: Review of dietary studies and knockout animal models examining the effects of altered PC/PE ratios on energy metabolism, lipid droplet dynamics, mitochondrial function, and disease progression.
finding: Abnormal PC/PE ratios in various tissues influence energy metabolism and are linked to fatty liver disease, impaired liver regeneration, atherosclerosis, insulin resistance, and obesity. Hepatic PC synthesis impairment disrupts VLDL secretion.
dataset: dietary studies, knockout animal models, mammalian cell membranes
metric: PC/PE molar ratio, VLDL secretion, lipid droplet size
result: abnormal PC/PE ratios linked to fatty liver, atherosclerosis, insulin resistance, obesity

problem: Developing a mathematical model linking bursting electrical activity in pancreatic β-cells to whole-body glucose-insulin dynamics.
dataset: pancreatic β-cells, glucose-insulin regulatory system

problem: Understanding the regional heterogeneity of the mammalian sinoatrial node in terms of cellular properties, ionic currents, gap junctions, and its functional implications for cardiac pacemaking.
method: Review of experimental studies from single SA node cells to whole animals, examining morphology, action potential configuration, ionic current densities, connexin expression, and autonomic regulation.
finding: SA node heterogeneity (gradient or mosaic model) is essential for dependable pacemaking: it allows the SA node to drive atrial muscle without electrotonic suppression, promotes antegrade propagation, prevents reentry, and enables pacemaker shift under pathophysiological conditions.
dataset: mammalian sinoatrial node, single cells to whole animal
metric: ionic current densities, connexin expression, action potential configuration
result: heterogeneity enables electrotonic resistance, antegrade propagation, and pacemaker shift

problem: Flux balance analysis (FBA) assumes evolutionary optimality to predict metabolic fluxes, but this assumption may not hold for genetically engineered knockouts not exposed to long-term evolutionary pressure.
method: Introduced minimization of metabolic adjustment (MOMA) using quadratic programming to predict knockout flux distributions closest to wild-type, compared with FBA using linear programming.
finding: MOMA showed significantly higher correlation with experimental flux data for E. coli pyruvate kinase mutant than FBA, and was further supported by knockout growth rate data.
dataset: E. coli JM101 (wild-type), E. coli PB25 (pyruvate kinase mutant)
metric: Correlation with experimental flux data
result: MOMA significantly higher correlation than FBA for knockout mutant

problem: How protein complexes around transmembrane proteins control the polarized architecture of epithelial cells and cellular junctions in a whole animal model.
method: Review of genetic and molecular analysis of epithelial polarity and cellular junctions in Drosophila.
finding: Membrane asymmetry and junction differentiation are controlled by complexes assembling around DE-cadherin, Crumbs, and Neurexin IV, but mechanisms of assembly and polarized distribution remain unclear.
dataset: Drosophila melanogaster epithelial cells

problem: Rebuilding whole genome 3D structures from sparse haploid single-cell Hi-C contact data.
method: Minimal Go-model approach for genome structure reconstruction from single-cell Hi-C data.
dataset: Haploid single-cell Hi-C data

problem: Comprehensive genomic analysis of single cells is needed to assess clonal evolution in heterogeneous populations such as micrometastatic cells.
method: PCR-based global DNA amplification from single cells followed by comparative genomic hybridization (CGH), loss of heterozygosity (LOH), and DNA sequencing.
finding: The technique reliably detects gross and fine-scale copy number variations (monosomy X, trisomy 21, tumor-specific alterations) from single cells, with congruent CGH, LOH, and sequencing data attesting to fidelity.
dataset: Single human cells (male, Down's syndrome, tumor cells, bone marrow micrometastatic cells from cancer patients)
result: Reliable detection of monosomic X, trisomic 21, and tumor-specific deletions/amplifications from single cells

problem: The role of host red blood cell deformability in determining circulatory characteristics and transmission competence of Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes is poorly understood.
method: Transgenic parasite line for 3D live imaging, in vitro capillary assays, and 3D finite element whole-cell modeling of gametocyte-infected red blood cells.
finding: Immature gametocytes show decreased RBC deformability (enabling sequestration) followed by rapid restoration in mature stages, and morphological changes also play a role in tissue distribution.
dataset: Plasmodium falciparum gametocyte-infected red blood cells
metric: Cell deformability, capillary transit
baseline: Uninfected red blood cells, asexual-stage infected RBCs
result: Deformability decrease in immature then restoration in mature gametocytes

problem: How the KCNQ1-G589D mutation, which disrupts AKAP-mediated PKA/PP1 targeting to the IKs channel, leads to ventricular arrhythmia during sympathetic stimulation.
method: Integrative computational models of beta-adrenergic signaling, myocyte excitation-contraction coupling, and 3D action potential propagation in a rabbit ventricular wedge.
finding: The mutation alone does not prolong QT, but coupled with beta-adrenergic stimulation it induces QT prolongation and afterdepolarizations; increasing heart rate is protective.
dataset: Rabbit ventricular wedge model (computational)
metric: QT interval, transmural dispersion of repolarization
result: QT prolongation and afterdepolarizations under beta-adrenergic stimulation with mutation

problem: CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing generates DNA double-strand breaks whose full spectrum of chromosomal structural consequences is poorly characterized.
method: Single-cell whole-genome sequencing of model cells and clinically relevant cells after CRISPR-Cas9 editing, combined with microscopy for nuclear defects.
finding: CRISPR-Cas9 editing generates micronuclei and chromosome bridges that initiate chromothripsis, extensive chromosome rearrangement, as an on-target consequence.
dataset: Model cell lines, clinically relevant human cells
result: Chromothripsis detected as on-target consequence of CRISPR-Cas9 DSBs

problem: Whether the spatial periodicity of entorhinal grid cell firing patterns corresponds to intrinsic electrophysiological properties of entorhinal neurons along the dorsal-ventral axis.
method: In vitro whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from rat entorhinal cortex layer II neurons at different dorsal-ventral positions.
finding: Subthreshold membrane potential oscillation frequency scales with anatomical position along the dorsal-ventral axis, matching grid cell field spacing and supporting a model of grid cell generation.
dataset: Rat entorhinal cortex layer II neurons
metric: Subthreshold oscillation frequency
result: Oscillation frequency correlates with dorsal-ventral position and grid field spacing

problem: Glucokinase plays a key role in glucose homeostasis; activating it pharmacologically could treat type 2 diabetes.
method: Discovery and characterization of small-molecule glucokinase activators (GKAs) that increase glucose affinity and Vmax, tested in isolated rodent islets and multiple rodent diabetes models.
finding: GKAs augmented hepatic glucose metabolism and insulin secretion, lowered blood glucose, improved glucose tolerance, and increased hepatic glucose uptake in rodent type 2 diabetes models.
dataset: Rodent pancreatic islets, multiple rodent models of type 2 diabetes
metric: Blood glucose levels, glucose tolerance test results, hepatic glucose uptake
result: Lowered blood glucose and improved glucose tolerance in multiple rodent diabetes models

problem: Evaluating whether fish embryo acute toxicity (FET) testing and RTgill-W1 cell line assays can serve as in vitro alternatives for whole-effluent toxicity (WET) testing.
method: Compared LC50 from fathead minnow FET test and EC50 from RTgill-W1 cells against fish larvae acute toxicity for 12 WET-relevant chemicals, including cardiotoxicity and hypoosmotic condition evaluations.
finding: Significant correlation between FET and larvae LC50 but not RTgill-W1 EC50 and FET LC50; hypoosmotic conditions improved RTgill-W1 sensitivity; both in vitro models showed good predictability overall.
dataset: Fathead minnow embryos, RTgill-W1 cells, 12 WET-relevant chemicals (Cd, Cu, Ni, ammonia, 3,4-DCA, benzalkonium chloride, etc.)
metric: LC50, EC50, correlation between in vitro and in vivo
baseline: Fish larvae acute toxicity test (in vivo)
result: Significant correlation between FET and larvae LC50; both in vitro models show good predictability

problem: Current AFM techniques for measuring cell mechanical properties require substrate adhesion, making them unsuitable for nonadherent cells like leukocytes.
method: Developed AFM method using microfabricated wells to immobilize nonadherent cells, applied Hertzian elastic model to compare deformability of myeloid and lymphoid leukemia cells and neutrophils.
finding: Myeloid HL60 cells are 18x stiffer than lymphoid Jurkat cells and 6x stiffer than neutrophils, which may contribute to leukostasis in acute leukemia.
dataset: Human HL60 (myeloid leukemia), Jurkat (lymphoid leukemia), neutrophils
metric: Young's modulus (E_infinity)
result: HL60: 855±670 Pa, Jurkat: 48±35 Pa, neutrophils: 156±87 Pa

problem: Existing equations for predicting tissue-to-plasma partition coefficients (Kpu) are inaccurate for moderate-to-strong bases due to unique electrostatic interactions with tissue acidic phospholipids.
method: Developed a mechanistic equation incorporating drug-acidic phospholipid electrostatic interactions estimated from blood cell binding data, validated for 28 moderate-to-strong bases across 11 rat tissues.
finding: The new equation achieved 89% of predictions within 3-fold of experimental values across 11 tissues, compared to 45% for published equations; brain and lung predictions were less accurate.
dataset: 28 moderate-to-strong bases (pKa >= 7), rat tissues (adipose, bone, gut, heart, kidney, liver, muscle, pancreas, skin, spleen, thymus, brain, lung)
metric: Percentage of Kpu predictions within 3-fold of experimental values
result: 89% within 3-fold vs 45% for published equations

problem: Whole-blood gene expression studies of sepsis have not resolved transcriptional changes at the level of specific immune cell types.
method: Single-cell RNA-sequencing of PBMCs (106,545 cells) and dendritic cells (19,806 cells) from sepsis patients (n=29) and controls (n=36) across three cohorts, validated on 18 public cohorts (n=1,467).
finding: Identified a unique expanded CD14+ monocyte state in sepsis that distinguishes patients from controls across multiple independent cohorts and disease etiologies.
dataset: Sepsis patients (n=29), controls (n=36), validation: 18 cohorts (n=1,467)
metric: Discriminatory power for sepsis vs control classification
result: CD14+ monocyte state validated across 18 cohorts, n=1,467 subjects

problem: Understanding electrocatalysts and catalyst layers for proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells.
dataset: PEM fuel cell catalyst systems

problem: Whether the relative contributions of phylogenetic groups to dissolved organic matter (DOM) utilization depend solely on their relative abundance in bacterial communities.
method: Combined microautoradiography with fluorescent rRNA-targeted oligonucleotide probe hybridization (MICRO-FISH) to track DOM utilization by different phylogenetic groups.
finding: DOM utilization differed across phylogenetic groups and did not correlate with relative abundance; no single group dominated all DOM consumption, suggesting diverse assemblages are essential for complete DOM degradation.
dataset: Natural marine bacterial assemblages from estuarine and coastal environments
metric: Relative representation in DOM-consuming assemblage vs whole community
baseline: Expected utilization based on relative abundance (null hypothesis)
result: Cytophaga-Flavobacter overrepresented in chitin/protein consumption; alpha-Proteobacteria dominated amino acid consumption

problem: Whether dendritic filtering causes distant synapses to have less efficacy than proximal ones at the soma of hippocampal pyramidal neurons.
method: Dual whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from soma and apical dendrite of CA1 pyramidal neurons combined with multi-compartmental computer modeling.
finding: Dendritic EPSP amplitude increases with distance from soma, counterbalancing dendritic filtering so that somatic EPSP amplitude is largely independent of synapse location, primarily due to progressive increase in synaptic conductance.
dataset: Rat hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons
metric: Somatic EPSP amplitude, dendritic EPSP amplitude vs distance
result: Somatic EPSP amplitude independent of synapse location

problem: The molecular identity and functional properties of light-activated channels in Drosophila photoreceptors, particularly the role of the trp gene whose mutation causes response decline during prolonged light.
method: Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from Drosophila photoreceptors comparing wild-type and trp mutant responses, with pharmacological Ca2+ channel blockade (La3+).
finding: Wild-type photoreceptors have at least two classes of light-sensitive channels; trp mutation and La3+ selectively abolish a class with high Ca2+ permeability required for sustained excitation.
dataset: Drosophila melanogaster photoreceptors (wild-type and trp mutant)
result: trp mutation selectively abolishes high-Ca2+-permeability channel class

problem: How kin selection theory explains the evolution of cooperation and altruism in social insect colonies, including the transition to superorganismal organization.
method: Review and theoretical analysis of kin selection theory applied to social insect evolution.
finding: Social insect colonies offer an accessible model for understanding major evolutionary transitions to higher-level organisms, spanning the full range from solitary to superorganismal.
dataset: Social insect colonies (Hymenoptera, Isoptera)

problem: Genomics alone is insufficient to identify therapeutic options for the majority of patients with advanced cancer, necessitating functional drug screening approaches.
method: Integrated whole-exome sequencing with high-throughput drug screening on patient-derived tumor organoids and PDX models from 769 enrolled patients (56 organoid cultures, 19 PDX models).
finding: High-throughput drug screening on patient-derived organoids identified effective drugs and combinations validated in 3D cultures and PDX models, providing personalized therapeutic options.
dataset: 769 cancer patients (56 organoid cultures, 19 PDX models; uterine and colon cancers highlighted)
metric: Drug sensitivity, validation in 3D cultures and PDX
result: Effective drugs identified for 4 cases where genomics alone was insufficient

problem: Modeling hippocampal neural circuitry using electrophysiological data from patch-clamp and dual intracellular recordings.
method: Whole-cell patch clamp and dual intracellular recordings in vitro for hippocampal circuit modeling.
dataset: Hippocampal neurons (in vitro)

problem: Understanding the mechanisms of cell differentiation using plant trichomes as a model system.
dataset: Plant trichomes

problem: Existing blood-brain barrier (BBB) models lack the ability to recapitulate complex BBB functions and model inheritable neurological disorders using patient-specific cells.
method: Created human BBB-Chip using iPSC-derived brain microvascular endothelial-like cells, astrocytes, and neurons on Organ-Chip microfluidic technology.
finding: BBB-Chip exhibited physiologically relevant TEER, accurately predicted blood-to-brain permeability, protected neural cells from plasma toxicity upon whole blood perfusion, and patient-derived iPSCs predicted disease-specific barrier disruption.
dataset: Human iPSC-derived cells, patient-derived iPSCs from individuals with neurological diseases
metric: Transendothelial electrical resistance (TEER), blood-to-brain permeability prediction
result: Accurate permeability prediction; disease-specific transporter deficiency and barrier disruption detected

problem: Understanding the structural organization of pulmonary surfactant membranes and films, and how lipid-protein interactions determine surfactant function.
method: Review of in vivo and in vitro studies on surfactant structure, membrane polymorphism, and lipid-protein interactions, with emphasis on surfactant protein roles.
finding: Surfactant structure involves dynamic membrane polymorphism controlled by specific lipid-lipid and lipid-protein interactions; in vitro reconstituted systems have limited structural complexity compared to native surfactant.
dataset: Pulmonary surfactant (alveolar pneumocytes, lamellar bodies)

problem: Developing a kinetic model for whole-cell biocatalytic production of D-p-hydroxyphenylglycine using separately and co-expressed D-hydantoinase and N-carbamoylase.
method: Kinetic modeling incorporating substrate dissolution, enzymatic conversion, racemization, and membrane transport; parameters evaluated at different pH; model simulation compared with experimental data.
finding: The kinetic model accurately describes both separate and co-expressed enzyme systems; racemization and intermediate transport limit the separate system at neutral pH, while co-expression eliminates intermediate accumulation.
dataset: Bacillus stearothermophilus SD1 (D-hydantoinase), Agrobacterium tumefaciens NRRL B11291 (N-carbamoylase) whole cells
metric: Model fit to experimental reaction data
result: Co-expressed system showed improved performance with negligible intermediate accumulation

problem: The cellular origins of newly regenerated cardiac muscle after injury in zebrafish are unknown due to lack of lineage-tracing technology.
method: Genetic fate-mapping using Cre-recombinase-based lineage tracing of gata4+ and cmlc2+ cardiomyocytes, combined with optical voltage mapping in whole ventricles.
finding: Existing cardiomyocytes, particularly gata4+ cells, are the primary source of regenerated cardiac muscle; electrical conduction is re-established between existing and regenerated cardiomyocytes within 2-4 weeks.
dataset: Zebrafish (ventricular apex resection model)
metric: Labeled cardiac muscle in regenerate, electrical conduction restoration timing
result: gata4+ cardiomyocytes contribute to most regenerated cardiac muscle; conduction restored 2-4 weeks post-injury

problem: Whether neurons are lost in diabetic mouse retinas and whether such loss involves apoptosis, given limited knowledge in mouse models of diabetic retinopathy.
method: Streptozotocin-induced diabetes in C57Bl/6 mice studied over 14 weeks with morphometric analysis, TUNEL, activated caspase-3 detection, and electron microscopy.
finding: Diabetic mice showed ~20-25% fewer cells in the ganglion cell layer at 14 weeks, with evidence of apoptotic cell death (TUNEL+, caspase-3+, chromatin margination).
dataset: C57Bl/6 mice, streptozotocin-induced diabetes model
metric: Ganglion cell layer cell count, retinal layer thickness
result: ~20-25% fewer ganglion cell layer cells at 14 weeks; significant decrease in retinal thickness at 10 weeks

problem: Generating human induced pluripotent stem cells without genetic modification by direct delivery of reprogramming proteins.
method: Direct delivery of reprogramming proteins to human cells.
dataset: Human somatic cells

problem: Existing numerical simulators for single-porosity reservoirs cannot model multiphase flow in naturally fractured (double-porosity) reservoirs.
method: Developed a 3D, multiple-well numerical simulator for two-phase water-oil flow in fractured reservoirs extending Warren-Root single-phase equations, solved by finite difference method.
finding: Imbibition is significant for oil recovery from matrix blocks in fractured reservoirs; the simulator handles uniform and non-uniform fracture distributions and water-oil displacement.
dataset: Conceptual models: five-spot reservoir quadrant, five-well dipping reservoir with water drive
metric: Water-oil displacement, oil recovery
result: Demonstrated significance of imbibition for oil recovery in fractured reservoirs

problem: Optimizing integrated solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) systems requires improved thermomechanical management accounting for interactions among all system components.
method: 3D coupled thermofluid-thermomechanical transient computational model of complete SOFC system including stack, pre-reformer, air pre-heater, and afterburner, validated against measurements.
finding: The computational model enables investigation of heating-up concepts, efficiency gains, and identification of thermomechanically critical regions, replacing prohibitive experiments.
dataset: Forschungszentrum Jülich integrated SOFC module
metric: Thermomechanical stress, temperature distribution
result: Model predictions validated against experimental measurements

problem: How membrane properties and intracellular resistivity of the dendritic tree determine voltage attenuation and synaptic integration in neocortical pyramidal neurons.
method: Simultaneous whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from soma and apical dendrite of layer 5 pyramidal neurons combined with morphologically realistic compartmental models.
finding: Intracellular resistivity is relatively low (~70-100 Ohm-cm) but nonuniformly distributed Cs-sensitive resting conductances create a 'leaky' apical dendrite that differentially influences integration of spatially segregated synaptic inputs.
dataset: Neocortical layer 5 pyramidal neurons (rat)
metric: Voltage attenuation, EPSP integral and peak, intracellular resistivity
result: Intracellular resistivity ~70-100 Ohm-cm; nonuniform conductances significantly increase voltage attenuation

problem: Current IAV vaccines in pigs provide homosubtypic protection but fail against heterologous infections; novel prime-boost strategies are needed.
method: Compared intradermal T-cell epitope DNA vaccine (PigMatrix EDV), intramuscular inactivated whole virus vaccine (FluSure XP), and heterologous prime-boost in 39 pigs challenged with pandemic H1N1 at 9 weeks.
finding: The heterologous prime-boost (epitope DNA + inactivated vaccine) provided the best protection with reduced lung lesions, no IAV antigen in lung tissue at necropsy, and highest IFN-gamma secreting cells.
dataset: 39 IAV-free pigs (3-week-old), pandemic H1N1 challenge
metric: Macroscopic lung lesion scores, IAV antigen in lung tissue, IFN-gamma secreting cells, seroconversion
baseline: Inactivated vaccine alone (INACT-INACT), unvaccinated challenged controls
result: EPITOPE-INACT: no IAV antigen in lung, reduced lesion scores; INACT-INACT: bimodal response

problem: Updating the neurodevelopmental model of schizophrenia in light of new genomic, imaging, and stem cell findings since 2005.
method: Selective review of longitudinal population studies, genetic variant analyses (common and rare/CNVs), post-mortem gene expression, and developmental brain imaging studies.
finding: Genetic variants associated with schizophrenia are diagnostically nonspecific (shared with autism, epilepsy); dimensional rather than categorical psychosis concept is supported; placental pathology and iPSC neuronal phenotyping are promising new directions.
dataset: Whole-population longitudinal studies, post-mortem brain samples, developmental brain imaging cohorts

problem: Accurate parameter identification of photovoltaic cell and module models is needed for evaluation, control, and maximum power point tracking of PV systems.
method: Proposed performance-guided JAYA (PGJAYA) algorithm with probability-based self-adaptive evolution strategies and chaotic perturbation mechanism, tested on single diode, double diode, and PV module models.
finding: PGJAYA consistently obtains the most accurate parameters with strong robustness across all compared algorithms and performs well at different irradiance and temperature conditions.
dataset: Three standard PV model datasets (single diode, double diode, PV module), experimental data from different PV module types
metric: Root mean square error (RMSE), robustness (statistical tests)
result: Most accurate parameters with best robustness among all compared algorithms

problem: Developing effective sentence embedding representations for information retrieval and document matching tasks.
method: LSTM-RNN trained on user click-through data from a commercial web search engine for sentence embedding, applied to web document retrieval.
finding: LSTM-RNN automatically detects salient keywords and allocates them to different cells by topic; the resulting embeddings significantly outperform existing state-of-the-art methods for web document retrieval.
dataset: Commercial web search engine click-through data
metric: Document retrieval accuracy (NDCG implied)
result: Significantly outperforms existing SOTA and Paragraph Vector for web document retrieval

problem: The contribution of the serotonin system to autism spectrum disorder (ASD) pathophysiology remains incompletely understood despite hyperserotonemia being present in >25% of affected children.
method: Review of whole blood serotonin studies, neuroimaging, postmortem analyses, genetic linkage/association studies, and SERT Gly56Ala knock-in mouse model characterization.
finding: Multiple converging lines of evidence implicate the serotonin system in ASD, including heritable hyperserotonemia, rare SERT variants increasing serotonin uptake, and knock-in mouse models recapitulating social/communication/repetitive behavioral deficits.
dataset: ASD patients/families, founder population, SERT Gly56Ala knock-in mice
metric: Whole blood serotonin levels, heritability, behavioral assays
result: Hyperserotonemia in >25% of ASD; SERT Gly56Ala mouse recapitulates biomarker and behavioral phenotypes

problem: The mechanism by which cells with impaired mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) show increased retention of the hypoxia imaging agent Cu(atsm) is not demonstrated in whole-cell models.
method: Used mitochondrial xenocybrid cells with genetically compromised ETC function, metal-responsive element reporter constructs, and parallel hypoxia experiments to study Cu retention from Cu(atsm).
finding: Compromised ETC function leads to upregulated glycolysis, NADH accumulation, and increased intracellular Cu retention through dissociation of Cu(atsm), confirmed in both xenocybrid and hypoxic cell models.
dataset: Mitochondrial xenocybrid cells, control cells, hypoxic cell cultures
metric: Intracellular Cu retention, bioavailable Cu levels, NADH levels
baseline: Control cells with normal ETC function
result: Xenocybrid cells retained more Cu than controls; confirmed bioavailable Cu increase via reporter

problem: The essential factors for improvement of hyperglycemia by bone marrow transplantation in streptozotocin-induced diabetic mice are not fully elucidated.
method: Compared whole body, abdomen-only, and abdomen-excluded irradiation with or without bone marrow cell infusion in STZ diabetic mice; tested on beta cell-specific Vegfa knockout mice.
finding: Whole body irradiation alone (without bone marrow cells) is sufficient to restore beta cell mass; VEGF-A from beta cells is essential for this effect via islet revascularization.
dataset: C57Bl/6 mice, STZ-induced diabetes, beta cell-specific Vegfa knockout mice
metric: Blood glucose levels, relative beta cell mass, islet vascularization
result: Whole body irradiation alone improved hyperglycemia and partially restored islet mass

problem: Lack of a genetically tractable whole-cell system for studying the phagocyte NADPH oxidase, as native phagocytes are resistant to genetic manipulation.
method: Functionally reconstituted the NADPH oxidase in COS-7 cells by transfection of gp91phox, p22phox, p47phox, and p67phox; characterized activation, membrane translocation, and phosphorylation mutants.
finding: COS-phox cells achieved up to 80% of neutrophil superoxide production (arachidonic acid-induced) and recapitulated key regulatory features including p47phox phosphorylation dependence and Rac involvement.
dataset: COS-7 monkey kidney cells, human neutrophils
metric: NADPH oxidase activity (superoxide production), % of neutrophil activity
result: Up to 80% of neutrophil activity (AA-induced), 20% (PMA-induced)

problem: Understanding durability and lifetime of fuel cell systems requires detailed 3D multiphysics modeling of coupled processes across all system components.
method: 3D full-scale system-level multiphysics model using coupled CFD-FEM for thermomechanical fatigue analysis under cyclic load conditions.
finding: The model predicts life expectation and identifies critical regions for thermomechanical fatigue, supporting commercialization of fuel cell technology.
dataset: Fuel cell system (stack, reformer, afterburner, heat exchanger)
metric: Thermomechanical fatigue behavior, life expectation
result: Life expectation predicted using coupled CFD-FEM system model

problem: Characterizing genetic fidelity of patient-derived xenografts (PDXs) for functional breast cancer studies and identifying endocrine-therapy-resistance mechanisms.
method: Whole-genome comparison of PDXs with originating breast cancers (structural, copy number, single-nucleotide variants), combined with endocrine-therapy response studies.
finding: Structural and copy number aberrations were retained with high fidelity in PDXs; ER+ PDXs carried ESR1 mutations, amplification, or translocation producing distinct endocrine-resistance phenotypes not captured by standard cell lines.
dataset: Breast cancer PDXs across major intrinsic subtypes
metric: Variant allele frequency preservation, clonal representation
baseline: Originating tumor genome, standard cell lines
result: High fidelity retention of structural/copy number aberrations; ESR1 resistance variants identified

problem: Understanding the biological roles of PARP-1 beyond DNA repair, as revealed by three independent PARP-1 knockout mouse models.
method: Review of studies using three independently generated PARP-1 deficient mouse models created by homologous recombination, compared with chemical PARP inhibitor approaches.
finding: PARP-1 knockout confirmed its caretaker function under genotoxic stress and unexpectedly revealed roles in cell death after ischemia-reperfusion and inflammation; residual PARP activity was attributed to PARP-2.
dataset: Three independent PARP-1 knockout mouse models
result: Confirmed caretaker function; revealed ischemia-reperfusion and inflammation roles

problem: How translation and ribosome abundance regulate bacterial cell size and shape under growth perturbations is poorly understood.
method: Developed a whole-cell model linking ribosomal abundance with cell geometry, division control, and extracellular environment for rod-shaped bacteria, calibrated with experimental E. coli data.
finding: Cell size maintenance requires a balanced trade-off between ribosome and division protein synthesis; deviations under translation inhibition produce distinct morphological changes matching single-cell experimental data.
dataset: Escherichia coli single-cell data
metric: Cell size, growth rate, morphological changes
result: Model predictions match single-cell E. coli morphological data under translation inhibition

problem: Determining the role of UNC-13 protein in synaptic vesicle fusion and release at the C. elegans neuromuscular junction.
method: Whole-cell voltage-clamp electrophysiology of GABAergic and cholinergic synapses in unc-13 mutant C. elegans, combined with electron microscopy and pharmacological assays.
finding: UNC-13 is essential for synaptic vesicle fusion: unc-13 null mutants have 2-3x more vesicles but almost absent evoked release, and morphologically docked vesicles are not competent for release.
dataset: C. elegans neuromuscular junction (wild-type and unc-13 mutants)
metric: Evoked release amplitude, vesicle density, spontaneous release
result: 2-3x more vesicles but near-zero evoked release in unc-13 nulls

problem: Developing a structured mathematical model of substrate-limited bacterial growth in continuous culture based on feedback control of RNA and protein synthesis.
method: Mathematical model with two interconnected feedback control systems (substrate concentration and intracellular building block concentration), simulating biomass, RNA, protein, and substrate dynamics.
finding: Three types of steady-states exist in continuous culture; the model describes dynamic responses to step changes in dilution rate, substrate concentration, and temperature, with RNA/protein ratio as a physiological state measure.
dataset: Continuous bacterial culture (theoretical model)
metric: RNA/protein ratio, growth rate, steady-state stability
result: Three steady-state types identified; model describes responses to environmental perturbations

problem: Characterizing the properties of sodium and calcium channels in neuroendocrine chromaffin cells.
method: Patch-clamp technique (cell-attached, outside-out, and whole-cell configurations) with pharmacological isolation of Na+ and Ca2+ currents using Co2+ and TTX.
finding: Na+ channels have ~1 pA elementary current at -10 mV with density 1.5-10/um2; Ca2+ channels have density 5-15/um2, show minimal inactivation, and require a two-Lorentzian kinetic model; Ca2+ channel properties depend on permeant ion identity.
dataset: Bovine adrenal chromaffin cells
metric: Single-channel amplitude, open time, channel density, kinetic time constants
result: Na+ channel: 1 pA at -10 mV, 1 ms open time; Ca2+ channel: 0.9 pA at -5 mV, density 5-15/um2

problem: Inferring cell-cell communication from single-cell transcriptomics data by predicting enriched cellular interactions between different cell types.
method: Statistical framework integrated with a database of ligands, receptors, and their interactions, accounting for subunit architecture of multi-subunit complexes.
finding: CellPhoneDB enables evaluation of meaningful biological interactions from scRNA-seq data in ~2 hours for typical datasets.
dataset: Published single-cell transcriptomics datasets (example: 10,000 cells, 19 cell types)
metric: Statistical enrichment significance; runtime performance
baseline: Other ligand-receptor repositories without subunit architecture consideration
result: ~2h runtime for 10GB dataset with 10,000 cells and 19 cell types

problem: Reveal cellular diversity in mouse brain cortex and hippocampus beyond morphological classification.
method: Single-cell RNA-seq transcriptome analysis and clustering of brain cells.
finding: Identified multiple molecular subtypes within seemingly homogeneous cell classes; interneurons of similar type span dissimilar brain regions; oligodendrocytes classified into ~6 molecular subtypes.
dataset: Mouse cortex and hippocampus cells
metric: RNA expression clustering; molecular subtype identification
result: >6 oligodendrocyte subtypes; microglia distinguished from perivascular macrophages

problem: Efficient calculation of molecular polar surface area for predicting drug transport properties in high-throughput screening.
method: Topological PSA (TPSA) based on summation of tabulated surface contributions of polar fragments.
finding: TPSA provides results practically identical to 3D PSA calculation with dramatically reduced computational time.
dataset: 34,810 molecules from World Drug Index
metric: Correlation coefficient between 3D PSA and TPSA; computation speed
result: Correlation = 0.99; 2-3 orders of magnitude speedup

problem: Design efficient drug-like chemical libraries by identifying physicochemical property ranges of known drugs.
method: Quantitative characterization of drug databases by computed properties (log P, molar refractivity, MW, atom count) and qualitative analysis of functional groups and substructures.
finding: Identified property ranges covering >80% of known drugs in multiple therapeutic categories.
dataset: Comprehensive Medicinal Chemistry database and subsets (CNS, cardiovascular, cancer, inflammation, infection)
metric: Property ranges covering >80% of compounds
result: log P: -0.4 to 5.6 (avg 2.52); MW: 160-480 (avg 357); molar refractivity: 40-130 (avg 97); atoms: 20-70 (avg 48)

problem: Correcting batch effects in scRNA-seq data from different laboratories and times that compromise data integration.
method: Mutual nearest neighbors (MNN) detection in high-dimensional expression space without assuming equal or known cell population composition across batches.
finding: MNN correction method outperforms existing methods and scales to large numbers of cells without batch effect bias.
dataset: Multiple droplet-based scRNA-seq datasets
metric: Batch effect correction accuracy; scalability to cell number
baseline: Existing batch correction methods (assuming equal/known population composition)
result: Superior performance vs existing methods; scales to large datasets

problem: Comprehensive assessment of ADMET properties for drugs, pesticides, chemicals, and other compounds.
method: ADMET structure-activity relationship database with 22 qualitative classification models and 5 quantitative regression models trained on curated literature data.
finding: Provides predictive models with high accuracy for estimating ADMET properties of novel chemicals.
dataset: >96,000 unique compounds with >210,000 ADMET annotated data points; 45 ADMET-associated properties
metric: Model predictive accuracy
result: 96,000+ compounds; 45 ADMET properties; high-accuracy prediction models

problem: Quantify absolute metabolite concentrations in E. coli to enable quantitative understanding of cellular metabolism.
method: Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to measure >100 metabolite concentrations in aerobic exponentially growing cells.
finding: Total intracellular metabolite pool ~300 mM with glutamate most abundant; metabolite concentrations exceed Km for most substrate-enzyme pairs except in lower glycolysis where reactions are near equilibrium.
dataset: E. coli (aerobic, exponential growth; glucose, glycerol, or acetate as carbon source)
metric: Absolute metabolite concentration (mM); Km ratios
result: ~300 mM total pool; lower glycolysis intermediates near equilibrium

problem: Understand continuous developmental progression in B cell lymphopoiesis as a continuum of transitional states rather than discrete cell subsets.
method: Single-cell mass cytometry combined with Wanderlust algorithm to align cells onto unified developmental trajectory.
finding: Trajectory reveals regulatory checkpoints where signals are rewired (IL-7/STAT5 pathway) paralleling cellular developmental state changes and immunoglobulin rearrangement events.
dataset: Human hematopoietic stem cells through naive B cells
metric: Trajectory alignment quality; regulatory signal correlation with developmental stage
result: Complete trajectory from hematopoietic stem cells to naive B cells with identified regulatory checkpoints

problem: Understand how bacterial cell composition (RNA, protein) and gene expression change with exponential growth rate.
method: Review and synthesis of studies on chemical composition of E. coli and S. enterica cells at different exponential growth rates.
finding: RNA and protein amounts linked to growth rate through ribosome function; gene expression spectrum varies with growth medium despite same growth rate due to ribosome binding site availability.
dataset: E. coli and S. enterica at different exponential growth rates
metric: Growth rate (doubling time); RNA and protein content; C-period, D-period, ribosome activity
result: Equations describing macromolecular composition as function of doubling time and five parameters

problem: Understand how finite cellular resources create trade-offs that affect protein synthesis, gene expression, and growth rate.
method: Mathematical cell model incorporating protein synthesis mechanisms and resource constraints that respect cellular trade-offs.
finding: Model quantitatively recovers typical growth behavior of individual cells and cell populations despite resource constraints.
dataset: Single growing cells; bacterial and eukaryotic cell populations
metric: Growth rate; protein synthesis rate; resource allocation efficiency
baseline: Models not accounting for cellular trade-offs
result: Quantitative recovery of observed cell growth behavior with resource constraints

problem: Explain presence of memory-phenotype CD4+ T cells specific to viral antigens in adults who were never infected.
method: Flow cytometry analysis of CD4+ T cell populations; TCR specificity analysis and cross-reactivity studies with environmental antigens.
finding: 20-90% (average >50%) of CD4+ T cells show memory phenotype to viral antigens in unexposed adults; mechanism is TCR cross-reactivity to environmental antigens; contrast with newborn naive repertoire.
dataset: Adult blood samples; newborn blood; HIV-1 and influenza-reactive T cell populations
metric: Percentage of memory-phenotype cells; TCR cross-reactivity frequency
baseline: Newborn T cell repertoire (predominantly naive phenotype)
result: 20-90% memory phenotype in unexposed adults (avg >50%) vs naive in newborns

problem: Characterize quantitative relationships between bacterial cell composition and growth rate; understand coupling of gene expression to growth state.
method: Review of bacterial growth laws and phenomenological approaches exploiting quantitative empirical relationships.
finding: Growth laws reveal underlying design principles that enable predictive manipulation of cell behavior without detailed molecular knowledge.
dataset: Bacterial cells across multiple species and growth conditions
metric: Growth rate; cell composition ratios (RNA/protein); gene expression level
result: Quantitative empirical growth laws identified; design principles revealed

problem: Understand the context-dependent and evolvable nature of gene essentiality across organisms.
method: Review of gene essentiality screens in bacteria, yeasts, animals, and human cells.
finding: Gene essentiality is not a static binary property but context-dependent and evolvable; has important implications for cancer and infectious disease treatment strategies.
dataset: Genes from bacteria, yeasts, animals, human cells
metric: Essential vs non-essential classification; context dependency
result: Context-dependent, non-absolute nature of gene essentiality across kingdoms

problem: Enable flexible and reconfigurable robotic assembly systems to adapt to product personalization and hybrid human-robot workflows.
method: Digital twin infrastructure with virtual shopfloor representation (CAD+sensor fusion), semantic data modeling, and real-time synchronization via ROS framework.
finding: Digital twin successfully applied to automotive assembly; infrastructure designed for generic multi-case deployment.
dataset: Automotive assembly case study (car front axle assembly)
metric: System reconfiguration capability; simulation-reality synchronization
baseline: Fixed robotic assembly systems without digital representation
result: Functional digital twin for adaptive robotic assembly in automotive production

problem: Enable computational modeling and simulation of biochemical networks for systems biology analysis without requiring programming expertise.
method: COPASI software package providing integrated tools for steady-state simulation, time-course simulation, parameter scanning, sensitivity analysis, optimization, parameter estimation, and stochastic simulation.
finding: COPASI provides convenient access to multiple analyses through user interface without requiring deep knowledge of numerical algorithms.
dataset: Published biochemical models from BioModels database in SBML format
metric: Model simulation accuracy; convergence; parameter sensitivity
result: Integrated software with multiple analysis capabilities for biochemical networks

problem: Understand iterative development of computational models of cardiac ventricular cell action potentials through cycles of model refinement and experimental validation.
method: Review of cardiac ventricular cell model development history from 1960 onward, analyzing model failures and successes to guide improvements.
finding: Learning from model failures is as important as successes; iterative cycles reveal design principles and point toward next-generation models.
dataset: Cardiac ventricular cells; experimental action potential recordings
metric: Model-experiment agreement; action potential waveform accuracy
result: Iterative improvement trajectory from 1960 to present; demonstrated value of failure analysis

problem: Create a physically and biochemically consistent atomistic model of bacterial cytoplasm integrating structural biology and systems-level metabolism.
method: Reconstruction of metabolic network from gene annotations; assembly of complete atomistic model with predicted protein structures, RNA, complexes, metabolites, ions, and solvent.
finding: Model successfully bridges atomistic and cellular scales, connects physical and biochemical aspects, and unifies structural and systems views.
dataset: Mycoplasma genitalium cytoplasm
metric: Model completeness; structure prediction accuracy; metabolic network coverage
baseline: Prior partial/separate structural and metabolic models
result: Complete atomistic cytoplasm model of M. genitalium for multi-scale simulation studies

problem: Predict temporal stochastic fluctuations in signaling molecules in small cellular compartments and test threshold-crossing models of signal detection.
method: Stochastic simulation of CheYp molecule fluctuations in bacterial chemotactic pathway using molecular-level interaction probabilities derived from experimental concentrations.
finding: Stochastic fluctuation amplitude and duration increase with decreasing volume; at 10% bacterial volume, average fluctuation duration (80.7 ms) is much shorter than observed motor switching behavior (~2.6 s), inconsistent with simple threshold model.
dataset: E. coli chemotactic signaling (flagellar motor control)
metric: Fluctuation amplitude; temporal duration; frequency distribution
result: 80.7 ms average fluctuation at 10% bacterial volume; incompatible with simple threshold model
